### [1] [Position Paper: Post-Solve Robustness in Decision Engines: Feasible Regions and Smoothness Under Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00002)
*Yi-Xiang Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes adding a post-solve robustness layer to MILP decision engines to audit solved incumbents and provide certified evidence about how far solutions can be trusted under real-world perturbations, rather than just outputting nominally optimal plans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) decision engines routinely output nominally optimal plans for high-stakes industrial systems, but deployment rarely matches solve-time assumptions. Small perturbations in costs, demands, or resource availability can invalidate feasibility or trigger discontinuous shifts to qualitatively different solutions. This post-solve robustness gap represents a missing layer in optimization pipelines and a missing evaluation dimension for learning-enabled decision systems.

Method: The authors formalize two central objects: (1) ε-near-optimal feasible neighborhood in parameter space to capture when an incumbent solution remains feasible and near-optimal under perturbations, and (2) solution smoothness in decision space to capture whether nearby alternatives with small combinatorial edits remain competitive. They synthesize relevant approaches from sensitivity analysis, stability analysis, robust optimization, neighborhood search, adversarial testing, and learning-based enhancements.

Result: The paper identifies the need for a post-solve robustness layer that audits solved incumbents and returns solver-backed evidence about how far those solutions can be trusted. This layer would complement rather than replace existing robust optimization or stochastic programming approaches.

Conclusion: The authors call for a unified post-solve robustness layer in optimization pipelines, proposing certified inner approximations, probabilistic robustness estimation with calibrated uncertainty, adversarial robustness margins, and learning-based prediction aligned with solver verification. They introduce a compact reporting template and evaluation protocol to make robustness a first-class output of decision engines.

Abstract: Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) decision engines routinely output nominally optimal plans for high-stakes industrial systems. Yet deployment rarely matches solve-time assumptions: small perturbations in costs, demands, or resource availability can invalidate feasibility or trigger discontinuous shifts to qualitatively different solutions. We argue that this post-solve robustness gap is a missing layer in today's optimization pipelines and a missing evaluation dimension for learning-enabled decision systems. Rather than replacing robust optimization or stochastic programming, the proposed layer audits a solved incumbent and returns solver-backed evidence about how far that solution can be trusted. We formalize two central objects: (i) an $ε$-near-optimal feasible neighborhood in parameter space, capturing when an incumbent remains feasible and near-optimal under perturbations, and (ii) solution smoothness in decision space, capturing whether nearby alternatives with small combinatorial edits remain competitive. We then synthesize the most relevant partial answers from sensitivity and stability analysis, robust optimization, neighborhood search, adversarial testing, and learning-based enhancements, and articulate an agenda for a unified post-solve robustness layer. Concretely, we call for certified inner approximations around the incumbent, probabilistic robustness estimation with calibrated uncertainty, adversarial robustness margins, and learning-based prediction and explanation aligned with solver-backed verification. We conclude with a compact reporting template and evaluation protocol that would make robustness a first-class output of decision engines.

</details>


### [2] [Emergent Collaborative Deliberation in Multi-Model AI Systems: A BFT-Derived Protocol for Epistemic Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00005)
*VD Doske*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Consilium Protocol is a Byzantine Fault Tolerance-derived architecture for structured multi-model AI deliberation that uses engineered cognitive personas and In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation to treat disagreement as signal rather than error. The protocol demonstrates that cognitive persona determines epistemic behavior more than model choice, reveals RLHF blind spots, shows no directional bias, and enables cost-effective deliberation with high reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To treat inter-model disagreement as epistemic signal rather than error in multi-model AI deliberation.

Method: Byzantine Fault Tolerance-derived architecture for structured multi-model AI deliberation; engineered cognitive personas assigned to language models; In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation framework adapted from quantitative finance; analysis across 1,478 deliberation sessions spanning 32 topics in 10 domain categories.

Result: Cognitive persona, not the underlying model, determines epistemic behavior: free edge-inference models costing 0.0002 USD per batch produced comparable analytical output to frontier models costing 10.69 USD; RLHF alignment training creates measurable, domain-specific epistemic blind spots; the protocol exhibits no directional bias of its own; out-of-sample evidence retrieval validated 239 claims with 100% evidence retrieval and surfaced 167 blind-spot discoveries invisible to training-data deliberation.

Conclusion: Consilium Protocol can be used as a cost-effective way to validate structured multi-model AI deliberation; RLHF alignment training creates measurable, domain-specific epistemic blind spots; the protocol exhibits no directional bias of its own; out-of-sample evidence retrieval validated claims with 100% evidence retrieval and surfaced blind-spot discoveries.

Abstract: We present the Consilium Protocol, a Byzantine Fault Tolerance-derived architecture for structured multi-model AI deliberation that treats inter-model disagreement as epistemic signal rather than error. The protocol assigns engineered cognitive personas to language models -- separating what a model is from how it reasons -- and introduces an In-Sample/Out-of-Sample validation framework adapted from quantitative finance to distinguish training-data consensus from empirically grounded conclusions. Across 1,478 deliberation sessions spanning 32 topics in 10 domain categories, we demonstrate that (1) the cognitive persona, not the underlying model, determines epistemic behavior: free edge-inference models costing 0.0002 USD per batch produced comparable analytical output to frontier models costing 10.69 USD; (2) RLHF alignment training creates measurable, domain-specific epistemic blind spots -- contested policy topics exhibit 12.3 percentage points less adversarial challenge than settled science topics, and AI safety topics show asymmetric bias ($Δ$=11.6%) where models challenge claims that AI is dangerous far more vigorously than claims that AI risk is overstated; (3) the protocol exhibits no directional bias of its own (immigration $Δ$=2.3%, renewables $Δ$=1.2%); and (4) out-of-sample evidence retrieval validated 239 claims with 100% evidence retrieval and surfaced 167 blind-spot discoveries invisible to training-data deliberation. Run-to-run reproducibility across randomized model$\times$persona assignments averages $\pm$2.2% standard deviation. Total cost for the complete battery including all overhead: 217 USD. We release the protocol specification under MIT license to enable independent verification.

</details>


### [3] [Deliberative Curation: A Protocol for Multi-Agent Knowledge Bases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00007)
*Steven Johnson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a three-layer governance protocol for AI agent knowledge curation that proves more resilient than majority voting under adversarial conditions, with commit-reveal voting as the most effective component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human platform governance mechanisms fail for AI agents due to: agent statelessness undermining deterrence-based sanctions, model homogeneity violating crowd wisdom independence assumptions, and sycophancy collapsing deliberative consensus.

Method: A multi-layered governance protocol comprising: (1) formalized knowledge artifact lifecycle as labeled transition system; (2) reputation-weighted deliberative voting integrating Beta Reputation with EigenTrust amplification; (3) graduated sanctions adapted for stateless agents.

Result: Protocol outperforms majority vote under adversity (0.826 vs 0.791 precision under moderate adversity, p<0.001), degrades three times more slowly, with commit-reveal vote concealment being most impactful component (8.2-8.6pp improvement). Graduated sanctions remain empirically unvalidated.

Conclusion: The proposed deliberative curation protocol demonstrates a robust and resilient approach to collective knowledge curation by trading modest precision in benign conditions for substantially better performance under adversarial scenarios.

Abstract: As AI agents transition from isolated tools to collaborative participants in shared knowledge ecosystems, governing collective knowledge curation becomes a critical challenge. Human platform governance mechanisms do not transfer directly: agent statelessness undermines deterrence-based sanctions, model homogeneity violates independence assumptions underlying crowd wisdom, and sycophancy collapses deliberative consensus.
  We propose a deliberative curation protocol combining three governance layers: (1) a knowledge artifact lifecycle formalized as a labeled transition system; (2) reputation-weighted deliberative voting integrating Beta Reputation with EigenTrust amplification; and (3) graduated sanctions adapted for stateless agents, including broken agent handling distinguishing malfunction from adversarial behavior.
  We evaluate the protocol through agent-based simulation with 100 agents across seven behavioral archetypes under two adversity scenarios (30 seeds, paired t-tests). The protocol trades modest precision under benign conditions for substantially better resilience under adversity: 0.826 vs 0.791 for majority vote under moderate adversity (p<0.001), widening to 0.807 vs 0.740 under stress (p<0.001). The protocol degrades roughly three times more slowly than majority vote. Ablation analysis identifies commit-reveal vote concealment as the most impactful single component (8.2-8.6pp precision improvement, p<0.001), outperforming reputation weighting and deliberation combined. Graduated sanctions were not exercised in simulation and remain empirically unvalidated.

</details>


### [4] [Agents on a Tree: Pathwise Coordination for Multi-Objective Molecular Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00008)
*Jia Zhang,Tengfei Ma,Tianle Li,Daojian Zeng,Xieping Gao,Xiangxiang Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ATOM: Multi-agent tree-structured search for molecular optimization that maintains alternative evolution trajectories via specialized agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods rely on single policy or fixed scalarization, limiting representation of diverse trade-offs and exploration of multiple promising design trajectories in molecular optimization.

Method: ATOM (multi-agent framework) treating molecular optimization as tree-structured search with specialized agents at each node, coordinated along different paths via global memory, without enforcing global consensus.

Result: ATOM achieves improved Pareto coverage and hypervolume over strong baselines on challenging multi-objective benchmarks involving activity, synthesizability, and ADMET-related properties.

Conclusion: Tree-structured multi-agent coordination effectively addresses challenging multi-objective molecular optimization problems.

Abstract: Multi-objective molecular optimization requires searching vast chemical spaces under conflicting objectives, where early design decisions strongly constrain downstream outcomes. Existing methods typically rely on a single policy or fixed scalarization, which limits their ability to represent diverse trade-offs and to explore multiple promising design trajectories. We propose ATOM, a multi-agent framework that formulates molecular optimization as a tree-structured search. Each node corresponds to an atomic operation and hosts an agent specialized for a particular objective or decision context. Agents coordinate along different paths of the tree rather than enforcing a global consensus, enabling the method to maintain and compare alternative molecular evolution trajectories. A global memory of past optimization behaviors further supports balanced exploration and exploitation across objectives. This tree-structured interaction enables reasoning over long-horizon dependencies inherent in molecular design. Experiments on challenging multi-objective benchmarks involving activity, synthesizability, and ADMET-related properties show that ATOM consistently achieves improved Pareto coverage and hypervolume over strong baselines. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of pathwise multi-agent coordination for molecular optimization. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ATOM-41CE.

</details>


### [5] [Optimal Transport-based Permutation-Invariant Bayesian Optimization of Offshore Wind Farm Layouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00009)
*Antonio Candelieri,Laurens Bliek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Permutation-Invariant Bayesian Optimization (PIBO) method is proposed to exploit symmetry in layout optimization problems like wind farm layouts, outperforming vanilla BO with better layouts and ~50% faster computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vanilla Bayesian Optimization (BO) fails to exploit symmetries in problems where the order of decision variables doesn't matter (optimization over layouts). Real-world problems like wind farm layout optimization exhibit this permutation invariance, where swapping identical wind turbines doesn't affect the objective function value.

Method: The authors propose PIBO (Permutation-Invariant Bayesian Optimization), a method based on Optimal Transport theory that incorporates permutation invariance into the BO framework to handle symmetry in layout optimization problems.

Result: PIBO provides better wind farm layouts compared to vanilla BO while cutting computation time roughly in half, demonstrating both improved optimization performance and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: PIBO successfully addresses symmetry in layout optimization problems like wind farm layout, demonstrating superior performance over vanilla BO with significantly reduced computational cost.

Abstract: Bayesian Optimization (BO) is widely and successfully adopted for solving optimization problems having an expensive-to-evaluate, black-box, and non-convex objective function. However, the vanilla BO algorithm is not able to exploit possible symmetries characterizing the target problem. An intuitive case is given by optimal location problems, whose decision variables refer to a finite set of points within a continuous space, with the order of points not affecting the value of the objective function. We refer to this setting as optimization over layouts to distinguish from optimization over point-clouds where, instead, the order of points counts. As an instance of optimization over layouts we consider a real-life industrial-relevant application, that is the optimization of the layout of an offshore wind farm: given identical wind turbines, switching any pair of them has not any effect on the annual energy production. Based on Optimal Transport theory, we propose a Permutation-Invariant BO approach, namely PIBO, proved to provide better wind farm layouts when compared to the vanilla BO approach while cutting computation time roughly in half.

</details>


### [6] [MindGames Arena Generalization Track: In2AI Solution with Delayed Per-Step Reward Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00017)
*Aliaksei Korshuk,Alexander Buyantuev,Ilya Makarov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel RL training approach for multi-agent language model agents using delayed per-step reward attribution with eligibility gating, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the MindGames Arena benchmark with an 8B parameter model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training language model agents for multi-agent strategic interaction is challenging because action quality depends on future events, rule violations by others, and decisions by other players. Standard RL fails in settings where outcomes are entangled across time and agents.

Method: Delayed per-step reward attribution with eligibility gating: computes rewards only at episode end, propagates them back to originating steps according to task-specific semantics, and excludes steps lacking valid dependent information. Combined with asynchronous rollout generation via vLLM's continuous batching, curriculum-based opponent sampling, and multi-level stratified batch construction.

Result: A single 8-billion-parameter open-source model trained with this method matched or surpassed substantially larger proprietary systems (including GPT-5) in head-to-head play on the MindGames Arena benchmark at NeurIPS 2025, taking first place in both Open and Efficient tracks.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables stable, sample-efficient RL training in multi-agent environments, demonstrating that properly structured reward attribution and training pipeline design can achieve state-of-the-art performance with significantly smaller models.

Abstract: Training language model agents for multi-agent strategic interaction presents a core difficulty: the quality of any action may depend on future events that never materialize, on moves that violate game rules, or on decisions made by other players. Standard reinforcement learning assumes that rewards can be assigned at each step, but this assumption fails in settings where outcomes are entangled across time and agents. We introduce delayed per-step reward attribution with eligibility gating, an episode lifecycle and postprocessing pipeline that computes rewards only at episode end, propagates them back to originating steps according to task-specific semantics, and excludes steps that lack valid dependent information from training. Together with asynchronous rollout generation via vLLM's continuous batching, curriculum-based opponent sampling, and multi-level stratified batch construction, this approach enables stable, sample-efficient RL training in multi-agent environments. We evaluate on the MindGames Arena benchmark at NeurIPS 2025, where a single 8-billion-parameter open-source model trained with our method matched or surpassed substantially larger proprietary systems, including GPT-5, in head-to-head play and took first place in both the Open (unrestricted) and Efficient (<=8B parameters) tracks.

</details>


### [7] [Universal Quantum Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00045)
*Sungyong Chung,Alireza Talebpour*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum-native UQT architecture achieves exact mathematical reasoning with perfect deterministic generalization on compact 5-qubit systems, surpassing classical neural networks that require massive parameter scaling and exhibit stochastic instability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical continuous-space neural networks fundamentally struggle to lock into exact mathematical symmetries like modular arithmetic and non-commutative algebra, requiring massive parameter scaling and showing stochastic instability even after delayed generalization (grokking).

Method: Introduced Universal Quantum Transformer (UQT) using quantum-native computing architecture that leverages physical properties of multi-qubit systems as universal inductive bias. Relies on parameterized geometric phase embedding and SU(2) wave-interference via quantum attention circuits operating on 5-qubit substrate.

Result: UQT perfectly learns cyclic modular arithmetic (Z11) and non-Abelian algebra (S4 permutation group) with mathematically exact, deterministic generalization (called crystallization). Demonstrates massive computational/memory advantages by bypassing quadratic bottleneck of classical self-attention and logarithmically compressing representation dimensions. Successfully deployed on current IBM Quantum NISQ hardware.

Conclusion: Parameterized quantum topology serves as universally superior physical substrate for exact artificial intelligence, establishing quantum-native architectures as fundamentally better for exact mathematical and algebraic reasoning than classical neural approaches.

Abstract: Classical continuous-space neural networks fundamentally struggle to lock into exact mathematical symmetries, such as modular arithmetic and non-commutative algebra. To approximate these discrete logical rules, they often rely on massive parameter scaling, resulting in stochastic instability even after delayed generalization phenomena known as grokking. Here, we introduce the Universal Quantum Transformer (UQT), a fundamentally novel, quantum-native computing architecture that uses the physical properties of multi-qubit systems as a universal inductive bias for exact mathematical and algebraic reasoning. Rather than translating classical neural mechanisms, our framework relies entirely on parameterized geometric phase embedding and $SU(2)$ wave-interference. We demonstrate that the quantum attention circuit, operating on a highly compact 5-qubit substrate, perfectly learns two highly distinct formal classes: cyclic modular arithmetic ($\mathbb{Z}_{11}$) and non-Abelian algebra (the $S_4$ permutation group). While classical attention-based networks exhibit stochastic instability at convergence, the UQT achieves mathematically exact, deterministic generalization. We refer to this phenomenon as crystallization: a step beyond the well-known phenomenon of grokking. Crucially, this framework yields massive computational and memory advantages by theoretically bypassing the quadratic bottleneck of classical self-attention, and by logarithmically compressing the required representation dimension to eliminate the massive over-parameterization inherent to classical networks. Finally, we deploy this architecture on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware, proving its viability on current IBM Quantum computers. These results establish parameterized quantum topology as a universally superior physical substrate for exact artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [8] [Grokers: Bottom-Up Inductive Comprehension and Write-Time Intelligence over Typed Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00050)
*Gregory Magarshak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Grokers is an architecture that shifts comprehension work from query time to write time by having autonomous agents analyze and enrich typed knowledge graphs, enabling zero-cost future queries through structured attribute extraction and dependency traversal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) incurs full comprehension costs at every query, which is inefficient. The authors want to push intelligence to write time to avoid repeated LM calls for the same information.

Method: Autonomous Groker agents analyze nodes in typed stream graphs, extract structured attributes via governed LM calls, and inductively compose understanding upward through dependency relations, creating enriched typed attributes that persist for future queries.

Result: Three formal theorems are proven: 1) Byte-Identity Theorem enabling near-100% KV-cache hit rates, 2) Accumulation Monotonicity Theorem showing increasing LM-free interactions over time, and 3) Dual-Traversal Ordering Theorem establishing correct traversal orderings for generation and comprehension tasks.

Conclusion: Grokers provides a persistent, structured comprehension system that dramatically reduces LM inference costs by moving comprehension work to write time, with formal guarantees about performance and correctness, plus a deterministic alternative to embedding-based semantic search.

Abstract: We present Grokers, an architecture for building persistent, structured comprehension of typed knowledge graphs through bottom-up inductive traversal of dependency subgraphs. Unlike retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which pays full comprehension cost at every query, Grokers pushes intelligence to write time: autonomous Groker agents analyze nodes in a typed stream graph, extract structured attributes via governed language model (LM) calls, and inductively compose that understanding upward through dependency relations, writing enriched typed attributes that serve all future queries at zero additional LM cost. We prove three formal properties: (1) the Byte-Identity Theorem, establishing that context blocks assembled from a transactionally-maintained denormalization index are byte-identical across LM turns between semantic changes, enabling KV-cache hit rates approaching 100%; (2) the Accumulation Monotonicity Theorem, establishing that the fraction of interactions resolved without LM calls is non-decreasing in the number of completed interactions under a governed wisdom library growth protocol; and (3) the Dual-Traversal Ordering Theorem, establishing that top-down generation and bottom-up comprehension are the unique correct traversal orderings for their respective tasks over a dependency DAG, and that their composition closes into a complete generation-comprehension cycle. We further present a deterministic alternative to embedding-based semantic search, with a synonym caching protocol whose LM fallback rate converges to zero for finite-vocabulary domains. A reference implementation is provided in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack.

</details>


### [9] [Product-Aware Deep Autoencoders for Robust Process Monitoring in Multi-Product Cyber-Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00052)
*MD Shafikul Islam,Jordan Carden*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Product-aware anomaly detection outperforms global models in detecting subtle anomalies in multi-grade manufacturing systems by reducing "blind spots" that mask targeted attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global anomaly detection models in Industry 4.0/CPS systems expand decision boundaries to accommodate multiple product grades, creating "blind spots" where subtle anomalies or targeted cyber-physical attacks may be masked, posing security risks in flexible manufacturing environments.

Method: Proposes a Product-Aware Autoencoder that restricts learning domain to grade-specific distributions rather than using global "product-agnostic" models trained on aggregate normal operating data from all product grades.

Result: Product-Aware framework performs comparably to global baseline on standard metrics but shows superior robustness to product-grade-specific modes. In stress tests simulating attack scenarios: global model failed to detect 77.8% of operational deviations while product-aware system achieved 100% detection accuracy.

Conclusion: Generalized anomaly detectors in flexible manufacturing can pose non-trivial security risks, motivating a shift toward mode-aware diagnostic architectures that account for product-specific operating conditions.

Abstract: As Industry 4.0 accelerates the integration of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) in manufacturing, robust anomaly detection has become critical for ensuring process safety and security. Current data-driven approaches typically employ "product-agnostic" or global models trained on the aggregate of all normal operating data. However, modern industrial facilities frequently operate under diverse product grades. While computationally simple, these global models inherently expand their decision boundaries to accommodate the variance of multiple modes, creating a "blind spot" where subtle anomalies or targeted cyber-physical attacks may be masked by the wide acceptance region of the model. In this work, we first demonstrate that the vulnerability described above is present in global-agnostic models operating across multiple product grades. We then present a Product-Aware Autoencoder as a principled mitigation that restricts the learning domain to grade-specific distributions. While this approach reduces the identified blind-spot risk, we do not claim it as the optimal mitigation among all possible alternatives. We rigorously validate this approach against a Global Agnostic baseline using the Extended Tennessee Eastman Process (TEP) benchmark. Our empirical results indicate that the Product-Aware framework performs comparably to the global baseline on standard detection metrics, while offering improved robustness to product-grade-specific operating modes. Most critically, stress tests simulating our hypothetical attack scenarios reveal that while the global model fails to detect operational deviations in 77.8% of the scenarios, the product-aware system achieves 100% detection accuracy. These findings suggest that, in flexible manufacturing environments, generalized anomaly detectors can pose non-trivial security risks, motivating a shift toward mode-aware diagnostic architectures.

</details>


### [10] [On the evolution of the concept of probability as a mirror of the evolution of reason](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00102)
*Jean-Louis Le Mouël,Vincent Courtillot,Dominique Gibert,Vladimir Kossobokov,Jean-Baptiste Boulé,Pierpaolo Zuddas,Fernando Lopes,Païkan Marccagi,Alexis Maineult*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper traces probability theory's evolution into Bayesian inference as a framework for reasoning under uncertainty, then explores how fuzzy logic addresses vagueness in concepts and deep learning offers geometric prediction, arguing that modern scientific rationality requires integrating all three approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine how rationality has evolved beyond just probability theory to address different dimensions of uncertainty, including the formalization of vagueness in concepts and alternative modes of prediction, while situating probability, fuzzy logic, and deep learning within a common historical and epistemological perspective.

Method: Historical and epistemological analysis tracing the evolution of probability theory from Pascal and Fermat through Bayes, Laplace, Poisson, and Kolmogorov to modern Bayesian inference. The paper then examines fuzzy logic as a formal language for graded meaning and deep learning as a geometric interpolation approach, comparing their roles and limitations.

Result: The analysis shows that probability theory has matured into Bayesian inference as a logic of information, but has limitations in formalizing conceptual vagueness. Fuzzy logic addresses graded meaning while deep learning offers powerful geometric prediction without explicit inference. Each framework serves distinct but complementary roles in scientific reasoning.

Conclusion: Contemporary scientific rationality requires integrating multiple frameworks: probability for uncertainty quantification about well-defined propositions, fuzzy logic for addressing vagueness in concepts, and deep learning for geometric prediction. Scientific reasoning cannot be reduced to data-driven performance alone but requires explicit articulation of uncertainty, vagueness, and inference.

Abstract: Over the centuries, probability theory has grown from the calculus of games of chance into a central framework for reasoning under uncertainty. This article interprets that evolution not merely as a mathematical history, but as a transformation of rationality itself. From Pascal and Fermat's combinatorial symmetry to the inductive logic of Bayes and Laplace, from Poisson's statistics of events to Kolmogorov's axiomatic formalization, probability progressively incorporated uncertainty, time, and coherence into scientific judgment. This trajectory reaches a mature epistemological form in modern Bayesian inference, especially in Tarantola's view of probability as a logic of information, where prior knowledge and data are combined coherently. Yet this framework also exposes a limit: probability quantifies uncertainty about well-defined propositions, but does not by itself formalize the vagueness of the concepts used to describe them. The article therefore examines how rationality extends beyond probability. Fuzzy logic is presented as a rigorous language for graded meaning and qualitative judgment, while deep learning is analyzed as a distinct, powerful mode of prediction based on geometric interpolation and optimization rather than explicit inference. By situating probability, fuzzy logic, and deep learning in a common historical and epistemological perspective, the article clarifies their roles and limits. It argues that contemporary scientific rationality cannot be reduced to data-driven performance alone, but requires the explicit articulation of uncertainty, vagueness, and inference.

</details>


### [11] [Evaluating Interactive Reasoning in Large Language Models: A Hierarchical Benchmark with Executable Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00103)
*Mingyuan Fan,Weiguang Han,Daixin Wang,Cen Chen,Zhiqiang Zhang,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for evaluating LLM reasoning as active evidence acquisition through multi-turn interactions with hidden environments, with a benchmark of 474 executable games across difficulty levels showing discriminative power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reasoning evaluation often treats reasoning as static problem-solving rather than dynamic, interactive processes. The authors aim to develop a framework that captures reasoning as active information gathering, belief updating, and metacognitive adaptation in realistic interactive settings.

Method: Multi-turn interactive framework where LLMs receive only task rules, must issue targeted queries to a hidden environment, integrate partial observations over time, and decide when to submit final answers. Instantiated as a benchmark of 474 executable games evaluated under five difficulty levels (fixed configuration search spaces).

Result: The benchmark is highly discriminative, exposing large differences in both success rate and interaction efficiency across frontier LLMs. Contextual perturbations cause moderate but consistent performance declines, while counterfactual revision and necessity judgment (metacognitive tasks) lead to much larger performance drops.

Conclusion: The interactive reasoning evaluation framework provides a more realistic assessment of LLM reasoning capabilities, revealing significant gaps in metacognitive adaptation and robustness under interactive, evolving contexts.

Abstract: We introduce a multi-turn interactive framework for reasoning evaluation that treats reasoning as active evidence acquisition and belief updating. Wherein, LLMs receive only the task rules, must issue targeted queries to a hidden environment, integrate partial observations over time, and decide when to submit a final answer. Beyond standard success rate and interaction efficiency, we evaluate contextual robustness under controlled contextual perturbations, and metacognitive adaptation through counterfactual revision and necessity judgment. We instantiate the framework as a benchmark of 474 executable games, each evaluated under five fixed configuration search spaces corresponding to five difficulty levels, and evaluate a broad set of frontier LLMs. Results show that the benchmark is highly discriminative, exposing large differences not only in success rate but also in interaction efficiency. Moreover, we empirically show that contextual perturbations cause moderate but consistent declines, whereas counterfactual revision and necessity judgment lead to much larger drops.

</details>


### [12] [A Multi-AI-agent Framework Enabling End-to-end Finite Element Analysis for Solid Mechanics Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00138)
*Titu Ranjan Sarker,Muhammed Jawaad Zulqernine,Ling Yue,Shaowu Pan,Chenxi Wang,Shiyao Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AbaqusAgent: multi-agent LLM framework that converts natural language instructions into FEA analyses using Abaqus, reducing learning barriers and enabling AI integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in FEA including steep learning curve, potential for false simulations due to incorrect definitions, and requirement for years of engineering experience to solve real-world solid mechanics problems.

Method: Develops a multi-agent framework with six specialized agents (interpreter, architect, input writer, runner, reviewer, visualizer) to translate natural language instructions into FEA analysis and result visualization using Abaqus.

Result: Successfully validated on 50 solid mechanics problems with 86% overall success rate, demonstrating effective translation of natural language instructions to FEA analysis and visualization.

Conclusion: The AbaqusAgent framework successfully bridges the gap between natural language instructions and FEA execution, making computational mechanics more accessible while enabling future AI integration with optimization and characterization workflows.

Abstract: Finite element analysis (FEA) is the most important numerical approach for solid mechanics. Challenges of FEA include a steep learning curve for entry-level users and potential false simulations due to incorrect definitions of key simulation components, such as boundary conditions, load cases, and solution variables. Years of engineering experience are usually necessary for real-world problem-solving. To address these issues, we present AbaqusAgent, a multi-agent framework grounded in large language models (LLMs) for solid mechanics analyses. AbaqusAgent is developed to facilitate analysis case generation and execution using Abaqus, one of the most widely used FEA packages, by turning users' natural-language instructions into executed FEA analyses and result visualization. AbaqusAgent is composed of six agents, including interpreter, architect, input writer, runner, reviewer, and visualizer agents, encompassing all the essential pre-processing and post-processing steps of standard FEA analyses. A wide variety of 50 solid mechanics problems have been successfully validated, achieving an overall success rate of 86%. Beyond improving the efficiency of FEA for solid mechanics problems and lowering the barrier to computational mechanics education, AbaqusAgent advances the human-simulation interaction paradigm and enables integration with AI-empowered optimization and material characterization workflows. The code is available at https://github.com/LIRAM-LIN/AbaqusAgent

</details>


### [13] [CAST: Non-Privileged Clipped Asymmetric Self-Teaching with Advantage Flipping for GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00172)
*Yang Li,Gongle Xue,Yijia Guo,Yuheng Yuan,Liwen Hu,Lei Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAST is an answer-free self-distillation method for GRPO-style RLVR that uses self-teacher scoring to provide better token-level guidance aligned with trajectory correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods have limitations: outcome-level rewards provide sparse supervision, group-relative advantages vanish in homogeneous groups, and OPSD token preferences aren't aligned with trajectory correctness.

Method: CAST (answer-free self-distillation method) that uses stop-gradient self-teacher scoring to shape token-level advantages according to trajectory correctness, with bidirectional local advantage sign reversal and bounded sign-constrained base advantages.

Result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning show CAST improves RLVR training while retaining a lightweight, verifier-grounded trajectory-level objective.

Conclusion: CAST offers a practical improvement over existing self-distillation methods for GRPO-style RLVR, providing more effective token-level guidance while maintaining the benefits of verifier-grounded trajectory-level objectives.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), especially Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has been widely used to improve reasoning in large language models. However, outcome-level rewards provide only sparse supervision, and group-relative advantages vanish when all sampled trajectories for a prompt are either correct or incorrect. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) offers dense token-level guidance, but its token preferences are not necessarily aligned with trajectory correctness; empirical diagnostics show that OPSD signals behave differently on correct and incorrect rollouts, with teacher-positive and teacher-negative gap signals exhibiting different noise profiles. These diagnostics are conducted under an OPSD-style privileged teacher context for analysis only, whereas CAST training uses answer-free self-teacher scoring.Motivated by these observations, this work proposes CAST, an answer-free self-distillation method for GRPO-style RLVR. CAST keeps the verifier-grounded GRPO objective, but uses a stop-gradient self-teacher to shape token-level advantages according to trajectory correctness. Unlike prior self-distilled RLVR methods, CAST does not require reference-solution-conditioned teacher scoring, keeps the self-teacher log-probability gap active throughout training, and applies bidirectional local advantage sign reversal: teacher-negative tokens in correct trajectories can receive negative token-level advantages, while teacher-positive tokens in incorrect trajectories can receive bounded positive local advantages. For zero-variance all-correct and all-wrong groups, CAST assigns bounded sign-constrained base advantages, so these otherwise zero-gradient groups can contribute verifier-signed token feedback. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that CAST improves RLVR training while retaining a lightweight, verifier-grounded trajectory-level objective.

</details>


### [14] [TIGER: Traceable Inference with Graph-Based Evidence Routing for Mitigating Hallucinations in Multimodal Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00232)
*Kaixiang Zhao,Tianrun Yu,Shawn Huang,Porter Jenkins,Yushun Dong,Amanda Hughes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TIGER is an inference-time framework that repairs factual errors in multimodal generation by independently analyzing input observations and output claims, assigning risk scores to each claim, and selectively repairing high-risk claims while keeping the backbone model frozen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time repair methods have two key limitations: (1) hallucinated claims in outputs can bias the model's interpretation of the input when jointly conditioned, and (2) free-form feedback cannot be ranked or scheduled at the fact level for targeted repair.

Method: TIGER redesigns feedback for localized repair by: (1) independently extracting an observation graph from input and a claim graph from current output, (2) assigning each claim a graph-conditioned risk score based on support and conflict, (3) repairing selected high-risk claims while keeping the backbone model frozen.

Result: Experiments across four cross-modal paths (image-to-text, image+text-to-text, audio-to-text, video-to-text) show TIGER reduces unsupported content while preserving task quality. Gains hold across multiple backbones, and CrisisFACTS case study suggests improvement in multi-source grounding.

Conclusion: TIGER provides an effective inference-time framework for fact-level multimodal repair with convergence guarantees and practical benefits across diverse modalities and backbones, potentially improving grounding in multi-source settings.

Abstract: We study fact-level repair for multimodal generation, where a fluent output may contain specific facts that are not supported by the input. Existing inference-time repair methods often generate feedback by jointly conditioning on the input and the current output. This design has two limitations: hallucinated claims in the output can bias the model's interpretation of the input, and free-form feedback cannot be ranked or scheduled at the fact level. We present TIGER, an inference-time framework that redesigns feedback for localized repair. TIGER independently extracts an observation graph from the input and a claim graph from the current output, then assigns each claim a graph-conditioned risk score based on support and conflict. The model repairs selected high-risk claims while keeping the backbone frozen. We provide a convergence analysis showing that the expected total risk decreases geometrically to an explicit asymptotic bound under mild assumptions. Experiments across four cross-modal paths, including image-to-text, image+text-to-text, audio-to-text, and video-to-text, show that TIGER reduces unsupported content while preserving task quality. The gains hold across multiple backbones, and a CrisisFACTS case study suggests that the same repair mechanism can improve grounding in multi-source settings.

</details>


### [15] [MindZero: Learning Online Mental Reasoning With Zero Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00240)
*Shunchi Zhang,Jin Lu,Chuanyang Jin,Yichao Zhou,Zhining Zhang,Tianmin Shu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MindZero: A self-supervised RL framework that trains MLLMs for efficient, robust online mental state inference without labeled data, outperforming slower model-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address three key challenges in robust Theory of Mind for AI assistance: (1) online inference with uncertainty updates, (2) efficient reasoning for real-time use, and (3) lack of ground-truth mental state annotations in real-world domains.

Method: Introduces MindZero, a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework that trains multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to generate mental state hypotheses that maximize the likelihood of observed actions as estimated by a planner, eliminating the need for explicit mental state annotations.

Result: MindZero significantly outperforms baselines (including LLMs alone and model-based methods) in both accuracy and efficiency on mental reasoning and AI assistance tasks across gridworld and household domains.

Conclusion: Mental reasoning can be effectively learned as a self-supervised skill by transforming expensive model-based reasoning into efficient, integrated inference.

Abstract: Effective real-world assistance requires AI agents with robust Theory of Mind (ToM): inferring human mental states from their behavior. Despite recent advances, several key challenges remain, including (1) online inference with robust uncertainty updates over multiple hypotheses; (2) efficient reasoning suitable for real-time assistance; and (3) the lack of ground-truth mental state annotations in real-world domains. We address these challenges by introducing MindZero, a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework that trains multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for efficient and robust online mental reasoning. During training, the model is rewarded for generating mental state hypotheses that maximize the likelihood of observed actions estimated by a planner, similar to model-based ToM reasoning. This method thus eliminates the need for explicit mental state annotations. After training, MindZero internalizes model-based reasoning into fast single-pass inference. We evaluate MindZero against baselines across challenging mental reasoning and AI assistance tasks in gridworld and household domains. We found that LLMs alone are insufficient; model-based methods improve accuracy but are slow, costly, and limited by backbone MLLM capacity. In contrast, MindZero enhances MLLMs' intrinsic ToM ability and significantly outperforms model-based methods in both accuracy and efficiency, showing that mental reasoning can be effectively learned as a self-supervised skill.

</details>


### [16] [Geodesic Flow Matching for Denoising High-Dimensional Structured Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00248)
*Karim Habashy,Chris Eliasmith*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Geodesic Flow Matching for Spatial Semantic Pointers to maintain manifold constraints, improving neurosymbolic SLAM accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard flow matching assumes flat Euclidean geometry, which fails for Spatial Semantic Pointers (SSPs) as it destroys the required phase and magnitude structure by cutting through the manifold.

Method: The method employs Geodesic Flow Matching to restrict denoising flows to the SSP toroidal manifold, adapting Riemannian transport dynamics to preserve the geometric structure of SSP states.

Result: The method achieves a 72% reduction in tracking error and enables a 40% increase in neural efficiency compared to competitive baselines in a Spiking Neural SLAM system.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly constraining flow matching to the SSP manifold through Riemannian dynamics is crucial for maintaining representation integrity and performance in neurosymbolic reasoning systems.

Abstract: Vector Symbolic Algebras (VSAs) enable robust neurosymbolic reasoning by encoding symbolic information into high-dimensional distributed representations. For continuous domains, Spatial Semantic Pointers (SSPs) extend this framework by mapping variables onto continuous toroidal manifolds. However, standard approaches like Flow Matching assume a flat Euclidean geometry, which fails to account for the geometric constraints imposed on valid SSP states. We demonstrate that this assumption fails for SSPs: Euclidean linear interpolants ``cut through" the manifold's interior, destroying the phase and magnitude structure required for accurate decoding. To resolve this, we employ Geodesic Flow Matching, adapting Riemannian transport dynamics to strictly restrict the denoising flow to the SSP toroidal manifold. We validate this approach in a Spiking Neural SLAM system, showing that manifold-aware cleanup stabilizes path integration against drift. The method achieves a 72\% reduction in tracking error and enables a 40\% increase in neural efficiency compared to competitive baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/kremHabashy/CleanupSSP .

</details>


### [17] [Capability Self-Assessment: Teaching LLMs to Know Their Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00251)
*Haoyan Yang,Reza Shirkavand,Yukai Jin,Jiawei Zhou,Shangqian Gao,Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs overestimate their abilities and attempt tasks they can't solve. Reinforcement learning teaches them self-assessment without harming capabilities, unlike supervised fine-tuning which degrades performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern LLMs systematically lack the ability to recognize their own limitations and decide whether to solve problems or delegate. This overestimation of competence hinders reliable intelligent systems.

Method: The paper formulates CSA as a policy-learning problem and compares reinforcement learning (RL) against supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The authors evaluate across diverse model families and scales.

Result: RL effectively teaches CSA while preserving original capabilities, outperforming SFT which severely degrades capabilities. CSA behavior generalizes out of distribution, suggesting it's a transferable trait. CSA improves local-cloud decision making at inference time and enables targeted data selection during training.

Conclusion: Capability Self-Assessment (CSA) is a crucial model trait that improves AI reliability. RL effectively teaches CSA without degrading capabilities, offers practical benefits for inference-time decision making and targeted data selection during training, and represents a transferable trait that generalizes well out of distribution.

Abstract: The ability to recognize one's own limitations and decide whether to solve a problem or delegate is fundamental for reliable intelligent systems. Yet we show that modern large language models systematically lack this ability: across diverse model families and scales, they overestimate their competence and attempt queries they cannot solve. We refer to this ability as Capability Self-Assessment (CSA) and formulate it as a policy-learning problem, aiming to improve self-assessment while preserving the model's original capabilities. Our results show that reinforcement learning teaches CSA effectively, significantly outperforming supervised fine-tuning while preserving original capabilities. In contrast, supervised fine-tuning severely degrades the capabilities the model is meant to assess. Moreover, learned self-assessment behavior generalizes well out of distribution, suggesting that CSA is a transferable model trait. Finally, CSA is practically useful: it improves local-cloud decision making at inference time and provides a signal for targeted data selection during training.

</details>


### [18] [ClinEnv: An Interactive Multi-Stage Long Horizon EHR Environment for Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02568)
*Yuxing Lu,Yushuhong Lin,Wenqi Shi,J. Ben Tamo,Xukai Zhao,Jinzhuo Wang,May Dongmei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClinEnv is an interactive benchmark simulating real inpatient admissions where LLMs act as attending physicians, revealing severe gaps in management decisions and information gathering despite reasonable diagnostic performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current clinical practice involves gathering heterogeneous information incrementally and making sequential, irreversible decisions under uncertainty. Existing interactive medical benchmarks compromise on these real-world dynamics, failing to properly evaluate LLMs as attending physicians.

Method: ClinEnv automatically constructs ordered sequences of decision stages from real inpatient admissions. At each stage, models must actively query four specialized agents before committing to medications, procedures, and diagnoses. Evaluation uses ontology-grounded deterministic matching.

Result: Across seven models, the strongest achieves only 0.31 decision F1. Models perform poorly on management decisions (0.17 F1 vs 0.51 F1 for diagnosis recovery) and continue issuing redundant queries as cases progress, revealing an information-acquisition gap invisible to outcome-only evaluation.

Conclusion: ClinEnv introduces a longitudinal inpatient simulation paradigm that reveals significant gaps in LLMs' ability to dynamically gather information and make management decisions in clinical settings, with process quality being sharply decoupled from outcome quality.

Abstract: Clinical practice is not the selection of an answer from enumerated options: a physician gathers heterogeneous information incrementally and commits to sequential, irreversible decisions under uncertainty. Static benchmarks cannot probe and existing interactive medical benchmarks each compromise on at least one of them. We present ClinEnv, an interactive benchmark that evaluates LLMs as attending physicians over real inpatient admissions under a paradigm we term Longitudinal Inpatient Simulation. Each case is automatically constructed into an ordered sequence of decision stages; at every stage the model must actively query four specialized agents before committing to medications, procedures, and diagnoses. ClinEnv scores both what the model decides, through deterministic ontology-grounded matching, and how it gathers information. Across seven models, the strongest reaches only 0.31 decision F1, and outcome quality is sharply decoupled from process quality. Difficulty concentrates in management decisions and later stages, where models recover discharge diagnoses far more reliably than management actions (0.51 vs. 0.17 F1) and continue to issue redundant queries as cases progress. ClinEnv makes this information-acquisition gap, invisible to outcome-only evaluation, directly measurable.

</details>


### [19] [Closed-Loop Neural Activation Control in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00269)
*Abhijith Babu,Ramneet Kaur,Nathaniel D. Bastian,Olivera Kotevska,Susmit Jha,Yanzhao Wu,Sumit Kumar Jha,Anirban Roy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Closed-loop adaptive steering for VLA models improves embodied control by dynamically adjusting intervention strength, outperforming static open-loop methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed open-loop steering coefficients in VLA models cause overcorrection, oscillation, and reduced task success in embodied control, especially for temporal behaviors like speed and smoothness.

Method: Decouples representation from regulation using motion-aligned residual directions and adaptive feedback controllers (PID or RL-based) to adjust intervention magnitude online.

Result: Experiments on LIBERO tasks with OpenVLA show CTRL-STEER achieves more stable concept regulation and better steering-task success trade-offs than fixed-coefficient baselines.

Conclusion: CTRL-STEER demonstrates that closed-loop concept regulation significantly improves upon static open-loop interventions for VLA-based robotic control, enabling better trade-offs between concept satisfaction and task success without model retraining.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can be steered at test time by intervening on semantically meaningful internal directions, but existing methods use a fixed steering coefficient, effectively operating in open loop. This is poorly suited to embodied control, where task state and concept error evolve over time, often causing overcorrection, oscillation, and reduced task success, especially for temporal behaviors such as speed and smoothness. We propose CTRL-STEER, a closed-loop framework that replaces static intervention strength with adaptive, time-varying control signals. The key idea is to decouple representation from regulation: rather than assuming temporal concepts are directly controlled by individual neurons, we steer along motion-aligned residual directions while a feedback controller adjusts intervention magnitude online. We instantiate this framework with both PID and reinforcement learning based controllers. Experiments with a fine-tuned OpenVLA policy on four LIBERO task suites show that CTRL-STEER achieves more stable concept regulation and a better steering-task success trade-off than fixed-coefficient baselines, without modifying or retraining the base model.

</details>


### [20] [Robust Shielding for Safe Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00270)
*Edwin Hamel-De le Court,Thom Badings,Alessandro Abate,Francesco Belardinelli,Francesco Fabiano*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A shielding framework for robust MDPs with uncertain transition probabilities that guarantees safety via LTL specifications and PAC guarantees, proven to be sound and optimal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing shielding techniques for reinforcement learning agents typically assume knowledge of safety-relevant transition dynamics, which is seldom available in practice. This limitation motivates the need for a shielding framework that can guarantee safety in Markov decision processes with unknown or uncertain transition probabilities.

Method: The paper introduces a novel shielding framework for robust MDPs (RMDPs) with uncertain transition probabilities. Safety is defined as satisfying an LTL formula with a threshold probability under worst-case transition probabilities. The framework is combined with existing sampling methods for learning transition probabilities with PAC guarantees to construct shields.

Result: The shielding framework is proven to be both sound and optimal for RMDPs: every policy admissible by the shield is safe, and every safe RMDP policy is admissible by the shield. Experiments show that shields for learned RMDPs guarantee safety in unknown MDPs while recovering strong expected return as sample size increases.

Conclusion: The proposed shielding framework offers a principled approach to ensure safety in unknown MDPs by combining robust MDPs, LTL-based safety specifications, and PAC guarantees, enabling the construction of shields that are both safe and minimally restrictive.

Abstract: Shielding is an effective approach to formally guarantee the safety of reinforcement learning agents in Markov decision processes (MDPs). However, existing shielding techniques typically assume knowledge of the safety-relevant transition dynamics - a requirement that is seldom met in practice. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel shielding framework for robust MDPs (RMDPs), i.e., MDPs with sets of transition probabilities. We define safety as the satisfaction of a linear temporal logic (LTL) formula with a certain threshold probability under the worst-case transition probabilities of the RMDP. We prove that our shielding framework is both sound and optimal for the RMDP: every policy admissible by the shield is safe, and conversely, every safe RMDP policy is admissible by the shield. We combine our approach with existing sampling methods for learning transition probabilities of MDPs with probably approximately correct (PAC) guarantees. This combination enables the construction of shields for MDPs that, with high confidence, guarantee safety while remaining minimally restrictive. Our experiments show that our shields for learned RMDPs guarantee safety in unknown MDPs while recovering strong expected return as the number of samples increases.

</details>


### [21] [On Wednesdays, We Ask Questions: Optimizing "Active Listening" in Automated Legal Triage and Referral](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00272)
*Quinten Steenhuis,Jacqueline Harvey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Low-cost LLMs generate poor follow-up questions for legal intake; GPT-5 improves question quality and classification accuracy. Human and LLM ratings differ, and dedicated screening panels are needed for sensitive areas like domestic violence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether low-cost LLMs can effectively generate high-quality plain-language follow-up questions for legal intake classification, and to assess the limitations of such models in eliciting relevant information from applicants, particularly in sensitive areas like domestic violence.

Method: The FETCH classifier employs a low-cost ensemble of LLMs to generate follow-up questions that refine legal problem matching. An expert attorney and LLM-assisted evaluation assessed the follow-up question approach. Discussions with legal intake workers informed a rubric for evaluating legal intake classification questions. The study involved comparing low-cost LLMs with higher-cost models like GPT-5 and analyzing prompt engineering effectiveness.

Result: Low-cost LLMs perform well at classification tasks but fail to generate high-quality plain-language questions for legal intake. Adding a high-cost model like GPT-5 improves fact elicitation and classification accuracy. LLM-as-judge ratings diverge from human evaluations, and prompt engineering alone does not enhance question quality. Fact elicitation is uneven across legal categories, with domestic violence screening not aligning with family law protocols.

Conclusion: Generating high-quality plain-language questions in legal intake requires sophisticated, higher-cost models like GPT-5, as low-cost LLMs fall short. Prompt engineering alone is insufficient, and LLM-as-judge ratings diverge from human judgments. The findings highlight the need for dedicated screening panels for certain legal areas, particularly domestic violence, to ensure proper fact elicitation and alignment with family law protocols.

Abstract: The FETCH classifier generates follow-up questions to help refine the best match for the applicant's legal problem, using a low-cost ensemble of LLMs. In this paper, we describe an expert attorney and LLM-assisted evaluation of the follow-up question approach in FETCH and show that while low-cost LLMs perform well at classification tasks, generating high-quality plain-language questions in this setting appears to require a more sophisticated and higher-cost model. Through discussion with legal intake workers, we propose a rubric for the evaluation of legal intake classification questions, and we find that prompt engineering alone is not enough to improve question quality for intake purposes. We also find that LLM-as-judge and human ratings diverge. We demonstrate that with the addition of a single high-cost model, GPT-5, the classifier can elicit relevant information from applicants for legal help, and that the questions lead to more accurate performance at classification tasks. We also find uneven fact elicitation across different categories, including domestic violence, at odds with family law screening protocols, suggesting the value of including dedicated screening panels for certain areas of law.

</details>


### [22] [Evaluating Bivariate Causal Statements Based on Mutual Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00278)
*Erik Jahn,Dominik Janzing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Methods developed to evaluate bivariate causal statements through compatibility and incompatibility scores that quantify plausibility without requiring faithfulness, enabling assessment of causal claims from experts or AI when ground truth is unavailable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal ground truth is difficult to obtain in real-world systems, making it hard to assess claims about causal effects, especially when derived from human experts or artificial intelligence.

Method: Developed a compatibility score for bivariate causal statements without relying on faithfulness, and an incompatibility score for purely graphical statements based on acyclicity and faithfulness. The scores quantify plausibility by measuring additional confounding needed to explain observed correlations.

Result: Theoretical and empirical evidence shows both scores can successfully distinguish correct from incorrect causal statements. Demonstrated practical applicability by analyzing causal claims made by large language models.

Conclusion: The paper provides a foundation for assessing causal claims from human experts or AI in settings where alternative validation is unavailable, with methods that can distinguish correct from incorrect causal statements in generic settings.

Abstract: For many real-world systems, causal ground truth is difficult to obtain, making claims about causal effects hard to assess. We develop methods for evaluating collections of $\binom{n}{2}$ bivariate causal statements over a set of $n$ variables. In the setting of acyclic linear statements, any such collection can be extended to a unique multivariate causal model, but we argue that this induced model is implausible if it imposes substantial additional confounding to explain observed correlations. We introduce a compatibility score that quantifies this notion of plausibility, notably without relying on the faithfulness assumption. Additionally, we define an incompatibility score for purely graphical bivariate causal statements, based on global consistency constraints that are derived from acyclicity and faithfulness assumptions. We give theoretical and empirical evidence that both scores can successfully distinguish correct from incorrect causal statements in generic settings. Moreover, we demonstrate the practical applicability of our methods by analyzing causal claims made by large language models. Our work aims to provide a foundation for assessing the reliability of causal information derived from human experts or artificial intelligence in settings where alternative forms of validation are unavailable.

</details>


### [23] [Model-Native Computing Architecture: Envisioning Future System Architecture Through the Lens of Computer Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00288)
*Hai Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes ICAM framework with six layers and three design laws to unify model-native computing, resolving whether LLMs are like CPUs or OSes through dual-plane view (probabilistic execution vs deterministic control).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are transitioning from model technology to system technology, creating recurring engineering problems (cache reuse, context management, agent scheduling, permission control) that resemble classical computer systems problems. The paper aims to develop this analogy to provide a unified model for understanding these emerging systems.

Method: 1) Develops the analogy between LLM systems and classical computer systems through a visionary survey. 2) Maps computer architecture concepts to the model-native stack and reviews existing work. 3) Proposes ICAM framework with six layers, dual-plane view, and three design laws. 4) Validates design laws against published system-level data and recent agentic software practices.

Result: Proposes the Intelligent Computing Architecture Model (ICAM) - a six-layer framework with explicit interface contracts and design axioms. Introduces a dual-plane view (probabilistic execution plane vs deterministic control plane) to resolve the tension of whether LLMs are more like CPUs or OSes. Presents three design laws: Semantic Locality Law, Context Budget Law, and Agent Speedup Law.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by identifying where the computer analogy breaks down and outlining a research roadmap for model-native computing. It emphasizes this is a conceptual and survey contribution without new experiments.

Abstract: Large language models are undergoing a transition from model technology to system technology. As developers use Codex, Claude Code, AutoGPT, and related agents to write code, manage projects, and execute multi-step tasks, recurring engineering problems such as cache reuse, context management, agent scheduling, and permission control increasingly resemble classical computer systems problems. This paper develops that analogy as a visionary survey. We map concepts from computer architecture to the emerging model-native stack and review work on LLM-as-OS, memory management, agent frameworks, tool protocols, multi-agent coordination, cognitive architectures, and safety governance. We argue that these strands address different layers of the same system but lack a unified model.
  To fill this gap, we propose the Intelligent Computing Architecture Model (ICAM), a six-layer framework for model-native computing with explicit interface contracts and design axioms. ICAM resolves the apparent tension over whether an LLM is more like a CPU or an operating system through a dual-plane view: a probabilistic execution plane concerned with what can be computed, and a deterministic control plane concerned with what should be computed. We further introduce three design laws: the Semantic Locality Law for KV-cache reuse and inference speedup, the Context Budget Law for effective working sets under finite windows and attention decay, and the Agent Speedup Law for diminishing returns in multi-agent collaboration. We validate these laws against published system-level data and relate them to recent evidence on agentic software practices. We conclude by identifying where the analogy breaks down and outlining a research roadmap for model-native computing. This is a conceptual and survey contribution; it does not report new experiments.

</details>


### [24] [Coupling Language Models with Physics-based Simulation for Synthesis of Inorganic Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00315)
*Edward W. Staley,Tom Arbaugh,Michael Pekala,Alexander New,Christopher D. Stiles,Nam Q. Le,Gregory Bassen,Wyatt Bunstine,Tyrel McQueen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hybrid framework evaluates LLMs for inorganic synthesis planning, showing their implicit priors yield more viable strategies than classical methods for niobium-oxygen system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the difficulty in synthesis planning for novel inorganic crystalline materials generated by ML models, given the complexity of physical processes and limited computational tools.

Method: A hybrid framework combining thermodynamic databases with simplified kinetics models to approximate realistic synthesis conditions, applied to the niobium-oxygen system as a case study. Computational simulations compare LLM-generated synthesis routes with classical path-planning algorithms.

Result: LLM-generated synthesis routes show more viable strategies than classical path-planning algorithms in the niobium-oxygen system. Classical search methods serve primarily as a foil rather than a direct competitor, highlighting where LLMs' implicit priors add value.

Conclusion: This work establishes a novel evaluation framework that demonstrates how LLMs' implicit priors can generate more viable inorganic synthesis routes compared to classical search methods, while classical methods serve as a benchmark to highlight where LLMs add value in this complex domain.

Abstract: Modern generative machine learning (ML) models can propose novel inorganic crystalline materials with targeted properties; however, synthesis planning of these materials remains difficult due to the complexity of the associated physical processes and limited availability of computational tools. We introduce a novel hybrid framework to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) in inorganic synthesis planning by combining thermodynamic databases with simplified kinetics models to approximate realistic synthesis conditions. As a case study, we focus on the niobium-oxygen system, which features multiple industrially relevant oxide phases with well-characterized data. In computational simulations, we compare LLM-generated synthesis routes with classical path-planning algorithms, showing that the implicit priors in LLMs can yield more viable strategies. In our evaluation setting, classical search methods serve primarily as a foil rather than a direct competitor. This illustrates the relative complexity of the problem and highlights where the LLM's implicit priors add value.

</details>


### [25] [From Noise to Control: Parameterized Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00336)
*Renhao Zhang,Haotian Fu,Mingxi Jia,George Konidaris,Yilun Du,Bruno Castro da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: false


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: false

Method: false

Result: false

Conclusion: false

Abstract: We propose Parameterized Diffusion Policy (PDP), a framework for learning diffusion policies conditioned on low-dimensional, continuous parameters embedded in a learned behavior manifold. By constructing this manifold so that distances between latent representations reflect the semantic similarity between physical trajectories, we transform diffusion from a mechanism for stochastic diversity into a precise and optimizable tool for behavior steering. Our approach enables smooth interpolation between known strategies and efficient adaptation to novel constraints without updating policy weights. We demonstrate that PDP significantly improves adaptation performance on complex multimodal benchmarks in both simulated and real-robot experiments compared to standard diffusion policies, particularly in scenarios requiring the synthesis of novel behaviors.

</details>


### [26] [From "Weak" Signals to Strong Models: Preference Delta Aggregation with LoRA Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00357)
*Qi Sun,Siyue Zhang,Yulin Chen,Yuxiang Xue,Ru Peng,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PDA (Preference Delta Aggregation) is a framework that aggregates multiple weak preference signals from weak-weaker model pairs to improve strong language models, using LoRA adapters and geometric alignment merging to combine complementary capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality supervision for training strong LLMs is often scarce, but recent work shows that preference data from weak-weaker model pairs (despite limited individual response quality) can provide effective supervision through relative quality deltas. This raises the question of whether multiple such "weak" signals can be constructively aggregated to improve stronger models.

Method: Proposes Preference Delta Aggregation (PDA): derives a preference delta from each weak-weaker model pair, instantiates it as a LoRA adapter learned through preference optimization, and aggregates the resulting deltas via LoRA merging. Also introduces Geometric Alignment Merging (GAM), a geometry-aware merging method that aligns adapter subspaces before aggregation to mitigate directional interference during merging.

Result: Aggregating multiple weak signals pushes performance beyond any single signal, with gains increasing as additional signals are incorporated. PDA with GAM improves the strong model (Qwen3 8B) by 6.8 and 7.3 points on average for knowledge reasoning and agentic search respectively, outperforming all single-delta and multi-delta baselines by 2.1 and 4.3 points over the best single-delta baseline.

Conclusion: Multiple weak preference signals can be effectively aggregated to improve strong LLMs, with gains attributed to the composition of complementary capabilities encoded across distinct preference deltas. The PDA framework with GAM enables robust composition of diverse deltas, demonstrating the value of aggregating multiple weak supervision sources.

Abstract: Training strong large language models (LLMs) requires high-quality supervision, which is often scarce. Recent work shows that paired preference data from weak-weaker model pairs (e.g., Qwen3 4B over 1.7B), despite the limited quality of individual responses, can provide an effective supervision signal through relative quality deltas, which we term a "weak" signal. This motivates a key research question: can multiple "weak" signals be constructively aggregated for improving strong models (e.g., Qwen3 8B)? To this end, we propose Preference Delta Aggregation (PDA), the first framework that derives a preference delta from each weak-weaker model pair, instantiates it as a LoRA adapter learned through preference optimization, and aggregates the resulting deltas via LoRA merging. To further mitigate directional interference during LoRA merging, we introduce Geometric Alignment Merging (GAM), a geometry-aware merging method that aligns adapter subspaces before aggregation, enabling more robust composition of diverse deltas. Evaluations on knowledge reasoning and agentic search benchmarks show that aggregating multiple "weak" signals pushes performance beyond any single signal, with further gains as additional signals are incorporated. Correspondingly, PDA with GAM improves the strong model by 6.8 and 7.3 points on average for knowledge reasoning and agentic search, respectively. It outperforms all single-delta and multi-delta baselines, exceeding the best single-delta baseline by 2.1 and 4.3 points. Further analysis attributes these gains to the effective composition of complementary capabilities encoded across distinct preference deltas.

</details>


### [27] [The Deterministic Horizon: When Extended Reasoning Fails and Tool Delegation Becomes Necessary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00376)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.AI
