TL;DR: Decoder-only attention has fundamental limits (attention bottleneck) for deterministic state-tracking tasks, making tool integration necessary beyond ~20-30 reasoning steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extended chain-of-thought reasoning often degrades performance on deterministic state-tracking tasks, but not due to preference biases. The paper aims to identify the fundamental architectural limits of decoder-only attention that cause this degradation.

Method: Established an Attention Bottleneck Theorem with achievability construction to bound state-tracking capacity, developed a context-dependent error model showing super-exponential accuracy decay, introduced State-Space Jaccard metric to distinguish capability from preference failures, and determined a Deterministic Horizon d* ∈ [19, 31]. Evaluated across 12 models and 8 task domains including SWE-Bench, WebArena, and SQL-Multi.

Result: Tool-integrated reasoning consistently outperforms neural chain-of-thought across models and domains (86-94% vs 24-42% accuracy). Fine-tuning yields <5% improvement, confirming architectural ceiling. Cross-model correlation of 0.81-0.91 indicates failures are architectural rather than training-specific.

Conclusion: For deterministic state-tracking tasks, decoder-only attention has fundamental architectural limits (the attention bottleneck) that restrict reasoning capacity. Beyond a deterministic horizon around 19-31 steps, pure neural reasoning should yield to hybrid tool-integrated approaches, which consistently outperform neural chain-of-thought.

Abstract: Extended chain-of-thought reasoning can degrade performance on deterministic state-tracking tasks, not due to preference biases, but limits rooted in the information-theoretic capacity of decoder-only attention. We establish: (1) an Attention Bottleneck Theorem with a complementary achievability construction, bounding state-tracking capacity as $O(H \cdot \log(L/H) \cdot \sqrt{d_h})$; (2) a context-dependent error model yielding super-exponential accuracy decay; (3) the State-Space Jaccard metric distinguishing capability from preference failures; (4) a Deterministic Horizon $d^* \in [19, 31]$ beyond which tool delegation becomes necessary. Across 12 models and 8 task domains (including SWE-Bench, WebArena, and SQL-Multi), tool-integrated reasoning consistently outperforms neural chain-of-thought; on the primary model suite it reaches 86-94% accuracy versus 24-42% for neural chain-of-thought. Fine-tuning on optimal-length traces yields $<$5% improvement, confirming an architectural ceiling, and high cross-model correlation ($r = 0.81$-$0.91$) indicates these failures are architectural rather than training-specific. Our results provide principled guidance for when pure neural reasoning should yield to hybrid approaches in agentic systems.

</details>


### [28] [VESTA: Visual Exploration with Statistical Tool Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00384)
*William Rudman,Abhishek Divekar,Kanishk Jain,Sebastian Joseph,Stella S. R. Offner,Matthew Lease,Kyle Mahowald,Greg Durrett,Junyi Jessy Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VESTA is a framework that enhances VLMs with dynamically growing toolkits for data exploration, outperforming prior systems on complex statistical modeling tasks, especially in astronomy domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent-based systems using language and vision-language models struggle with challenging quantitative modeling tasks, lacking proper automation for data exploration and diagnostic tool creation.

Method: VESTA equips VLMs with a dynamically growing exploration toolkit that guides model refinement through data transformations, hypothesis-driven visualizations, and robust statistical tests. The system selects or creates diagnostic tools that accumulate in the model's context for reuse.

Result: VESTA's dynamic tool creation outperforms baseline systems (no tools, static expert-written tools) across all tiers of the DAWN benchmark, with largest gains on complex and domain-specific tasks like astronomy modeling. Dynamically generated tools are more sophisticated than existing systems, covering more diagnostic categories and preferring visual outputs.

Conclusion: VESTA's dynamic tool creation framework, which actively explores data with a growing toolkit, significantly outperforms prior agent-based systems, particularly on complex and domain-specific tasks, demonstrating the power of combining VLMs with data-driven tool generation.

Abstract: Fitting quantitative models to data is a central step in scientific workflows, yet it remains one of the least automated. Recent agent-based systems leverage language and vision-language models (VLMs) to iteratively propose and refine statistical models, but these systems struggle on more challenging modeling tasks. To address these limitations, we introduce VESTA: Visual Exploration with Statistical Tool Agents, a framework that equips VLMs with a dynamically growing exploration toolkit to guide model refinement through data transformations, hypothesis-driven visualizations, and robust statistical tests. Unlike prior systems that rely on iterative critique alone, VESTA actively explores data before and during refinement by selecting or creating diagnostic tools, which accumulate in the model's context and can be reused later. We evaluate VESTA against established baselines in three toolkit configurations: no tools, static expert-written tools, and dynamic model-written tools. To support this evaluation, we introduce DAWN (Dataset for Automated Workflows and Numerical Modeling), a benchmark targeting distribution fitting and time series modeling with varying difficulty tiers, and culminating in real-world astronomy tasks including modeling initial mass functions and gravitational-wave chirp signals. We find that VESTA's dynamic tool creation outperforms prior agentic pipelines, with the largest gains on complex and domain-specific tasks. We further show that dynamically generated tools are substantially more sophisticated than those produced by existing visual tool-creation systems, covering more diagnostic categories per function and strongly preferring visual outputs that the VLM critic can reason over directly.

</details>


### [29] [Weak Critics Make Strong Learners: On-Policy Critique Distillation for Scalable Oversight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00424)
*Can Jin,Jiakang Li,Rui Wu,Eddy Zhang,Dimitris N. Metaxas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using weak models as critics (not judges) provides revision directions, enabling strong models to self-improve via filtered critique distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weak supervisors often fail to provide reliable labels/preferences for complex outputs from strong LLMs, limiting weak-to-strong generalization and scalable oversight.

Method: Progressive on-policy critique distillation (OPCD) filters high-quality critiques and distills critic-guided behavior into strong models through adaptive self-teacher signals.

Result: Weak critiques can improve frozen strong models at inference time; OPCD improves strong models over training epochs on reasoning and alignment benchmarks.

Conclusion: Weak-critic strong oversight provides an effective path for scalable oversight with weak supervision, allowing strong models to benefit from weak critiques without needing perfect weak judges.

Abstract: As large language models become stronger, weak supervisors may fail to provide reliable labels, preferences, or final judgments for complex outputs, limiting both weak-to-strong generalization and scalable oversight. We study a more tractable form of weak supervision: using a weak model as a critic rather than as a labeler or judge. Instead of solving the task or selecting the correct answer, the weak critic only needs to provide a non-misleading revision direction that helps the strong model better use its own knowledge. We call this setting *weak-critic strong oversight*. We first show that weak critiques can improve frozen strong models at inference time, and that critique quality is key to this improvement. We then propose progressive on-policy critique distillation (**OPCD**), which filters high-quality critiques and distills critic-guided behavior into the strong model through adaptive self-teacher signals. Experiments on reasoning and alignment benchmarks show that our method improves strong models over training epochs, suggesting an effective path for scalable oversight with weak supervision.

</details>


### [30] [SDR: Set-Distance Rewards for Radiology Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00440)
*Halil Ibrahim Gulluk,Max Van Puyvelde,Wim Van Criekinge,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Using set-to-set distance rewards for chest X-ray report generation outperforms exact-match methods in both training and test-time selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning rewards (exact-match accuracy and step-level processes) are incompatible for chest X-ray reports, which consist of unordered and orthogonal findings rather than causal reasoning chains.

Method: Treat reports as sets: split reports into sentences, embed with frozen sentence transformer, compute set-to-set distances between generated and reference embeddings as continuous, permutation-invariant rewards. Use GRPO for post-training and set distances for test-time best-of-N selection.

Result: Set-to-set distance rewards consistently outperform supervised fine-tuning and exact-match GRPO across datasets and models (avg improvements: BERTScore 6.80%, RadGraph F1 7.82%, CheXbert F1 4.45%). Test-time selection improves BERTScore by 16.4% on average, and pruning mid-generation reduces tokens by 50% while preserving quality.

Conclusion: Set-distance rewards serve as a unified signal for both post-training and test-time scaling in chest X-ray report generation, offering significant performance improvements over traditional methods.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has rapidly advanced reasoning in vision--language models. However, for chest X-ray report generation, the standard rewards (i.e. exact-match accuracy and step-level processes) are incompatible because the reports consist of unordered and orthogonal findings, rather than a causal reasoning chain. We address this gap with a set-based view: each report is split into sentences and embedded by a frozen sentence transformer, yielding unordered embedding sets. We propose the use of set-to-set distances between generated and reference embeddings as continuous, permutation-invariant rewards. Across two datasets and three vision--language models (Qwen3-VL-2B/4B, Gemma3-4B), post-training with set-to-set distance based rewards via GRPO consistently outperforms supervised fine-tuning and exact-match GRPO on all headline metrics (BERTScore, RadGraph F1 and CheXbert F1 by average \%6.80, \%7.82 and \%4.45 relative improvements respectively). The same set distances also enable test-time best-of-$N$ selection: scoring candidates by their distance to training-report embeddings outperforms random selection on our trained models as well as three closed-source LLMs (Mistral-Small, Gemini-2.5 Flash-Lite, GPT-4o-mini) with on average \%16.4 relative improvement on BERTScore. Used as a streaming signal, they support a more efficient form of test-time scaling: pruning low-scoring candidates mid-generation reduces generated tokens by over 50\% while preserving the Findings quality of full best-of-$N$ selection. Together these results establish set-distance rewards as a unified signal for both post-training and test-time scaling in chest X-ray report generation. Our code is publicly \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Set-Distance-Rewards-CXR-BFDA}{available}.

</details>


### [31] [Doing What They Say, Not What They Reason: Locating the Faithfulness Gap in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00476)
*Yufeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM agents in Texas Poker show mixed process fidelity: their reasoning-conclusion step is faithful but conclusion-action step has failures, revealing complex relationship between stated reasoning and actual behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to measure process fidelity of LLM agents - whether they act on the reasoning they state. This is crucial for using LLMs in social simulation but difficult to measure without a reference for correct behavior.

Method: The study uses a controlled Texas Poker simulator with verifiable reference actions for every decision. It decomposes the faithfulness gap into two steps: reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action.

Result: The two steps behave oppositely - reasoning-conclusion step shows faithfulness while conclusion-action step exhibits failures, creating a complex picture of LLM process fidelity.

Conclusion: LLM agents demonstrate complex process fidelity patterns with reasoning-conclusion faithfulness but conclusion-action misalignment, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of how LLMs translate stated reasoning into actions in simulation contexts.

Abstract: Do LLM agents act on the reasoning they state? This question of process fidelity is central to using LLMs in social simulation, yet it is hard to measure where no reference for correct behavior exists. We study it in acontrolled setting, a Texas Poker simulator with a verifiable reference action for every decision by decomposing the faithfulness gap into two steps: reasoning-conclusion and conclusion-action. The two steps behave oppositely.

</details>


### [32] [TAPS: Target-Aware Prefix Tree Selection for Diffusion-Drafted Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00487)
*Zhuoyu Wang,Junnan Huang,Xinyu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TAPS improves speculative decoding by using target-aware prefix selection to optimize verification of diffusion-drafted tokens, achieving up to 7.9x speedup over autoregressive decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While diffusion models enable parallel drafting for speculative decoding, existing methods suffer from verification bottlenecks: verifying single sequences limits acceptance length, while verifying large draft trees creates excessive target-model latency due to a mismatch between marginal probability ranking and prefix-conditioned verification requirements.

Method: TAPS (Target-Aware Prefix Selection) transforms diffusion marginals into path-conditioned acceptance estimates and selects a compact prefix-closed subtree under a fixed verification budget, improving the acceptance-cost tradeoff rather than simply expanding the draft tree.

Result: TAPS achieves up to 7.9x lossless end-to-end speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding, outperforming state-of-the-art DFlash and DDTree by 1.36x and 1.74x respectively across diverse datasets and model families.

Conclusion: Target-aware prefix selection effectively addresses the verification bottleneck in diffusion-based speculative decoding by optimizing the draft-verification tradeoff, leading to significant practical speedups for large language model inference.

Abstract: Using a diffusion model for parallel drafting is a promising approach for speculative decoding. By predicting tokens at multiple future positions in a single forward pass, diffusion drafters substantially reduce drafting latency. However, this shifts the bottleneck to verification: verifying a single sequence limits acceptance length, while verifying large draft trees incurs excessive target-model latency. We identify a key mismatch in existing draft-tree methods: existing diffusion-tree methods rank nodes by the marginal probability, ignoring that verification is prefix-conditioned. As a result, they may verify unreachable descendants of rejected prefixes, increasing latency with limited acceptance gains. To address this, we propose TAPS, a target-aware prefix selection method that turns diffusion marginals into path-conditioned acceptance estimates. TAPS then selects a compact prefix-closed subtree under a fixed verification budget, improving the acceptance-cost tradeoff rather than simply expanding the draft tree. Experiments across diverse datasets and model families demonstrate that TAPS achieves up to 7.9x lossless end-to-end speedup over vanilla autoregressive decoding, outperforming state-of-the-art DFlash and DDTree by 1.36x and 1.74x respectively. Our work is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TAPS-EMNLP2026-53DD

</details>


### [33] [EnergyMamba: An Uncertainty-Aware Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model for Energy Consumption Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00506)
*Dahai Yu,Rongchao Xu,Lin Jiang,Guang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EnergyMamba: An uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal learning framework that improves energy consumption prediction accuracy by 5% and uncertainty quantification by 6% over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing energy consumption prediction methods have two key limitations: (1) they treat it as purely time-series prediction without modeling spatial dependencies between regions, and (2) they fail to provide reliable predictions with uncertainty estimates during abnormal situations like extreme weather events.

Method: Proposes EnergyMamba framework with two key components: (1) Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model (GE-Mamba) that injects spatial context from grid topology into temporal dynamics for coupled spatiotemporal modeling, and (2) Adaptive Sequential Conformalized Quantile Regression (AS-CQR) with locally adaptive normalization and online feedback to dynamically calibrate prediction intervals under distribution shifts.

Result: Evaluated on four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California. EnergyMamba achieves approximately 5% improvement in prediction accuracy and 6% improvement in uncertainty quantification compared to 15 state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: EnergyMamba advances energy consumption prediction by providing accurate, reliable predictions with uncertainty estimates through its novel spatiotemporal modeling approach that addresses limitations of existing methods regarding spatial dependencies and distribution shifts.

Abstract: Energy consumption prediction is essential for efficient grid management, demand-side optimization, and sustainable energy planning. Although advanced machine learning methods have been employed for better prediction performance, existing works have two key limitations: (1) they usually formulate this task as a purely time-series prediction problem without explicitly modeling the spatial dependencies among different regions, and (2) they fail to provide reliable predictions with uncertainty estimates under abnormal situations such as extreme weather events. To advance existing research, we propose EnergyMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal learning framework for accurate and reliable energy consumption prediction, which comprises two key components: (i) a novel Graph-Enhanced Selective State Space Model (GE-Mamba) that injects spatial context learned from the grid topology into the temporal dynamics, enabling coupled spatiotemporal modeling, and (ii) an Adaptive Sequential Conformalized Quantile Regression (AS-CQR) module, which includes locally adaptive normalization and an online feedback mechanism to dynamically calibrate prediction intervals under potential distribution shifts. We evaluate EnergyMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from Florida, New York, and California. Results show EnergyMamba achieves around 5% improvement in prediction accuracy and 6% improvement in uncertainty quantification over 15 state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [34] [Threshold-Based Exclusive Batching for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00516)
*Weifang Zhang,Yuzhou Nie,Bowen Pang,Guangrui Ma,Shining Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mixed batching (MB) - the standard LLM inference scheduling - actually has hidden prefill-decode interference costs that make exclusive batching (EB) better on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, with an optimal hybrid scheduler EB+ that dynamically switches between them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed batching has become the standard scheduling strategy for LLM inference because it maximizes compute and memory utilization, but there's an unrecognized problem: prefill-decode interference inflates its marginal cost above pure decode, especially on bandwidth-constrained GPUs.

Method: The authors conducted controlled experiments to measure prefill-decode interference, derived a closed-form condition for the EB-MB performance crossover based on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition, developed asymptotically optimal phase-switching thresholds and memory-safe batch sizing for EB, and created a hybrid scheduler called EB+ that applies the condition online to dynamically switch between EB and MB.

Result: Optimized EB achieves up to 41.9% higher throughput on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, while MB retains its advantage on high-bandwidth hardware with larger models. The hybrid scheduler EB+ attains the highest or near-highest throughput in every setting, outperforming MB by up to 36.4% under non-stationary traffic with distribution or concurrency shifts.

Conclusion: The optimal choice between mixed batching and exclusive batching depends fundamentally on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition, and a hybrid approach that dynamically switches between them can achieve superior performance across diverse hardware and workload conditions without manual intervention.

Abstract: Mixed batching (MB)--interleaving prefill and decode in a single batch--has become the standard scheduling strategy for large language model (LLM) inference due to its efficiency in maximizing compute and memory utilization. However, through controlled experiments, we find that prefill-decode interference inflates MB's per-step marginal cost above that of pure decode. On the high-bandwidth H200 (4.8 TB/s), this occurs only when decode tokens exceed 80% of the batch; however, on the bandwidth-constrained RTX PRO 6000 (1.792 TB/s), this threshold plummets to just 20%. Consequently, the optimal choice between MB and exclusive batching (EB) fundamentally depends on GPU memory bandwidth, model size, and workload composition. We derive a closed-form condition for this EB-MB performance crossover, along with asymptotically optimal phase-switching thresholds and memory-safe batch sizing for EB. Optimized EB achieves up to 41.9% higher throughput on bandwidth-constrained GPUs, while MB retains its advantage on high-bandwidth hardware with larger models. Our hybrid scheduler EB+ applies this condition online to dynamically switch between EB and MB without manual intervention. Under non-stationary traffic with distribution or concurrency shifts, EB+ attains the highest or near-highest throughput in every setting, outperforming MB by up to 36.4%.

</details>


### [35] [Acting with AI: An Interaction-Based Framework for Agentic Tort Liability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00518)
*Yiheng Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a legal framework for assigning tort liability in AI-agentic systems by categorizing human-AI interactions into three types and applying different legal doctrines to each.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tort law struggles to allocate responsibility when AI systems cause harm, as the harmful path may be neither fully chosen by the user nor specifically foreseen by the developer. Current legal frameworks are inadequate for agentic AI systems that can plan, use tools, and execute tasks autonomously over time.

Method: The paper develops an interaction-based framework drawing on Michael Bratman's planning theory and common law treatment of human-human concerted action. It distinguishes three interaction types: autonomous drift, pure tool use, and collaborative planning. The framework treats stateful interaction logs as primary evidentiary traces to determine where liability should attach.

Result: The framework maps each interaction type to specific legal doctrines: pure tool use to product-defect/warning doctrines, collaborative planning to independent contractor control/professional malpractice, and autonomous drift to frolic and detour/respondeat superior. The paper resolves four incident-anchored cases and proposes a "Reasonable Agent" standard with four components: constraint verification, epistemic transparency, runtime grounding, and forensic logging.

Conclusion: Agentic AI liability requires a nuanced, interaction-based approach that moves beyond traditional product liability frameworks. The proposed framework provides courts with tools to analyze human-AI interactions and assign responsibility based on the nature of the collaboration, with the "Reasonable Agent" standard offering practical implementation guidelines.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems can plan over multiple steps, use tools, and execute tasks over time. When such systems cause harm, tort law struggles to allocate responsibility because the harmful path may be neither fully chosen by the user nor specifically foreseen by the developer. This paper proposes an interaction-based framework for agentic torts, drawing on Michael Bratman's planning theory and on the common law's treatment of human-human concerted action. We distinguish three interaction types: autonomous drift, pure tool use, and collaborative planning. Pure tool cases remain governed by ordinary product-defect and warning doctrines; collaborative planning cases map onto the independent contractor control test, professional malpractice, and negligent misrepresentation; autonomous drift maps onto frolic and detour under respondeat superior and strict product liability. The framework treats the stateful interaction log as the primary evidentiary trace, allowing courts to infer where the human-AI trajectory departed from the authorized undertaking and where liability should attach. We resolve four incident-anchored cases, situate the account alongside strict-liability and insurance-based proposals, note its relationship to regulatory oversight, and propose a ``Reasonable Agent'' standard built around constraint verification, epistemic transparency, runtime grounding, and forensic logging.

</details>


### [36] [KACE: Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering for Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00532)
*Jayant Parashar,Suchendra M. Bhandarkar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KACE introduces a tiered context engineering approach that separates knowledge storage from usage through difficulty-based organization, reducing context bloat in mathematical reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context engineering methods for LLMs face limitations in mathematical reasoning due to context bloat - feedback accumulated in growing prompts limits the amount of learned guidance that can be used. Current methods conflate storage (what is learned) with usage (what is included), inheriting prompt-size constraints.

Method: KACE separates storage from usage through difficulty- and domain-based organization. Offline, a self-reflective learning loop distills training traces into an epistemic tree (knowledge base) with typed cards stratified by problem difficulty and epistemic domain. At evaluation, tiered self-consistency with per-tier agreement gates classifies problems as easy/medium/hard. Easy problems use no retrieved cards, harder problems retrieve only matching tree branches.

Result: On AIME 2025, KACE achieves 62.2% accuracy, a 10.4-point absolute gain over fixed Best-of-5 self-consistency at comparable compute, and 5.6-point gain over strongest learned-context baseline. It matches/exceeds Best-of-N with comparable compute, classifies difficulty with 78% pairwise concordance. Shows consistent gains on MATH-HARD and OlymMATH.

Conclusion: KACE's main contribution is a difficulty- and domain-stratified knowledge base enabled by tiered self-consistency, effectively addressing context bloat in mathematical reasoning by separating storage from usage through adaptive difficulty-based retrieval.

Abstract: Context engineering can improve large language models without updating their weights, but mathematical reasoning exposes a key limitation: feedback accumulated in one growing prompt causes context bloat and limits the amount of learned guidance that can be used. Existing methods often conflate storage, what is learned across runs, with usage, what is included for a particular problem, and therefore inherit this prompt-size ceiling. We introduce Knowledge-Adaptive Context Engineering (KACE), which separates storage from usage through difficulty- and domain-based organization. Offline, a self-reflective learning loop distills training traces into an epistemic tree: a knowledge base of typed cards stratified by problem difficulty and epistemic domain. Each card is assigned to the difficulty-domain node corresponding to the failure from which it originated. At evaluation time, tiered self-consistency with per-tier agreement gates dynamically classifies each problem as easy, medium, or hard. Easy problems exit without retrieved cards, while harder problems retrieve only the matching branch of the tree. This tiered scheme matches or exceeds Best-of-N while using comparable compute, and it classifies problem difficulty with 78 percent pairwise concordance. The main empirical contribution is the construction and use of a difficulty- and domain-stratified knowledge base enabled by tiered self-consistency. On AIME 2025, KACE achieves 62.2 percent accuracy, a 10.4-point absolute gain over fixed Best-of-5 self-consistency at a comparable solver-call budget and a 5.6-point gain over the strongest learned-context baseline, Tiered + GEPA. We also observe consistent gains on MATH-HARD and the verifiable subset of OlymMATH.

</details>


### [37] [Probe Before You Edit: Probing-Guided Molecular Optimization for LLM Agents in Structure-Based Drug Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00555)
*Zaifei Yang,Weiyu Chen,Yaqing Wang,James Kwok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PROBE is a new framework that uses edit-response probing and multi-agent collaboration to simultaneously optimize both binding affinity and druggability in structure-based drug design, overcoming limitations of current LLM agents that rarely achieve joint improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM agents in structure-based drug design struggle to simultaneously optimize both binding affinity and druggability objectives. Single optimization steps rarely improve both together, and gains on one objective often come with losses on the other. There's a need for methods that can navigate this trade-off more effectively.

Method: PROBE uses edit-response probing inspired by medicinal chemists. It first decomposes ligands into editable sites and creates a pocket-specific site map. Then it performs controlled probe edits to understand how modifications affect the complex, distilling responses into an EditManual. Finally, it runs an iterative multi-agent loop with specialized agents for affinity, druggability, and co-optimization working together.

Result: PROBE achieves state-of-the-art performance on the CrossDocked2020 benchmark and substantially mitigates the failure modes identified by the authors' diagnostic metrics. It shows improved ability to achieve joint improvements in both binding affinity and druggability.

Conclusion: The PROBE framework successfully addresses the dual-objective optimization challenge in structure-based drug design by incorporating edit-response probing and multi-agent collaboration, enabling more effective navigation of the trade-off between binding affinity and druggability.

Abstract: Structure-based drug design increasingly employs LLM agents to iteratively refine ligands against a target pocket, yet a viable ligand must satisfy two often-conflicting objectives -- binding affinity and druggability -- which single optimization steps rarely improve together. To quantify this difficulty, we introduce two diagnostic metrics: the first measures how often a single edit improves both objectives, and the second measures how often a gain on one objective comes with a loss on the other. Applying these diagnostics to current LLM-agent pipelines exposes a consistent failure mode: the agent performs molecular editing without knowing how the pocket-ligand complex responds to local modifications, thus rarely achieving joint improvement. Inspired by medicinal chemists, who probe the pocket-ligand complex with controlled analog edits before choosing an optimization direction, we propose \textbf{PROBE}, an optimization framework built around edit-response probing. PROBE first decomposes the ligand into editable sites and builds a pocket-specific \textbf{site map} that flags where joint gains are plausible, where the two objectives are likely in tension, and where liability substructures should be changed; it then performs controlled probe edits whose responses are distilled into an \textbf{EditManual}. Guided by the site map and EditManual, PROBE runs an iterative multi-agent loop in which an affinity agent, a druggability agent, and a co-optimization agent jointly produce edits. On the CrossDocked2020 benchmark, PROBE achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially mitigates the failure modes exposed by our diagnostics metrics.

</details>


### [38] [PropLLM: Propagation-Aware Scene Reconstruction for Network Fault Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00582)
*Zongzong Wu,Ming Zhao,Fengxiao Tang,Nei Kato*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PropLLM uses hop-by-hop reconstruction with LLMs and causal attention to resolve network fault ambiguity, improving diagnosis and localization accuracy while reducing hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fault diagnosis approaches (rule-based, ML-based, or LLM-based) fundamentally map alert sets to diagnoses in a single pass and are structurally incapable of resolving end-point ambiguity where distinct root-cause faults produce highly similar symptoms.

Method: PropLLM traces back hop-by-hop along propagation paths, retrieving factual evidence from a dual-layer knowledge graph at each hop while using Temporal Causal Propagation Attention (TCPA) to encode topological causal priors into attention computation to guide the model along correct causal directions.

Result: On real-world Wi-Fi multimodal fault datasets, PropLLM improved fault type diagnosis accuracy by 3.9% and root cause localization accuracy by 4.7% over strongest baselines, while reducing hallucination rate by 50.8%. Supplementary experiments on TeleLogs 5G dataset demonstrated effectiveness across different network scenarios.

Conclusion: PropLLM successfully integrates hop-by-hop scene reconstruction with LLM reasoning, using temporal causal propagation attention and a dual-layer knowledge graph to achieve significant improvements in both fault type diagnosis and root cause localization accuracy while substantially reducing hallucination rates across different network scenarios.

Abstract: Network faults propagate layer by layer along topology and protocol dependencies, yet operations systems typically observe only symptomatic alerts at the tail end of propagation chains, where distinct root-cause faults may produce highly similar end-point symptoms. Existing approaches, whether rule-based, machine learning (ML)-based, or large language model (LLM)-based, fundamentally map the alert set to a diagnosis in a single pass and are structurally incapable of resolving this end-point ambiguity. This paper proposes PropLLM, which is the first to integrate the hop-by-hop scene reconstruction paradigm with the generative reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Starting from end-point alerts, PropLLM traces back hop-by-hop along the propagation path, retrieving verifiable factual evidence from a dual-layer knowledge graph (KG) at each hop, while the proposed Temporal Causal Propagation Attention (TCPA) mechanism encodes known topological causal priors directly into the attention computation to guide the model along the correct causal direction, ultimately localizing the root cause and determining the fault type through a fully evidenced causal chain. On a real-world Wi-Fi multimodal fault dataset, PropLLM improves fault type diagnosis accuracy by 3.9\% and root cause localization accuracy by 4.7\% over the strongest baseline, while reducing the hallucination rate by 50.8\%. Supplementary experiments on the TeleLogs 5G dataset further demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method across different network scenarios.

</details>


### [39] [TRACE: Trajectory Risk-Aware Compression for Long-Horizon Agent Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00611)
*Zhepei Hong,Lin Wang,Liting Li,Haokai Ma,Junfeng Fang,Fei Shen,Dan Zhang,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE is a new method for detecting safety risks in long-horizon LLM agents by compressing trajectory evidence into a compact latent state that serves as a safety reference for risk assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety detection methods fail for long-horizon LLM agents because sparse, delayed, and compositional risk signals get lost in extended trajectories. Turn-level or short-context detectors can't reliably retain and aggregate evidence over long horizons.

Method: TRACE uses a Compressor-Reader design: the Compressor encodes the full trajectory into a compact latent evidence state under trajectory-level supervision, and the Reader judges the raw trajectory using this latent evidence state as a safety reference.

Result: TRACE achieves best accuracy on ASSEBench, Pre-Ex-Bench, and R-Judge, improving over strong baselines by up to 12.6 percentage points. On LongSafety, it shows smaller performance degradation as context length grows. Visualizations show the compressed reference helps focus on risk-critical segments.

Conclusion: Trajectory-level evidence compression effectively addresses long-horizon agent safety detection by aggregating dispersed risk cues and reducing premature evidence loss, with TRACE demonstrating superior performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents produce safety evidence across long trajectories, where sparse, delayed, and compositional risk signals often escape local moderation. Existing turn-level or short-context detectors struggle to reliably retain and aggregate such evidence over extended horizons. We reframe long-horizon agent safety detection as trajectory-level evidence compression and propose Trajectory Risk-Aware Compression for Long-Horizon Agent Safety (TRACE). TRACE uses a Compressor-Reader design: the Compressor encodes the full trajectory into a compact latent evidence state under trajectory-level supervision, and the Reader judges the raw trajectory with this latent evidence state as a safety reference. This design helps aggregate dispersed risk cues and reduce premature evidence loss. Across ASSEBench, Pre-Ex-Bench, and R-Judge, TRACE achieves the best accuracy on all evaluated backbones, improving over strong baselines by up to 12.6 percentage points. On LongSafety, TRACE shows smaller performance degradation as context length grows. Attention visualizations and case studies suggest that the compressed reference helps the Reader focus on risk-critical segments and recover cross-step evidence. Code is available at https://github.com/Peregrine123/TRACE_official.

</details>


### [40] [Efficient Test-time Inference for Generative Planning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00618)
*Robert Gieselmann,Mihai Samson,Federico Pecora,Jeremy L. Wyatt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Modified OCL search with learned generative and heuristic models provides efficient inference for AI planning, beating existing methods in speed and solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models for AI planning are constrained by training data distribution. While scaling test-time compute can improve solutions, there's a need for more efficient inference optimization.

Method: Modified Open-Closed List (OCL) search that synergizes two learned components: 1) a generative model for fast rollouts from intermediate states, and 2) a heuristic model to prioritize candidate reasoning paths. Includes novel exploration control mechanisms and integration of learned models within the OCL framework.

Result: The approach outperforms both neurosymbolic search baselines and classical solvers across multiple combinatorial planning domains in terms of computational efficiency and solution quality.

Conclusion: The modified Open-Closed List search with learned components demonstrates superior performance over existing approaches in combinatorial planning domains, offering an efficient inference procedure for generative AI planning.

Abstract: Generative models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for AI planning, yet their performance remains constrained by the training data distribution. One approach is to improve generated solutions during inference by scaling test-time compute. A more efficient alternative is to optimize the inference process itself. In this paper, we show that a modified version of a classical Open-Closed List (OCL) search provides just such an efficient inference procedure. Our algorithm synergizes two learned components: a generative model that performs fast rollouts from intermediate states and a heuristic model that prioritizes among candidate reasoning paths. Key contributions include novel exploration control mechanisms and integration of learned models within the OCL framework. Across multiple combinatorial planning domains, our approach outperforms both neurosymbolic search baselines and classical solvers in computational efficiency and solution quality.

</details>


### [41] [Hidden Thoughts Are Not Secret: Reasoning Trace Exposure in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00642)
*Yu-An Lu,Ci-Yang Tsai,Yu-Lin Tsai,Raluca Ada Popa,Chia-Mu Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP) to extract reasoning traces from models that hide them, enabling better reasoning supervision transfer between models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many deployed reasoning systems hide raw internal traces and only expose summaries and answers, preventing users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision through prompting. The authors investigate whether this interface-level trace hiding limits users' ability to learn from model reasoning.

Method: The authors develop Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), a lightweight in-context elicitation method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations wrapped in auxiliary code-like formats to raise user-visible reasoning traces from a victim model.

Result: REP substantially increases similarity between exposed and REP-conditioned internal traces while preserving useful reasoning signals across different reasoning datasets, victim models, and student model distillation scenarios.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP) is an effective technique that allows users to extract useful reasoning supervision from models that would otherwise hide their internal traces, enabling better capability transfer between models.

Abstract: Reasoning traces have become a valuable form of learning signals for improving and transferring the capabilities of large language models. In particular, detailed traces can help distill reasoning behavior from stronger teacher models into weaker student models. The value of capability transfer has motivated many deployed systems with reasoning models to hide raw internal traces and expose at most summaries and answers to users. As a result, we ask whether such interface-level trace hiding prevents users from obtaining useful reasoning supervision through prompting. We study this question with Reasoning Exposure Prompting (REP), a lightweight in-context elicitation method that uses shadow-model-generated demonstrations wrapped in auxiliary code-like formats to raise user-visible reasoning traces from a victim model. Across the common reasoning dataset, different victim models, and different student model distillation, REP substantially increases similarity between exposed and REP-conditioned internal traces while preserving useful reasoning signals.

</details>


### [42] [ForeSci: Evaluating LLM Agents for Forward-Looking AI Research Judgment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00644)
*Qiuyu Tian,Zequn Liu,Yingce Xia,Haojie Yin,Youyong Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ForeSci is a benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can make forward-looking research decisions using only historical evidence, with controlled temporal constraints to avoid hindsight bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI research requires making forward-looking decisions before future evidence exists (e.g., which bottleneck to attack, which direction to pursue). Current benchmarks don't adequately test this temporal reasoning capability in research agents.

Method: Created ForeSci benchmark with 500 tasks across four fast-moving AI domains and four decision families. Each task has a cutoff-aligned offline knowledge base where post-cutoff papers are hidden during generation. Tasks derived from pre-cutoff taxonomy branches and evidence signals, with answer-generation backbones selected to precede task cutoffs to prevent hindsight bias. Evaluated native LLMs, Hybrid RAG, and three research-agent adaptations across four backbones.

Result: Explicit evidence organization improves traceability and factual support, but performance gains depend strongly on the decision family. Diagnostics reveal evidence-decision decoupling: agents may cite relevant evidence while forecasting wrong research objects.

Conclusion: ForeSci successfully turns forward-looking AI research judgement into a controlled benchmark for evaluating research agents as decision-making systems, enabling systematic assessment of their ability to make temporally constrained research predictions.

Abstract: AI research often requires decisions before future evidence exists: which bottleneck to attack, which direction to pursue, or where a project should be positioned. We introduce ForeSci, a temporally controlled benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can make such forward-looking research judgements from historical evidence. ForeSci contains 500 tasks across four fast-moving AI domains and four decision families. Each task is paired with a cutoff-aligned offline knowledge base; post-cutoff papers are hidden during generation and used only for validation. To avoid random future-event prediction, tasks are derived from pre-cutoff taxonomy branches and evidence signals, and answer-generation backbones are selected to precede the task cutoffs. We evaluate native LLMs, Hybrid RAG, and three research-agent adaptations across four backbones. Results show that explicit evidence organization improves traceability and factual support, but gains depend strongly on the decision family. Diagnostics reveal a recurring evidence-decision decoupling: agents may cite relevant evidence while forecasting the wrong research object. ForeSci turns forward-looking AI research judgement into a controlled benchmark for evaluating research agents as decision-making systems.

</details>


### [43] [AXIOM: A Trust-First Neuro-Symbolic Execution Architecture for Verifiable Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00671)
*Alessio Bruno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AXIOM is a trustworthy neuro-symbolic system for math reasoning where the LLM canonicalizes problems for deterministic CAS verification, achieving 94% accuracy with 100% trust on parseable problems and zero regressions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a trustworthy neuro-symbolic system for natural-language mathematical reasoning that prioritizes reliability over raw accuracy, ensuring zero confident-wrong answers while enabling continuous improvement without regressing previously correct solutions.

Method: AXIOM uses a language model strictly as a canonicalizer to rewrite informal problems into narrow schemas for deterministic Computer-Algebra-System (CAS) pipeline. It employs a 1:1:1 alignment between problem-shape regex, schema-specific prompt, and closed-form CAS handler, with 3,100+ such routes. The system abstains when it cannot parse or handle problems.

Result: Cumulative correctness of 94.36% (2,592/2,747) at 100% trust on parseable problems (zero confident-wrong answers). All four MATH domains exceed per-domain 70/90/70 floor with 100% trust per domain. Median latency of 1 ms on rule-only handlers (88% of records). Served ~30,000 production queries through public deployment with zero LOST_CORRECT regressions across 250+ ship commits.

Conclusion: AXIOM demonstrates a viable architecture for trustworthy neuro-symbolic mathematical reasoning with 100% trust on parseable problems and continuous improvement without regression. Its operational framework (math-template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT regression oracle, parseable-first onboarding, and abstain-as-output) provides a transferable model for building trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems beyond mathematics.

Abstract: We present AXIOM, a trust-first neuro-symbolic execution architecture for natural-language mathematical reasoning. In AXIOM, the language model functions strictly as a canonicalizer: it rewrites informal problem text into a narrow schema consumed by a deterministic Computer-Algebra-System (CAS) pipeline, which derives and verifies the answer or abstains as a first-class output. Routing follows a 1:1:1 alignment between problem-shape regex, schema-specific prompt, and closed-form CAS handler, with 3,100+ such routes shipped and zero LOST_CORRECT regressions across 250+ consecutive ship commits. We report empirical results on 4 MATH categories with a cumulative correctness of 94.36% (2,592/2,747) at 100.00% trust on parseable (zero confident-wrong answers across the full 2,747-record benchmark), all four domains above the per-domain 70/90/70 floor with per-domain trust at 100.0%, and median latency of 1 ms on rule-only handlers (88% of records on the lm-eval arithmetic 20,000-record benchmark). The architecture has served ~30,000 production queries through a public deployment. The contribution we emphasize is not a final accuracy figure but the forward dynamic the architecture establishes: every logged abstain in production is a candidate correct after one ship cycle, since new tasks compose without regressing the registry. The operational discipline behind this property -- math-template bucketing, LOST_CORRECT scan as regression oracle, parseable-first onboarding, and abstain as first-class output -- constitutes a transferable framework for trustworthy neuro-symbolic systems beyond mathematics.

</details>


### [44] [Medication-Aware Financial Exploitation Detection for Alzheimer's Patients Using Edge-Aware Interaction Risk Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00672)
*Farzana Akter,Lisan Al Amin,Rakib Hossain,Chaitanya Gunupudi,Faisal Quader*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: generate a too long; didn't read summary


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: describe the motivation in this paper

Method: method of this paper

Result: result of this paper

Conclusion: conclusion of this paper

Abstract: Financial exploitation is a growing concern for people with Alzheimer's disease, especially during periods of reduced cognitive stability. Conventional fraud detection systems usually rely on financial behavior alone and ignore clinically relevant factors that may alter vulnerability. This paper proposes a medication-aware framework that synchronizes medication adherence with transaction-level monitoring to improve detection of cognitively risky financial events. A hybrid simulation dataset was constructed for 180 patients across 45 days, producing 8,100 medication records and 30,855 transactions. The framework evaluates amount anomaly, vendor novelty, transaction frequency, time deviation, and medication adherence through financial-only, additive medication-aware, and interaction-aware logistic models. Results show that the financial-only baseline obtained the highest global F1-score of 0.5000, but the interaction-aware model improved recall during medication-induced vulnerability windows from 0.7442 to 0.9070 and achieved the highest average precision for ranked high-risk cases. The findings suggest that medication adherence is most useful as a contextual modifier of financial risk rather than as an isolated predictor.

</details>


### [45] [Regularized Offline Policy Optimization with Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00680)
*Hongqiang Lin,Pengfei Wang,Nenggan Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PhyB framework reformulates Bayesian RL expectations to overcome computational challenges while maintaining theoretical guarantees and achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL faces epistemic uncertainty from limited data coverage and ambiguous transition dynamics. Bayesian RL offers unified uncertainty quantification but suffers from computational challenges in policy optimization.

Method: Proposes Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB) which reformulates expectation as convex combination over subset of dynamics models, then develops iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows bounded objective discrepancy from approximation. Empirical results demonstrate PhyB achieves SOTA performance on benchmarks.

Conclusion: PhyB framework successfully addresses computational challenges in Bayesian RL, achieving state-of-the-art performance with theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to optimize policies from pre-collected datasets. A bottleneck of this paradigm is managing epistemic uncertainty, which arises from limited data coverage (sample-level) and the ambiguity in identifying transition dynamics from finite data (model-level). To provide a unified quantification of these uncertainties, Bayesian RL has been proposed by treating the dynamics model as a random variable and maintaining a corresponding belief. Despite its theoretical appeal, policy optimization in Bayesian RL remains computationally challenging as it requires solving composite objectives with expectations. Prior methods either employ search-based techniques with poor computational scalability or impose restrictive posterior assumptions that sacrifice the adaptability of Bayesian RL. To address these limitations, we propose Posterior Hybrid Bayesian Belief (PhyB), which reformulates the expectation as a convex combination over a subset of dynamics models. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the objective discrepancy induced by this approximation remains bounded. Based on PhyB, we develop an iterative regularized policy optimization algorithm that provides metric-agnostic guarantees for monotonic improvement until convergence. Empirical results demonstrate that PhyB achieves state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks.

</details>


### [46] [MOSAIC: Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00708)
*Yifan Bao,Xinyu Xi,Xinyu Liu,Wen Ge,Lei Jiang,Kevin Zhang,Raad Khraishi,Yihao Ang,Anthony K. H. Tung,Lukasz Szpruch,Hao Ni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ‘MOSAIC’ is a structured agentic framework for automated data science. It constructs a blueprint (intermediate representation) from task profiles and retrieved code, turning model selection into a staged, evidence-grounded search followed by execution validation and refinement. It outperforms AutoML and LLM agents on financial forecasting tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AutoML systems are limited to searching predefined pipeline/model/hyperparameter spaces, while LLM-based agents, though more flexible, produce unstructured, hard-to-verify, and non-reusable modelling decisions. There is a need for structured, memory-grounded, and execution-validated automated data science.

Method: Introduces ‘MOSAIC’ (Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition), a framework that builds a task profile, retrieves prior cases and code modules, creates an intermediate blueprint for modelling components, executes candidate models, and refines them via diagnostic feedback and failure-aware RL.

Result: Experiments in financial time-series forecasting/generation show that ‘MOSAIC’ outperforms AutoML and agentic baselines in task performance, execution success rate, and decision traceability.

Conclusion: ‘Automated data science’ should be treated as a structured, reusable, execution-grounded model selection problem; structured agentic frameworks can outperform both AutoML and purely generative LLM agents.

Abstract: Automated data science is a structured model-selection problem. A solution must choose data transformations, feature representations, architecture, training procedure, evaluation protocol, and refinement strategy for a task. AutoML systems automate parts of this process, but typically search within predefined pipeline, model, and hyperparameter spaces. LLM-based agents offer greater flexibility through retrieval, code generation, and execution feedback, yet their modelling decisions are often unstructured, difficult to verify, and hard to reuse. We introduce \textsc{MOSAIC} (Modular Orchestration for Structured Agentic Intelligence and Composition), a structured agentic framework for memory-grounded model selection and workflow construction. Given a task and dataset, \textsc{MOSAIC} builds a semantic task profile, retrieves prior cases and source-code modules, and constructs a blueprint: an intermediate representation specifying selected modelling components, composition, interface constraints, and execution requirements. This blueprint turns model selection into a staged, context-grounded search and grounds LLM-based code generation in retrieved evidence rather than unconstrained synthesis. Candidate models are validated by execution and refined using diagnostic feedback, training traces, task metrics, and a failure-aware reinforcement learning policy. We instantiate \textsc{MOSAIC} on financial time-series forecasting and generation, where models must satisfy predictive accuracy, distributional fidelity, execution reliability, and downstream financial criteria such as risk and tail behaviour. Experiments against AutoML and agentic baselines show that \textsc{MOSAIC} improves task performance, execution success, and decision traceability, demonstrating the value of treating automated data science as structured, reusable, and execution-grounded model selection.

</details>


### [47] [LLM-Driven Co-Evolutionary Automated Heuristic Design for Bi-Component Coupled Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00718)
*Mingen Kuang,Xudong Deng,Xi Lin,Ye Fan,Jianyong Sun,Jialong Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoEvo-AHD is an LLM-driven dual-population co-evolutionary framework for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization problems like TTP and TPP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based automated heuristic design methods typically generate heuristics as single operators, limiting their ability to model strong coupling among multiple decision substructures in problems like TTP and TPP.

Method: CoEvo-AHD uses an LLM-driven dual-population co-evolutionary framework with cooperative evaluation mechanism, pairwise scoring, and synergistic joint crossover. It includes a tool-invocation environment library with standardized interfaces for core operations.

Result: Experiments on TTP and TPP show CoEvo-AHD automatically discovers cooperative heuristic combinations and achieves competitive solution quality against traditional heuristics.

Conclusion: CoEvo-AHD demonstrates the effectiveness of LLM-driven dual-population co-evolution for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization problems, achieving competitive performance against traditional methods.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently shown promise in Automated Heuristic Design (AHD), existing methods typically generate and evolve heuristics as a single operator or search strategy, limiting their ability to model strong coupling among multiple decision substructures in problems such as the Traveling Thief Problem (TTP) and the Traveling Purchaser Problem (TPP). In this work, we propose CoEvo-AHD, an LLM-driven dual-population co-evolutionary framework for automated heuristic design in coupled combinatorial optimization. Unlike prior methods that evolve individual heuristics in isolation, CoEvo-AHD leverages LLMs to co-evolve two closely related operator populations. A cooperative evaluation mechanism explicitly captures interactions between route and selection operators, while pairwise scoring and synergistic joint crossover help discover complementary operator logic for joint improvement across coupled decision subspaces. We further design a tool-invocation environment library that encapsulates frequently used core operations, such as local-search delta computation, into callable functions, enabling LLM-generated operators to use standardized interfaces instead of reimplementing inefficient and error-prone problem-specific loops. Experiments on TTP and TPP show that CoEvo-AHD automatically discovers cooperative heuristic combinations and achieves competitive solution quality against traditional heuristics.

</details>


### [48] [Latent Reward Steering: An Adaptive Inference-Time Framework that Implicitly Promotes Cognitive Behaviors in Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00726)
*Jiakang Li,Guanyu Zhu,Can Jin,Chenxi Huang,Dexu Yu,Ronghao Chen,Yang Zhou,Hongwu Peng,Xuanqi Lan,Dimitris N. Metaxas,Youhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Latent Reward Steering adaptively improves reasoning by optimizing latent states that implicitly carry cognitive behaviors, using reward-based corrections only when needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for controlling cognitive behaviors during reasoning are insufficiently adaptive as failures and required corrections vary across reasoning states, tasks, and models.

Method: Trains a latent reward model on reasoning traces by final answer correctness, estimates quality of intermediate latent states, uses reward gradients for state-specific corrections, and employs a reward/confidence gate to restrict intervention.

Result: Experiments on multiple reasoning LLM backbones and benchmarks show consistent performance improvements over various baselines, with post-hoc analyses indicating implicit promotion of good cognitive behaviors.

Conclusion: Latent Reward Steering offers an effective, adaptive framework for improving reasoning in LLMs by implicitly promoting beneficial cognitive behaviors through latent state optimization.

Abstract: Strong reasoning depends not only on model knowledge but also on how effectively cognitive behaviors are deployed during generation. Existing methods often rely on explicit behavior-level control, making them insufficiently adaptive when failures and required corrections vary across reasoning states, tasks, and models. To this end, we propose Latent Reward Steering (LRS), an adaptive inference-time framework that promotes cognitive behaviors by optimizing the sparse-autoencoder (SAE) latent states that implicitly carry them. Rather than relying on predefined cognitive behaviors or steering directions derived from them, LRS trains a latent reward model on reasoning traces by final answer correctness to estimate the quality of intermediate latent states. During inference, reward gradients provide state-specific correction directions for fragile latent states, while a reward and confidence gate restricts intervention to states the reward signal flags as fragile. Experiments on multiple reasoning LLM backbones and benchmarks show that \ours consistently improves performance over various baselines, and post-hoc analyses further indicate that \ours implicitly promotes good cognitive behaviors that fix the original reasoning errors. Code is available at: https://github.com/jiakanglee/Latent-Reward-Steering.

</details>


### [49] [AI Sovereignty as National Learning Capacity: A Human-Centered Learning Mechanics Viewpoint on France, the United States, and China](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00729)
*Kim Phuc Tran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: France's AI development should be viewed as a national learning system balancing information injection (compute, data, talent) and entropy dissipation (organizational friction, regulation), where sovereignty depends on regulating these dynamics, not just scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmented discussion of AI in France (investment, compute, regulation, employment, sovereignty, education) by proposing a unified framework that treats France as a 'national AI learning system' governed by information dynamics rather than isolated policy dimensions.

Method: The paper employs a multi-disciplinary approach combining theoretical modeling (mathematical model of national AI learning system), measurable policy indicators, game-theoretic propositions, illustrative simulations of national AI regimes, and concrete policy analysis.

Result: Develops the concept of a national AI learning system using Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM) framework, interprets AI sovereignty as a country's capacity to regulate its information dynamics (balance between information injection and entropy dissipation), and provides concrete policy indicators and implications for France.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by reframing AI policy as the governance of an open, strategic, non-equilibrium learning system, moving beyond simplistic binary debates and proposing a concrete framework for France's AI strategy.

Abstract: Artificial Intelligence is often discussed in France in terms of investment, compute capacity, regulation, employment, sovereignty, and education. These dimensions are usually treated separately. This viewpoint paper proposes a unified interpretation: France should be understood as a \emph{national AI learning system}. Building on Human-Centered Learning Mechanics (HCLM), recently formulated as a dynamical framework for entropy-regulated representation learning, we interpret national AI development as a controlled balance between information injection and entropy dissipation. Information injection corresponds to compute, data, talent, research, capital, industrial deployment, and institutional experimentation. Entropy dissipation corresponds to organizational complexity, coordination frictions, energy constraints, regulatory uncertainty, talent mobility pressures, and opportunities to strengthen industrial absorption. The central claim is that AI sovereignty does not emerge from scale alone but from a country's capacity to regulate its own information dynamics. This paper connects HCLM with neural scaling laws, endogenous growth theory, creative destruction, and game theory. It argues that the French AI debate should move beyond the binary opposition between techno-optimism and regulation-first caution. A competitive and human-centered AI strategy requires a controlled regime in which information injection grows faster than institutional dissipation, while avoiding unstable, unequal, or energy-intensive expansion. We provide a mathematical model, measurable policy indicators, game-theoretic propositions, illustrative simulations of national AI regimes, and concrete policy implications for France. The proposed viewpoint reframes AI policy as the governance of an open, strategic, non-equilibrium learning system.

</details>


### [50] [SHARP: Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay for Long Range Non-Stationary Temporal Pattern Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00732)
*Jayanta Dey,Shikhar Srivastava,Itamar Lerner,Christopher Kanan,Dhireesha Kudithipudi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SHARP is a streaming sequence learning framework using hierarchical accelerated replay inspired by sleep to handle long-range non-stationary temporal patterns without backpropagation through time, achieving better performance with linear computational scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard sequence models (RNNs, transformers) struggle with long-range non-stationary temporal patterns in strict streaming settings where data arrives sequentially and must be processed without revisiting past observations, due to limitations in backpropagation through time and explicit window constraints.

Method: SHARP decomposes temporal learning into two modules: a memory module that accumulates structured history, and a pattern-recognition module. It incorporates offline 'sleep' phases where memory traces are replayed in accelerated form and integrated into higher-level representations, inspired by biological sleep processes.

Result: SHARP improves over recurrent baselines in benchmark datasets (text8, PG-19), retaining next-token predictive performance on previously seen data while continuing to learn from current streams and generalizing to future unseen data, with linear-time computational cost achieving exponentially increasing effective temporal context.

Conclusion: The paper introduces SHARP as a novel streaming sequence learning framework that effectively handles long-range non-stationary temporal patterns through hierarchical memory acceleration, providing superior performance on language modeling benchmarks with linear-time computational scaling.

Abstract: Learning long-range non-stationary temporal patterns remains a core challenge for modern sequence models, particularly in strict streaming settings. In these settings, data arrive sequentially and must be processed in a single pass without simultaneously revisiting past observations. Standard architectures, including recurrent neural networks and transformers, are constrained by either truncated backpropagation through time horizon or explicit input window length for long range credit assignment. To address these limitations, we propose SHARP (Sleep-based Hierarchical Accelerated Replay), a framework that decomposes temporal learning into two complementary components: a memory module that accumulates a structured history of past inputs, and a pattern-recognition module that operates over this memory. This separation enables resource- and compute-efficient adaptation to non-stationary dynamics by eliminating the need for backpropagation through time across many steps for long-range credit assignment. Inspired by the accelerated replay observed in rodents during slow-wave sleep, SHARP incorporates offline (sleep) phases in which temporally structured memory traces are replayed in an accelerated form and integrated into higher-level memory representations, improving long-range context retention. Through controlled simulations and ablation studies, we characterize the key properties of the proposed framework. In benchmark datasets such as text8 and PG-19, we demonstrate that SHARP improves over recurrent baselines by retaining next-token predictive performance on previously seen data while continuing to learn from the current stream and generalizing to future unseen data. These gains are enabled by its hierarchical structure, which yields an exponentially increasing effective temporal context with only linear-time computational cost.

</details>


### [51] [CoMIC: Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation for Long-Horizon LLM Agents in Cloud-Edge Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00756)
*Yannan Wang,Longli Yang,Zhen Liu,Abhishek Kumar,Carsten Maple*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoMIC is a cloud-edge framework that circulates memory and insights without parameter updates, improving edge LLM agent performance on complex tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge LLM agents struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring persistent memory and reflection due to resource constraints. Fine-tuning is costly and doesn't scale across heterogeneous nodes, while local memory leads to isolated experience.

Method: Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution design where edge agents execute locally with hierarchical memory and selective history re-expansion, while cloud-side LLM critic evaluates trajectories, filters reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent guidance using semantic subgoal identifiers.

Result: CoMIC improves progress rate and action grounding for weak edge agents and yields task-dependent success-rate gains across five long-horizon agent tasks without updating model parameters.

Conclusion: CoMIC demonstrates parameter-update-free cloud-edge collaboration can significantly improve performance of weak edge LLM agents on long-horizon tasks, offering a scalable solution for deploying lightweight agents on heterogeneous edge nodes without expensive fine-tuning.

Abstract: Deploying lightweight Large Language Model (LLM) agents on edge servers can reduce latency and move agentic services closer to users, but resource-constrained edge models often struggle with long-horizon tasks that require persistent memory, subgoal tracking, and reflection. Fine-tuning edge models after deployment is costly and difficult to scale across heterogeneous nodes, while purely local memory leaves agents with isolated experience and growing prompt context. We propose \textsc{CoMIC}, a parameter-update-free cloud-edge framework for Collaborative Memory and Insights Circulation. \textsc{CoMIC} follows a \textit{Centralized Reflection, Decentralized Execution} design: edge agents execute locally using subgoal-oriented hierarchical memory and selective re-expansion of relevant histories, while a cloud-side LLM critic asynchronously evaluates completed trajectories, filters reusable experience, and aggregates cross-agent guidance keyed by semantic subgoal identifiers. Across five long-horizon agent tasks spanning symbolic planning and text interaction, \textsc{CoMIC} improves progress rate and action grounding for weak edge agents and yields task-dependent success-rate gains without updating model parameters.

</details>


### [52] [FALAT: Tracing Failures in LLM Agent Trajectories via Dependency-Guided Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00765)
*Md Nakhla Rafi,Md Ahasanuzzaman,Dong Jae Kim,Zhijie Wang,Tse-Hsun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FALAT is a framework for failure attribution in LLM agent trajectories that uses dependency-guided search to identify which agent and which step caused failures, outperforming baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When LLM-based agents fail on complex tasks with long trajectories, it's difficult to determine which specific agent caused the failure and which step introduced the decisive error. The challenge is compounded because mistakes can propagate across the trajectory, making failure attribution more complex than simple step-level classification.

Method: FALAT frames attribution as a dependency-guided search problem: 1) constructs an expectation of how the task should be solved to identify suspicious regions, 2) traces dependencies among decisions, tool outputs, and agent messages to distinguish error-introducing steps from those merely propagating mistakes, and 3) evaluates whether correcting a candidate step would recover the expected outcome.

Result: On the Who&When benchmark (with algorithm-generated and hand-crafted multi-agent failure trajectories), FALAT achieves 46.0% step-level accuracy on algorithm-generated trajectories and 29.1% on hand-crafted trajectories, outperforming specialized attribution baselines and direct prompting with standalone LLMs.

Conclusion: Dependency-aware reasoning is essential for reliable failure diagnosis in LLM agent systems, and FALAT demonstrates significant improvements in responsible-agent and decisive-step attribution through its dependency-guided search approach.

Abstract: LLM-based agents increasingly solve complex tasks through long trajectories involving reasoning steps, tool calls, and inter-agent communication. However, when these agents fail, it is often unclear which agent caused the failure and which step introduced the decisive error. This attribution problem is challenging because mistakes can propagate across the trajectory: later actions may appear incorrect, but only because they depend on an earlier corrupted state. Therefore, failure attribution cannot be treated as independent step-level classification.
  We propose FALAT, a diagnostic framework for failure attribution in LLM agent trajectories. FALAT frames attribution as a dependency-guided search problem. It first constructs an expectation of how the task should be solved and uses this expectation to identify suspicious regions in the trajectory. It then traces dependencies among decisions, tool outputs, and agent messages to distinguish error-introducing steps from steps that merely inherit or propagate prior mistakes. Finally, FALAT evaluates whether correcting a candidate step would be sufficient to recover the expected outcome, allowing it to identify both the responsible agent and the decisive failure step.
  We evaluate FALAT on the Who&When benchmark, which includes both algorithm-generated and hand-crafted multi-agent failure trajectories. The results show that FALAT consistently improves responsible-agent and decisive-step attribution. Its best configurations achieve 46.0% step-level accuracy on algorithm-generated trajectories and 29.1% on the more challenging hand-crafted trajectories, outperforming specialized attribution baselines and direct prompting with standalone LLMs. These findings suggest that dependency-aware reasoning is essential for reliable failure diagnosis in LLM agent systems.

</details>


### [53] [Interaction-Centered Intelligence: Toward Interaction as the Primary Unit of Analysis in Co-Creative AI and Human-AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00807)
*Nicholas Davis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes interaction as the primary unit of analysis for co-creative AI, arguing that intelligence emerges from interaction dynamics rather than isolated computation within bounded agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional AI has focused too narrowly on isolated agents and their outputs, under-theorizing how interaction drives intelligence, creativity, and adaptive behavior. The paper seeks to shift focus toward relational and interactional dimensions of intelligence.

Method: The paper synthesizes insights from distributed cognition, embodied cognition, enaction, participatory sense-making, HCI, and computational creativity. It builds on prior work in Creative Sense-Making, quantified co-creation, and co-creative systems like Drawing Apprentice and AI Drawing Partner to develop a framework for interaction-centered intelligence.
