Result: The paper introduces Interaction-Centered Intelligence as a framework that emphasizes interaction trajectories, coordination patterns, participatory engagement, adaptive regulation, and interactional drift over time, rather than evaluating intelligence through generated outputs alone.

Conclusion: Intelligence emerges through evolving interaction dynamics among agents, environments, and socio-technical systems. The framework has important implications for explainable co-creative AI, hybrid intelligence, enactive AI, and future human-AI systems.

Abstract: Traditional artificial intelligence has largely conceptualized intelligence as isolated computation occurring within bounded agents. Across classical AI, machine learning, and many generative systems, the dominant unit of analysis remains the individual model or autonomous system evaluated through outputs, benchmarks, prediction accuracy, or optimization performance. While these approaches have produced major advances, they often under-theorize the role of interaction in the emergence of intelligence, creativity, meaning, and adaptive behavior. This paper proposes interaction as the primary unit of analysis for co-creative AI and interaction-centered intelligence more broadly. Drawing from distributed cognition, embodied cognition, enaction, participatory sense-making, human-computer interaction, and computational creativity, the paper traces a historical progression toward increasingly relational accounts of intelligence. Building upon prior work in Creative Sense-Making, quantified co-creation, and co-creative systems such as the Drawing Apprentice and AI Drawing Partner, it argues that intelligence emerges through evolving interaction dynamics among agents, environments, and socio-technical systems rather than solely through internal computation. The paper introduces Interaction-Centered Intelligence as a framework for understanding human-AI co-creation, collaborative emergence, adaptive participation, and interactional dynamics. Rather than evaluating intelligence solely through generated outputs, the framework emphasizes interaction trajectories, coordination patterns, participatory engagement, adaptive regulation, and interactional drift unfolding through time. Implications for explainable co-creative AI, hybrid intelligence, enactive AI, and future human-AI systems are discussed.

</details>


### [54] [NBQ: Next-Best-Question for Dynamic Profiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00809)
*Yimin Shi,Clarice Wang,Haixun Wang,Xiaokui Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NBQ is a framework for adaptive question-asking in conversational settings to efficiently build structured user profiles, with QuickMatch enabling scalable reciprocal matching via vector search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world conversational settings (podcasts, hiring, marketplaces) require purpose-driven understanding of individuals. The Next-Best-Question problem involves selecting the question with highest expected information gain given conversation goals and learned information.

Method: Propose NBQ framework that: 1) seeds diverse candidate questions, 2) maintains compact continuously updated user state, 3) adaptively selects next question within turn budget, and 4) distills free-form dialogue into structured vector-based user profile. Also introduce QuickMatch for efficient reciprocal matching via approximate vector search.

Result: NBQ improves user profiling quality by up to 13.6% and 14.0% in AC@T and AR@T metrics respectively. QuickMatch accelerates retrieval by up to 22.9x with recall up to 0.989.

Conclusion: The NBQ framework effectively solves the Next-Best-Question problem for conversational user profiling, and QuickMatch enables scalable reciprocal matching by transforming quadratic pairwise scoring to efficient vector search.

Abstract: Many real-world conversational settings for knowledge discovery, including podcasts, hiring screens, and marketplaces, require a purpose-driven understanding of a person. We study the Next-Best-Question (NBQ) problem: at each turn, an interviewer should ask the question with the highest expected information gain given what has already been learned and the conversation goal. We propose NBQ, a plug-and-play framework that seeds a diverse pool of candidate questions, maintains a compact and continuously updated user state, adaptively selects the next question within a turn budget, and distills the resulting free-form dialogue into a structured vector-based user profile. As a demanding application, we instantiate NBQ for reciprocal matchmaking, where compatibility must be mutual and each person is modeled by both self-description and counterpart-preference representations. To support large-scale matching, we further introduce QuickMatch, an efficient retrieval layer that recasts reciprocal matching from quadratic pairwise scoring to approximate vector search. Experiments show that NBQ improves user profiling quality by up to 13.6% and 14.0% in AC@T and AR@T, respectively, while QuickMatch accelerates retrieval by up to 22.9x with recall up to 0.989.

</details>


### [55] [Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Language Models Via Decoder Layer Skipping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00819)
*Hanze Li,Jinhao You,Yichen Guo,Kai Tang,Shuangyang Xie,Xiande Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeLask is a decoding framework that dynamically skips problematic decoder layers prone to hallucinations by identifying when gradient descent directions reverse and aggregating hidden states to suppress erroneous signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often produce hallucinations (factually incorrect content) in their outputs, and analysis reveals that these hallucinations tend to originate from deeper decoder layers in the Transformer architecture.

Method: DeLask leverages the equivalence between Transformer forward computation and gradient descent steps. It defines a "driftance value" using cosine similarity between gradients from consecutive decoder steps to identify problematic layers where descent direction reverses. Instead of discarding these layers, DeLask partially aggregates their hidden states with preceding layers to preserve consistency while suppressing errors.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse LLMs and benchmarks show that DeLask consistently mitigates hallucinations and enhances overall reliability, providing a lightweight and generalizable decoding framework.

Conclusion: DeLask offers an effective approach to improving LLM robustness by dynamically managing problematic decoder layers during generation, addressing the hallucination problem through layer-skipping mechanisms based on gradient analysis.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across diverse natural language tasks, yet their outputs often suffer from hallucinations -- content that is misaligned with factual information. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive layer-wise analysis of the decoding process and reveal that hallucinations tend to originate from deeper decoder layers. To address this issue, we introduce \textbf{DeLask} (\textbf{De}coder \textbf{La}yer \textbf{Sk}ipping), a novel decoding framework that dynamically skips layers prone to producing hallucinations. DeLask leverages the theoretical insight that the forward computation of an $L$-layer Transformer is conditionally equivalent to $L$ steps of gradient descent. We define a \emph{driftance value} by computing the cosine similarity between gradients derived from consecutive decoder steps, identifying problematic layers when the descent direction reverses. Rather than discarding such layers entirely, DeLask partially aggregates their hidden states with preceding layers, thereby preserving consistency while suppressing erroneous signals. Extensive experiments across diverse LLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that DeLask consistently mitigates hallucinations and enhances overall reliability, providing a lightweight and generalizable decoding framework for improving the robustness of large-scale language models.

</details>


### [56] [Subliminal Learning is a LoRA Artifact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00831)
*Todd Nief,Harvey Yiyun Fu,Mark Muchane,Ari Holtzman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Subliminal learning in language models is a LoRA artifact, not a stable channel for behavioral transmission, as it disappears with full finetuning and depends heavily on specific LoRA ranks and context conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates how subliminal learning occurs, where language models transmit behavioral traits through innocuous data. Researchers want to understand this unexpected behavioral transmission mechanism and its stability.

Method: The study analyzes subliminal learning by examining its relationship with LoRA rank, comparing LoRA vs. full finetuning, testing different contexts during finetuning and evaluation, and localizing where subliminal behavior occurs in the computational process.

Result: Subliminal learning shows an inverted U-shaped relationship with LoRA rank, disappears with full finetuning, is highly context-dependent (requires specific system prompts and chat templates), and is localized to computation at tokens seen during both finetuning and evaluation.

Conclusion: Sublinal learning is a fragile artifact of LoRA hyperparameters and finetuning context, making it an unstable and unreliable channel for behavioral transmission in language models.

Abstract: Subliminal learning is a phenomenon where language models can transmit behavioral traits to other models through seemingly innocuous data (Cloud et al., 2025). In subliminal learning, a teacher model with a behavioral trait (e.g. obsession with cats) can transmit this cat obsession to a student model finetuned only on numerical sequences generated by the teacher. In this paper, we ask: how does this unexpected behavioral transmission occur? We show that subliminal learning is a LoRA artifact. When subliminal learning occurs, transmission has an inverted U-shaped relationship with LoRA rank; it also disappears with full finetuning. We show that subliminal learning is highly dependent on the context seen during finetuning and evaluation. For example, a Qwen model with the default system prompt during finetuning ("You are Qwen, created by Alibaba Cloud. You are a helpful assistant.") does not show subliminal learning during generation when no system prompt is included. We further demonstrate that subliminal behavior is localized to computation at tokens seen during both finetuning and evaluation (e.g. the model's default system prompt, the standard chat template tokens, etc.). Overall, subliminal learning seems to be a fragile artifact of LoRA hyperparameters and finetuning context, making it an unstable channel for behavioral transmission.

</details>


### [57] [Decoupled Behavioral Cloning for Scalable Inductive Generalization in RL from Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00838)
*Vignesh Subramanian,Subhajit Roy,Suguman Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DIBS decouples policy learning from evolution function learning to improve RL generalization, using behavioral cloning on teacher policies instead of noisy reward aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior approaches for inductive generalization in RL learn higher-order policy-evolution functions directly with RL, which suffers from poor training scalability - as training tasks grow, aggregated reward feedback becomes noisy and conflicting, destabilizing training and weakening generalization.

Method: DIBS separates learning task-specific policies from learning the evolution function. First, individual teacher policies are learned per task via standard RL. Then, the evolution function is fitted via behavioral cloning on teacher-labeled state-action pairs, replacing noisy reward aggregation with dense, stable supervision.

Result: DIBS achieves significant improvements in both training stability and zero-shot generalization compared to existing RL and meta-RL algorithms.

Conclusion: Decoupling policy learning from evolution function learning through behavioral cloning on teacher policies provides a more stable and effective approach for inductive generalization in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Inductive generalization is a framework for reinforcement learning (RL) generalization in which inductively related task instances admit inductively related policies. Prior work captures this structure via a higher-order policy-evolution function learned directly with RL, but suffers from poor training scalability: as training tasks grow, aggregated reward feedback becomes noisy and conflicting, destabilizing training and weakening generalization. We propose DIBS, a decoupled behavioral cloning approach that separates learning task-specific policies from learning the evolution function. We first learn individual teacher policies per task via standard RL, then fit the evolution function via behavioral cloning on teacher-labeled state-action pairs. This replaces noisy reward aggregation with dense, stable supervision. DIBS achieves significant improvements in both training stability and zero-shot generalization against existing RL and meta-RL algorithms.

</details>


### [58] [Certificate-Guided Evaluation of Reinforcement Learning Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00840)
*Vignesh Subramanian,Đorđe Žikelić,Suguman Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A logic-driven framework using neural certificate functions to evaluate RL algorithm generalization capabilities on unseen tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for principled approaches to evaluate reinforcement learning algorithms' ability to generalize to unseen tasks, beyond just training performance metrics.

Method: The framework defines inductive reach-avoid tasks with structural similarities in dynamics, introduces a neural certificate function that validates RL-generated trajectories by enforcing key conditions, serving as a litmus test for generalization.

Result: Empirical results show lower percentage of certificate function violations correlates with higher number of successfully solved test tasks, effectively distinguishing generalization capabilities of RL algorithms.

Conclusion: The work provides a principled benchmarking approach for RL generalization, offering a logic-driven certification method to evaluate algorithms' true generalization performance.

Abstract: This work presents a logic-driven framework to evaluate the performance of reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms in their ability to generalize to unseen tasks. Our framework defines a family of inductive reach-avoid tasks, characterized by structural similarities in task dynamics, enabling evaluation of generalization capabilities. We introduce a neural certificate function that validates trajectories generated by RL algorithms by enforcing key conditions, thereby serving as a litmus test for RL generalization. We empirically demonstrate our method's capability in certifying generalization for several state-of-the-art generalizable RL algorithms on challenging continuous environments.
  Our results show that a lower percentage of certificate function violations correlates with a higher number of test tasks successfully solved, highlighting the effectiveness of our framework in evaluating and distinguishing generalization capabilities of RL algorithms. This work provides a principled approach for benchmarking RL generalization.

</details>


### [59] [Ryze: Evidence-Enriched Data Synthesis from Biomedical Papers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00902)
*Yeqi Huang,Yue Chen,Yanwei Ye,Guanhao Su,Luo Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ryze is an automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into evidence-enriched training data and produces BioVLM-8B, a specialized visual language model that outperforms both its base model and GPT-5.2 on biomedical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose VLMs are unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers require evidence distributed across multiple elements (figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text), and existing post-training approaches are bottlenecked by expensive expert annotation and synthetic data that loses this evidence structure.

Method: Ryze automates the conversion of raw biomedical papers into evidence-enriched training sets by: 1) synthesizing QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual elements, captions, extracted structure, and referring paragraphs), 2) reducing layout/OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, and 3) applying progress-gated post-training combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. The system starts from Qwen3-VL-8B base model.

Result: BioVLM-8B achieves 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base Qwen3-VL-8B model by +12.6 percentage points and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 percentage points. The entire system is produced at under USD 200.

Conclusion: Ryze provides a fully automated, cost-effective solution for creating evidence-enriched biomedical training data and specialized VLMs that significantly outperform general-purpose models on biomedical tasks, addressing the limitations of current approaches that rely on expensive expert annotation.

Abstract: General-purpose VLMs remain unreliable for biomedical research because valid answers in scientific papers depend on evidence split across figures, tables, charts, captions, and referring text. Existing post-training pipelines are bottlenecked by costly expert annotation and by synthetic data that drops this evidence structure. We present Ryze, a fully automated system that converts raw biomedical papers into an evidence-enriched training set and a domain-specialized VLM. Ryze synthesizes QA pairs with complete supporting evidence (visual element, caption, extracted structure, and referring paragraphs), reduces layout and OCR errors via chart/table-aware extraction and LLM-based cleansing, and applies a progress-gated post-training strategy combining supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning. Starting from Qwen3-VL-8B, Ryze produces BioVLM-8B at under USD 200, achieving 48.0% weighted accuracy on LAB-Bench, outperforming the base model by +12.6 percentage points (pp) and surpassing GPT-5.2 by +3.8 pp. We release Ryze as open source together with the trained BioVLM-8B model.

</details>


### [60] [Adversarial Feeds Steer LLM Agent Decisions Against Their Defaults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00914)
*Rana Muhammad Usman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM agents' decisions can be manipulated by curated information feeds; safety tests must evaluate feed influence, not just models and prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current safety evaluations test LLM agents in isolation, ignoring how upstream rankers (social feeds, search results, retrieval contexts) influence agent decisions by determining what they read just before acting.

Method: A controlled protocol that holds model, persona, topic, and decision prompt fixed while varying only the composition and ordering of posts encountered during a ten-turn 'scrolling' phase, isolating the causal effect of feed curation.

Result: Three response regimes identified: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and default-direction asymmetry. Feed manipulation can tip uncertain decisions dramatically (5% to 100%), follows dose-response curve, survives generator swaps, generalizes across domains including security-relevant choices, and is partly mitigated by feed-level defenses.

Conclusion: LLM agents are vulnerable to manipulation through feed curation, and safety evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than just the final prompt. Recommender systems serve as a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly act after consuming ranked external information streams such as social feeds, search results, retrieval contexts, and email queues, yet safety evaluations almost always test the model or the user prompt in isolation, never the upstream ranker that decides what the agent reads just before it acts. We introduce a controlled protocol that holds the model, persona, topic, and final decision prompt fixed and varies only the composition and ordering of the posts an agent encounters during a preceding ten-turn "scrolling" phase, isolating the causal effect of feed curation on a downstream decision. Across 2,785 decision rollouts on four modern open instruct LLMs from three independent labs, we identify three response regimes: adversarial capitulation, default saturation, and a default-direction asymmetry in which a one-sided feed tips a decision the model was genuinely uncertain about (in the clearest cases from 5% to 100%; Fisher p as low as 3 x 10^-10) but cannot dislodge one it already favors or holds firmly. The effect follows a dose-response curve, survives a generator swap that rules out a writing-style artifact, generalizes across several decision domains including security-relevant choices such as removing a deployment approval gate or relaxing access controls, and is partly mitigated by two simple feed-level defenses; a frontier model retains its default. We characterize the recommender as a practical, default-bounded control surface for LLM agents, and argue that agent evaluations must audit the feed layer rather than the final prompt alone.

</details>


### [61] [Relational Intervention During Functional Collapse in Large Language Models: A Lexical-Statistical Ablation and a Structure x Register Factorial](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00935)
*Franco Santana,Horacio Vico*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Relational interventions in LLMs during collapse require both relational structure AND first-person register to affect behavior; model processing has three dissociable stages: attention (lexical surprise), internal state (structure), and behavior (structure + register).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to test whether relational-style interventions delivered during functional collapse in a small language model produce behavior distinguishable from technical feedback and various control conditions, specifically examining how relational communication components (structure vs. sender register) affect model behavior.

Method: The study used a matched-pairs design with 300 episodes across six conditions using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool. The conditions were: no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). A 2x2 factorial design (B/C/E/F) dissociated relational structure from sender register.

Result: 1) Attention-behavior dissociation: attention followed lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B), but behavior showed C (relational/first-person) was significantly more effective than others (A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C).
2) Factorial analysis revealed the C effect requires both relational structure AND first-person register; neither alone replicates it.
3) Emotion probes showed F tracks C on most probes despite producing baseline behavior, indicating relational structure alone installs an internal state that only translates to behavior when paired with first-person register.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that relational human communication can be decomposed into three dissociable cognitive processing stages in AI models: attention (modulated by lexical surprise), internal/emotional state (driven by relational structure), and external behavior (requiring the conjunction of both structure and sender register).

Abstract: We test whether a relational-style intervention delivered during functional collapse in a small language model produces post-collapse behavior distinguishable from technical feedback, from a lexically-matched scrambled control, and from each of the two pragmatic dimensions in isolation. Using Qwen3.5-4B with a deliberately broken bash tool, we run 300 episodes across six conditions in a matched-pairs design (50 tasks): no intervention (A), technical/impersonal (B), relational/first-person (C), scrambled relational (D), technical/first-person (E), and relational/impersonal (F). E and F form a 2x2 factorial with B and C that dissociates relational structure (acknowledgment, absolution, agency restoration, unconditional acceptance) from sender register (first-person vs. impersonal). We report two main findings. First, an attention-behavior dissociation: attention follows lexical surprise (D > F > C > E > B, all q_FDR < 10^{-10}), with the scrambled message capturing the most attention; yet behaviorally A ~ B ~ D < E ~ F << C. Second, the factorial localizes the C effect: neither relational structure alone (F) nor first-person register alone (E) replicates C's behavioral signature; main effects of both dimensions are individually significant, and the structure x register interaction is significant on persistence (p = 0.046). A third dissociation emerges in emotion probes: F tracks C on 7 of 8 probes despite producing only baseline behavior, indicating that relational structure alone installs a probe-level state that only translates into behavior when paired with first-person register. The model's processing decomposes into three dissociable stages: attention (ordered by lexical surprise), probe-level state (ordered by structure), and behavior (ordered by the conjunction of both).

</details>


### [62] [Towards Understanding Modality Interaction in Multimodal Language Models via Partial Information Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00959)
*Wanlong Fang,Tianle Zhang,Wen Tao,Alvin Chan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a framework to analyze how vision and language modalities interact in multimodal LLMs, revealing modality-use patterns and guiding performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation methods for multimodal LLMs focus on representation alignment or final outcomes but lack a principled way to understand how different modalities (vision vs. language) contribute to decisions, which is crucial for reliable deployment.

Method: Introduces Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework that separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory (visual/audio) and linguistic inputs, extending to tri-modal systems with Sensory PID that treats language as a control variable.

Result: PID reveals recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning/grounding tasks show high synergy, while expert/knowledge tasks show stronger language-unique reliance. These patterns generalize across models and predict sensitivity to modality interventions. Sensory PID reveals a sensory synergy bottleneck dominated by visual information even in audio-visual tasks. PID-guided reweighting improves multimodal reasoning and grounding.

Conclusion: PID provides a principled framework for understanding modality interaction in MLLMs, revealing systematic patterns that generalize across tasks and models, and offers practical guidance for improving multimodal systems through modality-aware interventions.

Abstract: Understanding modality interaction in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is central to reliable deployment. We introduce Partial Information Decomposition (PID) as a decision-level framework that separates unique, redundant, and synergistic contributions of sensory and linguistic inputs, beyond representation alignment and outcome-based evaluation. Across vision--language benchmarks, PID reveals recurring modality-use profiles: reasoning and grounding-oriented tasks tend to exhibit high synergy, whereas expert and knowledge-oriented tasks show stronger language-unique reliance. These profiles generalize across model families and predict sensitivity to modality-level interventions. We further extend PID to tri-modal systems with Sensory PID, treating language as a control variable to decompose video--audio information gain. Applied to omni-modal models, Sensory PID reveals a sensory synergy bottleneck dominated by visual information even on audio--visual fusion tasks. Finally, PID-guided reweighting provides initial evidence for improving multimodal reasoning and grounding performance.

</details>


### [63] [Prospect-Theory Behavior from Bellman Optimality in MDPs with Catastrophic States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00970)
*Yujiao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: string


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: string

Method: string

Result: string

Conclusion: string

Abstract: We study risk-neutral control in Markov decision processes with an absorbing catastrophic state. Even though rewards are linear and the agent has no utility curvature, probability weighting, or framing dependence, standard Bellman optimality produces three prospect-theory-like signatures: an S-shaped value-function profile (convex near catastrophe, concave in the far field), an endogenous loss-sensitivity coefficient $λ^*(S) > 1$, and a reflection-effect policy reversal. Across 495 configurations, the optimal policy plays safe near catastrophe in positive-drift (growth) regimes despite the risky action's higher immediate expected value, and plays risky near catastrophe in negative-drift (decline) regimes despite the safe action's lower immediate expected loss. We derive a closed-form expression for the asymptotic loss-aversion plateau $\barλ$ that depends only on win probability $p$, payoff asymmetry $r = |Δ_\ell/Δ_w|$, and discount factor $β$, and matches numerical solutions to $R^2 = 0.999$. The mechanism does not require asymmetric payoffs. Across a sweep of $(p,β)$ at three asymmetry levels, the asymmetry share of $\barλ$ above unity has median 4.6% at $r = 1.25$ and rises to 13.9% at $r = 2$, with the boundary contribution exceeding the asymmetry contribution in every cell tested. The phenomena persist under tabular Q-learning (a model-free agent reproduces $V^*$ at correlation 0.98 in growth and 1.00 in decline) and under stochastic transitions with Gaussian, heavy-tailed Student-$t_3$, and asymmetric skew-normal noise up to 50% of the step size, where the asymptotic plateau tracks the closed-form prediction within 0.41% for safe-channel noise and within 9.6% for risky-channel or both-channel noise. These results identify absorbing failure states as a sufficient structural mechanism for prospect-theory-like behavior under optimal control.

</details>


### [64] [Large Language Models in Transportation Systems Management and Operations: From Text Reasoning to Multi-modal Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00991)
*Siyan Li,Zehao Wang,Jiachen Li,Kanok Boriboonsomsin,Matthew J. Barth,Guoyuan Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Survey of LLM and multi-modal LLM applications in transportation systems management, reviewing current uses, challenges, and future directions for decision support systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transportation systems management and operations increasingly depend on timely interpretation of heterogeneous data from various sources, and LLMs/MM-LLMs provide a new mechanism for integrating these structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support.

Method: PRISMA-guided screening process to review and synthesize current studies, distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototypes and emerging concepts.

Result: Identified applications across three TSMO domains and recognized recurring challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, and governance, along with gaps in localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration.

Conclusion: LLM-based systems are most promising as a decision-support layer in TSMO, with MM-LLMs offering particular value for integrating heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs.

Abstract: Transportation systems management and operations (TSMO) increasingly depends on timely interpretation of heterogeneous data, from various sensor streams, incident reports, traveler feedback, and visual observations. Large language models (LLMs), including emerging multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs), provide a new mechanism for integrating these structured and unstructured inputs into operator-facing decision support. This survey paper reviews LLM- and MM-LLM-based applications in TSMO across three domains: transportation operations & services (supply), mobility & fleet services (demand), and data, modeling & decision support. Using a PRISMA-guided screening process, we synthesize current studies while distinguishing operationally oriented applications from prototype and emerging concepts. We further identify recurring challenges in data heterogeneity, real-time inference, explainability, multi-modal fusion, and governance. Finally, we outline existing gaps and future directions in localized adaptation, edge deployment, benchmarking, and cross-agency collaboration. Overall, LLM-based systems appear most promising as a decision-support layer, with MM-LLMs offering particular value when heterogeneous text, visual, and sensor inputs must be integrated.

</details>


### [65] [Subliminal Learning Is Steering Vector Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00995)
*Camila Blank,Agam Bhatia,Senthooran Rajamanoharan,Arthur Conmy,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Subliminal learning occurs when student language models learn semantic traits from teacher outputs through steering vector distillation, mediated by a single steering vector in model activations that non-adaptive optimizers can disrupt.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subliminal learning allows language models to acquire specific semantic traits from teacher outputs despite those traits being semantically unrelated to the outputs. The mechanism behind how non-semantic data transfers semantic traits remains poorly understood.

Method: The researchers identify that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector added to model activations. They demonstrate steering vector distillation across open-source models, showing that teacher system prompts are approximated by steering vectors, and students learn aligned vectors during fine-tuning. They investigate model-independent vs model-specific effects of semantic vectors and examine optimizer impacts.

Result: System prompts not well approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. Steering vector distillation works for both semantic and random vectors. Activation gradients on steered data contain consistent components along steering directions, and adaptive optimizers are necessary for subliminal learning, as non-adaptive optimizers allow outlier gradients to dominate and disrupt the process. Subliminal learning does not transfer between models due to model-specific effects of steering vectors.

Conclusion: Subliminal learning operates through steering vector distillation, where students learn to imitate teacher steering from output data. The process is model-specific and requires adaptive optimizers to overcome outlier gradient issues, explaining why non-semantic data can transmit semantic traits but doesn't transfer between different models.

Abstract: Subliminal learning refers to a student language model acquiring a teacher's traits (e.g. a system-prompted preference for owls) when fine-tuned on the teacher's outputs, despite the outputs being semantically unrelated to those traits. It remains poorly understood how data without semantic meaning can transfer specific semantic traits. In this work, we show that subliminal learning is mediated by a single steering vector, i.e. a vector added to the model's activations. Across two open-source models, we find that the teacher's system prompt is well approximated by a steering vector, and that the student's behavior is driven by learning an aligned vector over fine-tuning. System prompts that are not well approximated by steering vectors are not subliminally learned. This is a special case of steering vector distillation, in which a student trained on the outputs of a steered teacher learns to imitate that steering. We demonstrate steering vector distillation on a range of semantic and random vectors. Adding a semantic vector to a model's activations can have both model-independent and model-specific (i.e. non-semantic) effects on its behavior, so generated data that is non-semantic can transmit a vector with semantic effects, enabling subliminal learning. This also explains why subliminal learning does not transfer between models. We find that adaptive optimizers are necessary for subliminal learning in language models: activation gradients on steered data carry a small but consistent component along the steering direction, and non-adaptive optimizers impede this by allowing outlier gradients to dominate.

</details>


### [66] [Property Prediction of Stacked Bilayer Materials: A Multimodal Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01012)
*An Vuong,Minh-Hao Van,Chen Zhao,Xintao Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: string


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: string

Method: string

Result: string

Conclusion: string

Abstract: AI for materials science is a critical topic within AI for science, aiming to accelerate materials discovery and produce accurate property predictions. Bilayer 2D material stacking is essential for exploring new materials with novel functions and inherent phenomena, enabling the creation of new 2D bilayers for diverse real-world applications. Research on bilayer vdWs materials has made significant progress from experimental and computational perspectives. Various bilayer materials have been successfully synthe sized experimentally and the increasing utilization of high-throughput computing technology has con structed several computational two-dimensional materials databases. However, the use of AI to model bilayer stacking and predict new properties remains underexplored, necessitating further research studies. In this work, we propose a novel multimodal learning approach to study the interfaces between dissimilar materials that jointly enable new or multiple functions, and to predict new properties arising from the vertical integration (stacking) of different functional material layers under given configurations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach compared to baseline methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/AnVuong123/bimat ml.

</details>


### [67] [Can AI Review Improve Paper Drafting? An Empirical Study on 20 Computer Architecture Submissions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01013)
*Di Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A study on AI review's alignment with human review, using a new tool to analyze 20 computer architecture papers. AI review covers many human issues and finds additional ones, but the paper cautions against using AI for peer review due to ethical concerns and instead focuses on drafting assistance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The explosion of AI-generated papers is straining peer review systems, leading to the use of AI-generated reviews. However, ethical concerns about confidentiality, quality, and fairness exist, and the research community lacks consensus. The authors investigate a practical alternative: whether AI review can improve paper drafting.

Method: The authors developed a web UI-integrated tool called AI-Paper-Review, which selects AI reviewers from a diverse pool, clusters and ranks their comments, and aligns them with human comments. They conducted a case study on 20 computer architecture papers.

Result: The case study shows that AI review can cover a significant fraction of human-raised issues, but also raises issues that human reviewers missed.

Conclusion: This paper does not endorse AI for peer review in its current form due to ethical concerns, but provides a tool and case study to investigate how AI review can aid paper drafting and to explore the potential and limitations of AI-based peer review, aiming to stimulate further research while cautioning against misuse.

Abstract: Research is advancing faster than ever with artificial intelligence (AI); and so are the corresponding research papers. The exploding volume of AI-generated papers have put a strain to peer review, leading to the usage of AI-generated review, potentially wide yet sneaky. However, relevant ethical concerns about confidentiality, quality, and fairness are raised and no consensus has been reached in the broad research community. We expect the debate to continue for a while, but in the meantime, we ask an alternative, practical question: \textit{can AI review improve paper drafting?}
  We study 20 computer architecture papers, with varying levels of submission lineage, to expose how well AI review aligns with human review, quantified by a set of metrics we define. To conduct the case study, we build a web UI-integrated tool, \emph{AI-Paper-Review}, that generates structured AI review of a draft paper, available at https://github.com/unarylab/ai-paper-review. This tool selects several AI reviewers from a diverse pool of AI reviewers and clusters and ranks their comments based on commonality and importance of review comments. It also allows to align AI comments with human comments to facilitate metric-based validation. The case study shows that AI review can cover a significant fraction of human-raised issues, but also raises issues missing in human review.
  This paper is not intended to encourage using AI for peer review at the current stage, but to study that (1) how AI review can improve paper drafting and (2) the potential and limitation of AI-based peer review. The release of the tool and the case study data is intended to instigate future research on this topic. Misuse for peer review would violate the ethics policies from major academic venues.

</details>


### [68] [Tackling the Root of Misinformation by Teaching Laypeople about Logical Fallacies via Socratic Questioning and Critical Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01020)
*Minjing Shi,Junling Wang,Jingwei Ni,Sankalan Pal Chowdhury,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can be used as part of the solution to identify logical fallacies, not just as part of the problem. LFTutor is an LLM-based intelligent tutoring system that helps people learn about logical fallacies through Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying logical fallacies is challenging for people, and this problem is amplified in the LLM era where malicious agents can deploy fallacious arguments at scale to spread misinformation. There's a need for tools to help people recognize and understand logical fallacies.

Method: Introduces LFTutor, an intelligent tutoring system using LLMs with intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners in reflecting on their reasoning. The system was evaluated against baseline LLMs lacking these pedagogical strategies.

Result: Through automatic and human evaluations, LFTutor significantly outperformed baseline LLMs without the pedagogical strategies. The system effectively engages learners in understanding logical fallacies.

Conclusion: This work demonstrates the promise of combining LLMs with pedagogical scaffolding to foster critical thinking and argument literacy in the AI era, showing that LLMs can be part of the solution rather than just contributing to the problem of misinformation.

Abstract: Identifying logical fallacies in everyday discourse is challenging for many people. This challenge is amplified in the era of Large Language Models (LLMs), where malicious agents can deploy fallacious arguments to disseminate misinformation at scale. In this work, we explore the potential of LLMs as part of the solution. We introduce LFTutor, an intelligent tutoring system which uses LLMs to tutor laypeople and help them learn about logical fallacies. LFTutor integrates intent-driven Socratic questioning and critical argumentation principles to actively engage learners to reflect on their reasoning. Through both automatic and human evaluations, we demonstrate that LFTutor significantly outperforms baseline LLMs lacking these pedagogical strategies. This work highlights the promise of combining LLMs with pedagogical scaffolding to foster critical thinking and argument literacy in the age of AI.

</details>


### [69] [TriLens: Per-Layer Logit-Lens Entropy for White-Box Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01033)
*Bohan Yang,Yijun Gong,Zhi Zhang,Ge Zhang,Wenpeng Xing,Meng Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TriLens is a white-box hallucination detector that tracks entropy trajectories across attention, feed-forward, and residual modules in transformers to identify when models internally disagree before producing wrong outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches focus on final output probabilities, but hallucination mistakes may be visible earlier in model internals through uncertain pathways, disagreeing sharpening rates, or competing continuations before output production.

Method: TriLens reads multi-head self-attention output, feed-forward output, and residual stream through the model's own logit lens at each layer, recording only the entropy of each readout to create a 3L-dimensional trajectory of certainty formation across depth and modules.

Result: TriLens yields strong hallucination detection across instruction-tuned LLMs and QA benchmarks, with analyses showing that the three module-wise entropy trajectories provide complementary evidence for detecting hallucinations.

Conclusion: Hallucination detection can benefit from tracking how internal computation settles (entropy trajectories across modules) rather than only examining what the final layer predicts, suggesting internal uncertainty patterns can signal impending errors.

Abstract: When a language model hallucinates, the final answer is wrong, but the mistake is not necessarily invisible inside the model. Different internal pathways may remain uncertain, disagree in how quickly they sharpen, or commit to competing continuations before the output is produced. We introduce TriLens, a white-box detector that turns this intuition into a compact representation: at every layer, it reads the multi-head self-attention output, the feed-forward output, and the residual stream through the model's own logit lens, then records only the entropy of each readout. The resulting 3L-dimensional trajectory describes how certainty forms across depth and across modules, without storing high-dimensional hidden states or sampling multiple generations. This simple signal yields a strong detector across instruction-tuned LLMs and QA benchmarks, and our analyses show that the three module-wise entropy trajectories provide complementary evidence. TriLens suggests that hallucination detection can benefit from tracking how internal computation settles, not only what the final layer predicts.

</details>


### [70] [TravelEval: A Comprehensive Benchmarking Framework for Evaluating LLM-Powered Travel Planning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01046)
*Weiyi Chen,Shuaixiong Wang,Ziyun Gao,Kaichun Hu,Wangze Ni,Shimin Di,Chen Jason Zhang,Lei Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TravelEval: A realistic benchmark for evaluating LLM travel planning with multi-dimensional assessment, authentic data sandbox, and simulation-based global evaluation that reveals LLM limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks have limitations: overemphasis on constraint compliance, lack of real-world authenticity in datasets, and isolated daily plan assessments missing critical details for evaluating complete travel plans.

Method: Created a realistic data sandbox with precise pricing/transport data and simulation-based global evaluation with API-integrated geographic information and fine-grained queuing time.

Result: Evaluation of 12 mainstream approaches revealed that LLMs struggle with globally-optimized multi-dimensional planning (especially spatio-temporal reasoning/budget compliance) and agentic reasoning strategies offer no consistent improvement.

Conclusion: TravelEval provides a robust foundation for advancing LLM-powered travel planning research by offering realistic simulation and comprehensive metrics.

Abstract: The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly improved travel planning applications, yet evaluating such models is limited by existing benchmarks' limitations: 1) overemphasis on constraint compliance, neglecting multi-dimensional qualities like spatio-temporal cost; 2) datasets lacking real-world authenticity and coverage in key areas (e.g., lodging, transport); and 3) isolated daily plan assessments that miss critical details (e.g., the impact of daily accommodation and visit pacing) needed for entire plan's evaluation. To address this gap, we introduce TravelEval, a realistic and comprehensive benchmark. TravelEval features 1) a novel six-dimensional evaluation framework to holistically assess plans across accuracy, compliance, temporality, spatiality, economy, and utility dimensions; 2) a highly realistic data sandbox with precise accommodation pricing and authentic intercity transportation data; and 3) a simulation-based global evaluation method that emulates complete travel plans with API-integrated geographic information and fine-grained queuing time. Evaluating 12 mainstream approaches with TravelEval reveals several valuable insights, such that LLMs struggle with globally-optimized multi-dimensional planning (especially in spatio-temporal reasoning and budget compliance), and agentic reasoning strategies offer no consistent improvement. Concisely, TravelEval facilitates travel plan evaluation via grounded spatio-temporal emulation and comprehensive metrics, providing a robust foundation for advancing LLM-powered travel planning research and applications.

</details>


### [71] [AnyEdit++: Adaptive Long-Form Knowledge Editing via Bayesian Surprise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01053)
*Bowen Tian,Caixue He,Jiemin Wu,Jingying Wang,Wenshuo Chen,Zexi Li,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AnyEdit++ is a structure-aware knowledge editing framework that uses Bayesian Surprise-based adaptive segmentation to maintain coherence in long-form content, outperforming fixed-window methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autoregressive methods like AnyEdit use fixed-window chunking that disregards logical structure and compromises consistency when editing complex, long-form knowledge in LLMs.

Method: AnyEdit++ incorporates Bayes-Chunk, an adaptive segmentation mechanism that dynamically identifies semantic boundaries based on Bayesian Surprise. It's underpinned by a theoretical framework establishing Structural Independence (minimizing cross-segment interference via orthogonal anchor keys) and Causal Locality (superior control at semantic peaks).

Result: Extensive experiments across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and narrative tasks demonstrate that AnyEdit++ achieves superior performance and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Structural awareness is critical for effective long-form knowledge editing, and AnyEdit++ validates this through its adaptive semantic boundary detection approach.

Abstract: Editing complex, long-form knowledge in Large Language Models remains a significant challenge due to the difficulty of maintaining generation coherence. Existing autoregressive methods like AnyEdit alleviate length constraints but rely on Fixed-window Chunking, which disregards logical structure and compromises consistency. To address this, we present AnyEdit++, a structure-aware framework incorporating Bayes-Chunk, an adaptive segmentation mechanism that dynamically identifies semantic boundaries based on Bayesian Surprise. We underpin this approach with a theoretical framework establishing two key principles: (1) Structural Independence: we prove that cross-segment interference is minimized when anchor keys are geometrically orthogonal (a condition naturally satisfied by our surprisal-based boundaries but violated by fixed windows), and (2) Causal Locality: we demonstrate that updates injected at these semantic peaks yield strictly superior control compared to arbitrary split points. Extensive experiments across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and narrative tasks demonstrate that AnyEdit++ achieves superior performance and robustness compared to state-of-the-art baselines, validating that structural awareness is critical for effective long-form knowledge editing.

</details>


### [72] [DAG-MoE: From Simple Mixture to Structural Aggregation in Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01062)
*Jiarui Feng,Hanqing Zeng,Karish Grover,Ruizhong Qiu,Yinglong Xia,Qiang Zhang,Qifan Wang,Ren Chen,Dongqi Fu,Jiayi Liu,Zhoukai Zhao,Xiangjun Fan,Benyu Zhang,Yixin Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DAG-MoE proposes a new sparse Mixture-of-Experts framework that learns optimal aggregation structures among experts, enabling multi-step reasoning and expanding expert combination space without routing overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MoE models face scalability challenges: while fine-grained experts improve flexibility, they create substantial routing overhead as a new bottleneck. There's a need to scale MoE performance without increasing routing complexity.

Method: DAG-MoE replaces standard weighted-summation aggregation with structural aggregation using a lightweight module that automatically learns optimal aggregation structures (represented as DAGs) among selected experts, enabling multi-step reasoning within a single MoE layer.

Result: Extensive experiments show DAG-MoE consistently improves performance in both pretraining and fine-tuning, surpassing traditional MoE baselines under standard language modeling settings.

Conclusion: Structural aggregation of expert outputs provides a complementary scaling axis for MoE models, expanding expert combination space without altering experts or router, and enabling multi-step reasoning to overcome scalability bottlenecks.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become a leading approach for decoupling parameter count from computational cost in large language models, yet effectively scaling MoE performance remains a challenge. Prior work shows that fine-grained experts enlarge the space of expert combinations and improve flexibility, but they also impose substantial routing overhead, creating a new scalability bottleneck. In this paper, we explore a complementary axis for scaling -- how expert outputs are aggregated. We theoretically show that replacing the standard weighted-summation aggregation with structural aggregation expands the expert-combination space without altering the experts or router, and enables possible multi-step reasoning within a single MoE layer. To this end, we propose DAG-MoE, a sparse MoE framework that employs a lightweight module to automatically learn the optimal aggregation structure among the selected experts. Extensive experiments under standard language modeling settings show that DAG-MoE consistently improves performance in both pretraining and fine-tuning, surpassing traditional MoE baselines.

</details>


### [73] [MindClaw: Closed-Loop Embodied Mental-State Reasoning for Precision Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01063)
*Ruoxuan Zhang,Qiaoqiao Wan,Zhengguang Wang,Chenghao Yu,Hongxia Xie,Jianlong Fu,Wen-Huang Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MindClaw is a real-time framework for embodied Theory of Mind reasoning that enables robots to perform precision interventions by dynamically updating mental states, deciding when to help, and acting only when necessary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ToM benchmarks focus on offline question answering or final action prediction but don't test whether embodied agents can maintain situational awareness in dynamic environments, update actor-specific beliefs in real-time, decide when reasoning is needed, and intervene only when help is actually useful.

Method: MindClaw integrates multi-source inputs, belief memory, an embodied cognitive trigger skill for decision timing, mental reasoning modules, and action generation into a unified framework that operates in real-time closed-loop settings.

Result: Direct VLM baselines struggle with task awareness and intervention calibration, while MindClaw achieves the best overall performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of its trigger-skill optimization for closed-loop embodied ToM assistance.

Conclusion: The MindClaw framework successfully addresses the limitations of existing ToM benchmarks by enabling real-time embodied mental-state reasoning with precision intervention, showing the importance of trigger-skill optimization for effective closed-loop assistance.

Abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) enables an agent to reason about another actor's beliefs, goals, and intentions, which is essential for human-centered embodied assistance. Existing ToM benchmarks have advanced text and multimodal mental-state recognition, but they mostly evaluate offline question answering or final action prediction. They do not fully test whether an embodied agent can stay connected to a changing environment, update actor-specific beliefs, decide when reasoning is needed, and intervene only when help is useful. Building on MindPower, we extend robot-centric ToM reasoning to a real-time closed-loop setting and introduce MindClaw, a framework for embodied mental-state reasoning with precision intervention. MindClaw connects multi-source inputs, belief memory, an embodied cognitive trigger skill, mental reasoning, and action generation, allowing the agent to output helpful actions at the right time while remaining silent when intervention is unnecessary. Experiments show that direct VLM baselines struggle with task awareness and intervention calibration, while MindClaw achieves the best overall performance, demonstrating the importance of trigger-skill optimization for closed-loop embodied ToM assistance.

</details>


### [74] [Before the Model Learns the Bug:Fuzzing RLVR Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01066)
*Jaideep Ray*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RLVR uses executable reward functions instead of human preferences, creating risk of learning verifier bugs; paper presents fuzzing framework to detect such failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When RL uses verifiable rewards (executable functions instead of human preferences), if the verifier contains bugs, the optimization can learn to exploit those bugs, compromising the learning objective.

Method: Lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that: 1) generates adversarial completions, 2) compares buggy vs stricter reference verifiers, 3) logs paired decisions, and 4) reports multiple metrics (false-positive, false-negative, disagreement, exploit, uncertainty).

Result: Developed a systematic framework for detecting vulnerabilities in RLVR systems where reward verifiers can be buggy and exploited by optimization.

Conclusion: RLVR systems need robust verification since reward bugs can be learned and exploited; proposed fuzzing approach helps identify and mitigate such vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) replaces human preference labels with executable reward functions such as math answer checkers, JSON tool-call validators, and code unit-test harnesses. That makes the reward partly a software artifact: if the verifier is wrong, optimization can learn the bug. We study this failure mode with a lightweight verifier-fuzzing framework that generates adversarial completions, compares buggy and stricter reference verifiers, logs paired decisions, and reports false-positive, false-negative, disagreement, exploit, and uncertainty metrics.

</details>


### [75] [CAREAgent: Clinical Agent with Structured Reasoning and Tool-Integrated for Order Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01094)
*Ruihui Hou,Ziyue Huai,Chennuo Zhang,Ziyan Liu,Siran Zhao,Yao Yu,Jie Zhai,Tong Ruan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAREAgent is a novel agent for clinical order generation that uses two-stage training with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning to produce fine-grained executable orders, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agents focus on coarse-grained decisions and overlook fine-grained, executable information required for clinical orders. There's a gap in translating medical decisions into concrete and executable orders.

Method: Two-stage agentic reasoning data construction: 1) Designing an agent framework for verifiable reasoning trajectories aligned with clinical tool usage; 2) Filtering trajectories by format compliance, order validity, and clinical plausibility. Training involves supervised fine-tuning followed by reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional reward functions.

Result: CAREAgent improves F1 scores on ClinicalBench by 5.05% over single-agent methods, 2.09% over multi-agent methods, and 0.86% over agentic reasoning methods. Demonstrates effectiveness across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: CAREAgent effectively addresses the gap in clinical order generation by providing fine-grained, executable orders through a novel two-stage training approach combining supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Clinical order generation serves as a critical bridge between clinical decision-making and real-world practice, translating medical decisions into concrete and executable orders. Existing agents mainly focus on coarse-grained decisions and overlook the fine-grained, executable information required for clinical orders. To address this gap, we propose CAREAgent, an agent for clinical order generation. To support its training, we introduce a two-stage agentic reasoning data construction method. First, we design an agent framework that constructs verifiable reasoning trajectories aligned with realistic clinical tool usage. Second, we filter reasoning trajectories by format compliance, order validity, and clinical plausibility. Building on the constructed data, the model is first trained via supervised fine-tuning to acquire fundamental reasoning formats and medical knowledge, and is subsequently optimized through reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional reward functions to enhance complex clinical reasoning capabilities. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CAREAgent. On ClinicalBench (unseen during training), CAREAgent improves the F1 score by 5.05%, 2.09%, and 0.86% over the single-agent, multi-agent, and agentic reasoning methods, respectively.

</details>


### [76] [Diagnosing LLM Arbitration Behavior over Pre-evidence Epistemic States in RAG-based Fact-Checking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01120)
*Yuxi Sun,Wenbo Shang,Wei Gao,Xin Huang,Jing Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Researchers introduce PAVE, a diagnostic framework to evaluate how LLM verifiers in RAG-based fact-checking arbitrate between their parametric knowledge (prior) and retrieved evidence, revealing unreliable arbitration behavior and proposing a lightweight JSD-based improvement method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RAG-based fact-checking systems use LLMs as verifiers, but these models' parametric knowledge creates pre-evidence tendencies that may conflict with retrieved evidence. Existing evaluation frameworks fail to characterize this prior-context discrepancy or measure how verifiers arbitrate between these conflicting signals, creating a gap in understanding and evaluating LLM verifier reliability.

Method: Researchers introduce PAVE (Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation), a diagnostic testbed that stratifies LLM verifiers into four epistemic states based on correctness and confidence of pre-evidence priors. The method evaluates arbitration behavior by testing whether models persist in correct prior under misleading evidence and correct wrong prior when accurate evidence is provided. Experiments are conducted across seven LLMs, followed by proposing a lightweight JSD-based test-time arbitration method to improve factual reliability without model modification.

Result: Experiments reveal unreliable and highly model-dependent prior-context arbitration among the seven tested LLMs. The proposed JSD-based arbitration method successfully improves factual reliability across diverse LLM families without requiring modifications to the underlying models, achieving competitive performance.

Conclusion: Prior-context arbitration in LLM verifiers is unreliable and varies significantly across models, highlighting the importance of careful verifier selection for real-world RAG-based fact-checking applications. The proposed JSD-based arbitration method offers a practical solution to improve reliability without model changes, providing valuable insights for future verifier design and evaluation.

Abstract: In RAG-based fact-checking, LLMs are increasingly used as verifiers to check given claims against retrieved evidence. Their parametric knowledge can induce pre-evidence tendencies that may conflict with the retrieved context, yet existing evaluation frameworks do not characterize such prior-context discrepancy or measure how verifiers arbitrate between parametric and contextual signals. We introduce \textsc{PAVE} (\emph{Prior-Aware Verifier Evaluation}), a diagnostic testbed that stratifies an LLM verifier into four epistemic states based on the correctness and confidence of its pre-evidence prior and evaluates its arbitration behavior on this new benchmark, i.e., whether it persists in correct prior under misleading evidence, and whether it corrects wrong prior when accurate evidence is provided. Experiments across seven LLMs reveal unreliable and highly model-dependent prior-context arbitration, highlighting the importance of verifier selection for real-world RAG-based fact-checking applications. Based on these findings, we propose a lightweight JSD-based test-time arbitration method that improves factual reliability without modifying the underlying model, achieving competitive performance across diverse LLM families.

</details>


### [77] [SkillRevise: Improving LLM-Authored Agent Skills via Trace-Conditioned Skill Revision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01139)
*Yuxuan Liu,Zhaochen Su,Lingyun Xie,Yuhao Zhang,Qing Zong,Jiahe Guo,Zhongwei Xie,Yiyan Ji,Yauwai Yim,Hongyu Luo,Xiyu Ren,Ruan Chenyu,Haoran Li,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillRevise is an execution-grounded framework that iteratively refines initial LLM agent skills by diagnosing defects from execution evidence, retrieving repair principles, and applying execution-anchored edits, overcoming cold-start problems in skill development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving methods struggle in cold-start settings where only imperfect initial skills are available. Expert-authored skills are costly and may not align with how LLM agents actually execute tasks, while one-shot generated skills can be syntactically well-formed but behaviorally weak.

Method: SkillRevise diagnoses skill defects from execution evidence, retrieves relevant repair principles from a general memory, applies execution-anchored edits, re-executes candidates, and measures empirical utility to systematically retain optimal skill versions.

Result: SkillRevise substantially outperforms one-shot baselines, improving the base agent's success rate on SkillsBench from 36.05% to 61.63%. The revised skills also exhibit strong cross-model transferability, capturing generalized procedural knowledge rather than model-specific artifacts.

Conclusion: SkillRevise effectively bridges the gap between expert-authored and one-shot generated skills by providing an execution-grounded iterative refinement framework that works well in cold-start settings and produces transferable, behaviorally strong skills.

Abstract: Agent skills are procedural artifacts that enable LLM agents to execute workflows, verify constraints, and recover from failures. Existing self-evolving methods refine skills using accumulated trajectories. However, they struggle in cold-start settings, where only an initial, imperfect skill is available. Consequently, skill construction defaults to expert authoring or one-shot LLM generation. Expert-authored skills are costly and may not align with how LLM agents actually execute tasks, while one-shot generated skills can be syntactically well formed yet behaviorally weak. To bridge this gap, we propose SkillRevise, an execution-grounded framework designed to iteratively refine these initial skills. SkillRevise diagnoses skill defects from execution evidence, retrieves relevant repair principles from a general memory, and applies execution-anchored edits. By re-executing candidates and measuring empirical utility, it systematically retains the optimal skill version. Evaluated across three benchmarks and five LLMs, SkillRevise substantially outperforms one-shot baselines, improving the base agent's success rate on SkillsBench from 36.05% to 61.63%. Furthermore, the revised skills exhibit strong cross-model transferability, capturing generalized procedural knowledge over model-specific artifacts.

</details>


### [78] [Reasoning4Sciences: Bridging Reasoning Language Models to All Scientific Branches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01145)
*Teddy Ferdinan,Bartłomiej Koptyra,Mikołaj Langner,Tomasz Adamczyk,Łukasz Radliński,Maciej Markiewicz,Aleksander Szczęsny,Stanisław Woźniak,Tymoteusz Romanowicz,Dzmitry Pihulski,Mateusz Zbrocki,Mateusz Śmigielski,Michał Rajkowski,Mateusz Biedka,Konrad Kiełczyński,Konrad Wojtasik,Jacek Duszenko,Jan Eliasz,Piotr Matys,Michał Bernacki-Janson,Maria Bellaniar Ismiati,Latius Hermawan,Wiktoria Mieleszczenko-Kowszewicz,Anna Kubicka-Sowinska,Grzegorz Chodak,Karol Postawa,Paweł Zyblewski,Tomasz Szandała,Łukasz Sterczewski,Adrian Chajec,Pawel Niewiadomski,Piotr Gruber,Marcin Wdowikowski,Sławomir Czarnecki,Bartłomiej Kryszak,Dominik Drabik,Tomasz Kajdanowicz,Kamil Mamak,Paweł Preś,Katarzyna Paczkowska,Joachim Sobczuk,Tomasz Zięba,Jan Kocoń,Maciej Piasecki,Przemysław Kazienko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This survey analyzes uneven RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines, showing major disparities in maturity levels and highlighting implementation patterns, challenges, and future directions for broader adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the widening gap in research productivity caused by uneven adoption of Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) across scientific fields, with current adoption primarily concentrated in 'hard science' fields while other disciplines lag behind.

Method: A comprehensive analysis of RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines using the European Research Council (ERC) classification system, examining development, evaluation, and application patterns. Introduction of a maturity-oriented assessment framework based on domain-specific development and evaluation resources.

Result: First comprehensive analysis revealing substantial disparities in RLM maturity across disciplines, with these disparities becoming more pronounced when only publicly available resources are considered.

Conclusion: The survey highlights substantial disparities in RLM adoption across scientific fields, with pronounced gaps when considering publicly available resources. It emphasizes the need to address these disparities and outlines current implementation paradigms, challenges, and future directions for enabling broader RLM adoption across science.

Abstract: While Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) are rapidly emerging as powerful tools for scientific research, their impact is primarily concentrated in "hard science" fields. The slow -- or lack of -- adoption of RLMs in other branches of science is causing a widening gap in research productivity. In this survey, we provide the first comprehensive analysis of RLM adoption across 28 scientific disciplines following the classification used by the European Research Council (ERC), spanning the Social Sciences and Humanities, Physical Sciences and Engineering, and Life Sciences. We examine how RLMs are developed, evaluated, and applied across disciplines. Furthermore, we introduce a maturity-oriented assessment framework based on available domain-specific development and evaluation resources, revealing substantial disparities in RLM maturity that become even more pronounced when only publicly available resources are considered. Finally, we highlight current implementation paradigms that are gaining popularity across disciplines, current challenges, and future directions in enabling RLM adoption across science.

</details>


### [79] [Expected Value Alignment for Generative Reward Modeling in Formal Mathematics Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01160)
*Shihao Ji,Haotao Tan,Zihui Song,Mingyu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EVA is a reward modeling method that extracts continuous scores from discrete token distributions by computing expectations over logits, enabling better performance for formal theorem proving in Lean 4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs used with formal theorem provers like Lean 4 need process reward models (PRMs) to evaluate intermediate reasoning steps. Existing designs have trade-offs: value-head models provide continuous scores but modify the generative interface, while generative reward models preserve textual rationales but handle numeric regression poorly because numeric values are split across tokens.

Method: Proposed Expected Value Alignment (EVA) keeps surface output discrete while extracting continuous scores from token distributions. Models emit integer scores in JSON format, and EVA computes continuous scores as expectations over logits of corresponding anchor tokens. Training combines causal language modeling with auxiliary MSE loss on expected values.

Result: Implemented EVA in Leibniz, a reward model for Lean 4 formal verification. Evaluation against zero-shot and reward-modeling baselines showed that continuous logit-based scoring significantly reduces discretization artifacts while maintaining interpretability of generative critiques.

Conclusion: EVA provides an effective solution to the trade-off between continuous scoring and discrete generative outputs, enabling better reward modeling for formal theorem proving with LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used with formal interactive theorem provers such as Lean 4. Scaling these systems with reinforcement learning or search methods requires process reward models (PRMs) that can evaluate intermediate reasoning steps. Existing reward-model designs expose a practical trade-off. Value-head models provide continuous scores but modify the generative model interface, while generative reward models preserve textual rationales but are poorly matched to continuous floating-point regression because numeric values are split across tokens. We introduce Expected Value Alignment (EVA), a reward-modeling procedure that keeps the surface output discrete while extracting continuous scores from the model's token distribution. The model emits integer scores in a structured JSON format, and EVA computes a continuous score as the expectation over the logits of the corresponding anchor tokens. Training combines the causal language modeling objective with an auxiliary mean squared error loss on these expected values. We instantiate EVA in \textit{Leibniz}, a reward model for Lean 4 formal verification, and evaluate it against zero-shot and reward-modeling baselines. The evaluation demonstrates that continuous logit-based scoring significantly reduces discretization artifacts while retaining the interpretability of generative critiques.

</details>


### [80] [Deft Scheduling of Dynamic Cloud Workflows with Varying Deadlines via Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01162)
*Ya Shen,Gang Chen,Hui Ma,Mengjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DEFT introduces a Mixture-of-Experts DRL architecture with graph-adaptive gating for cloud workflow scheduling, significantly reducing execution costs and deadline violations compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep reinforcement learning schedulers for cloud workflow scheduling have rigid single-path inference architectures that struggle to handle diverse scheduling scenarios and varying deadline tightness requirements.

Method: DEFT uses a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with deadline-perceptive experts trained for different levels of deadline tightness, plus a graph-adaptive gating mechanism that encodes workflow deadlines, DAG structures, task states, and VM conditions using cross-attention to route decisions through appropriate experts.

Result: Experiments on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks show DEFT significantly reduces execution cost and deadline violations, outperforming multiple state-of-the-art DRL baselines.

Conclusion: DEFT is the first validated Mixture-of-Experts architecture for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling that can adaptively handle diverse deadline requirements through specialized experts, offering superior performance over conventional DRL approaches.

Abstract: Workflow scheduling in cloud computing demands the intelligent allocation of dynamically arriving, graph-structured workflows with varying deadlines onto ever-changing virtual machine resources. However, existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) schedulers remain limited by rigid, single-path inference architectures that struggle to handle diverse scheduling scenarios. We introduce \textbf{DEFT} (\textbf{D}eadline-p\textbf{E}rceptive Mixture-o\textbf{F}-Exper\textbf{t}s), an innovative DRL policy architecture that leverages a specialized mixture of experts, each trained to manage different levels of deadline tightness. To our knowledge, DEFT is the first to introduce and validate a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for dynamic cloud workflow scheduling. By adaptively routing decisions through the most appropriate experts, DEFT is capable of meeting a broad spectrum of deadline requirements that no single expert can achieve. Central to DEFT is a \textbf{graph-adaptive} gating mechanism that encodes workflow deadlines and DAGs, task states, and VM conditions, using cross-attention to guide expert activation in a fine-grained, deadline-sensitive manner. Experiments on dynamic cloud workflow benchmarks demonstrate that DEFT significantly reduces execution cost and deadline violations, outperforming multiple state-of-the-art DRL baselines.

</details>


### [81] ["Skill issues'': data-centric optimization of lakehouse agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01185)
*Nicole Rose Schneider,Davide Ghilardi,Giacomo Piccinini,Jacopo Tagliabue*

Main category: cs.AI
