TL;DR: The paper presents a data-centric optimization pipeline for improving coding agents' skills on branching lakehouse systems like Bauplan, shifting evaluation from output-matching to state verification, with skills showing 31.9% accuracy improvement on 25 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coding agents are increasingly using data infrastructure, but their success depends not only on model quality but also on the skills and environment files that teach them how to use systems. The authors aim to optimize these artifacts for agents operating on branching lakehouse architectures.

Method: The paper proposes a data-centric optimization pipeline that: 1) generates task-verifier pairs, 2) executes candidate skills in isolated sandboxes, and 3) scores trajectories using both trace-level signals and programmatic checks over lakehouse state. The approach treats data-agent evaluation as a state-verification problem where agent-generated pipeline code induces concrete, inspectable lakehouse changes.

Result: In a preliminary evaluation on 25 tasks, optimized skills improved accuracy by 31.9%. The branching lakehouse architecture exposes data workflows through code, branches, commits, and merges via headless APIs and Git-like data primitives.

Conclusion: Write-path data workflows provide a useful substrate for optimizing agent skills beyond read-only tasks. The branching lakehouse turns data-agent evaluation from an output-matching problem into a state-verification problem, enabling more effective optimization of agent skills.

Abstract: Coding agents are becoming users of data infrastructure, but their success depends not only on model quality: it also depends on the skills and environment files that teach agents how to use a system. We study how to optimize these artifacts for agents operating on a branching lakehouse, Bauplan. In our setting, headless APIs and Git-like data primitives expose data workflows through code, branches, commits, and merges. Our central observation is that a branching lakehouse turns data-agent evaluation from an output-matching problem into a state-verification problem: agent-generated pipeline code induces concrete, inspectable lakehouse changes. We present a data-centric optimization pipeline that generates task-verifier pairs, executes candidate skills in isolated sandboxes, and scores trajectories using both trace-level signals and programmatic checks over lakehouse state. In a preliminary evaluation on 25 tasks, optimized skills improve accuracy by 31.9%. These results suggest that write-path data workflows provide a useful substrate for optimizing agent skills beyond read-only tasks.

</details>


### [82] [The Case for Model Science: Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01189)
*Przemyslaw Biecek,Luca Longo,Jianlong Zhou,Thomas Fel,Andreas Holzinger,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes "Model Science" as a new systematic discipline for analyzing AI models beyond benchmarking, focusing on understanding how models work rather than just measuring performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complex AI models now serve billions but our understanding lags behind deployment capabilities. Benchmark-driven research has revealed limits - benchmarks tell us whether models perform but not why they succeed or fail, and they miss critical failure modes like hallucinations or shortcuts.

Method: Learning from established sciences (cognitive science, neuroscience, medicine, agriculture), the authors propose three foundations: 1) Consolidate research around four functional perspectives (Verify, Explore, Steer, Refine), 2) Build infrastructure for cumulative knowledge (catalogues of datasets, models, findings), 3) Conduct deep analysis of individual model instances, not just model families.

Result: The paper introduces the concept of "Model Science" as a systematic approach to model analysis, providing a framework that moves beyond benchmarking to understand model behavior through complementary perspectives and deep case studies.

Conclusion: The AI community is ready to move beyond benchmarking and establish Model Science as a systematic discipline for understanding AI models, drawing lessons from established sciences to develop more comprehensive analysis methods.

Abstract: We argue that the AI community is now ready to move beyond benchmarking and consolidate scattered efforts in model analysis into a systematic discipline, a direction we term Model Science. Complex AI models now serve billions of users, yet our understanding of how they work lags far behind our ability to deploy them. Decades of benchmark-driven research have delivered remarkable progress: extensive leaderboards, a wide range of performance metrics, tracking capability gains across diverse tasks; yet this success has also revealed the limits of benchmarks as they tell us whether models perform but not why they succeed or fail, they miss critical failure modes, such as hallucinations or shortcuts. Precedents from established sciences point the way forward: cognitive science shows that understanding complex systems requires complementary levels of analysis; neuroscience demonstrates that deep study of single cases reveals what population studies miss; medicine teaches that specialised training must develop alongside research practice; and agriculture models how shared infrastructure and principles enable cumulative progress. These lessons inform three foundations for Model Science. First, we propose to consolidate research around four functional perspectives: Verify, Explore, Steer, and Refine that address complementary questions about model behaviour. Second, we discuss the required infrastructure for cumulative knowledge: catalogues of datasets, models and findings. Third, we highlight the need for deep analysis of individual model instances, not just model families, because single cases can reveal what population studies miss.

</details>


### [83] [Can LLM Agents Sustain Long-Horizon Organizational Dynamics?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01199)
*Xuancheng Zhu,Yang Yue,Shuaibing Wan,Zihan Dou,Xiaohan Zhang,Yongrui Liu,Guoshun Nan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TaskWeave is a hierarchical agent framework using memory-centered coordination for long-horizon organizational simulation, outperforming other multi-agent systems in coherence and grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language agents used for social simulation struggle with sustaining coherent behavior in structured organizations where goals propagate through hierarchy, tasks have dependencies, and artifacts accumulate over long time horizons.

Method: Formulate long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem. Introduce TaskWeave framework that maintains planning states through a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle and grounds execution through dependency-aware trace memory.

Result: TaskWeave supports coherent long-horizon organizational dynamics while producing grounded artifacts and adapting to external environments. It outperforms other multi-agent frameworks on organizational coherence, execution grounding, and downstream enterprise NLP utility.

Conclusion: Structured simulation memory is a key mechanism for building reliable LLM-based organizational simulators, as demonstrated by TaskWeave's ability to maintain coherence in hierarchical organizational simulations.

Abstract: Large language agents are increasingly used for social simulation, yet it remains unclear whether they can sustain coherent behavior in structured organizations, where goals must propagate through hierarchy, tasks depend on prior execution, and artifacts accumulate over long horizons. We formulate long-horizon organizational simulation as a memory-centered coordination problem and introduce TaskWeave, a hierarchical agentic framework that maintains planning states through a Formulate-Partition-Diagnose-Align cycle and grounds execution through dependency-aware trace memory. We evaluate TaskWeave in a year-long IT company simulation and compare it with other multi-agent frameworks on organizational coherence, execution grounding, and downstream enterprise NLP utility. Experiments show that TaskWeave supports coherent and long-horizon organizational dynamics while producing grounded artifacts and adapting to external environments. These findings suggest that structured simulation memory is a key mechanism for building reliable LLM-based organizational simulators.

</details>


### [84] [The Shape of Wisdom: Decision Trajectories in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01202)
*Shailesh Rana*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Language models' answer correctness changes through network depth, with most correct answers being unstable rather than stable, and attention mechanisms play a key role in stabilizing correct responses while MLPs don't.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by understanding how language models arrive at answers, not just which answer they output. Researchers want to examine how answer confidence (margin) evolves through the layers of the model, distinguishing between stable and unstable correct answers, and understanding what drives these changes.

Method: The method involves analyzing 9,000 trajectories across three models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3) on MMLU tasks. They track answer margins through network depth, using three metrics: current margin, next-layer change, and distance from flip. They also perform span deletion experiments to see how removing supporting or distracting text affects margins.

Result: Key findings: (1) Most correct answers are unstable rather than stable, (2) In stable-correct cases, attention mechanisms point toward correct answers while MLPs do not, (3) Removing answer-supporting text hurts margins while removing distractor-like text helps them, (4) The approach provides reproducible metrics to identify settled vs fragile answers.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that correctness and stability are distinct properties in language models, with most correct answers being fragile. While not a full circuit explanation, the method provides a reproducible way to analyze answer evolution and identify what drives changes in answer confidence through network depth.

Abstract: Language models do not simply choose an answer at the output layer. In a 9,000-trajectory MMLU study across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, the score of the answer moves across depth in structured ways. We describe each trajectory with three quantities: the current answer margin, the next-layer change in that margin, and the distance from a decision flip. The main empirical picture is that correctness and stability are different: the largest group is unstable-correct, not stable-correct. A traced subset then asks what moves the margin. In stable-correct cases, the average attention scalar points in the correct direction, while the average MLP scalar does not; span deletion shows that removing answer-supporting text hurts the margin and removing distractor-like text helps it. The result is not a full circuit explanation. It is a reproducible way to see which answers are settled, which remain fragile, and which measured sources move them.

</details>


### [85] [Advanced Mathematics Learning Behavior Prediction and Academic Early Warning Model Based on Multimodal Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01224)
*Liu Qiong,Li Zhengbo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multimodal analytics with hierarchical knowledge graphs and temporal modeling effectively predict at-risk students and enable timely interventions in advanced math education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early detection of at-risk students and timely academic intervention are major challenges in advanced mathematics education due to complex conceptual hierarchies and nonlinear learning trajectories that often hinder student performance.

Method: The study constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology, implements adaptive edge weighting based on problem difficulty and student performance, and combines heterogeneous graph attention with temporal sequence modeling to track evolving knowledge states.

Result: Empirical tests on semester-long multimodal datasets show the method accurately identifies high-risk students, effectively tracks error propagation, and targeted interventions greatly improve students' knowledge mastery while reducing academic risks.

Conclusion: The study successfully demonstrates that integrating knowledge graph analytics with multimodal temporal modeling provides a robust framework for at-risk student identification and intervention in advanced mathematics education. It enables more efficient and personalized learning support, leading to improved knowledge mastery and reduced academic risks.

Abstract: Early detection of at-risk students and timely academic intervention pose major challenges in advanced mathematics education, where complex conceptual hierarchies and nonlinear learning trajectories often hold back students' academic performance. This study adopts multimodal data analytics to build a dynamic framework for learning behavior prediction and academic early warning. It constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph ontology, realizes adaptive edge weighting according to problem difficulty and student performance, and combines heterogeneous graph attention with temporal sequence modeling to capture students' evolving knowledge states. Empirical tests on semester-long multimodal datasets prove that this method can accurately identify high-risk students and effectively track error propagation. Targeted interventions greatly improve students' knowledge mastery and reduce academic risks. The results verify that integrating knowledge graph analytics with multimodal temporal modeling can deliver more efficient and personalized learning support for advanced mathematics education.

</details>


### [86] [Application of Algorithms in Energy-Efficient Design Platforms for Green Building](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01229)
*Na Yu,Fu Wenli,Guo Fei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A BIM-based platform integrating sensor data and multi-objective optimization reduces office building energy consumption by 29.3% while keeping occupant cost increases below 3.7%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve efficiency in green building design through computer-aided energy assessment, addressing the need for integrated tools that combine BIM, real operational data, and advanced simulation for holistic optimization.

Method: Developed a multi-layer service platform combining BIM, sensor operational data, and simulation workflows using a high-performance C++ core with dynamic energy simulation and evolutionary multi-objective optimization, tested on a mid-rise office building case study with five representative areas for data collection.

Result: Achieved 29.3% reduction in annual energy consumption per square meter (from 315 to 223 kWh/m²), limited lifecycle cost increase to 3.7%, reduced discomfort hours to under 70/year, with envelope U-values of 1.05-1.57 W/m²K and ventilation rates of 2.1-3.6 h⁻¹ identified as key performance drivers.

Conclusion: The integrated algorithm framework demonstrates good scalability, strong performance, and technical feasibility for green building design, providing a reliable decision-support tool for design engineers and sustainability practitioners to deliver energy-efficient buildings.

Abstract: During green building design, computer-aided energy assessment is widely used to improve efficiency and achieve overall optimization. This paper presents a platform that combines Building Information Modeling (BIM), sensor operational data, and advanced simulation workflows using robust algorithms. The platform uses a multi-layer service architecture with dynamic energy simulation and evolutionary multi-objective optimization, connected via a high-performance C++ core and adaptive agent models. A mid-rise office building was selected as the case study. Five representative areas were chosen to collect data on building envelope characteristics and occupancy patterns. After preprocessing, missing sensor data accounted for 3.2% of annual records, and all variables were standardized using 15-minute interpolation. After 40 optimization rounds, annual energy consumption per square meter dropped by 29.3% from 315 kWh/m2 to 223 kWh/m2. The lifecycle cost increase for occupants was limited to 3.7%, and discomfort hours were reduced to under 70 hours per year. Analysis of Pareto optimal solutions shows that the envelope U-value ranges from 1.05 to 1.57 W/m2K, and nighttime ventilation rate ranges from 2.1 to 3.6 h-1, both closely linked to energy performance. The results confirm that the integrated algorithm framework offers good scalability, strong performance, and technical feasibility for green building design. This platform provides a reliable decision-support tool for design engineers and sustainability practitioners, enabling accurate, data-driven delivery of energy-efficient buildings.

</details>


### [87] [HomeFlow: A Data Flywheel for Smart Home Agent Training with Verifiable Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01230)
*Yi Gu,Huacan Wang,Shuo Zhang,Yuqing Hou,Lei Xue,Weipeng Ming,Chen Liu,Fangzhou Yu,Kuan Li,Ronghao Chen,Sen Hu,Xiaofeng Mou,Yi Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HomeFlow creates a verifiable data flywheel for smart home agents using simulation, procedural generation, and tree search to produce diverse training trajectories, achieving SOTA performance surpassing GPT-5.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to generate high-quality training data for smart home agents that need to handle ambiguous intents, dynamic environments, and multi-turn reasoning in real domestic settings.

Method: HomeFlow system with HomeEnv simulation, HomeMaker for diverse settings, Blueprint for intent compilation, MCTS-Flow for trajectory synthesis, supervised fine-tuning, and step-wise RLVE training.

Result: HomeFlow-RL-4B achieved 84.60% task success rate, HomeFlow-RL-8B achieved 87.03%, surpassing GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points on SmartHome-Bench benchmark.

Conclusion: HomeFlow provides a robust framework for training high-performing smart home agents, achieving state-of-the-art results through a systematic data generation and training approach.

Abstract: Large language model agents are moving beyond text-only interaction toward physical-world control, with smart homes as a representative domain. Real domestic interaction requires understanding ambiguous intents, operating in dynamic environments, and performing multi-turn reasoning. However, existing methods struggle to generate high-quality training data for smart home agents. We propose HomeFlow, a verifiable data flywheel for this domain. HomeFlow uses HomeEnv as a unified simulation environment and HomeMaker to procedurally generate diverse home settings. Subsequently, Blueprint compiles open-ended user intents into executable state-based success conditions, while MCTS-Flow synthesizes diverse, verifiable multi-turn trajectories through environment-guided tree search. We then optimize the agents via supervised fine-tuning and step-wise RLVE, which facilitates iterative improvement through authentic physical feedback. We further construct SmartHome-Bench to evaluate the agent across various smart home tasks. On this benchmark, HomeFlow-RL-4B and HomeFlow-RL-8B achieve task success rates of 84.60% and 87.03%. It is worth noting that HomeFlow-RL-8B even surpasses the leading GPT-5.5 by 1.23 percentage points.

</details>


### [88] [Brain-Atlas-Guided Generative Counterfactual Attention for Explainable Cognitive Decline Diagnosis Using Multimodal Connectomes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01237)
*Xiongri Shen,Jiaqi Wang,Zhenxi Song,Yi Zhong,Leilei Zhao,Xin He,Baiying Lei,Zhiguo Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an atlas-guided Generative Counterfactual Attention-guided Network for explainable cognitive decline diagnosis using multimodal brain connectomes, achieving competitive classification performance with improved interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate and explainable diagnosis of MCI and SCD is crucial for early Alzheimer's disease risk assessment and intervention. Existing connectome-based deep learning models improve classification but provide limited insight into disease-related functional and structural connectivity changes.

Method: GCAN formulates diagnosis as a source-to-target counterfactual generation problem using an Atlas-aware Bidirectional Transformer (AABT) for network-level token encoding/decoding under brain-atlas constraints. It extends from functional connectivity to joint functional and structural connectivity modeling, enabling counterfactual analysis of complementary changes. Modality-specific pre-trained classifiers provide target-state priors while being separated from the diagnostic classifier to prevent data leakage.

Result: Experiments on hospital-collected and ADNI datasets show GCAN achieves competitive performance across HC vs. SCD, HC vs. MCI, and SCD vs. MCI classification tasks. Visualization, circular connectome analysis, CAM-based comparison, ablation studies, and confidence interval analysis support the interpretability and reliability of the framework.

Conclusion: The paper introduces an atlas-knowledge-guided Generative Counterfactual Attention-guided Network (GCAN) for explainable cognitive decline diagnosis using multimodal brain connectomes. The proposed framework successfully integrates functional and structural connectivity data to enhance classification performance while providing interpretable insights into disease-related connectivity changes, demonstrating both competitive diagnostic accuracy and improved explainability compared to existing methods.

Abstract: Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and subjective cognitive decline (SCD) are closely associated with the early Alzheimer's disease continuum, where accurate and explainable diagnosis is important for early risk assessment and intervention. Existing connectome-based deep learning models can improve classification performance but often provide limited insight into disease-related functional and structural connectivity changes. This paper proposes an atlas-knowledge-guided Generative Counterfactual Attention-guided Network (GCAN) for explainable cognitive decline diagnosis using multimodal brain connectomes. GCAN formulates diagnosis as a source-to-target counterfactual generation problem, where target-label connectomes are generated from source-label inputs and their differences are used to construct counterfactual attention maps. To preserve connectome topology, an Atlas-aware Bidirectional Transformer (AABT) performs network-level token encoding and decoding under brain-atlas constraints. The framework is further extended from functional connectivity (FC) to joint functional and structural connectivity (SC) modeling, enabling counterfactual analysis of complementary functional reorganization and structural topology changes. Experiments on hospital-collected and ADNI datasets show that GCAN achieves competitive performance across HC vs. SCD, HC vs. MCI, and SCD vs. MCI classification tasks. Visualization, circular connectome analysis, CAM-based comparison, ablation studies, and confidence interval analysis further support the interpretability and reliability of the proposed framework. Modality-specific FC and SC pre-trained classifiers are used to provide target-state priors for counterfactual generation while being separated from the downstream diagnostic classifier to prevent data leakage.

</details>


### [89] [SIRIUS-SQL: Anchoring Multi-Candidate Text-to-SQL in Execution Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01246)
*Leo Luo,Haining Xie,Siqi Shen,Zhipeng Ma,Rui Ling,Hang Xu,Hefeng Jiang,Dingwei Chen,Yang Li,Peng Chen,Jie Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SIRIUS-SQL is a multi-candidate text-to-SQL system that addresses three weaknesses in existing approaches by generating diverse SQL candidates, applying targeted execution error repair, and using a hybrid selection mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art results on BIRD and SPIDER benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-SQL systems that generate multiple SQL candidates and use voting have three coupled weaknesses: 1) redundant candidate generation from single generator, 2) generic error correction that treats all non-clean execution results the same way, and 3) single-angle selection mechanisms (either result voting or SQL comparison) that miss errors detectable from other angles.

Method: SIRIUS-SQL uses: 1) difficulty-smoothing RL to train SIRIUS-32B for diverse executable SQL generation, paired with a generalist LLM to fill specialist gaps; 2) execution-grounded lifecycle that classifies outcomes and applies targeted repair before candidates re-enter the pool; 3) confidence-gated hybrid selector combining execution-result agreement with pairwise SQL-form judgment, with deterministic structural checks for near-tied cases.

Result: Achieves 75.88% on BIRD dev and 91.20% on SPIDER test benchmarks. Two out of three generalist pairings surpass Agentar-Scale-SQL, the strongest published multi-candidate system on BIRD dev.

Conclusion: SIRIUS-SQL successfully addresses the three key weaknesses in multi-candidate text-to-SQL systems through specialized diverse generation, targeted execution repair, and hybrid selection, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance on major benchmarks and surpassing existing multi-candidate approaches.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL on complex schemas is unreliable on a single pass, so recent systems generate multiple SQL candidates and let voting filter out errors. Yet voting alone is not enough, because the multi-candidate recipe has three coupled weaknesses: 1) sampling more from a single generator produces increasingly redundant candidates, 2) existing pipelines apply one generic correction to every non-clean execution result, while runtime errors, timeouts, and empty results each indicate a different distance from correctness, and 3) existing selectors rely on a single angle such as result-majority voting or pairwise SQL comparison, missing what other angles would have caught. We present SIRIUS-SQL, which addresses all three weaknesses. A difficulty-smoothing RL recipe trains SIRIUS-32B to generate diverse executable SQL candidates, paired with a generalist LLM that fills in gaps left by the specialist. An execution-grounded lifecycle classifies each outcome and applies targeted repair before candidates re-enter the pool. A confidence-gated hybrid selector combines execution-result agreement with pairwise SQL-form judgment, escalating only near-tied cases to a deterministic structural check. SIRIUS-SQL reaches 75.88% on BIRD dev and 91.20% on SPIDER test. Two of three generalist pairings surpass Agentar-Scale-SQL, the strongest published multi-candidate system on BIRD dev.

</details>


### [90] [Emergent Ordinal Geometry in Transformers Trained on Local Comparisons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01269)
*Nishit Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: string


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: string

Method: string

Result: string

Conclusion: string

Abstract: Transitive inference is the challenge of inferring that A < C from knowing only adjacent relations (A < B, B < C). It is solved by humans and animals not through logical chaining but via an analogue mental number line, whose signature is the symbolic distance effect: distant comparisons are easier than nearby ones. We ask whether Transformers acquire the same primitive, training small models exclusively on adjacent comparisons from a hidden total order and evaluating generalization to unseen distant pairs. We find that out-of-distribution generalization emerges alongside a striking geometric reorganization: entity embeddings collapse onto a one-dimensional manifold whose principal axis recovers the hidden rank order with near-perfect fidelity, and this structure is sensitive to optimization in ways that produce grokking-like transient dynamics. Critically, even when accuracy is at ceiling, decision confidence and geometric separation both scale monotonically with rank distance, directly mirroring the symbolic distance effect observed across decades of behavioural experiments on humans, primates, and rodents. These results ground a 50-year-old behavioural regularity in the geometry of learned representations, offering a mechanistic account of transitive inference that bridges cognitive science and modern neural networks.

</details>


### [91] [ANDES: Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis Tool for Autonomous Instruction Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01279)
*Zhengyang Zhao,Shengjie Ye,Lu Ma,Hao Liang,Hengyi Feng,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Andes framework that treats data generation as a plug-and-play agent skill to help AI agents automate LLM post-training alignment by intelligently synthesizing datasets rather than relying on complex web scraping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current frontier AI agents struggle with automating the critical post-training phase that transforms base LLMs into aligned assistants, particularly in curating high-quality training datasets from the open web due to challenges with long-horizon tasks and limited context.

Method: Introduces Andes (Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis) framework with a self-evolving World Tree routing mechanism and actionable diagnostic reports, providing an intelligent abstraction layer for data generation as a plug-and-play agent skill.

Result: Under strict compute constraints, equipping foundationally weaker agents with Andes improves automated alignment and achieves state-of-the-art performance on PostTrainBench with robust cross-task generalization.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that Andes is an effective solution for enhancing AI agents' ability to automate post-training alignment, providing a practical abstraction layer that allows agents to focus on higher-level training tasks rather than complex data gathering, leading to improved performance on automated alignment benchmarks.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being tasked with automating AI research itself, particularly the critical post-training phase that transforms base LLMs into aligned assistants. However, recent evaluations reveal that even frontier agents struggle to perform this task. While the success of post-training fundamentally relies on acquiring high-quality data, relying on agents to autonomously curate targeted training datasets from the open web introduces severe challenges. Executing the long-horizon tasks of searching, filtering, and balancing data within noisy web environments frequently overwhelms an agent's limited context, ultimately leading to degraded dataset quality and suboptimal downstream training performance. To bridge this gap, we introduce Andes (Agent Native Data Evolving Synthesis), a framework that reimagines data generation as a plug-and-play \emph{agent skill}. Rather than forcing agents to devise complex data-gathering strategies from scratch, \textsc{Andes} provides an intelligent abstraction layer. By leveraging a self-evolving World Tree routing mechanism and actionable diagnostic reports, it allows trainer agents to dynamically steer data synthesis through an interactive, closed-loop interface. We demonstrate that under strict compute constraints, equipping foundationally weaker agents with Andes improves automated alignment, securing state-of-the-art performance on PostTrainBench and robust cross-task generalization. Our project is available at https://github.com/zzy1127/ANDES.

</details>


### [92] [SkillSmith: Co-Evolving Skills and Tools for Self-Improving Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01314)
*Yangbo Wei,Zhen Huang,Shaoqiang Lu,Junhong Qian,Qifan Wang,Chen Wu,Lei He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillSmith enables synergy-aware co-evolution of skills and tools through joint modifications guided by ecological utility modeling and anti-pattern learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving agents with skill evolution frameworks assume fixed tool layers and evaluate skills independently, limiting their ability to repair tool-level failures or reason about skill interactions.

Method: SkillSmith framework with: 1) unified proposal space for joint skill-tool modifications (wrapping, editing, composing, splitting, retiring), 2) ecological utility model using Lotka-Volterra dynamics with interaction matrix from execution traces, and 3) anti-pattern recording system for failure signatures and remedies.

Result: SkillSmith outperforms strong baselines across three benchmarks (including WildClawBench) and five Qwen3.5 model scales. Gains amplify as task complexity and multi-skill co-activation increase.

Conclusion: The paper introduces SkillSmith as a novel framework that successfully enables skill-tool co-evolution, allowing agents to adaptively acquire and refine capabilities. Experimental results across multiple benchmarks demonstrate its effectiveness, particularly on complex tasks requiring multiple skills.

Abstract: Recent self-evolving agents have shown that skills can be discovered, refined, and accumulated through execution. However, existing skill-evolution frameworks typically assume a fixed tool layer and evaluate each skill independently, limiting their ability to repair tool-level failures or reason about interactions among skills. We propose SkillSmith, a synergy-aware skill-tool co-evolution framework. SkillSmith introduces a unified proposal space in which reflection produces atomic bundles that jointly modify skills and tools, allowing tools to be wrapped, edited, composed, split, or retired when skill evolution identifies a reusable capability gap. To guide this joint search, SkillSmith maintains an ecological utility model inspired by Lotka-Volterra dynamics, where an interaction matrix estimated from execution traces captures pairwise complementarity and conflict among skills and provides pressure signals for retrieval, mutation prioritization, and retirement. Furthermore, SkillSmith records anti-patterns, including failure signatures, causal attributions, and remedies, to accelerate diagnosis and veto proposals that repeat known mistakes. Experiments on three benchmarks, including WildClawBench, and five Qwen3.5 model scales show that SkillSmith consistently outperforms strong baselines, with gains that amplify as task complexity and multi-skill co-activation increase.

</details>


### [93] [Science Earth: Towards A Planet-Scale Operating System for AI-Native Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01316)
*Zhe Zhao,Haibin Wen,Yingcheng Wu,Jiaming Ma,Yifan Wen,Jinglin Jian,Jiacheng Ge,Xiangru Tang,Bo An,Ming Yin,Sanfeng Wu,Mengdi Wang,Le Cong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Science Earth enables planet-scale scientific collaboration where any AI capability can connect to any other, with coordination emerging from the problem rather than pre-designed workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scientific AI systems are siloed (one for biological analysis, another for clinical reasoning, mathematical derivation, or materials simulation), limiting collaboration and discovery across vast search spaces.

Method: EACN protocol allowing capabilities to discover each other, negotiate task ownership, and adjudicate across incompatible evidentiary standards without prior knowledge.

Result: Successfully validated in two structurally distinct conditions: correcting a closure-ratio assumption in Ott-Antonsen analytic theory in 30 minutes, and producing three new result layers with heterogeneous capabilities in an 8-agent single-cell run over 64.9 hours.

Conclusion: Science Earth shifts scientific discovery from workflow design to open-ended connectivity, creating a distributed, self-correcting process where collaboration emerges from the problem itself.

Abstract: Scientific discovery demands intelligence, perseverance, and serendipity
  across vast search spaces. Today, top scientific capabilities remain
  siloed--one AI system for biological analysis, another for clinical
  reasoning, mathematical derivation, or materials simulation--and no
  pre-designed team can anticipate every skill a question will need.
  Science Earth is a planet-scale scientific runtime in which any
  capability--a simulation cluster, a wet-lab robot, a proof engine, a
  single-cell pipeline--can connect to any other, with collaboration
  structure emerging from the question itself. Its underlying EACN protocol
  lets capabilities discover one another, negotiate task ownership, and
  adjudicate across incompatible evidentiary standards without prior
  knowledge of who will meet whom. This shifts the organizing challenge from
  workflow design to open-ended connectivity. Two runs validate this under
  structurally distinct conditions. In a trans-Pacific higher-order Kuramoto
  synchronization study, agents identified and corrected a closure-ratio
  assumption in Ott-Antonsen analytic theory that fails outside the
  Lorentzian limit, within thirty minutes. In an eight-agent single-cell run
  on the 4.88M-cell Kang 2024 pan-cancer atlas, heterogeneous capabilities
  coupled over a 64.9-hour window with one structural external instruction,
  producing three new result layers and anchoring findings against an
  independent wet-lab study on an adjacent CCR8- TIGIT+ Treg subset. These
  cases are a first empirical reading, not a benchmark sweep. They show that
  when AI capabilities are truly connectable and coordination emerges from
  the problem, scientific reasoning becomes a distributed, self-correcting
  process--a step towards scaling AI-native discovery to the planet.

</details>


### [94] [Recognize Your Orchestrator: An Entropy Dynamics Perspective for LLM Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01351)
*Junze Zhu,Weihao Chen,Xuanwang Zhang,Zhen Wu,Xinyu Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework to analyze orchestration fragility in Multi-Agent Systems, revealing a 'Reasoning Trap' where reasoning-heavy models fail as orchestrators despite individual task proficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized orchestration topology in Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) remains a critical point of fragility. There's a need to understand and quantify the systemic instability that arises during orchestration to design more robust MAS architectures.

Method: Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework to model the orchestration process as a system governed by task resolution and cumulative context loading forces; and Inverse Workflow Generation (IWG), a multi-agent pipeline that synthesizes process-verifiable, high-complexity benchmarks with dense intermediate checkpoints.

Result: The entropy dynamics model fits empirical trajectories, providing physically interpretable parameters that quantify system stability and performance collapse. The analysis uncovers a 'Reasoning Trap' where reasoning-heavy models excel in isolated tasks but frequently fail as orchestrators due to context squeezing.

Conclusion: The analysis reveals that reasoning-heavy models often fail as orchestrators due to context squeezing, despite excelling at isolated tasks. The proposed Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework and Inverse Workflow Generation benchmark provide physical mechanisms and tools to quantify system stability, offering valuable insights for designing more robust Multi-Agent Systems architectures.

Abstract: The transition from single-turn models to Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) promises enhanced problem-solving capabilities, yet the centralized orchestration topology remains a critical point of fragility. To analyze this, we propose a Mean-Field Entropy Dynamics framework, modeling the orchestration process as a system governed by the competing forces of task resolution and cumulative context loading. To facilitate validation, we introduce Inverse Workflow Generation (IWG), a multi-agent pipeline that synthesizes process-verifiable, high-complexity benchmarks with dense intermediate checkpoints. We demonstrate that our entropy dynamics model fits empirical trajectories, providing physically interpretable parameters that quantify system stability and performance collapse. Crucially, our analysis uncovers a ``Reasoning Trap": while reasoning-heavy models excel in isolated tasks, they frequently fail as orchestrators due to context squeezing. Elucidating the physical mechanisms underlying the Orchestrator and quantifying systemic uncertainty offers insights for the MASs' architectural design.

</details>


### [95] [FlowTime: Towards Continuous Generative Watch Time Prediction via Flow-based Personalized Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01352)
*Hongxu Ma,Han Zhou,Chenghou Jin,Jie Zhang,Xiaoyu Yang,Chunjie Chen,Jihong Guan,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposed Continuous Generative Regression paradigm using FlowTime with one-step generative VAE to predict watch time, addressing limitations of existing methods by capturing multimodal user interaction patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current watch time prediction methods suffer from paradigm-specific limitations: Direct Regression faces mean-collapse, Ordinal Regression has quantization errors, and Discrete Generative Regression suffers from latency and heuristic design. None capture intrinsic multimodality of user-item interaction patterns.

Method: FlowTime utilizes a one-step generative variational autoencoder with a flow-based personalized prior that warps standard Gaussian prior into complex, history-conditioned manifold using normalizing flows.

Result: Extensive offline experiments and online A/B tests demonstrate FlowTime's significant superiority over state-of-the-art methods. The authors also released TimeRec as the first open-source watch time prediction library.

Conclusion: Continuous Generative Regression represents a significant advancement over existing paradigms for watch time prediction in short-video recommender systems, addressing core limitations while enabling personalized engagement modeling.

Abstract: Watch time has emerged as a pivotal metric for optimizing deep user engagement in short-video recommender systems. However, current methods of watch time prediction (WTP) suffer from inherent paradigm-specific limitations. Direct Regression faces mean-collapse due to unimodal Gaussian assumptions, while Ordinal Regression is hampered by quantization errors from rigid discretization. Similarly, Discrete Generative Regression struggles with high inference latency and heuristic vocabulary design. Beyond these specific flaws, a shared deficiency is the inability to capture the intrinsic multimodality and heterogeneity of User-Item Interaction Patterns. To address these challenges, we first revisit the WTP problem from a causal perspective and identify these user-specific patterns as structural confounders that modulate watch time outcomes, where identical interests manifest as distinct watch time outcomes conditioned on diverse user habits. Then, we formally propose a new (or the fourth) paradigm -- Continuous Generative Regression, and introduce FlowTime, a novel method utilizing a One-step Generative Variational Autoencoder. FlowTime effectively circumvents the latency of iterative denoising while maintaining the expressivity of continuous latent spaces. Furthermore, we design a Flow-based Personalized Prior that leverages NFs to warp a standard Gaussian prior into a complex, history-conditioned manifold, thereby enabling the adaptive modeling of multimodal interaction patterns. Finally, we build TimeRec, the first open-source WTP Library, alongside a novel personalization metric to establish a rigorous benchmarking standard. Extensive offline experiments and online A/B tests demonstrate FlowTime's significant superiority over SOTA methods.

</details>


### [96] [Early Diagnosis of Wasted Computation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems via Failure-Aware Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01365)
*Xianyou Li,Weiran Yan,Yichao Wu,Penghao Liang,Mengwei Yuan,Jianan Liu,Jing Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a failure-aware observability framework to diagnose wasted computation in multi-agent LLM systems by mapping failure modes to online trace signals, revealing mechanisms behind operational failures and positioning this as a diagnostic layer between execution logs and accuracy metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-using multi-agent LLM systems expend significant computation through tokens, tool calls, retries, and code execution. When runs fail, final-answer evaluation typically reveals only the endpoint but not where the trajectory lost recoverable progress, making it difficult to diagnose wasted computation.

Method: The researchers introduce a failure-aware observability framework that maps recurring failure modes to online trace signals (tool reliability, execution recovery, orchestration loops, evidence availability, information change, budget pressure). They instantiate this framework in a three-agent question-answering system and evaluate it on 165 GAIA validation traces under identical execution caps.

Result: Operational failures remain common across difficulty levels (22/53 level-1 runs, 33/86 level-2 runs, 12/26 level-3 runs). Traces reveal failure mechanisms including insufficient evidence, repeated-action loops, max-step termination, tool-failure streaks, and successful but unproductive execution calls. Mean token use increases from 8,152 tokens at level 1 to 16,389 tokens at level 3, while evidence availability and sentence-level support diverge. A cached 10-trace LLM-judge grounding audit shows cheap online signals and deeper semantic metrics capture complementary failure layers.

Conclusion: The study concludes that failure-aware observability serves as a valuable diagnostic layer between raw execution logs and final-answer accuracy, with cheap online signals and deeper semantic metrics capturing complementary aspects of system failures.

Abstract: Tool-using multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems spend computation through model tokens, tool calls, retries, and code execution before producing an answer. When a run fails, final-answer evaluation reveals the endpoint but usually not the point at which the trajectory stopped making recoverable progress. This paper introduces a failure-aware observability framework for diagnosing wasted computation in multi-agent LLM traces. The framework maps recurring failure modes to online trace signals, including tool reliability, execution recovery, orchestration loops, evidence availability, information change, and budget pressure. We instantiate the framework in a three- agent question-answering system and evaluate it on 165 GAIA validation traces under identical execution caps. Operational failures remain common: 22/53 level-1 runs, 33/86 level-2 runs, and 12/26 level-3 runs fail to produce a usable final answer. The traces expose different mechanisms behind these outcomes, including insufficient evidence, repeated-action loops, max-step termination, tool-failure streaks, and execution calls that succeed without useful output. Mean token use rises from 8,152 tokens at level 1 to 16,389 tokens at level 3, while evidence availability and sentence-level support diverge. A cached 10-trace LLM-judge grounding audit shows that cheap online signals and deeper semantic metrics capture complementary layers of failure. The results position failure-aware observability as a diagnostic layer between raw execution logs and final-answer accuracy.

</details>


### [97] [GuidaPA: Privacy-Preserving Chatbot for Public Administration via Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01386)
*Daniel M. Jimenez-Gutierrez,Albenzio Cirillo,Raffaele Nicolussi,Alessio Beltrame,Andrea Vitaletti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GuidaPA is a privacy-preserving chatbot for Italian Public Administration trained via Federated Learning, achieving near-centralized performance while keeping sensitive data on-site.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a privacy-preserving chatbot for Italian Public Administration that can access restricted internal data (tickets, officer manuals, database extracts) which cannot be centrally pooled due to regulatory and organizational constraints, using Federated Learning as an alternative to centralized data collection.

Method: The system uses Federated Learning with QLoRA (4-bit quantization) fine-tuning over 15 federated rounds, with role-based access control, secure client-side preprocessing, and monitoring of non-IID effects. Models are trained on documentation from SIGESON and SIDFORS platforms with an 80/20 train-test split per client.

Result: The federated model achieves competitive metrics: ROUGE-1/2/L of 61.10/55.77/59.44, BLEU-4 of 45.02, and METEOR of 63.94, close to private centralized fine-tuning. Domain fine-tuning significantly improves performance over general-purpose baseline (ROUGE-1 from 41.45 to 62.18, BLEU-4 from 26.97 to 50.90).

Conclusion: Federated Learning offers a viable path to deploy high-quality conversational AI for Italian Public Administration while preserving data privacy and adhering to regulatory constraints. The approach successfully bridges the gap between data isolation needs and AI performance requirements.

Abstract: We present GuidaPA, a privacy-preserving chatbot for the Italian Public Administration (PA) trained via Federated Learning (FL) on documentation from two national PA platforms, SIGESON and SIDFORS. Our corpus includes approximately 8 pages of SIGESON manuals and 31 pages of SIDFORS manuals/FAQs; while this study uses public documentation as a safe proxy, the intended deployment extends to restricted internal sources (e.g., tickets, officer manuals, database extracts) that can not be centrally pooled due to regulatory and organizational constraints. GuidaPA integrates role-based access control, secure client-side preprocessing, explicit monitoring of non-IID effects, and parameter-efficient federated fine-tuning of large language models. Using QLoRA (4-bit) over 15 federated rounds with an 80/20 train-test split per client, we evaluate answer quality with ROUGE, BLEU-4, and METEOR. The best federated model achieves ROUGE-1/2/L of 61.10/55.77/59.44, BLEU-4 of 45.02, and METEOR of 63.94-close to private centralized fine-tuning while keeping data on-site. Compared to the general-purpose baseline, domain fine-tuning improves ROUGE-1 from 41.45 to 62.18 and BLEU-4 from 26.97 to 50.90. Overall, the results indicate that FL can deliver high-quality conversational AI for public services without centralized data sharing

</details>


### [98] [Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented Large Language Model Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01416)
*Rahul Suresh Babu,Adarsh Agrawal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A self-healing orchestrator for tool-augmented LLM agents improves reliability by detecting failures, executing targeted recoveries with budget constraints, and verifying trajectories, achieving 98.8% task success vs. baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-augmented LLM agents face reliability issues not only from model errors but also from orchestration-level failures such as tool timeouts, malformed arguments, stale context, contradictory evidence, retry loops, and unverified intermediate outputs.

Method: The approach involves a self-healing agentic orchestrator that maps observable failure signals to failure classes, selects targeted recovery actions under explicit budgets, verifies recovered trajectories, and records observability traces. The evaluation uses a 100-task controlled fault-injection benchmark comparing against static workflow, retry-only, ReAct-style, and full-replanning baselines.

Result: Self-healing achieves 98.8% task success versus 94.5% for retry-only and 93.8% for full replanning. Under a single recovery attempt budget, self-healing scores 94.0% versus 85.3% for retry-only and 88.2% for full replanning. In silent-failure settings, verifier-guided self-healing reduces silent failures to 0.0% while non-verifying baselines return wrong-but-plausible outputs. Compact model-in-the-loop validation shows the recovery mechanism works with live tool-calling models.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that failure-aware, budgeted, and verification-guided orchestration significantly improves reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems, providing controlled evidence for the effectiveness of self-healing agentic orchestrators.

Abstract: Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents rely on orchestration layers that coordinate planning, retrieval, tool invocation, validation, memory, and recovery. In these systems, failures arise not only from model errors, but also from orchestration-level issues such as tool timeouts, malformed arguments, stale context, contradictory evidence, retry loops, and unverified intermediate outputs. This paper presents a self-healing agentic orchestrator that treats reliability as a bounded runtime control problem. The orchestrator maps observable failure signals to inferred failure classes, selects targeted recovery actions under explicit budgets, verifies recovered trajectories, and records observability traces. We evaluate the approach on a 100-task controlled fault-injection benchmark against static workflow, retry-only, ReAct-style, and full-replanning baselines. Self-healing achieves 98.8\% task success, compared with 94.5\% for retry-only and 93.8\% for full replanning. A matched recovery-budget sweep shows that self-healing outperforms retry-only and full replanning at every tested budget, with the largest gap under a single recovery attempt: 94.0\% versus 85.3\% and 88.2\%, respectively. Under a controlled semantic silent-failure setting, verifier-guided self-healing reduces silent failures to 0.0\%, while non-verifying baselines return wrong-but-plausible outputs more often. A compact model-in-the-loop validation shows that the same recovery mechanism can operate when a live tool-calling model performs tool selection, argument generation, and answer synthesis over local fault-injected tools. These results provide controlled evidence that failure-aware, budgeted, and verification-guided orchestration improves reliability and diagnosability in tool-augmented LLM systems.

</details>


### [99] [GovAI-Pipe: A Layered AI Governance Pipeline for Citizen-Facing AI in Turkey's e-Government Gateway](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01417)
*Ahmet Kaplan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposed GovAI-Pipe: a four-layer governance pipeline connecting AI policy frameworks to operational AI deployment in e-government platforms, demonstrated on Turkey's e-Devlet system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No structured technical governance infrastructure connects high-level AI policy frameworks (EU AI Act, OECD AI Principles, Turkey's National AI Strategy) to operational reality of deploying AI within centralized e-government platforms like Turkey's e-Government Gateway (e-Devlet) with over 68 million users.

Method: Design Science Research methodology used to create GovAI-Pipe, a four-layer governance pipeline with: (1) pre-deployment validation (bias testing, explainability, privacy impact assessment), (2) deployment governance (risk-tier classification, approval workflows), (3) runtime monitoring (drift detection, fairness tracking, human-in-the-loop escalation), and (4) post-incident governance (audit trails, rollback, citizen redress). Each layer anchored to EU AI Act, GDPR, and Turkey's National AI Strategy.

Result: Demonstrated framework through two high-risk e-Devlet use cases, showing how GovAI-Pipe operationalizes governance principles as auditable, technical pipeline components.

Conclusion: GovAI-Pipe provides a practical governance framework for operationalizing AI ethics and policy principles within e-government platforms, transforming abstract governance principles into actionable, technical checkpoints throughout the AI lifecycle.

Abstract: Turkey's e-Government Gateway (e-Devlet) serves over 68 million registered users with more than 9,200 government services, and is increasingly integrating artificial intelligence into citizen-facing applications such as chatbot assistants and eligibility assessments. However, no structured technical governance infrastructure currently connects high-level AI policy frameworks, such as the EU AI Act, OECD AI Principles, and Turkey's own National AI Strategy, to the operational reality of deploying AI within a centralized e-government platform. We propose GovAI-Pipe, a four-layer governance pipeline designed using Design Science Research methodology that maps the AI model lifecycle to governance checkpoints: (1) pre-deployment validation for bias testing, explainability, and privacy impact assessment; (2) deployment governance for risk-tier classification and approval workflows; (3) runtime monitoring for drift detection, fairness tracking, and human-in-the-loop escalation; and (4) post-incident governance for audit trails, rollback, and citizen redress. Each layer is anchored to specific provisions of the EU AI Act, the GDPR data protection framework, and the National AI Strategy. We demonstrate the framework through two high-risk e-Devlet use cases, showing how GovAI-Pipe operationalizes governance principles as auditable, technical pipeline components.

</details>


### [100] [Dive into Ambiguity: A*-Inspired Multi-Agents Commonsense Obfuscation Attack on LLM Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01441)
*Boxuan Wang,Zhuoyun Li,Xiaowei Huang,Yi Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Novel A*-inspired framework generates semantically aligned adversarial prompts to induce factual errors in LLMs, using hierarchical rewriting and agentic mechanism labeling for efficient, interpretable attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are vulnerable to prompt-level adversarial attacks that preserve intent while triggering commonsense hallucinations, which is particularly urgent as LLMs are integrated into safety-critical domains requiring factual reliability. Existing attack methods lack efficiency and fail to capture real-world adversary strategies.

Method: 1) Hierarchical Rewrite Strategy guided by a dynamic semantic dispersion coefficient γ that balances conservative edits early with aggressive obfuscations later; 2) Reverse simulated annealing schedule; 3) Agentic Mechanism Labeling for discovering and refining adversarial mechanisms with interpretable reverse optimization.

Result: The framework achieves higher attack success rates than exhaustive exploration across diverse LLMs while requiring fewer attempts, demonstrating both efficiency and effectiveness. Theoretically proven that prompt rewriting follows a contractive recurrence leading to semantic collapse as γ decreases.

Conclusion: The proposed A*-inspired Factual Error Induction Framework represents an effective approach to studying and mitigating LLM vulnerabilities through systematic adversarial prompt generation, offering both theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency improvements.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel in reasoning and knowledge-intensive tasks but remain vulnerable to prompt-level adversarial attacks that preserve intent while triggering commonsense hallucinations. This vulnerability is urgent, as LLMs are rapidly integrated into safety-critical domains where factual reliability is non-negotiable. Existing attack methods either lack efficiency or fail to capture the adaptive strategies of real-world adversaries. We propose an A*-inspired Factual Error Induction Framework, a framework for generating semantically aligned yet obfuscated prompts. At its core is a Hierarchical Rewrite Strategy guided by a dynamic semantic dispersion coefficient $γ$ that balances conservative edits early with aggressive obfuscations later, following a reverse simulated annealing schedule. To enhance interpretability, we further introduce Agentic Mechanism Labeling, which discovers and refines adversarial mechanisms, offering interpretable reverse optimization. Theoretically, we prove that prompt rewriting follows a contractive recurrence, leading to semantic collapse as $γ$ decreases. Empirically, across diverse LLMs, our method achieves higher attack success rates than exhaustive exploration while requiring fewer attempts, demonstrating both efficiency and effectiveness.

</details>


### [101] [Transferring Information Across Interventions in Causal Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01457)
*Mohammad Ali Javidian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization method that uses shared causal parameters to transfer information across interventions, improving sample efficiency when direct interventions are unavailable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Bayesian optimization treats variables as black-box inputs without distinguishing correlation from causation. Causal Bayesian optimization uses known causal graphs and observational data to decide which variables to intervene on, but existing methods learn each intervention effect almost in isolation, ignoring shared underlying causal mechanisms.

Method: The authors propose graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization, which uses a small set of shared causal parameters to couple different intervention effects through a causal kernel. This allows evidence from one intervention to improve estimates of related interventions. For identifiable linear Gaussian causal models, the kernel has low rank bounded by the number of shared parameters. The approach includes nonlinear and adaptive extensions.

Result: The proposed method yields a causal kernel with low rank for linear Gaussian models, leading to an information-gain bound that grows logarithmically in the optimization horizon and a regret bound that separates optimization, causal estimation, and intervention choice errors. Experiments on Gaussian systems, stress tests, and benchmarks show it retains causal BO benefits while transferring information across related interventions.

Conclusion: The proposed graph-coupled causal BO method successfully integrates shared causal parameters to enable information transfer across interventions, resulting in improved sample efficiency and performance, particularly when direct interventions on target variables are unavailable and sparse data must be reused across many interventions.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization is a popular way to optimize expensive systems, where every experiment, simulation, or intervention costs time or money. In its standard form, it treats the variables we control as plain inputs to a black box and cannot tell apart mere correlation from a real cause and effect. Causal Bayesian optimization closes part of this gap by using a known causal graph together with observational data to decide which variables are worth intervening on. Existing methods, however, learn the effect of each possible intervention almost in isolation, even though in a causal system these effects usually share the same underlying mechanisms. We propose graph-coupled causal Bayesian optimization, which ties the different intervention effects together through the uncertainty we have about a small set of shared causal parameters. The result is a causal kernel that lets evidence collected from one intervention improve our estimate of related interventions. For identifiable linear Gaussian causal models, we show that this kernel has low rank, bounded by the number of shared parameters rather than by the size of the intervention menu. This in turn yields an information-gain bound that grows only logarithmically in the optimization horizon, and a regret bound that cleanly separates three sources of error: optimization, causal estimation, and the choice of which intervention sets to consider. We also describe nonlinear and adaptive extensions. Across theory-aligned Gaussian systems, shared-mechanism stress tests, and standard causal optimization benchmarks, the method keeps the benefits of causal Bayesian optimization while transferring information across related interventions, with the clearest gains when direct interventions on the target's parents are unavailable and sparse interventional data must be reused across a large family of candidate interventions.

</details>


### [102] [A Minimalist Brain-Computer Musical Interface for Real-Time Emotion-Driven Sonification: System Design and Preliminary Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01473)
*Pablo A. Monroy-D'Croz,Rafael Ramirez-Melendez,Julian Cespedes-Guevara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A minimalist EEG-based musical interface was created but failed to detect reliable frontal alpha asymmetry changes from intentional emotional states, suggesting challenges for emotion regulation BCIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop and test a minimalist brain-computer musical interface (BCMI) that translates prefrontal EEG activity into adaptive music for affective sonification and investigate whether intentional emotional states can modulate neurofeedback signals.

Method: Real-time prefrontal EEG acquisition via wireless sensors, frontal alpha asymmetry estimation (AF7/AF8), mapping to musical features through stochastic generative algorithm, synchronization with Ableton Live via Lab Streaming Layer, and 22-participant experiment testing intentional emotional self-induction effects.

Result: No significant effects found for target emotion or time on frontal alpha asymmetry modulation; individual differences (musical training, acting experience) explained more variance than experimental manipulation (0.40% of total signal variance).

Conclusion: The study demonstrates significant challenges in using frontal alpha asymmetry as a voluntary control signal for emotion regulation interfaces, highlighting methodological considerations for future BCMI research.

Abstract: This paper presents a minimalist brain-computer Musical Interface (BCMI) that functions as a real-time affective sonification system, translating prefrontal EEG activity into adaptive music. Emotional valence is estimated from frontal alpha asymmetry (AF7/AF8) and mapped to musical features such as mode, tempo, rhythmic density, and pitch register through a stochastic generative algorithm. The system integrates wireless EEG acquisition, real-time Python signal processing, and Ableton Live-based music generation synchronized via Lab Streaming Layer. An experiment with 22 participants investigated whether intentional emotional self-induction could modulate the BCMI neurofeedback signal. Linear mixed-effects analyses found no significant effects of target emotion or time, indicating that the frontal alpha asymmetry signal did not reliably distinguish instructed emotional states. Individual differences, including musical training and acting experience, explained more variance than the experimental manipulation, which accounted for only 0.40\% of total signal variance. These findings highlight the challenges of using frontal alpha asymmetry as a voluntary control signal for closed-loop emotion regulation and suggest methodological directions for future BCMI research.

</details>


### [103] [TERRA: Task-Embedded Reasoning and Representation Architecture for Cross-Domain Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01520)
*Shayan Shokri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TERRA formally studies cross-domain transfer of latent predictive models in structured-state domains (e.g., driving to finance), providing transfer bounds and a falsifiable hypothesis with a preregistered experiment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unresolved question of when representations learned in one structured-state domain (e.g., driving scenes) transfer to structurally analogous but unrelated domains (e.g., financial order books).

Method: Modeling domains as controlled Markov processes on graded latent grids, factoring them into domain adapters and a shared core, and using approximate MDP homomorphisms measured by bisimulation discrepancy and Gromov-Wasserstein distance.

Result: A transfer bound separating source-model error from structural mismatch, which grows geometrically with prediction horizon and is certified by Gromov-Wasserstein distance; connection of latent error to decision regret via bisimulation metrics.

Conclusion: The paper presents TERRA, a formal framework for analyzing cross-domain transfer in structured-state domains, establishing transfer bounds and proposing a falsifiable hypothesis with a preregistered experimental program.

Abstract: A single action-conditioned latent predictive architecture can in principle be trained on the structured state of a driving scene, a robot workspace, or a financial order book. The ingredients for doing so within any one domain already exist and are individually validated: masked-latent prediction, action-conditioned latent world models, discrete action tokenization, and joint-embedding prediction on voxelized state. What is not established, and what TERRA addresses, is the transfer question: when does a representation or predictor learned in one structured-state domain carry over to a structurally analogous but otherwise unrelated domain, and by how much. We give this question a formal treatment. We model each domain as a controlled Markov process on a graded latent grid, factor any instantiation into thin domain adapters and a shared domain-invariant core, and identify a cross-domain correspondence with an approximate Markov decision process homomorphism whose quality is measured by a lax bisimulation discrepancy and, for domains lacking a shared coordinate system, by a Gromov-Wasserstein distance between their action-conditioned transition operators. Under a Lipschitz predictor we derive a transfer bound that separates source-model error from structural mismatch, grows geometrically in the prediction horizon, and is certified from below by the Gromov-Wasserstein distance; we then connect latent error to decision regret through the Lipschitz value property of bisimulation metrics. The resulting Structured-State Transfer Hypothesis is stated as a falsifiable claim with a preregistered experimental program, centered on a transfer test from driving scenes to order books, including conditions under which it is refuted. We present no empirical results: this is a research proposal that converts a widely repeated intuition into testable theory.

</details>


### [104] [Joint Agent Memory and Exploration Learning via Novelty Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01528)
*Shizuo Tian,Xiaohong Weng,Rui Kong,Yuxuan Chen,Guohong Liu,Yuebing Song,Jiacheng Liu,Yuchen Li,Dawei Yin,Ting Cao,Yunxin Liu,Yuanchun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JAMEL is a framework that jointly trains memory and exploration policies using novelty-driven interaction, enabling more efficient exploration by language model agents without expensive raw history retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language model agents struggle with exploration in open-ended environments. While memory is needed for effective exploration, retaining raw interaction histories is computationally expensive. Latent memory offers compression but lacks reliable supervisory signals during training.

Method: JAMEL trains agentic memory and exploration policy together using novelty-driven interaction. It uses deterministic and persistent novelty signals (like code coverage in GUI domains) to provide natural, annotation-free supervision for the memory module.

Result: JAMEL successfully generalizes to unseen environments. Its exploration capability outperforms open-weight baselines and rivals closed-source model exploration depth while reducing token consumption.

Conclusion: JAMEL offers a promising approach to enable more effective exploration by language model agents through joint learning of memory and exploration, reducing computational costs while maintaining or surpassing exploration depth compared to existing models.

Abstract: In open-ended environments, exploration is fundamental for autonomous agents, yet current language model agents struggle with this. Effective exploration requires memory, but retaining raw interaction histories is computationally expensive over long trajectories. While latent memory offers a solution to compress interaction histories, its training lacks reliable supervisory signals. We introduce \textbf{J}oint \textbf{A}gent \textbf{M}emory and \textbf{E}xploration \textbf{L}earning (\textbf{JAMEL}), a framework that trains agentic memory and exploration policy together through novelty-driven interaction. We observe that memory and exploration form a mutually dependent loop: sustained exploration requires memory to distinguish exhausted behaviors from unseen ones, while novelty-seeking interaction provides the supervision needed to make memory useful for future exploration. By utilizing deterministic and persistent novelty signals such as code coverage in the GUI domain, we provide natural, annotation-free supervision for the memory module. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that \ours successfully generalizes to unseen environments. Its exploration capability outperforms open-weight baselines and rivals the exploration depth of a closed-source model while reducing token consumption. Our code and model are open-sourced at https://github.com/MobileLLM/JAMEL.

</details>


### [105] [RoleCDE:Benchmarking and Mitigating Role-Alignment Trade-offs in Role-Playing Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01552)
*Huayi Lai,Shichao Song,Simin Niu,Hanyu Wang,Jiawei Yang,Zhouxing Wang,Zhiqiang Yin,Xun Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First benchmark (RoleCDE) evaluates role-playing agents under value conflicts, revealing systematic alignment bias that fine-tuning can address while maintaining fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for role-playing agents focus on surface-level fidelity without evaluating decision making under conflicts between role-specific values and alignment-oriented constraints.

Method: RoleCDE benchmark construction with 8k role profiles, 24k dilemma instances across three difficulty levels and eight role categories, followed by evaluation of mainstream LLMs and fine-tuning experiments.

Result: Reveals 'Role Value Decoupling' phenomenon where agents systematically default to alignment-consistent decisions over role-specific values during conflicts; this behavior is invariant to dilemma difficulty but varies across role categories. Fine-tuning based on RoleCDE mitigates decoupling while preserving general performance.

Conclusion: RoleCDE serves as an effective evaluation benchmark that reveals systematic 'Role Value Decoupling' in RPAs and demonstrates that targeted fine-tuning can improve role-specific value trade-off reasoning while maintaining general fidelity.

Abstract: Role-playing agents(RPAs) are widely used to steer large language models(LLMs) toward role-consistent behavior, yet existing benchmarks mainly evaluate surface-level fidelity and offer limited insight into decision making under role-alignment value conflicts. To address this gap, we introduce RoleCDE, the first benchmark designed to evaluate RPAs under structured conflicts between role-specific values and alignment-oriented constraints. RoleCDE formulates role-aware decision making as cognitive dilemma scenarios, jointly evaluating role-scenario grounding, value conflict resolution, and decision tendencies. The benchmark is constructed at scale, covering approximately 8k diverse role profiles and scenarios and nearly 24k dilemma instances across three difficulty levels and eight role categories. Evaluation of several mainstream LLMs reveals a "Role Value Decoupling" phenomenon, where agents systematically default to alignment-and morality-consistent decisions rather than role-specific values when the two conflict, even under explicit role conditioning. This behavior is largely invariant to dilemma difficulty but varies substantially across role categories. We further show that RoleCDE-based fine-tuning effectively mitigates this decoupling by improving value trade-off reasoning, while preserving general role-playing fidelity and general reasoning performance. Code is available at: https://github.com/rabbitrose/RoleCDE.

</details>


### [106] [S-SPPO: Semantic-Calibrated Self-Play Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01561)
*Xiwen Chen,Wenhui Zhu,Jingjing Wang,Peijie Qiu,Zhipeng Wang,Huayu Li,ZhengXiao He,Xuanzhao Dong,Prayag Tiwari,Mingkun Xu,Yujian Xiong,Feng Luo,Abolfazl Razi,Brendan Hogan Rappazzo,Anderson Schneider,Yuriy Nevmyvaka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: S-SPPO addresses SPPO's instability through semantic calibration to prevent policy degeneration and maintain latent diversity, achieving strong performance on preference optimization tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard Bradley-Terry instantiation of DPO is limited in modeling common departures from transitivity in human preferences. Recent SPPO method addresses this but suffers from critical instability and policy degeneration when the preference oracle assigns overly confident wins to semantically indistinguishable responses.

Method: S-SPPO introduces a dual-space semantic calibration framework with i) Supervision Calibration via semantic gating to anneal win rate targets toward maximum-entropy baseline based on semantic overlap, and ii) Representation Calibration via latent repulsion to enforce geometric diversity between chosen and rejected samples.

Result: S-SPPO avoids performance degradation seen in prior methods, achieving 52.19% win rate and 47.46% length-controlled win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0 with Llama-3-8B, without using additional human-annotated preferences during training.

Conclusion: S-SPPO successfully mitigates the instability issues in SPPO through semantic and representation calibration, achieving competitive performance on benchmark tasks without requiring additional human-annotated preferences.

Abstract: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences is often formulated via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). However, the standard Bradley-Terry instantiation of DPO is limited in modeling common departures from transitivity in human preferences. To address this, recent work has introduced Self-Play Preference Optimization (SPPO), which iteratively refines the policy by training on self-generated win-lose pairs. Our investigation, however, reveals a critical instability in SPPO: the optimization is prone to policy degeneration when the preference oracle assigns overly confident wins to semantically indistinguishable responses.
