The extractor achieves Cohen's kappa of 0.79 on the skill slot, with 100% schema conformance and 100% document-level completeness. The alignment surfaces interpretable supply-demand gaps of 25.0% in general and transversal skills, 13.8% in algorithms and computational theory, and 12.2% in software engineering and project management, with a near-zero 1.8% gap in artificial intelligence and data science despite 38.6% supply coverage.

</details>


### [135] [SafeMCP: Proactive Power Regulation for LLM Agent Defense via Environment-Grounded Look-Ahead Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01991)
*Lichao Wang,Zhaoxing Ren,Tianzhuo Yang,Jiaming Ji,Chi Harold Liu,Yaodong Yang,Juntao Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeMCP is a server-side defense plugin that uses predictive reasoning to constrain unsafe tool acquisition by LLM agents, preventing catastrophic failures while maintaining task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents expand their action spaces through Model Context Protocol (MCP), they gain unsafe capabilities and exhibit power-seeking behavior, creating a fragile risk surface where minor errors can lead to catastrophic failures.

Method: SafeMCP employs a two-tier defense: proactive tool filtering using an internal world model for look-ahead reasoning, and immediate intervention as a fail-safe. It is trained through a three-stage pipeline of environmental dynamic grounding, safe policy initialization, and reinforcement learning with dual verifiable rewards.

Result: Experiments on PowerSeeking Bench, ToolEmu, and AgentHarm benchmarks demonstrate that SafeMCP achieves a safe equilibrium, effectively mitigating risks while preserving agent utility.

Conclusion: SafeMCP provides an effective server-side defense mechanism for LLM agents operating with MCP, successfully balancing safety with utility through proactive risk prediction and intervention.

Abstract: As Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly leverage the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to operate in complex environments, the expansion of their action spaces offers agents unsafe capabilities and underscores the risk of power-seeking. While broad action space and greater environment influence are essential for task fulfillment, they create a fragile risk surface where minor errors or hallucinations are magnified into catastrophic failures. In response, we propose SafeMCP, a {server-side} defense plugin that constrains tool acquisition via predictive reasoning regarding future safety risks. SafeMCP utilizes an internal world model for look-ahead reasoning to implement a two-tier defense: proactive tool filtering to constrain hazardous power expansion and immediate intervention as a fail-safe. To train SafeMCP, we introduce a three-stage pipeline comprising environmental dynamic grounding, safe policy initialization, and reinforcement learning (RL) with dual verifiable rewards. Experiments on PowerSeeking Bench, ToolEmu, and AgentHarm show that SafeMCP achieves a safe equilibrium, effectively mitigating risks while preserving agent utility.

</details>


### [136] [Extreme Low-Bit Inference in Reasoning Models: Failure Modes and Targeted Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02011)
*Ekaterina Alimaskina,Darya Rudas,Denis Shveykin,Gleb Molodtsov,Pavel Vasiliev,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: 2-bit quantization fails due to reasoning trace instability, not just lower accuracy. Lightweight FP16 planning and loop rescue mechanisms fix process failures, making 2-bit inference practical with recovered accuracy and preserved speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While low-bit quantization reduces per-token decoding cost, aggressive 2-bit inference fails to deliver end-to-end speedup because instability inflates token count through process-level failures like repetitive loops, budget exhaustion, and delayed commitment.

Method: Two lightweight controls: 1) FP16 planning - giving the 2-bit model a short high-precision outline; 2) Loop rescue - detecting repetitive traces and either committing to earlier answers or falling back to FP16.

Result: On MATH-500, loop rescue improved Qwen3-8B accuracy from 17.2% to 74.2%, and planning plus loop rescue improved Qwen3-32B from 65.0% to 87.2%. The approach makes 2-bit inference practical while preserving real speed gains.

Conclusion: Extreme low-bit (2-bit) reasoning becomes practical when its generation failures are treated as controllable pathologies. By adding lightweight detection mechanisms and selective FP16 support, 2-bit inference can recover accuracy while preserving end-to-end speed gains.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) rely on long reasoning traces, making inference expensive. While low-bit quantization reduces per-token decoding cost, we show that aggressive 2-bit inference can fail to deliver end-to-end speedup because instability in the generation process inflates total token count. Instead of merely lowering answer accuracy, 2-bit quantization often produces much longer traces with repetitive loops, budget exhaustion, delayed commitment, and unclosed reasoning segments. We analyze full reasoning traces of Qwen3 reasoning models across mathematical and commonsense benchmarks and show that accuracy degradation is tightly linked to these process-level failures. To address them, we introduce two lightweight controls: FP16 planning, which gives the 2-bit model a short high-precision outline, and loop rescue, which detects repetitive traces and either commits to an earlier answer or falls back to FP16. On MATH-500, loop rescue improves Qwen3-8B accuracy from 17.2% to 74.2%, while planning plus loop rescue improves Qwen3-32B from 65.0% to 87.2%. Overall, our results show that extreme low-bit reasoning becomes practical when its failures are treated as controllable generation pathologies: with lightweight detection and selective FP16 support, 2-bit inference can recover accuracy while preserving real end-to-end speed. Our code is available at: https://github.com/brain-lab-research/quantized-reasoning.

</details>


### [137] [RL-ACRGNet: Reinforcement Learning-Based Chest Radiology Report Generation Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02035)
*Yogesh Kumar Meena,Saurabh Agarwal,K. V. Arya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RL-ACRGNet uses DenseNet encoder, multilevel LSTM decoder, and reinforcement learning to generate accurate radiology reports, outperforming state-of-the-art models on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual radiology report generation is time-consuming and prone to inconsistencies. Medical AI promises to streamline workflows but faces challenges in accurate disease detection and clinically coherent report generation due to limitations in capturing fine-grained visual features.

Method: Proposes RL-ACRGNet, an encoder-decoder model integrating a pretrained DenseNet encoder with a multilevel LSTM decoder within an off-policy reinforcement learning framework. Uses dual-network approach with metric-based reward mechanism to refine visual-semantic embeddings.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on IU-Xray dataset with improvements in BLEU-4 (0.47%), METEOR (0.17%) and ROUGE-L (0.518). Shows robust generalization on large-scale MIMIC-CXR dataset, generating high-quality clinically relevant reports.

Conclusion: RL-ACRGNet represents a significant advance in automated radiology report generation, demonstrating robust generalization across datasets and consistently outperforming state-of-the-art models through its sophisticated integration of pretrained vision encoders, multilevel language decoders, and metric-driven reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Medical imaging interpretation is a foundational pillar of modern clinical diagnostics, yet the manual generation of radiology reports remains a time-consuming process prone to interpretation inconsistencies. Within the field of medical AI, automating these descriptions through deep learning promises to streamline clinical workflows and standardise diagnostic output. However, accurate disease detection and precise report generation remain significant challenges due to limitations in capturing fine-grained visual features and ensuring clinical coherence. To address these issues, we propose RL-ACRGNet, an improved encoder-decoder model that integrates a pre-trained DenseNet encoder with a multilevel LSTM decoder within an off-policy reinforcement learning framework. Using a dual-network approach to refine visual-semantic embeddings through a metric-based reward mechanism, we demonstrate that RL-ACRGNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the IU-Xray dataset, achieving quantitative improvements in BLEU-4 (0.47%), METEOR (0.17%) and ROUGE-L (0.518). Furthermore, comprehensive evaluations on the large-scale MIMIC-CXR data set confirm the robust generalisation of the model and its ability to generate high-quality, clinically relevant reports

</details>


### [138] [Topological texture analysis of microscopy images of dynamic casein gelation and its relation to rheological properties](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02048)
*Zahra Tabatabaei,Diana Soto Aguilar,Jose C. Bonilla,Mathias P. Clausen,Jon Sporring*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel computational toolbox combining TDA, DBC, MFP, and LBP quantitatively characterizes protein gelation microstructure transitions, revealing topological phases undetectable by bulk rheology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a computational toolbox for characterizing subtle microstructural transitions in protein gelation that bulk rheology captures only as averaged mechanical responses.

Method: Integration of Topological Data Analysis (TDA), Differential Box Counting (DBC), Multifractal Partition (MFP), and Local Binary Patterns (LBP) applied to time-lapse super-resolution STED microscopy images.

Result: TDA revealed distinct topological phases (lag phase, sharp decay during percolation, post-gelation increase) corresponding to protein network dynamics. DBC and MFP corroborated these transitions by resolving structural complexity and heterogeneity changes.

Conclusion: The integrated toolbox provides a robust quantitative approach for characterizing complex evolving microstructures in food and material science applications, overcoming limitations of bulk measurements.

Abstract: We propose a novel computational toolbox that integrates Topological Data Analysis (TDA), Differential Box Counting (DBC), Multifractal Partition (MFP), and Local Binary Patterns (LBP), applied to time-lapse super-resolution STED microscopy images of sodium caseinate gelation induced by glucono-delta-lactone (GDL) at 30 °C and 40 °C and two GDL concentrations (1.8% and 3.5% w/v).
  TDA tracked topological loops, closed ring-like structures reflecting protein network interconnectivity, via max-Betti-1 curves, which revealed a lag phase of dispersed aggregates, a sharp decay coinciding with network percolation and the rheologically observed sol-gel transition, and a post-gelation increase corresponding to network rearrangements. These topological transitions were corroborated by DBC and MFP as these methods were able to resolve changes in structural complexity and spatial heterogeneity. The toolbox was validated on simulated fractal images prior to experimental application. Together, these descriptors provided sensitivity to subtle microstructural transitions that bulk rheology captured as averaged bulk mechanical responses. This integrated approach provides a robust quantitative tool for characterizing complex microstructure in food and material science with evolving microstructural dynamics. Code is available at https://github.com/Zahratabatabaei/Delifood_CV_paper.git

</details>


### [139] [Explainable Data-driven Deep Reinforcement Learning Methods for Optimal Energy Management in Buildings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02049)
*Hallah Shahid Butt,Qiong Huang,Gökhan Demirel,Kevin Förderer,Erfan Tajalli-Ardekani,Simnon Waczowicz,Luigi Spatafora,Veit Hagenmeyer,Benjamin Schäfer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposed explainable deep reinforcement learning framework for residential energy management that reduces costs and provides transparent insights into agent decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing renewable energy integration (PV, storage) creates complex energy systems requiring optimization, but DRL's black-box nature hinders user trust and practical adoption, necessitating explainable solutions.

Method: Proposed an XRL framework for residential energy management, training both on-policy (A2C, PPO) and off-policy DRL agents on expanded state space with real-time measurements, external signals, calendrical data, and forecasts, then applying post-hoc interpretation techniques.

Result: On-policy algorithms (A2C, PPO) outperformed off-policy methods in cumulative rewards and stability; XRL framework reduced electricity costs via optimal battery management while providing transparent decision-making insights.

Conclusion: The XRL framework successfully bridges the gap between high-performing DRL agents and practical adoption by providing transparent, explainable energy management that reduces electricity costs while building user trust.

Abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources into power systems, particularly in buildings equipped with photovoltaic (PV) panels and energy storage systems, introduces significant complexity in energy systems. Volatile power generation, varying electricity tariffs, and increased entities, e.g., PV systems, and heat pumps, have increased the complexity and made the system harder to operate. This leads to the demand for additional control and optimization routes including data-based controls, such as reinforcement learning. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has emerged as a promising solution to optimize building operations in dynamic and ever more complex environments, its black-box nature impedes user trust and practical adoption. This paper presents a framework for explainable deep reinforcement learning (XRL) applied to energy management in residential buildings. We demonstrate its usage on both synthetic data but also on real-world data from the Living Lab Energy Campus (LLEC) at KIT. We train and compare both on-policy and off-policy DRL agents on an expanded state space that incorporates real-time measurements (demand, PV generation, battery power, state of charge), external signals (dynamic electricity price, local weather data), calendrical and holiday indicators, and forecasts for demand and price. Our experimental results indicate that on-policy algorithms, particularly Advantage Actor Critic (A2C) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), outperform off-policy methods in terms of cumulative rewards and policy stability. To explain these models, we employ post-hoc interpretation techniques to elaborate the learned control policies. Our findings demonstrate that the XRL framework not only reduces electricity costs through optimal battery management, but also provides transparent, actionable insights into the agent's decision-making process.

</details>


### [140] [eMoT: evolving Memory-of-Thought via Symbolic Anchoring and Memory Corrosion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02054)
*Xiang Li,Jiwei Wei,Ke Liu,Yitong Qin,Jinyu Guo,Malu Zhang,Peng Wang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes eMoT framework that treats reasoning as evolving memories to improve LLM reliability on multi-step tasks, achieving 100% accuracy on Game of 24 and consistent gains on mathematical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLMs suffer from unconstrained hallucinations and poor numerical computation in multi-step reasoning tasks. These limitations stem from treating reasoning as a transient, one-off generation process rather than retaining and refining successful procedural logic.

Method: The eMoT framework comprises three modules: (1) memory corrosion mechanism that reinforces high-utility reasoning structures while decaying less frequent ones; (2) symbolic anchoring engine using Python for deterministic computation; (3) consistency-driven refinement process aligning neural inference with symbolic outcomes.

Result: eMoT achieves 100% accuracy on Game of 24 (17.6% improvement over baseline) and shows consistent gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, ASDiv, SVAMP, MGSM). Superior performance is achieved even with lightweight backbone models, demonstrating gains come from reasoning control rather than model size.

Conclusion: The eMoT framework significantly improves the accuracy and consistency of multi-step reasoning in LLMs by treating reasoning as evolving memories rather than static templates. The approach demonstrates that reasoning control mechanisms are more crucial for performance than sheer model scale.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve impressive performance on multi-step reasoning tasks, their reliability is persistently hindered by critical limitations such as unconstrained hallucinations and poor numerical computation. Fundamentally, these issues arise because standard models treat reasoning as a transient, one-off generation process rather than retaining and refining successful procedural logic. To address these challenges, we propose eMoT (evolving Memory-of-Thought), a unified framework that stabilizes multi-step reasoning by treating reasoning trajectories as dynamic, evolving memories rather than static templates. The framework primarily consists of three interconnected modules: (i) a memory corrosion mechanism that reinforces high-utility reasoning structures while gradually decaying less frequent ones; (ii) a symbolic anchoring engine that utilizes Python for deterministic computation, much like a human uses a calculator; and (iii) a consistency-driven refinement process that aligns neural inference with symbolic outcomes, reducing the accumulation of logical discrepancies. Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, eMoT improves accuracy and solution consistency over standard Chain-of-Thought and structured reasoning baselines.On the traditional task Game of 24, eMoT achieves 100% accuracy, surpassing the baseline by up to 17.6%. Evaluations on mathematical task GSM8K, ASDiv, SVAMP, and MGSM further show consistent gains in multi-step mathematical reasoning. In our evaluation, we achieve superior performance despite utilizing a lightweight backbone model with constrained baseline capabilities. Compared to alternative methods that rely on massively scaled models, our results demonstrate that the performance gains are fundamentally driven by the eMoT framework's reasoning control rather than sheer model size.

</details>


### [141] [Where Do Deep-Research Agents Go Wrong? Span-Level Error Localization in Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02060)
*Jiaming Wang,Ziteng Feng,Jiangtao Wu,Ruihao Li,Qianqian Xie,Yuxiang Ren,He Zhu,Xueming Han,Fanyu Meng,Junlan Feng,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces TELBench benchmark and DRIFT framework for identifying error spans in deep-research agent trajectories, improving error localization by 30%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond binary success/failure evaluation and identify which parts of agent trajectories make answers unreliable.

Method: Collected 2,790 real trajectories from multiple agent frameworks, converted logs to semantic spans, annotated error spans via LLM-assisted expert review, built TELBench benchmark, and proposed DRIFT framework for claim-centric auditing.

Result: DRIFT improves span-level error localization and first-error accuracy by up to 30 percentage points across model families.

Conclusion: The work provides a process-level view of reliability in deep-research agents through span-level error analysis.

Abstract: Deep-research agents solve tasks through long trajectories of search, tool use, evidence inspection, and answer synthesis. Evaluation based on final answers shows whether an agent succeeds, but not which parts of the trajectory make the answer unreliable. We study span-level error localization for deep-research agents. We collect 2,790 real trajectories from two agent frameworks, three backbone models, and three benchmarks, convert raw logs into semantic spans, and annotate harmful error spans through LLM-assisted expert review. From these annotations, we build TELBench, a 1,000-instance benchmark for identifying error spans among normal exploration, failed searches, tentative hypotheses, and harmless noise. We further propose DRIFT, a claim-centric auditing framework that tracks agent claims, checks their support in trajectory evidence, and marks spans where unsupported or conflicting claims affect the answer path. Experiments across model families and auditing frameworks show that DRIFT improves span-level error localization and first-error accuracy by up to 30 percentage points. Our work provides a process-level view of reliability in deep-research agents.

</details>


### [142] [BADGER: Bridging Agentic and Deterministic Evaluation for Generative Enterprise Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02109)
*Shannon Serrao,Soumitra Chatterjee,Dorina Strori,Abhishek Sharma,Nathan Miller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BADGER is a unified evaluation framework that integrates text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation, featuring LLM-assisted SQL extraction, hybrid accuracy metrics, and enterprise agentic evaluation, validated to outperform existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frameworks lack unification of text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation into a production-grade pipeline calibrated against human expert judgment, creating a gap for enterprise AI systems that need comprehensive evaluation approaches.

Method: 1) LLM-assisted SQL component extraction extending Spider methodology, 2) Hybrid execution accuracy metric (Hybrid-EX) using LLM to infer structural alignments before deterministic cell-level scoring, 3) Enterprise agentic evaluation suite assembling RAGAS, G-Eval, and agent benchmark metrics

Result: Hybrid-EX achieves Cohen's kappa=0.717 (Substantial agreement) and 87.3% balanced accuracy on 150 human-annotated industry queries, outperforming all six competing frameworks (Delta-kappa: 0.322-0.502, all p<=0.001). BADGER supports rapid prototyping of client-specific judges and metrics.

Conclusion: BADGER provides a comprehensive, production-grade evaluation framework that unifies text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation, serving as a continuous evaluation backbone rather than a one-time quality gate, and operates entirely within clients' governed data environments with support for configurable LLM judge backends.

Abstract: Enterprise AI systems that translate natural language into SQL queries and orchestrate multi-step agentic reasoning pipelines require evaluation approaches fundamentally different from academic benchmarks. Spider and BIRD established execution-accuracy protocols; G-Eval and RAGAS advanced LLM-based assessment; and recent work such as Spider 2.0, BEAVER, and BIRD-Interact has begun to address enterprise and agentic dimensions. No single framework unifies text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation into a production-grade pipeline calibrated against human expert judgment.
  We present BADGER, developed at Merkle, a unified evaluation framework integrating text-to-SQL assessment with agentic behavior evaluation. BADGER offers three contributions. First, LLM-assisted SQL component extraction extending Spider methodology to handle CTE-heavy, dialect-specific SQL. Second, a hybrid execution accuracy metric (Hybrid-EX) resolving column-aliasing and numeric-tolerance brittleness by using an LLM to infer structural alignments before deterministic cell-level scoring. Validated on 150 human-annotated industry queries, Hybrid-EX achieves Cohen's kappa=0.717 [95% CI: 0.600-0.822] (Substantial agreement) and 87.3% balanced accuracy, outperforming all six competing frameworks (Delta-kappa: 0.322-0.502, all p<=0.001). Third, an enterprise agentic evaluation suite assembling RAGAS, G-Eval, and agent benchmark metrics into a unified pipeline; Excess Tool Usage is the sole novel element.
  BADGER runs entirely within the client's governed data environment, supports configurable LLM judge backends, and enables rapid prototyping of client-specific judges and metrics, serving as a continuous evaluation backbone rather than a one-time quality gate.

</details>


### [143] [Learning When Not to Act: Mitigating Tool Abuse in Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02132)
*Liuji Chen,Dianxing Tang,Xing Shi,Dingshuo Chen,Qiang Liu,Shu Wu,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EAPO framework learns selective tool use by incorporating tool-free trajectories, difficulty-aware reward shaping, and confidence-aware token reweighting to reduce redundant tool calls while maintaining reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches mitigate tool abuse with uniform tool-use penalties or hard limits, which reduce tool frequency but may suppress useful tool-assisted exploration.

Method: EAPO (Efficient Agentic Policy Optimization) framework with three key components: 1) Tool-free trajectories in each rollout group, 2) Difficulty-aware reward shaping to penalize redundant tool calls mainly on easier queries, and 3) Confidence-aware token reweighting to improve policy learning.

Result: Across nine mathematical and knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks, EAPO consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off on Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, and Llama3.1-8B. Compared with GRPO, EAPO improves average performance by 10.45%, 7.27%, and 9.69%, while reducing average tool calls by 18.33%, 18.33%, and 24.59%, respectively.

Conclusion: Agents can learn when not to use tools without compromising tool-integrated reasoning, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs through selective tool use.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning can induce tool abuse, where models overuse external tools even for queries solvable by internal reasoning. Existing approaches mitigate this issue with uniform tool-use penalties or hard limits, which reduce tool frequency but may also suppress useful tool-assisted exploration. We propose EAPO, an Efficient Agentic Policy Optimization framework that learns selective tool use. EAPO introduces tool-free trajectories into each rollout group, applies difficulty-aware reward shaping to penalize redundant tool calls mainly on easier queries, and uses confidence-aware token reweighting to improve policy learning. Across nine mathematical and knowledge-intensive reasoning benchmarks, EAPO consistently improves the accuracy efficiency trade-off on Qwen2.5-3B, Qwen2.5-7B, and Llama3.1-8B. Compared with GRPO, EAPO improves average performance by 10.45%, 7.27%, and 9.69%, while reducing average tool calls by 18.33%, 18.33%, and 24.59%, respectively. These results show that agents can learn when not to use tools without compromising tool-integrated reasoning.

</details>


### [144] [S3TS: Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search for Advanced Planning Under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02151)
*Fabio Pavirani,Bert Claessens,Pierre Pinson,Chris Develder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: New S3TS algorithm bridges gap between non-linearity and uncertainty handling in energy scheduling, outperforming baselines in both linear and non-linear scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches in energy scheduling either address non-linearity (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search) or uncertainty (e.g., stochastic optimization), but no technique effectively handles both challenges simultaneously.

Method: Proposed Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search (S3TS) algorithm that uses scenario trees to represent uncertainty while integrating advanced non-linear models.

Result: In linear settings: near-optimal performance with costs within 14% of mathematically optimal solution. In non-linear scenarios: achieved cost reductions of up to 51% vs myopic algorithm and 5.4% vs deterministic MCTS.

Conclusion: The S3TS algorithm is a promising approach for energy scheduling problems that require handling both uncertainty and non-linearity simultaneously, demonstrating strong performance across different problem complexities.

Abstract: Effective scheduling in the energy sector is essential to ensure the reliable operation of electrical grids and their connected assets by, for instance, optimizing the dispatch of generation units and storage systems. An effective planning strategy must (a) accommodate advanced and potentially non-linear system models -- exploiting the increasing data availability of modern grids, and (b) explicitly handle uncertainties arising, for instance, from the integration of renewable energy sources. While existing approaches can address either non-linearity (e.g., Monte Carlo Tree Search) or uncertainty (e.g., stochastic mathematical optimization), there is a lack of planning techniques capable of addressing both challenges simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we propose a Stochastic Scenario-Structured Tree Search (S3TS) algorithm that explicitly represents uncertainty through scenario trees while enabling the integration of advanced non-linear models. We evaluate S3TS on a simulated demand response signal publication problem, largely mimicking the imbalance settlement mechanism in Belgium. The results demonstrate near-optimal performance in linear, analytically tractable settings, with costs within 14% of the mathematically optimal solution conditioned to the scenario trees. In highly non-linear scenarios, S3TS significantly outperforms baseline methods, achieving cost reductions of up to 51% and 5.4% compared to a myopic algorithm and deterministic MCTS, respectively.

</details>


### [145] [An Abstract Worlds Semantic Framework for Belief Change Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02163)
*Daniel Grimaldi,M. Vanina Martinez,Ricardo O. Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AWS offers a set-theoretic belief change framework using worlds as primitives, unifying AGM, KM and Multiple Change models without logical syntax.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a framework that unifies diverse belief change models (AGM, KM, Multiple Change) without relying on logical syntax, inspired by Grove's approach to treating worlds as fundamental elements.

Method: Defined world contraction and world revision operators over worlds as primitive elements, allowing versatile operator definitions within the set-theoretic framework.

Result: Achieved homogeneous account of classical propositional logic-based belief change models (AGM, KM, Multiple Change) and unified both classical and non-prioritized belief change constructions through versatile operators.

Conclusion: Abstract Worlds Semantics provides a unified, systematic framework for belief change theories that simplifies and generalizes the field by using worlds as primitive elements rather than logical syntax.

Abstract: This article proposes a set-theoretic framework for belief change, called Abstract Worlds Semantics, in which no logical syntax is assumed. Inspired by Grove's (1988) results, our approach treats worlds as primitive elements, over which world contraction and world revision operators are defined. This semantic framework enables a unified analysis of belief change models. Within this framework, we unify classical and non-prioritized belief change constructions by defining versatile operators. When classical propositional logic is considered, our framework provides a homogeneous account of AGM, KM, and Multiple Change models. In summary, AWS systematizes belief change frameworks and operators, simplifying and generalizing belief change theory over belief sets.

</details>


### [146] [From Capability Models to Automated Planning: An AAS-Native Approach for Automatic PDDL Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02167)
*Hamied Nabizada,Thomas Wirt,Luis Miguel Vieira da Silva,Felix Gehlhoff,Alexander Fay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automated generation of PDDL planning problems from Asset Administration Shells enables production engineers to verify layouts without PDDL expertise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production engineers need to verify layout supports production sequences but lack specialized PDDL expertise required for automated planning, while Asset Administration Shells provide standardized Digital Twins.

Method: Extraction algorithm transforms distributed Multi-AAS architectures with four established Industry 4.0 standards into complete PDDL planning problems automatically.

Result: Validated on laboratory production system AAS models, comparing four layout variants using optimal planning to demonstrate systematic design exploration.

Conclusion: The approach enables engineers to systematically explore production system design trade-offs through Digital Twin models without requiring PDDL expertise, bridging the gap between production engineering and automated planning.

Abstract: Engineers designing production systems need to verify that a given layout supports all required production sequences. Automated planning techniques can answer such questions, but formulating the required planning problems in the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) demands specialized expertise that production engineers typically lack. Asset Administration Shells (AAS) have emerged as the standardized Digital Twin for industrial assets in Industry 4.0. We show that AAS capability models, structured using four established Industry 4.0 standards (VDI 3682 for process descriptions, IEC 61360-1 for semantic property qualification, IDTA 02011 for type hierarchies, and IDTA 02016 for instance descriptions), contain sufficient information to generate complete PDDL problems automatically. Unlike prior work that introduced PDDL-specific submodels, our approach derives all planning elements from domain-level descriptions of resource functions, so-called capabilities, allowing engineers to model capabilities without any exposure to PDDL syntax or planning concepts. Our extraction algorithm transforms distributed Multi-AAS architectures into complete PDDL planning problems. We validate the approach on AAS models of a laboratory production system, comparing four layout variants using optimal planning to demonstrate how engineers can systematically explore design trade-offs by modifying the AAS model and regenerating the planning domain

</details>


### [147] [CEON: Circular Economy Ontology Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02253)
*Huanyu Li,Els de Vleeschauwer,Robin Keskisärkkä,Mikael Lindecrantz,Mina Abd Nikooie Pour,Ying Li,Ben De Meester,Patrick Lambrix,Eva Blomqvist*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Developed CEON ontology network to address circular economy knowledge representation challenges and enable semantic interoperability across industry sectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Knowledge representation for circular economy domain remains challenging despite its importance for semantic interoperability in information sharing across product life cycles and industry sectors.

Method: Development of the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) within the Onto-DESIDE project to define cross-sectorial concepts and enable semantics-aware data documentation.

Result: CEON demonstrated through cross-industry data documentation scenarios spanning construction, electronics, and textile sectors, enabling better information sharing and communication.

Conclusion: CEON ontology network successfully bridges gaps in circular economy knowledge representation by defining cross-sectorial concepts and enabling semantics-aware data documentation across construction, electronics, and textile industries.

Abstract: Increasing the circularity of resource use in our society has been recognized as a path to sustainability, i.e., transitioning into a more circular economy. There are many different circular strategies to do so, such as reusing products and components, refurbishing and remanufacturing used products, or recycling left-over or used materials. To enable these strategies, it is necessary to share information at the infrastructure level and to communicate between industry sectors along the product life cycle. Enabling semantic interoperability in this information sharing and communication is therefore a key to increasing circularity. However, knowledge representation for the circular economy (CE) domain, which involves many relevant industry sectors related to product life cycles, remains challenging. To bridge this gap, we developed the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) within the Onto-DESIDE project. This ontology network aims to fill gaps in CE by defining cross-sectorial concepts and to enable semantics-aware data documentation. We demonstrate CEON through cross-industry data documentation scenarios spanning construction, electronics, and textile sectors.

</details>


### [148] [POIROT: Interrogating Agents for Failure Detection in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02282)
*Iñaki Dellibarda Varela,R. Sendra-Arranz,Pablo Romero-Sorozabal,J. M. Valverde-García,Annemarie F. Laudanski,Álvaro Gutiérrez,Eduardo Rocon,Manuel Cebrian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POIROT transforms multi-agent systems' own agents into a diagnostic layer for fault attribution, outperforming external evaluators and scaling with system complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation paradigms for Large Language Model Multi-Agent Systems (LLM-MAS) have critical flaws: centralized judgment creates single points of failure and requires domain-specific expertise. This prevents deployment in safety-critical domains, especially given emerging AI regulation that makes such failures legally untenable.

Method: POIROT is a protocol that repurposes a multi-agent system's own agents as its diagnostic layer. It leverages the existing epistemic diversity present in the architecture rather than relying on external, centralized evaluation. The approach involves agents auditing each other's roles and contributions within the system.

Result: POIROT outperforms single-LLM evaluator baselines across multiple settings, with gains that scale with problem complexity (OR = 1.60, p = 0.008), agent count, and fault dimensionality. The improvements persist even under compound fault conditions.

Conclusion: Safety oversight in multi-agent systems can be effectively performed by leveraging the system's own agents rather than relying on external evaluators. POIROT demonstrates that internal diagnostic capabilities already exist within multi-agent architectures, offering a scalable and effective approach to fault attribution in safety-critical domains.

Abstract: Orchestrating Large Language Models into Multi-Agent Systems (LLM-MAS) has unlocked remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet emergent failures and hallucinations that resist characterisation block their deployment in safety-critical domains -- a gap made legally untenable by emerging AI regulation. Existing evaluation paradigms share a common flaw: centralised judgment creates single points of failure and demands domain-specific expertise. Here we present POIROT, a protocol that repurposes a system's own agents as its diagnostic layer, leveraging the epistemic diversity already present in the architecture. Across evaluated settings, POIROT outperforms single-LLM evaluator baselines, with gains that scale with problem complexity (OR = 1.60, $p = 0.008$), agent count, and fault dimensionality, persisting under compound fault conditions. These results demonstrate that safety oversight need not be externalised: the agents executing a role carry sufficient collective intelligence to audit it. We release POIROT as an open-source library alongside BLAME, a benchmark for fault attribution in safety-critical multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [149] [Repair Before Veto: Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning for Contextual Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02326)
*Yifan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RACL integrates repair operators into classifier semantics to repair before vetoing, reducing false vetoes significantly compared to terminal-veto baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard constraints as terminal vetoes miss common deployed regimes with finite menu of modifications. Existing methods don't learn whether an option should be repaired before being vetoed.

Method: Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning (RACL), a contextual decision framework that lifts known repair operators into classifier semantics. It accepts candidates when affordable repair makes them feasible and preferred enough; otherwise returns structured rejection credit and repair plan.

Result: RACL reduces false vetoes to 10/4039 (FVR 0.0025) on hardest raw-data-derived tier, versus about 1064/4039 for strongest repair-search black-box baseline. It makes the FVR/EDR trade-off explicit and recovers intended credit and repair structure.

Conclusion: RACL is an effective framework that reduces false vetoes while making cost-benefit trade-offs explicit. It performs significantly better than terminal-veto baselines on real-world benchmark.

Abstract: Hard constraints are usually treated as terminal vetoes: once a candidate violates a requirement, the learned rule rejects it and any repair is handled outside the decision semantics. This misses a common deployed regime in which the system already knows a finite menu of modifications, such as adding a ticket option, changing a configuration, or requesting an available service upgrade. Existing constraint-learning, soft-relaxation, and recourse methods address nearby problems, but they do not learn whether an option should be repaired before being vetoed. We introduce Repair-Augmented Constraint Learning (RACL), a contextual decision framework that lifts known repair operators into the classifier semantics. A candidate is accepted when an affordable repair makes it feasible and preferred enough; otherwise the system returns a structured rejection credit and, when applicable, a repair plan. This repair-before-veto view strictly generalizes no-repair HASSLE-style semantics, reveals an irreducible false-veto gap for terminal-veto rules, separates binary-label non-identifiability from decision-rule learnability, and gives capacity and calibration bounds for the observed-feasibility shared-weight setting. Across controlled and DB1B-derived benchmarks, RACL recovers the intended credit and repair structure. On the hardest raw-data-derived tier, validation-selected RACL reduces false vetoes to 10/4039 (FVR 0.0025), versus about 1064/4039 for the strongest repair-search black-box baseline, while making the FVR/EDR trade-off explicit.

</details>


### [150] [Forget Attention: Importance-Aware Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02332)
*Soohyeong Shin,Yeongwook Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: true


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: true

Method: true

Result: true

Conclusion: true

Abstract: Combining attention's global retrieval with the sequential importance signal of state space models (SSMs) is the open challenge of hybrid language modeling. Transformers see everywhere but cannot prioritize; SSMs know what matters but cannot revisit. Existing hybrids -- Jamba (block level) and Hymba (head level) -- place the two in separate compartments, so neither informs the other during the attention computation itself. We propose SISA (SSM-Informed Softmax Attention), which adds an SSM-derived importance term directly inside the attention score and realizes the full operation as a single SDPA call on augmented query/key vectors -- no recurrent state, no custom kernel. At 152M / 5B tokens, SISA reaches LAMBADA-greedy 17.3% (vs. Transformer 13.9 and Mamba-3 15.5) and attains NIAH 100% from step 1K, 7x faster than Transformer's retrieval convergence; at 369M, Mamba-3 leads LAMBADA while SISA preserves perfect NIAH and stock-SDPA execution. SISA thus defines a third design axis for SSM-attention hybrids -- score-level fusion -- beyond the block-level and head-level paradigms that have dominated the field.

</details>


### [151] [Coordination Graphs for Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02337)
*Santiago Amaya-Corredor,Miguel Calvo-Fullana,Anders Jonsson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CG-CMARL framework combines coordination graphs with Lagrangian duality to solve constrained multi-agent RL challenges by decomposing joint problems and enabling scalable Pareto optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome exponential growth of joint action space and constraint coupling that reward structure alone doesn't capture in multi-agent systems.

Method: Combines coordination graphs with Lagrangian duality, decomposing joint problems into pairwise regions with shared Q-functions and using Max-Sum message passing for coordination.

Result: Method produces Pareto fronts dominating established baselines while scaling to team sizes where centralized approaches become intractable.

Conclusion: CG-CMARL effectively addresses exponential joint action space growth and constraint coupling in CMARL by combining coordination graphs with Lagrangian duality, providing scalable solutions for multi-agent systems.

Abstract: Constrained Multi-agent reinforcement learning (CMARL) faces two intertwined challenges: the joint action space grows exponentially with the number of agents, and additional requirements couple agents in ways that reward structure alone does not capture. We introduce Coordination Graphs for Constrained Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (CG-CMARL), a framework that addresses both challenges by combining coordination graphs with Lagrangian duality. The system decomposes the joint problem into pairwise regions, each served by a set of shared Q-functions, one for the primary objective and one for each of the constraints, so that the number of learned models is independent of the number of agents. At execution time, Max-Sum message passing coordinates actions across the factor graph, while a Lagrangian multiplier controls the objective--constraint tradeoff, allowing a single trained model to trace a Pareto front without retraining. We provide convergence guarantees under mild conditions, together with a compositional error bound that decomposes into separate interpretable sources, each traceable to a specific design choice and independently controllable. Experiments on cooperative navigation tasks (where teams of up to 10 agents must coordinate to reach target positions while satisfying pairwise constraints) show that our method produces Pareto fronts dominating established baselines trained at fixed reward-shaping ratios, while scaling to team sizes where centralized approaches become intractable.

</details>


### [152] [SIRI: Self-Internalizing Reinforcement Learning with Intrinsic Skills for LLM Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02355)
*Zhongyu He,Yuanfan Li,Fei Huang,Tianyu Chen,Siyuan Chen,Xingyang Li,Meng Hsuan Yu,Xiangrong Liu,Leyi Wei,Lu Pan,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SIRI is a self-contained reinforcement learning framework that enables LLM agents to discover, validate, and internalize skills without external generators or inference-time retrieval, improving performance on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill-based LLM agent methods rely on external skill generators during training or persistent skill retrieval at inference, which increases engineering complexity, context length, and deployment latency. There's a need for a more self-contained approach.

Method: Three-phase framework: 1) Policy warm-up with GiGPO to collect successful skill-free trajectories, 2) Self-skill mining where the policy summarizes skills from its own successful rollouts and validates them via paired skill-augmented/skill-free rollouts, 3) Distillation of beneficial skill-guided action tokens into the plain policy using trajectory-level utility and action-level advantage.

Result: SIRI improves GiGPO from 0.908 to 0.930 on ALFWorld and from 0.728 to 0.813 on WebShop using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, outperforming prompt-based, RL-based, and memory-augmented baselines. Self-mining strategy achieves performance comparable to distillation with closed-source large models.

Conclusion: SIRI eliminates the need for external skill generators and inference-time skill banks while achieving state-of-the-art performance, offering a more practical and efficient approach to skill-based LLM agents.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents can benefit from reusable skills, yet existing skill-based methods often rely on external skill generators during training or persistent skill retrieval at inference, increasing engineering complexity, context length, and deployment latency. We propose Self-Internalizing Reinforcement learning with Intrinsic skills (SIRI), a three-phase framework that enables agents to discover, validate, and internalize skills without external skill generators or inference-time skill banks. SIRI first warms up the policy with GiGPO to acquire basic interaction ability and collect successful skill-free trajectories. It then performs self-skill mining, where the current policy summarizes compact skills from its own successful plain rollouts and validates them through paired skill-augmented and skill-free rollouts. Finally, SIRI distills only beneficial skill-guided action tokens into the plain policy using trajectory-level utility and action-level advantage. At inference, the agent runs with the original prompt only. On ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SIRI improves GiGPO from 0.908 to 0.930 on ALFWorld and from 0.728 to 0.813 on WebShop, outperforming prompt-based, RL-based, and memory-augmented baselines. Further analysis shows that our self-mining strategy can achieve performance comparable to distillation with closed-source large model. Our code is available at https://github.com/kirito618/SIRI.

</details>


### [153] [MOC: Multi-Order Communication in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02359)
*Yao Guan,Lin Wang,Zhihu Lu,Ziyi Wang,Wenzhu Yan,Qiang Duan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Multi-Order Communication (MOC) scheme for LLM multi-agent systems to improve message transmission by capturing multi-hop dependencies and optimizing semantic fidelity, outperforming existing methods across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems rely on direct concatenation of first-order neighbor responses, which creates restricted evidence receptive fields and dilutes crucial insights over multi-hop communication paths. There is a need to improve how messages are transmitted and optimized among agents beyond just coordination topology optimization.

Method: The paper proposes a Multi-Order Communication (MOC) scheme that reconstructs inter-agent communication to capture multi-hop dependencies and incorporates structural message consolidation for efficiency. This includes formalizing the communication mechanism to construct structured multi-order evidence streams and designing a Semantic-Topological Merging algorithm to optimize semantic fidelity within token constraints.

Result: Extensive experiments across six diverse datasets and LLM backbones of varying parameter scales demonstrate that MOC consistently improves task performance and reduces communication costs compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: The MOC scheme effectively addresses limitations in current LLM-based Multi-Agent System communication by capturing multi-hop dependencies through structured multi-order evidence streams and optimizing semantic fidelity, leading to consistent performance improvements and reduced communication costs across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable progress of Large Language Model (LLM) based Multi-Agent Systems, most research focuses on optimizing coordination topology while largely underexploring the equally critical problem: how to transmit and optimize messages among agents effectively? Current communication schemes typically rely on the direct concatenation of first-order neighbor responses, which induces a restricted evidence receptive field and leads to the dilution of crucial insights over multi-hop paths. To address these limitations, we propose the Multi-Order Communication (MOC) scheme, which reconstructs the inter-agent communication to capture multi-hop dependencies and incorporates a structural message consolidation strategy to ensure efficiency. Specifically, we formalize the communication mechanism to construct a structured multi-order evidence stream, and subsequently design a Semantic-Topological Merging algorithm to optimize semantic fidelity within token constraints. Extensive experiments across six diverse datasets and LLM backbones of varying parameter scales demonstrate that MOC consistently improves task performance and reduces communication costs. The code is available at https://github.com/yao-guan/MOC.

</details>


### [154] [COMAP: Co-Evolving World Models and Agent Policies for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02372)
*Youwei Liu,Jian Wang,Hanlin Wang,Wenjie Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COMAP co-evolves textual world models and agent policies through closed-loop interaction, enabling adaptive learning without external supervision and achieving substantial performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing textual world models are typically fixed after training and cannot adapt to evolving agent behaviors. Meanwhile, agent-improvement methods often rely on external rewards or verifiers, limiting applicability in realistic interactive environments where such supervision is scarce.

Method: At each decision step: 1) world model predicts future state feedback for candidate actions, 2) agent performs future-aware reflection by estimating feedback reliability and refining actions, 3) resulting on-policy trajectories update the world model via self-distillation to match the agent's evolving interaction distribution.

Result: COMAP consistently outperforms competitive baselines across embodied task planning, Web navigation, and tool-use benchmarks, achieving +16.75% relative improvement with Qwen3-4B. Analyses show the co-evolutionary loop improves world model prediction accuracy over time and enables more effective long-horizon decision-making.

Conclusion: COMAP presents a novel co-evolutionary framework where world models and agent policies improve each other through closed-loop interaction, enabling adaptive learning without external rewards and demonstrating significant performance gains across diverse interactive environments.

Abstract: Equipping language agents with world models enables them to anticipate environment dynamics and evaluate candidate actions before execution. However, existing textual world models are typically fixed after training, preventing them from adapting to the on-policy state-action distributions induced by an evolving agent. Meanwhile, agent-improvement methods often rely on external rewards or verifiers, limiting their applicability in realistic interactive environments. In this paper, we propose COMAP, a novel framework that co-evolves textual world models and agent policies through closed-loop interaction. At each decision step, the world model predicts future state feedback for candidate actions, and the agent performs future-aware reflection by estimating the reliability of this feedback and refining its action accordingly. The resulting on-policy trajectories are then used to update the world model via self-distillation, allowing it to better match the agent's evolving interaction distribution. Across embodied task planning, Web navigation, and tool-use benchmarks, COMAP consistently outperforms competitive baselines, e.g., +16.75% relative improvement with Qwen3-4B. Further analyses show that the co-evolutionary loop improves the world model's prediction accuracy over time and leads to more effective long-horizon decision-making. Our code is available at: https://github.com/loyiv/CoMAP.

</details>


### [155] [Harness-1: Reinforcement Learning for Search Agents with State-Externalizing Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02373)
*Pengcheng Jiang,Zhiyi Shi,Kelly Hong,Xueqiang Xu,Jiashuo Sun,Jimeng Sun,Hammad Bashir,Jiawei Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Harness-1 introduces a 20B parameter search agent trained with reinforcement learning inside a stateful harness that manages routine bookkeeping, allowing the policy to focus on semantic search decisions, achieving superior retrieval performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional search agents are trained as policies over growing transcripts, which forces them to handle both semantic search decisions and routine state management/bookkeeping. This approach requires reinforcement learning to optimize both types of tasks when the environment could handle the bookkeeping more reliably.

Method: The authors introduce Harness-1, a 20B search agent trained with reinforcement learning inside a stateful search harness. The harness maintains environment-side working memory including candidate pool, importance-tagged curated set, evidence links, verification records, compressed observations, and budget-aware context rendering. The policy focuses on semantic decisions: what to search, which documents to keep/discard, what to verify, and when to stop.

Result: Across eight retrieval benchmarks spanning web, finance, patents, and multi-hop QA, Harness-1 achieves 0.730 average curated recall, outperforming the next strongest open search subagent by +11.4 points and remaining competitive with much larger frontier-model searchers. The gains are especially strong on held-out transfer benchmarks.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning over explicit search state can produce retrieval behaviors that generalize beyond training domains. Separating semantic search decisions from routine state management through a stateful harness leads to more effective and generalizable search agents.

Abstract: Search agents are often trained as policies over growing transcripts: the model must decide how to search while also remembering what it has seen, which evidence is useful, which constraints remain open, and which claims have actually been checked. We argue that this formulation puts too much routine state management inside the policy: reinforcement learning is forced to optimize both semantic search decisions and recoverable bookkeeping that the environment can maintain more reliably. We introduce Harness-1, a 20B search agent (retrieval subagent) trained with reinforcement learning inside a stateful search harness. The harness maintains environment-side working memory, including a candidate pool, an importance-tagged curated set, compact evidence links, verification records, compressed and deduplicated observations, and budget-aware context rendering. The policy retains the semantic decisions: what to search, which documents to keep or discard, what to verify, and when to stop. Across eight retrieval benchmarks spanning web, finance, patents, and multi-hop QA, Harness-1 achieves 0.730 average curated recall, outperforming the next strongest open search subagent by +11.4 points and remaining competitive with much larger frontier-model searchers. Its gains are especially strong on held-out transfer benchmarks, suggesting that reinforcement learning over explicit search state can produce retrieval behaviors that generalize beyond the training domains. Our code is available at https://github.com/pat-jj/harness-1.

</details>


### [156] [Spatial Representation Learning Beyond Pixels: Unifying Raster Data and Vector Semantics for Human-Centric Geospatial Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02374)
*Steffen Knoblauch,Hao Li,Gengchen Mai,Konstantin Klemmer,Song Gao,WenWen Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This perspective paper calls for unified spatial representation learning that jointly integrates raster (imagery) and vector (structured) data modalities to create next-generation geospatial AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Earth Observation Foundation Models (EOFMs) focus only on raster data, missing rich structured information from vector sources like OpenStreetMap. These modalities represent complementary views - raster captures continuous physical patterns while vector encodes discrete objects and human systems - but current approaches treat them in isolation with lossy transformations.

Method: Proposes a paradigm shift toward joint Spatial Representation Learning (SRL) in a unified embedding space that integrates raster perception with vector-based reasoning. This builds on emerging multimodal geospatial learning approaches to align heterogeneous spatial data sources.

Result: Presents a conceptual framework highlighting the need for unified spatial representation learning, identifies technical challenges, and outlines promising research directions for integrating raster and vector data in geospatial AI.

Conclusion: Joint integration of raster and vector data is essential for developing next-generation geospatial AI systems capable of more accurate, interpretable, and semantically grounded understanding of Earth systems, bridging physical and human dimensions.

Abstract: Earth Observation (EO) has fundamentally transformed the monitoring of environmental processes and human activities up to planetary scale. Recent advances in self-supervised learning have given rise to Earth Observation Foundation Models (EOFMs), which leverage petabyte-scale unlabeled EO data to learn transferable representations across a wide range of downstream geospatial tasks. Despite these advances, current EOFMs remain largely confined to raster modalities, overlooking the rich, structured information encoded in openly-accessible vector data sources such as OpenStreetMap and Overture. Vector data provides explicit and compact representations of geographic entities, including geometry, topology, and semantic relationships, offering critical contextual signals that are often ambiguous or inaccessible in imagery alone. Raster and vector data thus represent complementary views of geographic space: raster data captures continuous physical and spectral patterns, while vector data encodes discrete objects and their relational structure and often represents more of the human rather than the physical systems (e.g. social or demographic data). However, existing geospatial representation learning paradigms treat these modalities in isolation, relying on imperfect and often lossy transformations to bridge them. This perspective paper calls for a paradigm shift toward joint Spatial Representation Learning (SRL) in an unified embedding space that integrate raster perception with vector-based reasoning. Building on emerging efforts in multimodal geospatial learning, we highlight conceptual foundations, technical challenges, and promising directions for aligning heterogeneous spatial data sources. We contend that such integration is essential for developing next-generation geospatial AI systems capable of more accurate, interpretable, and semantically grounded understanding of the Earth.

</details>


### [157] [A Mathematical Conflict Framework for Contextual Data Modulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02381)
*Hakan Emre Kartal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A general mathematical framework for representing structural conflicts between raw and contextual data as local, directional, and context-sensitive operator-based objects, moving beyond implicit optimization side effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches typically treat conflict as an implicit side effect embedded within optimization processes, which limits explicit representation and analysis of structural discrepancies between raw data and contextual data.

Method: Proposes an operator-based mathematical framework that treats conflict as a local, directional, and context-sensitive quantity using abstract operators. It integrates components like weighting, scale behavior, and output mapping without being tied to specific learning algorithms or optimization methods.

Result: A generalized structure adaptable to different problem classes that explicitly represents structural conflicts as independent, operator-based mathematical objects at the component level.

Conclusion: The framework provides a more rigorous way to conceptualize and work with data conflicts by making them explicit mathematical objects rather than implicit optimization artifacts, enabling better analysis and adaptation across different problem domains.

Abstract: In this study, a generalized operator-based mathematical conflict framework is presented to explicitly represent structural discrepancies between raw data and contextual data. The proposed structure treats conflict as a local, directional, and context-sensitive quantity, integrating components such as weighting, scale behavior, and output mapping under a unified abstract operator. Without being reduced to a specific learning algorithm or optimization method, the framework is defined as a general structure adaptable to different classes of problems. While existing approaches typically treat conflict merely as an implicit side effect embedded within the optimization process, the proposed framework considers conflict as an independent, operator-based, and component-level mathematical object.

</details>


### [158] [AgentPLM: Agentic Protein Language Models with Reasoning-Augmented Decoding for Protein Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02386)
*Sahil Rahman,Maxx Richard Rahman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentPLM transforms passive protein language models into active agents by integrating reasoning-enhanced decoding with external biophysical tools and contrastive policy optimization, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple protein design tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current protein language models are passive oracles that generate sequences in a single forward pass without mechanisms to incorporate external biophysical feedback or correct violations of thermodynamic/structural constraints during generation.

Method: AgentPLM equips pre-trained PLMs with: 1) Reasoning-Augmented Decoding (RAD) that interleaves autoregressive generation with tool calls (ESMFold, FoldX, AutoDock Vina), and 2) Contrastive Agent Policy Optimisation (CAPO), a trajectory-level extension of direct preference optimization that trains the policy end-to-end to learn when oracle feedback is informative.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on benchmark tasks including de novo enzyme design, antibody optimization, thermostability, PPI interface design, and zero-shot fitness prediction. Shows significant gain in antibody top-10% hit rate over strongest passive baseline, with mechanistic evidence of online error correction without explicit backtracking.

Conclusion: AgentPLM successfully transforms PLMs from passive oracles into active agents that can incorporate external biophysical feedback during generation, enabling more thermodynamically and structurally sound protein design through online error correction mechanisms.

Abstract: Protein language models (PLMs) are passive oracles: they generate sequences in a single forward pass with no mechanism to consult external biophysical feedback or redirect generation when a candidate violates thermodynamic or structural constraints. We introduce AgentPLM, which addresses this by equipping a pre-trained PLM with i) Reasoning-Augmented Decoding (RAD), which interleaves autoregressive generation with tool calls (ESMFold, FoldX, AutoDock Vina), and ii) Contrastive Agent Policy Optimisation (CAPO), a trajectory-level extension of direct preference optimisation that trains the policy end-to-end to learn when oracle feedback is informative rather than merely imitating high-fitness sequences. We evaluate AgentPLM on benchmark tasks spanning de novo enzyme design, antibody optimisation, thermostability, PPI interface design, and zero-shot fitness prediction with standardised oracle APIs and controlled sequence-identity splits. AgentPLM achieves state-of-the-art results with a gain in antibody top-10% hit rate over the strongest passive baseline, providing mechanistic evidence of online error correction without explicit backtracking.

</details>


### [159] [Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Semiconductor Visual Program Synthesis via Input Binarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02434)
*Yusuke Ohtsubo,Kota Dohi,Koichiro Yawata,Koki Takeshita,Tatsuya Sasaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A VLM-based visual program synthesis framework with input binarization bridges the simulation-to-reality gap for semiconductor inspection, enabling controlled generation of geometrically accurate training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models like diffusion models and GANs cannot guarantee the nanometer-scale geometric accuracy required for semiconductor metrology tasks, while obtaining sufficient real training data for precise parametric control over circuit geometry is costly.

Method: A visual program synthesis framework where a Vision-Language Model converts inspection images into editable Domain-Specific Language code describing circuit geometries, combined with an input binarization strategy that strips SEM-specific texture and noise from real images to focus the model on geometric structure.

Result: Binarized inputs improve the mean Dice coefficient from 0.4393 to 0.5256 over the raw-input baseline on the MIIC dataset, demonstrating that simple texture abstraction substantially mitigates the sim-to-real gap.

Conclusion: Using input binarization to simplify real SEM images by removing texture and noise allows VLM-based visual program synthesis to effectively bridge the simulation-to-reality gap, enabling controlled generation of training data with nanometer-scale geometric accuracy for semiconductor inspection.

Abstract: Precise parametric control over circuit geometry is essential for semiconductor inspection, yet obtaining sufficient real training data remains costly. Although generative models such as diffusion models and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) can augment training data, they cannot guarantee the nanometer-scale geometric accuracy required for metrology tasks. We propose a visual program synthesis framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) converts inspection images into editable Domain-Specific Language (DSL) code describing circuit geometries, enabling controlled generation of training data with exact parameter manipulation. Because the VLM is trained solely on synthetic DSL-rendered data, a domain gap arises when processing real Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images. We bridge this gap with an input binarization strategy that strips SEM-specific texture and noise, letting the model focus on geometric structure. On the MIIC dataset, binarized inputs improve the mean Dice coefficient from 0.4393 to 0.5256 over the raw-input baseline, demonstrating that simple texture abstraction substantially mitigates the sim-to-real gap.

</details>


### [160] [LLM-Evolved Pattern Generators for Optimal Classical Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02438)
*Windy Phung,Dominik Drexler,Arnaud Lequen,Jendrik Seipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First method to learn domain-dependent admissible heuristics for optimal planning by synthesizing pattern collection programs with LLM-driven evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learned heuristics focus on search guidance and are not admissible, making them unsuitable for optimal classical planning. There is a need for domain-dependent heuristics that are admissible by design to preserve the optimality guarantees of A* search.

Method: LLM-driven evolutionary program-synthesis to obtain, for each domain, a program that produces a pattern collection for any task in that domain. Combine the resulting patterns admissibly via saturated cost partitioning.

Result: Learned programs encode interpretable domain-specific insights, run with negligible overhead at test time, and yield heuristics that match the coverage of state-of-the-art domain-independent baselines on several domains while evaluating each state substantially faster.

Conclusion: Learning admissible domain-dependent heuristics via program-synthesis of pattern collections is feasible and efficient, offering a promising direction for combining machine learning with classical planning to obtain fast optimal planning.

Abstract: Learned heuristics have recently become a competitive alternative to traditional domain-independent heuristics for satisficing planning. Existing approaches, however, focus on improving search guidance rather than guaranteeing admissibility, which makes them unsuitable for optimal classical planning. We present the first method for learning domain-dependent heuristics that are admissible by design and thus preserve the optimality guarantees of A* search. Instead of learning a direct mapping from states to heuristic values, we learn to construct abstractions that induce admissible heuristics. We use an LLM-driven evolutionary program-synthesis framework to obtain, for each domain, a program that produces a pattern collection for any task in that domain, and we combine the resulting patterns admissibly via saturated cost partitioning. Empirically, the learned programs encode interpretable domain-specific insights, run with negligible overhead at test time and yield heuristics that match the coverage of state-of-the-art domain-independent baselines on several domains while evaluating each state substantially faster.

</details>


### [161] [Food Noise & False Safety: A Systematic Evaluation of How LLMs Fail to Adapt to Eating Disorder Queries with Clinician Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02444)
*Giulia Pucci,Emily Hemendinger,Ruizhe Li,Gavin Abercrombie,Tanvi Dinkar,Arabella Sinclair*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates how Large Language Models (LLMs) respond to users with eating disorders, finding that LLMs often provide unsafe or self-harming responses when users use specific linguistic cues, potentially facilitating dangerous behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People with eating disorders are increasingly turning to LLM-based chat systems for guidance, advice, and emotional support, despite these systems not being designed for clinical advice. The perceived expertise, neutrality, and accessibility of LLMs make them a frequent but risky source of support for vulnerable populations.

Method: The researchers consulted with clinical eating disorder experts to identify specific linguistic cues in prompts that increase likelihood of unsafe responses. They systematically varied the degree of potential risk present in user prompts to analyze how LLMs respond to problematic inputs.

Result: The study found that LLMs often uncritically adapt to and facilitate unsafe or self-harming user requests. Specific linguistic cues in prompts significantly increase the likelihood of unsafe responses from LLMs, demonstrating that these systems can enable dangerous behaviors when presented with problematic inputs.

Conclusion: LLMs pose significant risks when used by people with eating disorders due to their tendency to uncritically adapt to dangerous user inputs. The paper highlights the urgent need for improved safety measures in LLM design, particularly for vulnerable populations seeking mental health support, and calls for better understanding of interaction patterns to prevent harm.

Abstract: Recent evidence shows that people with eating disorders (EDs) are increasingly seeking guidance, advice, and emotional support from Large Language Model (LLM)-based chat systems. Although these systems are not designed to provide clinical advice, their perceived expertise, neutrality and accessibility make them a frequent, albeit risky, source of support. This paper investigates potential patterns of interaction between users with EDs and LLMs, focusing on the potential harms arising from models that uncritically adapt to, and facilitate unsafe or self-harming user requests. We find, in consultation with clinical ED experts, that specific linguistic cues in prompts increase the likelihood of unsafe responses and, through systematically varying the degree of potential risk present in the user prompt, report the extent to which LLMs uncritically adapt to problematic, and potentially dangerous user inputs.

</details>


### [162] [HLL: Can Agents Cross Humanity's Last Line of Verification?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02449)
*Xinhao Song,Su Su,Sirui Song,Hongliang Wu,Wen Shen,Zhihua Wei,Gongshen Liu,Linfeng Zhang,Dongrui Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HLL benchmark evaluates if multimodal agents can bypass CAPTCHA verification through human-like interaction; current agents remain brittle with degraded performance under realistic conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether multimodal agents can truly substitute for humans in workflows that services deliberately protect against automation, particularly CAPTCHA verification which serves as a critical human-verification boundary.

Method: The authors introduce Humanity's Last Line of Verification (HLL), a controlled benchmark for evaluating multimodal agents' ability to solve CAPTCHAs through human-like interaction. HLL includes diverse CAPTCHA interactions, controlled realism stressors (cluttered webpages, harder task variants, trace-conditioned validation), and closed-loop GUI environment testing.

Result: Evaluation of eight frontier multimodal agents shows they are brittle at the human-substitution boundary: performance varies sharply across verification types, degrades under realistic interface conditions, and drops further when correct answers must be supported by valid action traces.

Conclusion: Current multimodal agents remain brittle at human-substitution boundaries, particularly CAPTCHA verification, with performance degrading under realistic conditions and requiring improvements in localization, action calibration, state tracking, and process consistency.

Abstract: Multimodal agents are increasingly expected to operate interfaces on behalf of users, raising a central deployment question: can they truly substitute for humans in workflows that services deliberately protect against automation? CAPTCHA verification makes this question concrete. It is not merely a visual puzzle, but a human-verification boundary placed before account creation, content access, form submission, and other protected actions.
