Result: Self-verification with a training-free virtual-thinking prefix works well in most settings, but no scoring method has fixed quality—its effectiveness depends on both the decoder used and model capabilities.

Conclusion: The best approach for identifying correct outputs without supervision requires jointly selecting both the scoring function and decoding method based on model capabilities, as no single scoring method is universally optimal across different decoding strategies.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate even when the answer lies within their parameters. While inference-time scaling can surface this latent knowledge, the most effective methods require supervision: a trained verifier or reward model. We ask what can be done with only a base language model: which intrinsic signal best identifies correct outputs, and how should it be decoded? We cast this as a score~$\times$~decoder grid pairing four scores (perplexity, contrastive, power-distribution likelihood, and self-verification) with three decoding families (optimization, sampling, consensus), and evaluate every cell on MATH500 with the base and instruction-tuned Qwen3-1.7B. While self-verification, which prompts the model to judge its own answer and is sharpened by a training-free virtual-thinking prefix, works well in most settings, no score has a fixed quality: its value depends on the decoder that consumes it and on model capability. When no supervision is available, the score and the decoding family must be chosen together.

</details>


### [274] [Quantum Tunneling-Aware Machine Learning: Physics-Derived Noise Models for Robust Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00741)
*Uiwon Hwang,Jaeho Hwang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces quantum tunneling-aware ML that models structured weight errors from quantum tunneling, achieving high accuracy with dramatically reduced error correction overhead via closed-form compensation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transistor scaling is approaching quantum-mechanical limits where thin gate oxides cause electron leakage through quantum tunneling. AI inference can tolerate such errors if their structure is properly modeled, unlike conventional digital systems.

Method: Quantum tunneling-aware machine learning (QTAML) using WKB approximation to derive deployment-time weight-error distribution, resulting in Tunneling-Aware Compensation (TAC) algorithm with closed-form mean correction and optimal layer-adaptive bit-budget allocation.

Result: TAC reaches 95% of clean accuracy with 3.4× to 33.6× less ECC overhead than baseline across four convolutional architectures at p_flip=0.10 and a transformer encoder at p_flip=0.05. WKB-derived scoring outperforms magnitude-based allocation by up to 24 percentage points at small budgets.

Conclusion: WKB tunneling physics can be systematically connected with noise-aware deep learning, suggesting a principled path toward hardware-software co-design beyond conventional transistor scaling limits. TAC achieves substantial gains in error correction efficiency without requiring retraining, labels, or inference-time overhead.

Abstract: Transistor scaling is approaching a quantum-mechanical limit, as thin gate oxides induce electron leakage through quantum tunneling. Unlike conventional digital systems, AI inference can tolerate such errors provided their structure is modeled correctly. In this paper, we introduce quantum tunneling-aware machine learning (QTAML). We derive the deployment-time weight-error distribution from first principles using the Wentzel-Kramers-Brillouin (WKB) approximation and show that it has structure that generic Gaussian noise models miss: an exact affine mean drift, a per-bit variance hierarchy dominated by the most-significant bit, and a per-layer dependence on $\|W_\ell\|_\infty$ and the trained-network Jacobian. We package these three structural properties into a single deployment-time algorithm, Tunneling-Aware Compensation (TAC), that combines closed-form mean correction with an optimal layer-adaptive bit-budget allocation derived from the WKB variance decomposition. Across four convolutional architectures at $p_\mathrm{flip}$=0.10 and a transformer encoder at $p_\mathrm{flip}$=0.05, TAC reaches $95\%$ of clean accuracy with 3.4$\times$ to 33.6$\times$ less ECC overhead than Uniform-MSP, the natural baseline derived from the same physics. The closed-form saturation ratio $ρ^*$ predicts these gains in advance, and on heterogeneous architectures WKB-derived scoring outperforms magnitude-based allocation by up to 24 percentage points at small budgets. The algorithm requires no retraining, no labels, and no inference-time overhead. We also verify the WKB-derived distributional theorems to Monte Carlo precision. These results connect WKB tunneling physics with noise-aware deep learning and suggest a principled path toward hardware--software co-design beyond conventional scaling limits.

</details>


### [275] [A multimodal dataset of photoplethysmography and continuous behavioral responses to ASMR and nature videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00752)
*Tushar Das,Daigo Hozaki,Koushlendra Kumar Singh,Hirohito M. Kondo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REST-ASMR is a new multimodal dataset addressing the lack of standardized ASMR resources, featuring physiological data, stimuli, and annotations that enable effective ASMR detection and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASMR research has been hindered by a dearth of standardized, open-access multimodal datasets, limiting the ability to study this phenomenon systematically.

Method: A multimodal dataset collection approach using synchronized high-resolution photoplethysmography (PPG), time-aligned audiovisual stimuli, and continuous subjective annotations from 34 participants. Nature-relaxation videos were used as control stimuli. A Bidirectional Long-Short Term Memory model was employed for prediction.

Result: Technical validation showed high stimulus efficacy (97% responder rate), significant stimulus-specific inter-subject agreement (p < 0.05), robust PPG-derived ASMR-specific cardiovascular deceleration, and a Bidirectional Long-Short Term Memory model successfully predicted subjective ASMR tingle states with perfect video-level classification and good frame-level performance.

Conclusion: REST-ASMR constitutes a dense temporal foundation for affective computing, multimodal research, and the development of personalized models of relaxation-related responses.

Abstract: Autonomous Sensory Meridian Response (ASMR) is a somatosensory phenomenon characterized by pleasant tingling sensations and cardiovascular slowing. However, ASMR research has been hindered by a dearth of standardized, open-access multimodal datasets. To address this limitation, we present REST-ASMR (Response to Environmental & Sensory Triggers), a synchronized multimodal dataset designed to capture behavioral reports and physiological dynamics during ASMR, with nature-relaxation videos as control stimuli. The dataset includes high-resolution photoplethysmography (PPG), time-aligned audiovisual stimuli, and continuous subjective annotations from 34 participants. Technical validation showed high stimulus efficacy (97% responder rate), significant stimulus-specific inter-subject agreement (p < 0.05), and a robust PPG-derived ASMR-specific cardiovascular deceleration. Additionally, a Bidirectional Long-Short Term Memory model successfully predicted subjective ASMR tingle states, achieving video-level ASMR vs. Nature classification with perfect accuracy and a frame-level global mean accuracy of 75.51%, macro F1-score of 71.86%, and 100% Nature-baseline specificity, under a strict, leakage-free subject-video double-independent 4-fold cross-validation. REST-ASMR constitutes a dense temporal foundation for affective computing, multimodal research, and the development of personalized models of relaxation-related responses.

</details>


### [276] [RADE: Random Add-Drop Edge as a Regularizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00757)
*Danial Saber,Amirali Salehi-Abari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RADE: Stochastic graph augmentation that adds & drops edges to simultaneously address GNN overfitting and over-squashing, with adaptive rate selection making it hyperparameter-free.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph augmentations (edge deletion) regularize but cause train-inference misalignment and don't address over-squashing; rewiring methods improve connectivity but don't regularize training. Need a single solution for both overfitting and over-squashing.

Method: Proposes Random Add-Drop Edge (RADE) - stochastic graph augmentation that jointly drops and adds edges, plus a mini-batch gradient-norm balancing algorithm that adapts deletion/addition rates during training.

Result: Experiments on node/graph-classification benchmarks show RADE is a strong regularizer that mitigates over-squashing, with ablations supporting the importance of train-inference alignment, adaptive rate selection, and complementary edge operations.

Conclusion: RADE effectively addresses both overfitting and over-squashing in GNNs through a theoretically-aligned train-inference approach, with adaptive rate selection and complementary edge operations making it hyperparameter-free and practical.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) suffer from overfitting and over-squashing of long-range information. Stochastic graph augmentations (e.g., edge deletion) regularize training against overfitting but can introduce train-inference misalignment and do not improve over-squashing. In contrast, rewiring methods improve connectivity to mitigate over-squashing, but are not designed to regularize training. We propose Random Add-Drop Edge (RADE), a stochastic graph augmentation method that jointly drops and adds edges to address both overfitting and over-squashing simultaneously. RADE is provably designed to align training and inference so that random augmentations regularize training without distribution shift, while supporting long-range communication at inference. We further propose and study a mini-batch gradient-norm balancing algorithm that adapts deletion and addition rates during training, rendering RADE hyperparameter-free in practice. Experiments on node- and graph-classification benchmarks show that RADE is a strong regularizer and mitigates over-squashing. Ablations support the roles of train-inference alignment, adaptive rate selection, and the complementary effects of random edge deletion and edge addition.

</details>


### [277] [Distributed GNEP Algorithms without Multiplier Sharing and Applications to Multi-Robot Coordination and Contextual Bandit-Based Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00759)
*Shao-An Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two papers: 1) Distributed algorithms for GNEPs without multiplier exchange, 2) Contextual bandits to adaptively select active learning strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: First paper: Distributed algorithms for GNEPs often require exchanging Lagrange multipliers, increasing communication and reducing privacy. Second paper: Handcrafted active learning strategies perform well only on specific datasets, but dataset characteristics are often unknown in advance.

Method: First paper: Continuous-time distributed algorithms with local multiplier estimates, no exchange of Lagrange multipliers, plus discretization schemes. Second paper: Contextual bandits to adaptively select among active learning strategies based on dataset context.

Result: First paper: Algorithms converge to GNE without exchanging multipliers, reducing communication overhead while preserving privacy. Second paper: Contextual bandits effectively select appropriate active learning strategies, outperforming individual strategies on public datasets.

Conclusion: The first paper presents distributed algorithms for computing GNE in strongly monotone games with convex constraints, reducing communication overhead. The second paper shows that contextual bandits can effectively adapt active learning strategies to dataset characteristics.

Abstract: Recent advances in artificial intelligence have expanded the focus from classical optimization to include equilibrium analysis in noncooperative games. Many such games involve shared constraints, leading to Generalized Nash Equilibrium Problems (GNEPs). Existing distributed algorithms typically require agents to exchange Lagrange multipliers to enforce consensus and compute variational-GNEs (v-GNEs).
  This work introduces fully distributed continuous-time algorithms and establishes convergence without requiring multiplier exchange, thereby reducing information exchange per iteration while improving privacy preservation. The analysis focuses on strongly monotone games with convex individual constraints and linear shared constraints. I also propose several discretization schemes for the continuous-time algorithms. The proposed approach converges to general GNEs, rather than being restricted to v-GNEs, with the attained equilibrium depending on the initialization. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated through applications in multi-robot coordination and placement.
  In the second part, this work includes research conducted in collaboration with Amazon scientists. One of the most challenging problems in real-world machine learning is labeled data collection, which typically requires substantial human effort and cost. Active learning aims to reduce this labeling requirement. Existing handcrafted active learning strategies, however, generally perform well only on specific types of datasets, which are often unknown in advance. In this work, I propose using contextual bandits to adaptively select the most suitable active learning strategy. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated on publicly available external datasets.

</details>


### [278] [Confidence-Adaptive SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00761)
*Shaohua Li,Xiuchao Sui,Xiaobing Sun,Yuhang Wu,Liangli Zhen,Yong Liu,Rick Siow Mong Goh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learns gate sharpness based on routing confidence to improve Mixture-of-Experts MLPs


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SwiGLU gate sharpness is fixed, but confidence-aware adjustment could improve MoE models

Method: κ-SwiGLU parameterizes SiLU gate sharpness as a learnable function of router logit

Result: Improves mean CORE performance across MoE Transformer models with negligible parameters/small overhead

Conclusion: Confidence-aware gate sharpness adjustment in SwiGLU improves MoE MLPs

Abstract: SwiGLU has become a standard gated activation in modern Transformer MLPs, yet its gate sharpness -- the smoothness and selectivity of the gating function -- is typically fixed throughout training. In this work, we propose Confidence-Aware SwiGLU ($κ$-SwiGLU), a variant of SwiGLU for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models that adjusts expert gate sharpness according to token-level routing confidence. Specifically, $κ$-SwiGLU parameterizes the SiLU gate sharpness coefficient as a learnable function of the router logit, enabling each expert gate unit to interpolate between smooth, broadly active gating and sharp, selective gating. We evaluate $κ$-SwiGLU on the FineWeb-Edu dataset across MoE Transformer models ranging from 8 to 28 layers. Across these settings, $κ$-SwiGLU improves mean CORE performance while adding negligible parameters and incurring only a small computational overhead, demonstrating that confidence-aware gate sharpness is a promising mechanism for improving MoE MLPs. The code is available at https://github.com/askerlee/kappa-swiglu.

</details>


### [279] [Logit Distillation on Manifolds: Mapping by Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00771)
*Yiru Yang,Junling Wang,Nishant Kumar Singh,Luohong Wu,Haoran Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel knowledge distillation method using projection mapping and LoRA injection to efficiently compress ensemble models for deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensemble models improve ML performance but are too computationally expensive for large-scale deployment, especially for large neural networks.

Method: Layer and point wise projection mapping that aligns student and teacher representations in a high-dimensional embedding space during training, combined with LoRA injection to reduce trainable parameters.

Result: The approach reduces student model trainable parameters to less than 1% of teacher model while significantly improving Word Error Rate (WER) compared to other distillation methods. It can be trained rapidly and in parallel unlike mixture of experts.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a novel knowledge distillation approach using layer-wise projection mapping and LoRA injection to efficiently compress ensemble models while maintaining performance. This method enables efficient deployment of powerful ensemble models to large user bases.

Abstract: A simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning model is not to train a single but several models with diverse algorithms which will make slightly distinct kinds of predictions and errors on the same data, and thus improve the average predictions and robustness. However, making predictions using a whole ensemble of models is cumbersome and computationally too expensive to allow deployment to a large number of users, especially if the models are large neural nets. In response to this, we introduce a layer and point wise projection mapping, which maps student and teacher representations into an aligned high-dimensional embedding space during training process. The proposed approach combined with LoRA injection reduces the student model trainable parameters to less than 1% of the teacher model, while significantly improving word error rate (WER) compared to other distillation methods, as demonstrated in ablation studies. Unlike a mixture of experts, our method can be trained rapidly and in parallel.

</details>


### [280] [Latent Diffusion Pretraining for Crystal Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00776)
*Shrimon Mukherjee,Kishalay Das,Partha Basuchowdhuri,Pawan Goyal,Niloy Ganguly*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CrysLDNet is a latent diffusion pretraining framework for crystal property prediction that outperforms baselines by ~4-5% on benchmark datasets and handles data scarcity effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph neural networks and Transformer models for crystal property prediction require large labeled datasets, which are scarce in practice. Existing pretraining-finetuning strategies, especially diffusion-based methods, offer potential but need improvement to effectively handle data scarcity.

Method: A latent diffusion pretraining framework that combines a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with a diffusion model. The VAE encoder maps 3D crystal structures to a smooth latent space where diffusion is applied to learn structural and chemical semantics from unlabeled data.

Result: CrysLDNet achieves improvements of 4.26% on JARVIS and 4.90% on MP datasets compared to training-from-scratch and pretrained baselines. It also shows robustness in sparse-data conditions and can correct DFT errors when finetuned with limited experimental data.

Conclusion: CrysLDNet introduces an effective latent diffusion pretraining framework that significantly outperforms existing methods for crystal property prediction, demonstrating robustness in data-scarce scenarios and the ability to correct DFT errors with limited experimental data.

Abstract: Fast and accurate prediction of crystal properties is a central challenge in new materials design. Graph neural networks and Transformer-based models have emerged as powerful tools for this task due to their ability to encode the local structural environment of atoms within a crystal. However, these models are data-hungry, and in practice, labeled data for crystal properties are scarce. Pretraining-finetuning strategies, particularly those based on diffusion models, have shown promise in addressing these limitations. In this work, we introduce a novel latent diffusion based pretraining framework, CrysLDNet, designed to mitigate data scarcity. Our approach integrates a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with a diffusion model during the pretraining stage. The VAE encoder maps 3D crystal structures into a smooth latent space within which the diffusion process is applied. This latent diffusion pretraining enables the graph encoder to effectively capture structural and chemical semantics from large-scale unlabeled data, which can then be finetuned for specific property prediction tasks. Comprehensive experiments on popular DFT datasets for property prediction reveal that CrysLDNet significantly outperforms both training-from-scratch and pretrained baselines, with improvements of 4.26% and 4.90% on the JARVIS and MP datasets, respectively. Additionally, the learned representations remain robust in sparse-data conditions and are expressive enough to correct DFT errors when finetuned with limited experimental data. Code is available at: https://github.com/shrimonmuke0202/CrysLDNet.git.

</details>


### [281] [Behavior-Invariant Task Representation Learning with Transformer-based World Models for Offline Meta-Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00780)
*Fuyuan Qian,Menglong Zhang,Song Wang,Quanying Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a novel offline meta-RL framework combining information-theoretic task representation learning with Transformer-based stochastic world modeling and conservative value penalties to address distribution shifts and achieve robust generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of context and policy distribution shifts in offline meta-reinforcement learning, particularly under sparse-reward settings where agents get trapped in inherent pattern dilemmas and fail to achieve robust generalization.

Method: The method integrates information-theoretic task representation learning to extract behavior policy-invariant task-defining latent variables, combined with a Transformer-based stochastic world model. To handle policy shift and model exploitation, a conservative value penalty is applied to imagination-based rollouts.

Result: The method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches with superior stability and generalization under out-of-distribution and sparse-reward settings.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the key challenges of offline meta-RL by integrating information-theoretic task representation learning with Transformer-based stochastic world modeling and conservative value penalties, achieving superior performance, stability, and generalization compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Abstract: Offline meta-reinforcement learning leverages static datasets to enable agents to generalize to unseen environments by combining offline efficiency with meta-learning adaptability, yet it faces key challenges from context and policy distribution shifts. These issues hinder agents from adapting to online environments, and are further exacerbated under sparse-reward settings. As a result, agents often become trapped in an inherent pattern dilemma, failing to achieve robust generalization. In this work, we propose a novel framework that integrates information-theoretic task representation learning with a Transformer-based stochastic world model. Our approach extracts task-defining latent variables that are invariant to behavior policy, thereby effectively mitigating the context distribution shift. To further handle policy shift and model exploitation, we apply a conservative value penalty to imagination-based rollouts, preventing the policy from exploiting model inaccuracies while maintaining robust adaptation. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, with superior stability and generalization under out-of-distribution and sparse-reward settings.

</details>


### [282] [Extending Causal Metamodeling to a non-Markovian Queue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00795)
*Pracheta Amaranath,Anant Bhide,David Jensen,Peter Haas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extension of modular dynamic Bayesian networks (MDBNs) to non-Markovian queues using phase-type distributions, enabling efficient and accurate probabilistic and causal query answering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous work on MDBNs for metamodeling was limited to Markovian systems, leaving a gap for handling non-Markovian queues. There is a need for metamodels that can efficiently answer probabilistic and causal queries for non-Markovian systems without running expensive simulations.

Method: The approach extends modular dynamic Bayesian networks (MDBNs) to non-Markovian systems by approximating non-exponential service time distributions using phase-type distributions. It addresses challenges including balancing metamodeling accuracy and tractability in choosing the number of phases, efficiently learning metamodel parameters, and selecting appropriate sampling intervals for discrete-time approximation of continuous-time simulations.

Result: Experiments on a G/M/1 queue demonstrate that the extended MDBN can produce accurate answers to probabilistic and causal queries while achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup in inference times compared to direct simulation.

Conclusion: The paper presents the first causal metamodeling technique for non-Markovian systems, achieved by extending MDBNs to handle non-Markovian queues via phase-type approximations.

Abstract: Metamodels for discrete-event simulations approximate the behavior of simulation models without running expensive simulations. Prior work introduced modular dynamic Bayesian networks (MDBNs) -- a class of metamodels that can estimate a range of probabilistic and causal queries (PCQs) using a single, trained model -- but the method was limited to Markovian systems. In this paper, we initiate an extension of MDBNs to non-Markovian queues by approximating non-exponential distributions using phase-type distributions. This approach raises novel challenges, including balancing metamodeling accuracy and tractability when choosing the number of phases, efficiently learning metamodel parameters, and choosing the sampling interval that is used to approximate a continuous-time simulation by a discrete-time MDBN. We provide preliminary solutions to these challenges, yielding the first causal metamodeling technique for non-Markovian systems. Experiments on a G/M/1 queue demonstrate that the MDBN can produce accurate answers to PCQs with orders-of-magnitude speedup of inference times relative to direct simulation.

</details>


### [283] [Safe-Subspace Pseudo-Label Refinement for Source-Free Graph Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00808)
*Yingxu Wang,Xinwang Liu,Siyang Gao,Nan Yin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A selective pseudo-labeling approach for source-free graph domain adaptation that identifies a safe subspace where pseudo-label noise can be controlled, and uses both semantic and structural evidence to refine pseudo-labels while handling uncertain samples with noise-tolerant regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Source-free graph domain adaptation faces a critical challenge of pseudo-label reliability. Under feature and topological shifts between source and target domains, source-induced predictions may become confidently wrong, and indiscriminate self-training can amplify systematic errors through graph message passing, leading to performance degradation.

Method: Proposes SafeSubspace Pseudo-Label Refinement (S²PLR), which: 1) estimates semantic reliability using source-committee confidence and disagreement, 2) learns target-intrinsic structural representation via graph contrastive learning, 3) verifies pseudo-labels through neighborhood consistency, and 4) exploits uncertain samples with noise-tolerant soft regularization instead of unreliable hard labels.

Result: Experiments on image and real-world graph benchmarks under different domain shifts demonstrate that S²PLR achieves robust and competitive performance across diverse source-free transfer settings.

Conclusion: The S²PLR framework represents a principled approach to SF-GDA that moves beyond indiscriminate self-training by developing confidence-aware selection mechanisms and noise-tolerant regularization strategies, leading to more robust adaptation across diverse graph domain shifts.

Abstract: Source-free graph domain adaptation (SF-GDA) aims to adapt source-trained graph models to unlabeled target graphs when source graphs are no longer accessible. A central obstacle is pseudo-label reliability: under feature and topological shifts, source-induced predictions may become confidently wrong, and indiscriminate self-training can amplify systematic errors through graph message passing. This paper studies SF-GDA from a selective pseudo-labeling perspective. Instead of assuming globally bounded pseudo-label noise over the entire target domain, we identify a confidence-consistent safe subspace on which pseudo-label noise can be controlled under restricted posterior discrepancy, and derive a target-risk decomposition that separates safe-subspace fitting error, selected-label noise, and uncertain-set risk. Guided by this analysis, we propose SafeSubspace Pseudo-Label Refinement (S$^2$PLR), a source-free graph adaptation framework that applies hard pseudo-label supervision only to target graphs supported by both semantic and structural evidence. Specifically, S$^2$PLR estimates semantic reliability using source-committee confidence and disagreement, learns a targetintrinsic structural representation via graph contrastive learning, verifies pseudo-labels through neighborhood consistency, and exploits the remaining uncertain samples with noise-tolerant soft regularization rather than unreliable hard labels. Experiments on image and real-world graph benchmarks under different domain shifts demonstrate that S$^2$PLR achieves robust and competitive performance across diverse source-free transfer settings.

</details>


### [284] [OmniEEG-Bench: A Standardized Evaluation Benchmark for EEG Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00815)
*Ziling Lu,Zongsheng Li,Xinke Shen,Kexin Lou,Yingyue Xin,Xiaoqi Chen,Shinan Wang,Xiang Chen,Jiahao Fan,Chenyu Huang,Xin Xu,Zhoujie Hou,Chen Wei,Quanying Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniEEG-Bench is a unified benchmark for EEG foundation models that standardizes evaluation across 54 datasets, revealing scaling laws where model performance improves with larger architectures and more diverse pretraining data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmented evaluation of EEG foundation models due to heterogeneous datasets and inconsistent task protocols, enabling systematic assessment of model capabilities.

Method: OmniEEG-Bench organizes evaluation into six task families and standardizes model deployment, task definitions, and metrics through task-card specifications. It unifies 54 EEG datasets with consistent evaluation protocols and benchmarks 10 representative EEG foundation models.

Result: Pretraining dataset diversity and model size are significantly associated with better average ranks across datasets, revealing scaling-law behavior in EEG foundation models.

Conclusion: Scaling EEG foundation models requires both larger architectures and broader, more diverse pretraining data, as evidenced by the scaling-law behavior observed in the benchmark results.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) supports a variety of brain-computer interface (BCI) tasks ranging from brain-state monitoring to human-LLM interactions. EEG foundation models are emerging, but evaluation remains fragmented due to heterogeneous datasets and nconsistent task protocols. Here, we introduce OmniEEG-Bench, a unified benchmark and downstream task roadmap for EEG foundation models (FMs). It organizes evaluation of EEG FMs into six task families spanning (i) signal reliability, (ii) biometrics and disease, (iii) consciousness and state, (iv) cognition and emotion, (v) naturalistic stimulus decoding, and (vi) motor and interaction, introducing a new generation of tasks not systematically benchmarked in prior EEG FM work. OmniEEG-Bench standardizes model deployment, task definitions, and metrics through a task-card specification, and unifies 54 EEG datasets with consistent evaluation protocols. We benchmark 10 representative EEG foundation models and report a leaderboard that covers diverse evaluation settings. Both pretraining dataset diversity and model size are significantly associated with better average ranks across datasets, revealing scaling-law behavior in EEG foundation models (Figure 1). These results suggest that scaling EEG foundation models requires not only larger architectures but also broader and more diverse pretraining data. The benchmark code is available at https://github.com/ncclab-sustech/omni-eegbench.git.

</details>


### [285] [A Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Algorithms for Multi-Task Prediction of the Parameters of the Pectin Hydrolysis--Extraction Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00821)
*Mullosharaf K. Arabov,Shavkat Yo. Kholov,Zainiddin K. Muhiddin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Machine learning ensemble methods optimized pectin hydrolysis-extraction control using 1000 experiments, with CatBoost achieving R²~0.946, revealing raw material type as most important factor, deployed as interactive web tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of controlling complex, multi-parameter technological processes like pectin hydrolysis-extraction more efficiently using machine learning methods, reducing the need for extensive physical experimentation while enabling intelligent production control.

Method: The research utilized a unique database of 1,000 laboratory experiments with four variable process factors and four output characteristics. Eleven machine learning algorithms were trained and compared for multi-task regression, including regularised linear models, ensemble methods, k-nearest neighbours, support vector regression, and multilayer perceptron. Feature importance analysis was conducted to interpret the results.

Result: CatBoost demonstrated the best performance with average R-squared approximately 0.946 after hyperparameter optimization. Feature importance analysis revealed raw material type as the dominant factor (63.6% of total importance), followed by temperature and holding time. The developed pipeline was exported and deployed as an interactive web interface.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that ensemble machine learning methods combined with rigorous statistical analysis and interpretable AI can significantly reduce the need for physical experiments in complex multi-parameter processes like pectin hydrolysis-extraction, forming the basis for intelligent production control.

Abstract: This study addresses the challenge of controlling a complex, multi-parameter technological process -- pectin hydrolysis--extraction -- using machine learning methods. The experimental foundation is a unique database comprising 1,000 laboratory experiments conducted under controlled conditions on seven types of plant raw material with four variable process factors (temperature 85--130 C, pressure 0.9--2.2 atm, holding time 3--10 min, pH 1.5--2.0). Four output characteristics were recorded: pectin yield, galacturonic acid content, molecular weight, and degree of esterification. To solve the multi-task regression problem, 11 algorithms were trained and compared: regularised linear models, ensemble methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, CatBoost, Extra Trees), k-nearest neighbours, support vector regression, and a multilayer perceptron. The best results were demonstrated by CatBoost (average R-squared approximately 0.946 after hyperparameter optimisation). Feature importance analysis revealed the dominant role of the raw material type (63.6% of total importance), followed by temperature and holding time. The developed pipeline was exported in a production-ready format and deployed as an interactive web interface. The findings demonstrate that ensemble methods combined with rigorous statistical analysis and interpretable AI significantly reduce the need for physical experiments and form the basis for intelligent pectin production control.

</details>


### [286] [Partial Fairness Awareness: Belief-Guided Strategic Mechanism for Strategic Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00826)
*Xinpeng Lv,Chunyuan Zheng,Yunxin Mao,Renzhe Xu,Hao Zou,Shanzhi Gu,Liyang Xu,Huan Chen,Yuanlong Chen,Wenjing Yang,Haotian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes partial fairness awareness to mitigate fairness exposure dilemma in strategic classification by publicly releasing candidate fairness constraints while concealing the actual constraint, using belief-guided learning to align agent beliefs with system fairness constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fundamental dilemma in strategic machine learning: public fairness constraints enable manipulation leading to fairness reversal, while private constraints reduce welfare and discourage genuine improvement.

Method: Introduces belief-guided strategic mechanism where agents iteratively interact with decision system, maintain belief distribution over candidate fairness constraints, and update beliefs based on feedback to align with actual fairness constraint.

Result: Extensive experiments show PFA achieves lower group fairness gaps, higher acceptance of qualified individuals, and more stable outcomes compared to fully public or private fairness regimes.

Conclusion: Partial fairness awareness (PFA) provides a more nuanced approach than binary public/private regimes by strategically revealing candidate fairness constraints while concealing the grounding constraint, achieving better fairness-utility trade-offs while mitigating manipulation risks.

Abstract: Strategic machine learning investigates scenarios where agents manipulate their features to receive favorable decisions from predictive models. To address fairness concerns intrinsic to strategic classification, recent work has introduced group-specific fairness constraints. However, current fairness-aware approaches face a fundamental dilemma in the issue of fairness exposure: making these constraints public enables strategic manipulation and can lead to fairness reversal, while keeping them hidden may reduce social welfare and discourage genuine improvement. To fill this gap, we subsequently propose the problem of partial fairness awareness (PFA), as our theoretical analysis informs that such a dilemma can be mitigated by releasing the candidate set of fairness constraints and concealing the grounding constraint. To be specific, we introduce a belief-guided strategic mechanism, wherein agents iteratively interact with the decision system and maintain a belief distribution over the candidate set of fairness constraints. This belief-guided process enables agents, through iterative interaction and feedback, to update their belief distribution over the candidate set, thereby gradually aligning their belief with the grounding fairness constraint employed by the system. Extensive experiments on real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that PFA achieves lower group fairness gaps, higher acceptance of truly qualified individuals, and more stable outcomes compared to fully public or private fairness regimes.

</details>


### [287] [Beyond Independent Manipulation: Individual Fairness-aware Strategic Classification with Peer Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00827)
*Xinpeng Lv,Chunyuan Zheng,Yunxin Mao,Renzhe Xu,Jinxuan Yang,Yuanlong Chen,Wangrong Huang,Shaowu Yang,Wenjing Yang,Xinwang Liu,Peng Cui,Haotian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes individual fairness-aware strategic classification (IFSC) framework addressing interdependent strategic manipulations under individual fairness where agents imitate similar positively decided peers, improving fairness consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fairness-aware strategic classification approaches focus on group fairness and assume independent agent responses, but individual fairness requirements create interdependent manipulations where agents imitate nearby positively decided peers, creating a mismatch with classical SC formulations.

Method: Introduces IFSC framework that models peer-driven manipulation as similarity-based imitation, learns classifiers under resulting post-manipulation distributions, and employs robust learning with stochastic perturbations to account for peer observability uncertainty.

Result: IFSC improves individual-fairness consistency and mitigates imitation-induced distortions compared to existing approaches, as demonstrated through experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The IFSC framework effectively addresses the interdependence of strategic manipulations under individual fairness requirements, demonstrating improved fairness consistency and mitigation of imitation-induced distortions in experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Abstract: Strategic classification (SC) investigates scenarios where agents manipulate their features to obtain favorable decisions from predictive models. Existing fairness-aware SC approaches primarily focus on group fairness and typically assume that agents respond independently. However, when individual fairness is required, ensuring similar individuals receive similar outcomes, agents' manipulation becomes interdependent: an agent's preferred manipulation depends on the neighborhoods' outcomes. This induces a mismatch between classical SC formulations and fairness-aware decision settings, where independent models no longer accurately characterize strategic manipulations. To address this issue, we introduce individual fairness-aware strategic classification (IFSC), a framework that models peer-driven manipulation arising from individual fairness, where agents imitate nearby positively decided peers to obtain favorable outcomes. IFSC characterizes strategic manipulation as similarity-based imitation toward visible accepted peers and learns classifiers under the resulting post-manipulation distributions. To account for uncertainty in peer observability, IFSC employs a robust learning process that introduces stochastic perturbations during manipulation simulation. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that IFSC improves individual-fairness consistency and mitigates imitation-induced distortions.

</details>


### [288] [Online Packet Scheduling with Deadlines and Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00835)
*Gianmarco Genalti,Achraf Azize,Vianney Perchet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses online packet scheduling with deadlines under bandit feedback, connecting it to sleeping bandits to achieve optimal α-regret bounds and improved competitive ratios that surpass the golden ratio barrier for finite packet types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Network routers must make QoS decisions about packet transmission at every clock cycle without knowing packet values until after processing, creating a challenging online scheduling problem with partial feedback.

Method: The authors establish a connection to sleeping bandits, provide algorithms for different system constraints (randomization allowed vs not allowed), and analyze performance under different slackness spans and deadline bounds.

Result: Algorithms achieve α-regret upper bounds of Õ(√KT) matching the standard bandit lower bound. For 2-bounded deadline instances, a deterministic algorithm achieves the tightest possible competitive ratio. When K≥2 packet types are finite, a tighter competitive ratio θ_K∈[√2,Φ) breaks the Φ barrier.

Conclusion: The paper proposes algorithms for the Online Packet Scheduling with Deadlines problem under partial bandit feedback, achieving optimal α-regret bounds and competitive ratios that improve upon previous barriers when the number of packet types is finite.

Abstract: Network routers that enforce Quality-of-Service (QoS) guarantees must decide, at every clock cycle, which expiring packet of information to transmit, even when the value of the packet is unknown until it is processed. We frame this problem as the Online Packet Scheduling with Deadlines (OPSD) problem under Partial Feedback: packets arrive at every clock cycle, with different deadlines, but the weights are only observed after execution. Under a stochastic assumption on the unknown weights, we explore different variants of the OPSD problem with bandit feedback. We establish a connection between our setting and the sleeping bandits problem, and set our learning goal to $α$-regret minimization. We provide algorithms with provable $α$-regret guarantees under different spans of slackness, distinguishing systems allowing for randomization and systems that do not. In every scenario, our algorithms achieve an $α$-regret upper bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\sqrt{KT}\right)$, matching the lower bound for the standard bandit setting. In the practically relevant case of $2$-bounded deadline instances, where the deadline is set at most one clock cycle away from the arrival, our deterministic algorithm achieves the provably tightest possible competitive ratio. Remarkably, when the number of distinct packet types $K\ge 2$ is finite, it is possible to break the well-established $Φ= \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$ competitive ratio barrier and attain a tighter competitive ratio $θ_K$ ranging in $[\sqrt{2}, Φ)$.

</details>


### [289] [CUPID in the Model Zoo: Online Matchmaking for Selecting Your Dream LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00846)
*Son Nguyen,Xinyuan Liu,Ransalu Senanayake*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active learning framework uses dueling bandits to efficiently discover user preferences and match appropriate LLMs while respecting cost/time constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users face challenges selecting appropriate LLMs from a growing pool with opaque properties and lack vocabulary to articulate their preferences explicitly.

Method: An active learning framework with dueling bandit algorithm that iteratively selects LLM pairs, collects user feedback, and updates belief about latent preferences using a novel belief-aware upper confidence bound strategy.

Result: Experimental verification through diverse LLM experiments and human studies shows the model can efficiently match well-aligned LLMs to users at lower cost.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides an efficient solution for matching well-aligned LLMs to users at lower cost by leveraging interactive learning with user feedback.

Abstract: Users increasingly face the challenge of selecting an appropriate LLM for a given task from a rapidly growing pool of LLMs, each with distinct but often opaque latent properties. Compounding this challenge, users may lack the vocabulary or awareness to explicitly articulate the characteristics they value in an LLM's responses or deployment. We propose an interaction-efficient active learning framework in which a dueling bandit algorithm iteratively selects pairs of LLMs, collects user feedback about their responses, and updates its belief about the user's latent preferences. We introduce a novel belief-aware upper confidence bound strategy that balances exploration of the model pool with exploitation of inferred preferences, enabling efficient alignment between user needs and LLM capabilities under user-specified cost and time budgets. Through diverse experiments on LLMs and human studies, we experimentally verify that our model can efficiently match well-aligned LLMs to users at a lower cost.

</details>


### [290] [Enhancing LLM Metacognition via Cognitive Pairwise Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00869)
*Weitao Li,Hao Zhou,Xuanyu Lei,Fandong Meng,Yuanhang Liu,Jingyi Ren,Ante Wang,Xiaolong Wang,Yuanchi Zhang,Fuwen Luo,Guangwen Yang,Lin Gan,Weizhi Ma,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CPT addresses limitations of current RL methods by using pairwise comparisons of reasoning traces to teach models to distinguish trustworthy from flawed reasoning, improving reasoning reliability without overfitting abstention behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods with outcome-level rewards can make models give confident answers even when evidence or reasoning is unreliable. SFT or RL methods often teach LLMs to refuse or express uncertainty at response level, which can overfit abstention behavior rather than improve reasoning reliability.

Method: Cognitive Pairwise Training (CPT), a cognitive mid-training alignment stage that uses pairwise comparisons over reasoning traces to generate reusable alignment signals

Result: Across five model scales and three families, CPT improves reasoning-metacognition trade-off. At 14B scale, CPT+RL outperforms standard SFT+RL by +2.2 math-average points and +5.2 abstention-F1 points. CPT improves trace quality and shows strong robustness and scalability.

Conclusion: CPT improves reasoning reliability by encouraging models to internalize reasoning-quality discrimination boundaries rather than memorizing surface refusal patterns.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become central to LLM reasoning, but its outcome-level rewards can make models more willing to give confident answers when evidence or reasoning is unreliable. Existing SFT or RL methods mainly teach LLMs to refuse or express uncertainty at the response level, which can overfit abstention behavior rather than improve reasoning reliability. To address this limitation, we propose Cognitive Pairwise Training (CPT), a cognitive mid-training alignment stage that turns pairwise comparisons over reasoning traces into a reusable alignment signal. By learning to distinguish trustworthy from flawed reasoning, CPT encourages the model to internalize a reasoning-quality discrimination boundary rather than memorize surface refusal patterns. Across five model scales and three model families, CPT improves the reasoning--metacognition trade-off. At 14B, CPT+RL outperforms the standard SFT+RL pipeline by +2.2 math-average points and +5.2 abstention-F1 points. Further analyses show that CPT improves trace quality and exhibits strong robustness and scalability across evaluation and training settings. Code and models are released at https://github.com/Tsinghua-dhy/CPT.

</details>


### [291] [Task diversity produces systematic transfer but inhibits continual reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00880)
*Purab Seth,Neil Shah,Kunal Jha,Samuel J. Gershman,Max Kleiman-Weiner,Wilka Carvalho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Task diversity in Banyan RL domain provides beneficial local transfer when distribution shifts occur, but doesn't guarantee sustained continual learning across multiple shifts due to forgetting and performance plateaus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether task diversity not only enables zero-shot generalization (post-training) but also improves an agent's ability to continue learning across distribution shifts in continual reinforcement learning settings.

Method: Banyan, a GPU-accelerated continual RL domain with three independently controllable axes of task diversity: map layouts, interactive objects, and hierarchical sub-goal dependency structures.

Result: Increased diversity along each axis enabled agents to start new tasks near the performance of previous ones, even when optimal policy structure changed. However, as the number of shifts increased, this local transfer alone did not yield sustained continual learning: longer-horizon tasks plateaued, and earlier task distributions were forgotten after later training.

Conclusion: The Banyan domain serves as a valuable benchmark for studying when controlled task diversity produces meaningful transfer, when that transfer persists across distribution shifts, and where it falls short of achieving proper continual learning. While task diversity provides beneficial local transfer at the onset of new tasks, it does not guarantee sustained continual learning over multiple shifts, highlighting the need for additional mechanisms to address forgetting and performance plateaus.

Abstract: Continual reinforcement learning aims to produce agents that learn not only to improve at their current tasks but also to adapt as task distributions change. Training an agent on many diverse tasks can induce zero-shot generalization, but previous work generally evaluates this generalization after training -- with frozen weights. Whether task diversity also improves an agent's ability to continue learning across distribution shifts remains unclear. We introduce Banyan, a GPU-accelerated continual RL domain in which task diversity factors into three independently controllable axes: the map layouts an agent must navigate, the objects it must interact with, and the hierarchical structures of sub-goal dependencies. Across individual distribution shifts, increasing diversity along each axis causes agents to begin training on the new tasks near the performance attained on the previous one, even when the shift changes the structure of the optimal policy. However, as the number of shifts increases, this local transfer does not by itself yield sustained continual learning: longer-horizon tasks plateau, and earlier task distributions are forgotten after later training. Banyan is a benchmark for studying when controlled task diversity produces transferable learning, when that transfer persists, and where it falls short of proper continual learning.

</details>


### [292] [Dive into Waves: Morlet Spectral Transformer for Cross-Subject Emotion Decoding from EEG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00884)
*Jiaxin Qing,Lexin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Morlet Spectral Transformer for cross-subject EEG emotion recognition, using wavelet tokenization, temporal normalization, and frequency-specific spatial projection to outperform existing methods without pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-subject emotion recognition from EEG is practically important but challenging due to weak, noisy, and highly variable signals. Existing approaches either rely on large pretrained foundation models that struggle with cross-subject variability or frequency-domain encoders that have representation mismatches and lack band-specific spatial modeling.

Method: Morlet Spectral Transformer with three key components: Morlet wavelet tokenization for time-frequency representation, long-context baseline removal for temporal normalization, and frequency-specific spatial projection for band-specific channel mixing, integrated with spatiotemporal Transformer backbone.

Result: MST consistently outperforms both large pretrained EEG foundation models and frequency-based methods across all SEED-family datasets without requiring pretraining.

Conclusion: Careful representation design through Morlet Spectral Transformer provides an accurate, cost-effective, and interpretable alternative to large-scale pretraining for cross-subject emotion recognition from EEG.

Abstract: We study cross-subject emotion recognition from EEG, a practically important yet challenging problem in brain-computer interfaces. Unlike tasks with clear waveform signatures, emotion-related EEG signals are primarily encoded in spectral power and are weak, noisy, and highly variable across subjects. Existing approaches rely either on large pretrained EEG foundation models, which require massive data yet still struggle with cross-subject variability, or frequency-domain encoders, which better reflect spectral structure but suffer from mismatched representations, drift-dominated tokenization, and lack of band-specific spatial modeling. In this article, we propose the Morlet Spectral Transformer (MST), built around three key components and integrated with a spatiotemporal Transformer backbone. First, Morlet wavelet tokenization provides a time-frequency representation that matches the multi-scale structure of brain rhythms, and extends classical differential entropy features to a form suitable for Transformers. Second, long-context baseline removal acts as a simple temporal normalization that removes subject-specific drift and redundancy across nearby windows. Third, frequency-specific spatial projection learns a separate channel mixer for each frequency band, capturing interpretable band-specific patterns and reducing cross-channel mixing. We show that, even without pretraining, MST consistently outperforms both large pretrained EEG foundation models and frequency-based methods across all SEED-family datasets. These results suggest that careful representation design can yield an accurate, cost-effective, and interpretable alternative to large-scale pretraining.

</details>


### [293] [Memory-Efficient LLM Training with Dynamic Sparsity: From Stability to Practical Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00888)
*Qiao Xiao,Boqian Wu,Patrik Okanovic,Tomasz Sternal,Maurice van Keulen,Elena Mocanu,Mykola Pechenizkiy,Decebal Constantin Mocanu,Torsten Hoefler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposed SMET: stabilizes Dynamic Sparse Training for LLMs via optimizer warm-up and density-aware learning-rate scaling, reducing memory consumption while enabling efficient sparse pre-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) suffers from optimization instability in LLM training, showing loss spikes after topology updates due to cold-start issues for newly regrown parameters

Method: proposes Sparse Memory-Efficient Training (SMET) with optimizer warm-up and density-aware learning-rate scaling

Result: extensive experiments show SMET enables stable, scalable, and memory-efficient sparse pre-training of LLMs

Conclusion: SMET enables stable, scalable, and memory-efficient sparse pre-training of LLMs, paving the way for sparse training as a practical alternative to dense training.

Abstract: Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) offers a promising paradigm for improving the training and inference efficiency of deep neural networks; however, we find that in large language model training, DST can suffer from optimization instability, manifested as loss spikes after topology updates. In this work, we show that the naive use of standard Adam-based optimizers leads to a cold-start issue for newly regrown parameters, resulting in excessively large updates and disrupted training dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Sparse Memory-Efficient Training (SMET), which stabilizes DST with optimizer warm-up and improves training progress through density-aware learning-rate scaling. SMET further reduces memory consumption by storing gradients and optimizer states only for active parameters. We provide a theoretical analysis of the update behaviors under SMET, showing improved optimization stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SMET enables stable, scalable, and memory-efficient sparse pre-training of LLMs, paving the way for sparse training as a practical alternative to dense training. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/QiaoXiao7282/SMET.

</details>


### [294] [An Exploratory Study into using Machine-Learning for Fast Step-by-step Emulation of Numerical Mechanical Thrombectomy Simulations for Ischemic Stroke](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00892)
*Thijs Stessen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis tests if machine learning surrogates can quickly and accurately emulate numerical physics simulations for mechanical thrombectomy. Results show models can speed up single-step predictions but struggle with stability for complex, long-term simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanical thrombectomy for ischemic stroke requires rapid decision-making under time pressure. While numerical physics simulations could theoretically inform treatment approaches and device selection, they are too slow for practical clinical use. This thesis investigates whether current machine learning surrogates can accurately and quickly emulate these simulations.

Method: The study trained three machine learning surrogate models on two simulations of simplified aspiration procedures, with varying levels of geometric complexity. The evaluation focused on step-by-step emulation accuracy and speed, and tested the impact of specific data augmentations.

Result: Two of the three surrogate models accurately predicted individual simulation steps and provided substantial speed improvements, especially when enhanced with specific data augmentations. However, when emulating simulations with complex geometries over extended time periods, the models demonstrated a lack of stability.

Conclusion: This work provides a foundation for future studies to develop stable methods that scale to realistic numerical physics simulations of mechanical thrombectomy. Overall, while promising results were achieved for singular steps and simpler geometries, stability issues remain for complex, long-term emulation, pointing to the need for further research.

Abstract: The treatment of ischemic stroke using mechanical thrombectomy involves difficult decisions under intense time constraints. Numerical physics simulations can in theory inform operators to make better decisions regarding treatment approaches and device selection, but are too slow to do so in practice. In this thesis, we investigate if current machine learning based surrogates can accurately emulate these simulations in a step-by-step manner while making them significantly faster. To do this we train three surrogate models on two simulations that involve a simplified aspiration procedure, with varying levels of geometric complexity. Our results show that two of our models accurately predict singular simulation steps and provide substantial speedups, especially when combined with specific data augmentations. However, the models showed a lack of stability when emulating simulations with complex geometries over longer time periods. Overall, this work provides a foundation for future studies to develop stable methods that scale to realistic numerical physics simulations of mechanical thrombectomy.

</details>


### [295] [Accuracy, Stability, and Repeated-Run Reliability of Large Language Models on Deterministic Programming Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00920)
*Yongxi Zhou,Lai Yun Choi,Jiaxi Wen,Wenbo Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standard run-level accuracy metrics significantly overestimate actual retry-free performance in LLM code generation; repeated-run evaluation reveals stability gaps up to 17.8% that can change model rankings, showing stability analysis is essential for reliable assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard code-generation benchmarks focus on single-run accuracy or eventual success under repeated sampling, but many deployment settings require stability - consistent outcomes across repeated invocations under the same task description. Current evaluation methods may overstate actual performance.

Method: Developed a repeated-run evaluation protocol with metrics for run-level accuracy, retry-free coverage, and per-problem variability. Applied this to 16 models from 5 provider families on 100 LeetCode-style problems using two prompt templates with 5 repeated runs per problem, yielding 16,000 evaluation instances.

Result: Run-level pass rate overstates retry-free coverage by up to 17.8 percentage points, with the gap largest for mid-performing systems. Although pass rate and stability are strongly correlated (r=0.985), pass rate consistently exceeds retry-free coverage, and this gap can reverse model rankings among closely matched systems. Prompt effects are model-dependent rather than uniformly beneficial.

Conclusion: Repeated-run stability analysis is a necessary complement to conventional accuracy reporting for deterministic text-conditioned generation tasks, as run-level pass rate alone can significantly overstate actual retry-free coverage and lead to misleading model rankings.

Abstract: Run-level pass rate overstates retry-free coverage by up to 17.8 percentage points -- and the gap is largest precisely for mid-performing systems. We investigate this accuracy--stability relationship in large language model (LLM) evaluation for deterministic text-conditioned generation, using programming tasks as a concrete testbed. Standard code-generation benchmarks emphasize single-run accuracy or eventual success under repeated sampling, but many deployment settings also require stability: consistent outcomes across repeated invocations under the same task description. We present a repeated-run evaluation protocol with metrics for run-level accuracy, retry-free coverage, and per-problem variability. On a recency-based benchmark of 100 LeetCode-style problems, we evaluate 16 models from five provider families under two prompt templates with five repeated runs per problem, yielding 16,000 evaluation instances. Although run-level pass rate and perfect stability rate are strongly correlated (r=0.985), pass rate consistently exceeds retry-free coverage -- a gap that reaches 17.8 percentage points and reverses model rankings even among closely matched systems. Prompt effects are model-dependent rather than uniformly beneficial. These results suggest that repeated-run stability analysis is a necessary complement to conventional accuracy reporting for deterministic text-conditioned generation tasks.

</details>


### [296] [Task Structure Reverses Layerwise State Encoding in Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00926)
*Yuhang Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mechanistic study shows state encoding patterns in sequence models depend on both architecture and task, not just architecture; computational structure (not algebraic properties) determines these patterns across five model types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how computational structure versus algebraic structure affects state encoding patterns in sequence models, challenging the view that layerwise state encodings are fixed architectural traits.

Method: Analyze five architectures (Transformers, Mamba, Mamba-2, LSTMs, GRUs) on three tasks (Parity, bounded-depth Dyck-k, non-commutative S_3 permutation composition) using layerwise probing, causal interventions, activation patching, and attribution analysis.

Result: Architectures reverse state encoding profiles when tasks change; S_3 groups with Parity not Dyck across all architectures, indicating computational structure rather than commutativity determines patterns; probing localizes where state is linearly available but not always where computation is bottlenecked; fine-tuned models show different bottleneck patterns than pretrained ones.

Conclusion: Mechanistic signatures are properties of both architecture and task together, not fixed architectural traits.

Abstract: Mechanistic studies of sequence models often treat layerwise state encodings as architectural traits: recurrent models concentrate readable state, attention-based models distribute it. We find that the same architecture reverses this profile when the task changes. Across Transformers, Mamba, Mamba-2, LSTMs, and GRUs, Parity is concentrated late in Mamba and the recurrent baselines and built gradually by Transformer; on bounded-depth Dyck-k the pattern flips. The same flip appears in fine-tuned Mamba-130M and Pythia-160M, and the Pythia Dyck bottleneck persists at 410M. Two explanations are conflated in the literature: algebraic structure (commutativity) versus computational structure (prefix update vs. stack). To separate them we add a third task: non-commutative S_3 permutation composition. S_3 groups with Parity, not Dyck, on layerwise probing across all five architectures and on Mamba-specific Conv1D attribution, so the grouping tracks computational structure rather than commutativity.
  Causal interventions show that, in the 4-layer formal models, linearly readable directions are often functionally necessary and can remain important at out-of-distribution lengths on Parity and Dyck. At pretrained scale the picture splits. Fine-tuned Pythia Dyck has a strong middle-layer bottleneck (L6-L7 ablation drops accuracy by roughly 81% at 160M; broader L4-L18 plateau at 410M), far weaker at the best-probe layer. Pretrained Mamba shows the complementary failure mode: its final layer is highly readable, no single probe direction breaks the task on Parity, Dyck, or S_3, yet mid-position activation patching there recovers about 97-98% of the clean-corrupted logit gap. Probing localizes where state is linearly available, not always where the computation is bottlenecked. Mechanistic signatures are properties of architecture and task together.

</details>


### [297] [Cellular Sheaf Neural Operators for Structure-Preserving Surrogate Modeling of Constrained PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00937)
*Lennon J. Shikhman,Shane Gilbertie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural operators using cellular sheaves on oriented cell complexes preserve physical structure better than standard grid-based approaches for constrained multiphysics systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard neural operators treat geometry/discretization as secondary to field data, ignoring how different physical quantities belong to different geometric entities (vertices, edges, faces, cells) and must satisfy compatibility constraints.

Method: Represent PDE states on oriented cell complexes with learned restriction maps, use incidence/Hodge-informed message passing, and implement learned update heads passing through coboundary/flux maps to enforce constraints structurally.

Result: Improved structure-sensitive diagnostics including rollout behavior, divergence control, spectral error, and equilibrium-regression accuracy on turbulent MHD and fusion-equilibrium surrogate tasks.

Conclusion: Cellular sheaf structure serves as a valuable inductive bias for neural PDE surrogates in constrained multiphysics systems, enhancing physical structure preservation.

Abstract: Neural operators provide fast surrogate models for PDE simulations, but standard architectures often treat geometry and discretization as secondary to field data. Physical states are usually represented as grid-channel stacks, even when different quantities naturally belong on vertices, edges, faces, cells, boundaries, or interfaces and must satisfy compatibility constraints. We propose Cellular Sheaf Neural Operators, a discretization-aware framework for structure-preserving neural PDE surrogates. The method represents PDE states on oriented cell complexes, couples local feature spaces through learned restriction maps, and uses incidence/Hodge-informed message passing to follow computational geometry. Learned update heads pass through coboundary or flux maps, allowing selected constraints to arise from cell-complex structure rather than only from loss penalties. For magnetohydrodynamics, this yields face-based magnetic-flux updates driven by edge electromotive fields and finite-volume-style fluid updates driven by learned face fluxes and cell sources. On turbulent MHD and fusion-equilibrium surrogate tasks, the method improves structure-sensitive diagnostics, including rollout behavior, divergence control, spectral error, and equilibrium-regression accuracy. These results indicate that cellular-sheaf structure is a useful inductive bias for neural PDE surrogates in constrained multiphysics systems.

</details>


### [298] [PRISM: Gauge-Invariant Tangent-Space Differentially Private LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00944)
*Shihao Wang,Xueru Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRISM introduces a novel differential privacy mechanism for LoRA that solves the gauge dependency problem inherent in naive DP-LoRA approaches, preventing unbounded noise amplification while maintaining privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses a fundamental challenge in applying differential privacy to Low-Rank Adaptation: LoRA's low-rank parameterization is inherently non-identifiable, leading to gauge-dependent perturbations when applying DP-SGD directly to the factors, which can cause unbounded noise amplification.

Method: The authors propose PRISM, an intrinsic DP mechanism for LoRA that is gauge invariant by construction. It avoids bilinear noise amplification, admits an efficient low-dimensional noise sampler, and includes a DP-aware, gauge-invariant adaptive update rule that prevents adaptive optimization from amplifying privacy noise.

Result: PRISM yields a closed-form characterization of effective intrinsic noise induced on Z, enabling stable privacy-utility trade-offs through bounded, gauge-invariant perturbations. The method establishes standard (ε,δ)-DP guarantees and improves numerical stability in practice.

Conclusion: PRISM provides a novel solution to the privacy-utility trade-off problem in DP-LoRA by offering gauge-invariant perturbations, bounded noise amplification, efficient noise sampling, and improved numerical stability through adaptive optimization.

Abstract: Applying differential privacy (DP) via DP-SGD to Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a natural approach for privacy-preserving fine-tuning. However, LoRA's low-rank parameterization poses a fundamental challenge. In LoRA, each trainable update is represented as a low-rank matrix $Z = AB^\top$, but this factorization is inherently non-identifiable: many factor pairs $(A,B)$ represent the same update $Z$. As a result, applying DP-SGD directly to the factors induces gauge-dependent perturbations on $Z$, and we show that this naive DP-LoRA can lead to unbounded noise amplification. We propose PRISM, an intrinsic DP mechanism for LoRA that is gauge invariant by construction, avoids bilinear noise amplification, and admits an efficient low-dimensional noise sampler. Moreover, PRISM yields a closed-form characterization of the effective intrinsic noise induced on $Z$, enabling stable privacy-utility trade-offs through bounded, gauge-invariant perturbations. We establish standard $(ε,δ)$-DP guarantees for PRISM and introduce a DP-aware, gauge-invariant adaptive update rule that prevents adaptive optimization from amplifying injected privacy noise, improving numerical stability in practice.

</details>


### [299] [Silent Failures in Federated Personalization of Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00947)
*YongKyung Oh,Alex Bui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated learning of personalized foundation models creates 'silent failures' - hidden trustworthiness issues like bias amplification and fairness collapse that are hard to detect due to privacy constraints, requiring new privacy-preserving evaluation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The convergence of personalized foundation models trained via federated learning with growing regulatory requirements for post-market monitoring creates a distinct class of trustworthiness failures that may remain difficult to detect. Federated learning's privacy constraints limit visibility into model behavior, allowing failures like amplified bias, fairness collapse, and alignment erosion to go unnoticed.

Method: Introduces a taxonomy of six silent failure modes arising from the interaction of foundation model personalization, dataset shift, and core federated constraints. Also analyzes the structural divide between federated benchmarks (evaluating system performance) and centralized trustworthiness benchmarks (assessing model behavior).

Result: Identifies silent failures as an under-recognized class of trustworthiness failures specific to federated foundation models. Reveals structural limitations in existing benchmarks: federated benchmarks lack behavioral insights while centralized trustworthiness benchmarks violate federated privacy constraints.

Conclusion: Silent failures should become a standard diagnostic category for trustworthy federated AI. The paper proposes a research agenda for privacy-preserving behavioral evaluation and argues that privacy-preserving training alone is insufficient for trustworthy deployment.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly personalized on decentralized private data through federated learning and are now deployed at scale under growing regulatory requirements for post-market monitoring. We argue that this convergence creates a distinct and under-recognized class of trustworthiness failures, which we term "Silent Failures." These include amplified bias, fairness collapse, and alignment erosion that may remain difficult to detect because federated learning's privacy constraints limit visibility into model behavior. A landscape analysis of existing benchmarks reveals a structural divide. Federated benchmarks evaluate system performance but provide limited insight into model behavior, whereas centralized trustworthiness benchmarks assess behavior but require model access incompatible with federated privacy. We introduce a taxonomy of six silent failure modes arising from the interaction of foundation model personalization, dataset shift, and core federated constraints. Our analysis shows that privacy-preserving training alone is insufficient for trustworthy deployment. We conclude with a research agenda for privacy-preserving behavioral evaluation and propose that silent failures become a standard diagnostic category for trustworthy federated artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [300] [Explainable deep reinforcement learning reveals energy-efficient control strategies for turbulent drag reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.00949)
*Federica Tonti,Ricardo Vinuesa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Combining MARL with SHAP-guided XDL for turbulent flow drag reduction achieves 34%+ efficiency with minimal actuation cost through pressure-gated strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve drag reduction in wall-bounded turbulent flows by combining Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MARL) with eXplainable Deep Learning (XDL) techniques.

Method: Three SHAP-guided approaches: 1) Reward from SHAP attributions of U-net predicting future velocity field 2) Reward from SHAP attributions of U-net predicting skin-friction coefficient 3) Reward from combined SHAP attributions of two U-nets predicting skin-friction coefficient and wall pressure fluctuations

Result: Best approach achieves 34.44% drag reduction, 34.01% net energy saving with only 0.43% normalized input power, outperforming opposition control by 49.41% drag reduction improvement and reducing actuation cost from 5.90% to 0.43% compared to baseline.

Conclusion: The combined SHAP strategy based on skin-friction coefficient and wall-pressure fluctuations achieves state-of-the-art performance for drag reduction in wall-bounded turbulent flows.

Abstract: We propose a method combining Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and eXplainable Deep Learning (XDL) to reduce drag in wall-bounded turbulent flows.
