### [355] [Feature to Dynamics: Feature-space to Autoregression strategy for Zero-shot Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01289)
*Yifan Wu,Junjie Wu,Kai Wu,Xiaoyu Zhang,Jian Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSA proposes a feature-to-strategy framework for zero-shot time series forecasting that maps interpretable features to autoregressive strategies, outperforming Transformers in generalization when data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundation models for time series forecasting rely on broad data coverage and implicit pattern memorization, which limits generalization when data is scarce or source/target domains are disjoint.

Method: FSA learns a structured mapping from interpretable feature space to autoregressive strategy space, introducing explicit inductive biases that disentangle global trends, periodic components, and local temporal dynamics.

Result: Empirical results show FSA outperforms Transformer-based architectures in controlled zero-shot settings under identical pretraining data, training protocol, and comparable parameter budgets.

Conclusion: FSA demonstrates superior performance compared to Transformer-based architectures in controlled zero-shot settings by employing an interpretable feature-to-strategy mapping approach.

Abstract: Zero-shot time series forecasting aims to predict future values for previously unseen series, requiring models to generalize temporal dynamics beyond the training distribution. While recent foundation models achieve strong in-domain performance through large-scale pretraining, their effectiveness often relies on broad data coverage and implicit pattern memorization, which can limit generalization when data are scarce or source and target domains are disjoint. In this work, we propose FSA, a feature-to-strategy framework for controlled zero-shot univariate forecasting. Instead of directly modeling raw sequences in the observation space, FSA learns a structured mapping from an interpretable feature space to an autoregressive strategy space. This design introduces explicit inductive biases that disentangle global trends, periodic components, and local temporal dynamics, enabling the model to capture transferable time-series structure with fewer data assumptions. Empirical results show that, under identical pretraining data, training protocol, and comparable parameter budgets, FSA outperforms Transformer-based architectures in our controlled zero-shot setting.

</details>


### [356] [Estimating Mutual Information between Time Series and Temporal Event Sequences Across Diverse Analysis Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01602)
*Haoji Hu,Huaqing Mao,Yijun Lin,Xiaowei Jia,Jinwei Zhou,Minoh Jeong,Yao-Yi Chiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Robust mutual information estimator for heterogeneous temporal data that handles continuous time series and discrete event sequences without transformation or discretization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pairwise dependence measures lack principled and robust ways to quantify dependence between heterogeneous data types, especially between continuous time series and discrete temporal event sequences. Current approaches rely on ad hoc transformations or mutual-information estimators that are highly sensitive to quantization, repeated values, and event redundancy, leading to biased or unstable results.

Method: Proposes a nonparametric mutual information estimator that directly measures dependence without data transformation or discretization. Uses a continuous-discrete duality model to handle quantization/repeated-value artifacts and introduces latent event clustering to mitigate bias from event co-occurrence and redundancy.

Result: The proposed estimator shows consistent improvements over existing methods in accuracy, robustness, and interpretability across four tasks: discrete-continuous time-delayed mutual information for causality analysis, global and local temporal repetition discovery, discrete covariate selection for time series forecasting, and continuous feature selection for classification.

Conclusion: This paper introduces a robust, nonparametric mutual information estimator that effectively bridges discrete and continuous mutual information, providing a principled way to quantify dependence between heterogeneous temporal data types. It overcomes limitations of existing approaches and demonstrates strong performance across diverse tasks, positioning it as a general-purpose dependence operator for heterogeneous temporal data.

Abstract: Pairwise dependence measures such as correlation and causality are fundamental to temporal data mining, yet there is still no principled and robust way to quantify dependence between heterogeneous data types, especially between continuous time series and discrete temporal event sequences. Existing approaches rely on ad hoc transformations or mutual-information estimators that are highly sensitive to quantization, repeated values, and event redundancy, leading to biased or unstable results in practice. We propose a nonparametric mutual information estimator that directly measures the dependence between time series and event sequences without data transformation, learning, or ad hoc discretization. Our method models the continuous-discrete duality of real-world time series to handle quantization and repeated-value artifacts and introduces a latent event clustering strategy to mitigate bias from event co-occurrence and redundancy. Together, these yield a robust and unified framework that bridges discrete and continuous mutual information. We evaluate the proposed estimator on four representative tasks: discrete-continuous time-delayed mutual information for causality analysis, global and local temporal repetition discovery, discrete covariate selection for time series forecasting, and continuous feature selection for classification. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show consistent improvements over existing methods in accuracy, robustness, and interpretability, positioning our approach as a general-purpose dependence operator for heterogeneous temporal data, similar to Pearson correlation for homogeneous time series. Code available at: https://github.com/HaojiHu/Multimodal-Temporal-Data-Quantification

</details>


### [357] [FedMTFI: Feature Importance Based Optimized Multi Teacher Knowledge Distillation in Heterogeneous Federated Learning Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01607)
*Nazmus Shakib Shadin,Aaron Cummings,Xinyue Zhang,Bobin Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedMTFI is a federated learning framework combining multi-teacher knowledge distillation with SHAP-based feature importance to handle device heterogeneity and non-IID data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in FL with heterogeneous devices (differing computing power, memory) and non-IID data distribution, which degrade model performance consistency.

Method: Clients are clustered by hardware and model type, train specific models on non-IID data locally, with server aggregating cluster models via FedAvg to create prototypes. These prototypes serve as teachers for global student model via MTKD, enhanced with SHAP-based feature importance weighting.

Result: FedMTFI achieves higher accuracy than traditional FL algorithms, especially under non-IID data conditions, with improved interpretability via SHAP.

Conclusion: FedMTFI is an effective FL framework that handles heterogeneity through cluster-based MTKD with SHAP-based feature importance, achieving superior performance and interpretability compared to traditional FL methods.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a decentralized approach that enables collaborative model training without exposing raw data. Instead of transferring sensitive data, it allows devices to share only model weights, keeping personal data locally and secure. However, in real world settings, the data held by devices is often not evenly distributed and devices mostly differ in computing power and memory capacity. These differences make FL harder to maintain consistent performance across the system. To address these issues, we propose FedMTFI, a novel architecture that combines multi-teacher knowledge distillation (MTKD) with feature importance to improve the FL process in heterogeneous environments. In FedMTFI, clients are clustered based on similar hardware and model types. Each cluster trains a specific model on not independently and identically distributed (non-IID) data. Within a cluster, every client updates that model using only its own local private data. The server then aggregates the locally trained models in each cluster using FedAvg to form multiple prototype models. Then these prototypes serve as teacher models to train a global generalized student model using MTKD. What makes FedMTFI more unique is the integration of Shapley values (SHAP) to emphasize important features during distillation, which enhances both accuracy and interpretability. Experimental results show that FedMTFI achieves higher accuracy than traditional FL algorithms and performs more effectively under non-IID data conditions.

</details>


### [358] [E4GEN: Event-level Explainable Extreme-Enhanced Time-series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01634)
*Lin Jiang,Dahai Yu,Ximiao Li,Guang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: E4GEN is an explainable diffusion framework for generating time series that faithfully captures extreme events through adaptive control mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time-series generation methods fail to faithfully capture extreme events while focusing on overall distributional fidelity.

Method: E4GEN consists of three components: E-Activator for adaptive extreme-control signal activation, E-Predictor for self-driven semantic prediction with data-conditioned training, and E-Control for injecting layer-wise control signals.

Result: Extensive experiments on six datasets with 17 metrics show that E4GEN outperforms state-of-the-art models across multiple dimensions.

Conclusion: E4GEN provides a novel and effective approach for extreme event-aware time-series generation, offering superior performance in overall fidelity, extreme-event fidelity, and downstream utility.

Abstract: Generating realistic time series is essential for scientific research and real-world applications. However, existing methods often emphasize overall distributional fidelity while failing to faithfully capture extreme events. To advance existing research, we propose E4GEN, an explainable diffusion framework for extreme event-aware time-series generation. E4GEN provides systematic insights into when, what, and how to control extreme-event generation through three key components. First, E-Activator learns the dataset-adaptive extreme-control signal activation step during the denoising process without interfering with regular temporal components, including trend and seasonality. Second, E-Predictor determines what control signal to enforce through Self-Driven Semantic Prediction, where each sample derives its own control signal by inferring latent extreme-event information during generation. It also includes a novel Data-Conditioned Training, Noise-Initiated Sampling mechanism to address the issue of unavailable training labels. Third, E-Control specifies how to control extreme-event generation through a trainable Extreme Control Network, which transforms the semantic control signal into layer-wise signals and injects it into the denoising process. We evaluate E4GEN on six datasets with 17 metrics, and extensive experiments show that E4GEN outperforms state-of-the-art models across multiple dimensions, including overall fidelity, extreme-event fidelity, and downstream utility.

</details>


### [359] [Structure and Scale in Simplicial Sequence Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01302)
*Matthew Farrugia-Roberts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper finds preliminary evidence that behavioral scaling laws in deep learning are connected to emergent mechanisms - predictable performance improvements correspond to predictable changes in internal computational structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether there's a connection between two empirical phenomena in large-scale deep learning: behavioral scaling laws (predictable performance gains with scale) and emergent mechanisms (structured internal representations and circuits). The hypothesis is that predictable behavioral changes result from predictable changes in internal computational structure.

Method: The authors trained small transformers to predict outputs of a hidden Markov model and analyzed the correlation between scaling patterns in performance and internal representations. They studied how residual activations linearly encode a belief distribution over latent states in a probability simplex.

Result: The authors found a correlation between scaling patterns in performance and representations in small transformers trained on hidden Markov model prediction tasks. They observed that residual activations linearly encode belief distributions over latent states.

Conclusion: The preliminary evidence supports a connection between scaling laws and emergent mechanisms, where predictable performance improvements from scaling correspond to predictable changes in the model's internal computational structure.

Abstract: Modern large-scale deep learning exhibits two striking empirical phenomena: behavioural scaling laws (predictable performance gains with increasing scale) and emergent mechanisms (structured internal representations and circuits in deep neural networks). We hypothesise that these two phenomena are connected: that predictable changes in behaviour are the result of predictable changes in internal computational structure. In this paper, we report preliminary evidence of such a connection. We find a correlation between scaling patterns in performance and representations in small transformers trained to predict the outputs of a hidden Markov model, for which residual activations are known to linearly encode a belief distribution over latent states in a probability simplex.

</details>


### [360] [DOT-MoE: Differentiable Optimal Transport for MoEfication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01666)
*Udbhav Bamba,Arnav Chavan,Aryamaan Thakur,Steve Teig,Deepak Gupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DOT-MoE converts dense LLMs into sparse MoEs using differentiable optimal transport, achieving better performance than heuristic methods while cutting active parameters by 50%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models face inference efficiency challenges despite performance gains. While MoE architectures can address this, training MoEs from scratch is unstable and expensive. Conversion methods exist but rely on heuristic approaches.

Method: The method formulates decomposition as a Differentiable Optimal Transport problem using Sinkhorn-Knopp iterations for strict expert capacity constraints, and employs Straight-Through Estimators to jointly learn neuron-to-expert assignments and token-to-expert routing end-to-end.

Result: DOT-MoE outperforms baselines like structured pruning, heuristic clustering, and random splitting, retaining 90% of the original dense model's performance while reducing active parameters by 50%.

Conclusion: The proposed DOT-MoE framework effectively converts dense LLMs into sparse MoEs by formulating the decomposition as a differentiable optimal transport problem. This approach outperforms existing methods while maintaining high performance with reduced active parameters.

Abstract: The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven significant performance gains but created substantial challenges in inference efficiency. While Mixture of Experts (MoEs) architectures address this by decoupling model size from inference cost, training MoEs from scratch is often unstable and compute intensive. Conversion of pre-trained dense models into sparse MoEs has emerged as an alternative solution; however, existing methods typically rely on heuristic neuron clustering or random splitting to partition the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) into experts. In this work, we propose DOT-MoE, a novel framework that formulates the decomposition of dense layers as a Differentiable Optimal Transport (DOT) problem. Instead of static heuristics, we model neuron assignment as a balanced transport problem, utilizing differentiable Sinkhorn-Knopp iterations to enforce strict expert capacity constraints. Furthermore, we utilize Straight-Through Estimators (STE) to jointly learn the discrete neuron-to-expert assignment and the token-to-expert routing policy end-to-end. Extensive experiments across multiple architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that DOT-MoE significantly outperforms structured pruning, heuristic clustering, and random-split baselines, retaining 90% of the original dense model's performance while reducing active parameters by 50%.

</details>


### [361] [When Hard Negatives Hurt: Bridging the Generative-Discriminative Gap in Hard Negative Synthesis for Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01304)
*Zhicheng Zhang,Jiwei Tang,Kuicai Dong,Xiaopeng Li,Jieming Zhu,Jingyu Li,Qianhui Zhu,Fengyuan Lu,Wang Jiaheng,Gang Wang,Hai-Tao Zheng,Zhaocheng Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalNeg overcomes limitations of hard negative mining by generating LLM-synthesized negatives using counterfactual perturbation and entropy maximization to bridge the generative-discriminative gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hard negative mining faces limitations: bounded by corpus availability, selected by retriever score rather than diagnostic value, and susceptible to false positives. LLM-based synthesis offers an alternative but often degrades performance due to generative-discriminative gap.

Method: 1. CoT-guided counterfactual perturbation: decomposes query requirements and surgically violates them to create controlled hardness negatives. 2. Query-view entropy maximization: disperses negatives across similarity spectrum to suppress source-dependent shortcuts.

Result: The proposed CausalNeg approach generates unconstrained, targeted negatives free from false positive risk while preventing the failure modes of naive LLM-based negative generation.

Conclusion: CausalNeg successfully bridges the generative-discriminative gap in retrieval training by generating targeted, hard negatives while preventing shortcut learning, establishing a new paradigm for effective retriever training.

Abstract: Hard negative mining has become the dominant strategy for training retrievers, yet it faces intrinsic limitations: negatives are bounded by corpus availability, selected by retriever score rather than diagnostic value, and increasingly contaminated by false positives as the retriever improves. LLM-based synthesis offers a principled alternative, where negatives that are unconstrained, targeted, and free from false positive risk. But we show that naively incorporating generated negatives into contrastive learning often degrades retrieval performance. We identify and formalize the root cause as a generative-discriminative gap: LLM generation optimizes for fluent, plausible text, while contrastive learning demands strategic violations of relevance at the decision boundary. Our analysis reveals two compounding failure modes: discriminative-agnostic generation, where the LLM lacks an explicit model of query information needs and defaults to generic or topic-drifted text that provides no contrastive signal; and source-dependent shortcuts, where distributional artifacts enable the model to distinguish negatives by origin rather than relevance, causing gradient drift that actively corrupts optimization. To close this gap, we propose CausalNeg consisting of two main modules: (1) CoT-guided counterfactual perturbation for data construction: decomposes why a document satisfies a query into explicit information requirements, then surgically violates individual requirements to construct negatives with controlled, interpretable hardness. (2) Query-view entropy maximization during training: disperses generated negatives across the similarity spectrum, minimizing the mutual information between source identity and similarity scores to suppress shortcut exploitation. We make our code publicly available at https://github.com/mzhangzhicheng/CausalNeg.

</details>


### [362] [Two-Fidelity Best-Action Identification for Stochastic Minimax Tree](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01708)
*Peter Chen,Xi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 2FFS algorithm for fixed-confidence BAI in stochastic minimax trees adaptively uses cheap biased evaluations and expensive accurate evaluations, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses tradeoff in modern AI planning where deep minimax search and MCTS face fundamental limitations: heuristic evaluations are cheap but biased, while accurate rollouts are reliable but prohibitively expensive.

Method: Proposes 2FFS algorithm that combines minimax-style fast expansion with MCTS-style stochastic sampling, adaptively using cheap biased evaluations and expensive accurate evaluations for local certification.

Result: Proves fixed-confidence correctness, establishes finite stopping for exact identification, gives polynomial-depth cost upper bound, and shows substantial reduction in samples and computational operations compared to existing BAI-MCTS baselines.

Conclusion: 2FFS is an effective two-fidelity tree-search algorithm for BAI in stochastic minimax trees, offering substantial improvements over existing methods.

Abstract: We study fixed-confidence best-action identification (BAI) in stochastic minimax trees. This problem is increasingly relevant in modern AI planning, where deep minimax search and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with language model long rollouts face a fundamental tradeoff: heuristic evaluations are cheap but biased, while accurate rollouts are reliable but prohibitively expensive. We propose 2FFS, a two-fidelity tree-search algorithm that brings multi-fidelity flat bandit ideas into trees. The algorithm combines minimax-style fast expansion with MCTS-style stochastic sampling, adaptively deciding when to exploit cheap biased evaluations and when to invoke expensive accurate evaluations for local certification. We prove fixed-confidence correctness, establish finite stopping for exact identification, and give a polynomial-depth cost upper bound for general-depth trees. Across numerical stochastic-tree experiments, 2FFS uses substantially fewer samples and computational operations comparing to existing BAI-MCTS baseline.

</details>


### [363] [FAiT: Frequency-Aware Inverted Transformer for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01306)
*Peng He,Yao Liu,Yanglei Gan,Run Lin,Yuxiang Cai,Qiao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAiT is a Frequency-Aware inverted Transformer for multivariate time series forecasting that addresses the low-pass nature of self-attention using Inverted Attention to recover high-frequency signals and Dynamic Temporal-Frequency Modulation to handle evolving spectral characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Transformer self-attention acts as a low-pass filter that smooths high-frequency signals crucial for sharp local changes in multivariate time series forecasting. Most existing frequency-domain approaches use fixed spectral bases and uniform modulation, overlooking that real-world series have time-evolving spectral characteristics.

Method: FAiT introduces two key innovations: 1) Inverted Attention which interprets attention maps as learnable low-pass operators and constructs complementary high-pass branches by inverting attention matrices, and 2) Dynamic Temporal-Frequency Modulation which synthesizes instance-conditioned weights to adaptively recalibrate spectral sub-band energy.

Result: FAiT outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based and frequency-enhanced baselines on widely used benchmarks.

Conclusion: Extensive experiments show FAiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based and frequency-enhanced baselines while maintaining computational efficiency, demonstrating the effectiveness of addressing both internal spectral bias and evolving temporal-frequency dynamics.

Abstract: While Transformer-based architectures have established themselves as a dominant paradigm in Multivariate Time Series Forecasting (MTSF), their core self-attention mechanism inherently functions as a low-pass filter, systematically smoothing out high-frequency signals vital for sharp local changes. Recent advancements have increasingly incorporated frequency-domain operations to address this bias, however, most existing designs rely on fixed spectral bases and apply sequence-wise (uniform) modulation, implicitly assuming a time-invariant frequency response. This overlooks a key property of real-world series that their spectral characteristics often evolve over time, making uniform modulation insufficient for capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics. To tackle these limitations, we propose FAiT, a Frequency-Aware inverted Transformer. Specifically, FAiT rectifies the spectral bias internally through Inverted Attention, which interprets the attention map as a learnable low-pass operator and constructs a dedicated complementary high-pass branch by inverting the attention matrix to recover attenuated transient signals. Furthermore, FAiT introduces Dynamic Temporal-Frequency Modulation (DTFM), which synthesizes instance-conditioned weights to adaptively re-calibrate the energy of spectral sub-bands, enabling fine-grained control over evolving multi-scale patterns. Extensive experiments on widely used benchmarks demonstrate that FAiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based and frequency-enhanced baselines, while maintaining computational efficiency.

</details>


### [364] [Fair Finetuning Mitigates Distribution Inference Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01719)
*Rakshit Naidu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FFt is a fairness-based defense against distribution inference attacks that establishes a theoretical connection between Equalized Odds fairness and privacy protection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses like differential privacy don't explore the link between fairness constraints and distributional leakage in ML models vulnerable to distribution inference attacks.

Method: Fair Fine-tuning (FFt): fine-tuning a trained model on samples from the complementary distribution under Equalized Odds constraint.

Result: FFt consistently reduces adversarial accuracy gap below detection threshold across six datasets, with ACS Income gap falling from ~15% to under 4%. The theoretical bound Adv(A, M_f) ≤ Δ_EO * W characterizes performance.

Conclusion: FFt provides the first formal bound connecting a model's measured Equalized Odds disparity to its adversarial advantage in the DIA game, offering a unified approach to fairness and privacy defenses.

Abstract: Machine learning models trained on sensitive data can inadvertently leak population-level information about their training distributions -- a threat known as distribution inference attack (DIA). An adversary with black-box access can infer sensitive demographic properties, such as subgroup proportions, without observing any training data directly. While defenses such as differential privacy and property unlearning have been proposed, the link between fairness constraints and distributional leakage remains unexplored. We propose Fair Fine-tuning (FFt): a trained model is fine-tuned on samples from the complementary distribution under an Equalized Odds (EO) constraint. We provide a complete theoretical characterization, proving the tight bound $\text{Adv}(\mathcal{A},M_f) \le Δ_{\text{EO}} \cdot W$, where $W$ quantifies how distinguishable the two training distributions are by their sensitive-attribute composition. We also establish a necessary condition for FFt to reduce adversarial advantage and prove tightness of the bound. We evaluate across six datasets spanning tabular (ACS Income, COMPAS, German Credit), image (UTKFaces), and NLP (Bias in Bios) modalities. Rehearsal-based FFt consistently reduces the adversarial accuracy gap below the detection threshold $τ!=!0.1$ across all settings; on ACS Income, the gap falls from $\sim!15%$ to under $4%$. Our work provides the first formal bound connecting a model's measured EO disparity directly to its adversarial advantage in the DIA game, opening a new avenue for unified fairness-and-privacy defenses.

</details>


### [365] [Conditioned free-energy density of proteins using unbalanced solutions to constraint satisfaction problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01329)
*Pratik Worah,Subhash Khot,Srinivasa Varadhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Log-partition function computation reduces to unbalanced 2→1 norm optimization; polynomial-time SDP algorithm developed. Applied to Ubiquitin protein to explore conformational landscape and identify flexible regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop efficient computational methods for exploring free-energy landscapes of proteins, specifically to identify flexible regions while preserving native secondary structure, using conditioned inhomogeneous Curie-Weiss spin Hamiltonians as a framework.

Method: Develop a polynomial-time SDP algorithm for computing log-partition functions by reducing the problem to an unbalanced 2→1 norm computation, with proof of lower bounds for unbalance achieved. Apply to protein Ubiquitin by starting from known crystal structure and exploring alternative backbone conformations.

Result: Successfully reduces log-partition function computation to unbalanced 2→1 norm optimization; polynomial-time SDP algorithm designed with proven lower bounds. Applied to Ubiquitin: explores alternative backbone conformations across free-energy landscape and identifies flexible regions while maintaining native secondary structure.

Conclusion: The research demonstrates how converting protein free-energy landscape exploration into an unbalanced 2→1 norm optimization problem enables efficient computational identification of flexible regions while preserving native structure, with applications validated on Ubiquitin.

Abstract: We show that computing the log-partition function (free-energy) of conditioned inhomogeneous Curie--Weiss spin Hamiltonians reduces to an unbalanced $2 \to 1$ norm computation, and design a polynomial-time SDP algorithm for this problem with a lower bound proof for the amount of unbalance achieved. Applied to the protein Ubiquitin, the framework starts from a known crystal structure, explores alternative backbone conformations across the free-energy landscape, and identifies flexible regions of the protein while preserving its native secondary structure.

</details>


### [366] [Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems: A New Foundation for Trustworthy Autonomous Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01722)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems (PDDS) for coordinating heterogeneous environments with deterministic code, stochastic models, and autonomous agents, arguing deterministic execution can no longer be the universal assumption, and outlines five architectural pillars plus new failure classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional distributed systems assume participants execute with deterministic semantics. However, modern systems increasingly integrate autonomous reasoning engines, stochastic models, and policy-driven actors that produce divergent reasoning paths while achieving equivalent outcomes. This challenges the universal applicability of deterministic participant assumptions.

Method: The paper introduces the concept of Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems (PDDS) as a research and engineering model. It demonstrates that classical distributed computing models are a special case of PDDS. The method involves defining five architectural pillars: Protocol-Driven Development, Verifiable Agentic Infrastructure, Autonomous State Control Planes, Semantic Quorum Assurance, and Epistemic State Replication.

Result: The paper shows classical distributed computing models form a zero-ambiguity special case of PDDS. It defines five architectural pillars for post-deterministic infrastructure and introduces Epistemic State Replication which extends consistency models from data to knowledge visibility. A taxonomy of failure classes specific to PDDS is also defined.

Conclusion: This paper proposes Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems (PDDS) as a new framework for heterogeneous environments where deterministic and non-deterministic agents coexist. It argues deterministic execution can no longer be the universal assumption, outlines five key architectural pillars for PDDS, and defines new failure classes specific to this context.

Abstract: For decades, distributed systems have typically assumed that correct participants execute protocol-specified behavior with stable, externally defined, and deterministic semantics. Classical theory has extensively parameterized network timing, communication topologies, and failure domains, but this participant model has remained comparatively fixed. The integration of autonomous reasoning engines, stochastic model-driven agents, and policy-driven actors into cloud control planes, incident response systems, and financial infrastructure challenges the universality of this assumption. These agents often produce divergent reasoning paths, distinct operational traces, and heterogeneous internal representations while achieving semantically equivalent and correct outcomes. In this paper, we introduce Post-Deterministic Distributed Systems (PDDS) as a research and engineering model for coordinating heterogeneous environments where deterministic code, stochastic models, and autonomous agents coexist. We show that classical distributed computing models form a zero-ambiguity special case of this participant-general model. We do not argue that deterministic systems disappear; rather, deterministic execution can no longer serve as the universal participant assumption for autonomous infrastructure. Finally, we outline five architectural pillars of post-deterministic infrastructure: Protocol-Driven Development, Verifiable Agentic Infrastructure, Autonomous State Control Planes, Semantic Quorum Assurance, and Epistemic State Replication. Epistemic State Replication extends persistence and consistency models from data visibility to knowledge visibility, enabling agentic memory, Verifiable Semantic Rollback, and coherence across reasoning participants. We also define a taxonomy of failure classes that arise in this setting.

</details>


### [367] [Shortcut to Nowhere: Demystifying Deep Spurious Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01723)
*Guanrong Xu,Jessica Li,Hao Wang,Yuzhe Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Deep Spurious Regression (DSR) to address continuous spurious correlations in regression tasks, proposing novel strategies that exploit attribute similarities in both label and feature spaces to generalize across attribute-label combinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world regression often exhibits shortcuts (attributes spuriously correlated with continuous targets) that fail catastrophically under deployment shifts. Existing spurious correlation research focuses on classification, but many real-world tasks require continuous prediction where hard label boundaries don't exist.

Method: Exploit similarity among spurious attributes in both label and feature spaces by accounting for nearby targets and related groups while calibrating both label and learned feature distributions across attributes.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world DSR datasets spanning computer vision, environmental sensing, and LLM regression verify the superior performance of the proposed strategies.

Conclusion: The paper fills an important gap by establishing benchmarks and techniques for studying spurious correlations in continuous prediction through Deep Spurious Regression (DSR), which has been validated across diverse real-world domains.

Abstract: Real-world regression often exhibits shortcuts: attributes that are spuriously correlated with continuous targets in training, yet unreliable under deployment shifts; regressing targets using such shortcuts may fail catastrophically at test time. Existing studies on spurious correlations focus primarily on classification, where labels are categorical and groups are naturally defined. However, many real-world tasks require continuous prediction, where hard label boundaries or discrete group-label pairs do not exist. We define Deep Spurious Regression (DSR) as learning from regression data with attribute-label confounding, addressing continuous spurious correlations, and generalizing to all attribute-label combinations at test time. Motivated by the intrinsic difference between classification and regression shortcuts, we propose to exploit the similarity among spurious attributes in both label and feature spaces, thereby accounting for nearby targets and related groups while calibrating both label and learned feature distributions across attributes. Extensive experiments on common real-world DSR datasets that span computer vision, environmental sensing, and large language model (LLM) regression verify the superior performance of our strategies. Our work fills the gap in benchmarks and techniques for studying spurious correlations in continuous prediction.

</details>


### [368] [Sample Complexity and Decision-Theoretic Guarantees for Bayesian Model Averaging over Decision Trees with Catalan-Exponential Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01340)
*Livija Jakaite,Vitaly Schetinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Establishes closed-form rational commitment thresholds for Bayesian model averaging over decision trees with Dirichlet-Multinomial leaf models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine when Bayesian model averaging weights over decision trees contain sufficient epistemic information to justify committed exploitation of the averaging distribution, addressing a key practical question in Bayesian decision-making.

Method: Develops closed-form analysis for Bayesian decision trees with Dirichlet-Multinomial leaf models and a Catalan-exponential tree-size prior, using theoretical analysis to derive commitment thresholds.

Result: Derives complete non-asymptotic theory of rational commitment thresholds in closed form for the specified Bayesian decision tree framework.

Conclusion: The paper establishes rational commitment thresholds for Bayesian model averaging over decision trees, providing a complete non-asymptotic theory for when BMA weights justify committed exploitation of the averaging distribution.

Abstract: We ask: when do Bayesian model averaging (BMA) weights over decision trees carry sufficient epistemic information to justify committed exploitation of the averaging distribution? We answer this question in closed form for Bayesian decision trees (BDTs) with Dirichlet-Multinomial leaf models and a Catalan-exponential tree-size prior (Schetinin&Jakaite, 2025), establishing a complete non-asymptotic theory of rational commitment thresholds.

</details>


### [369] [Adaptive Auto-Harness: Sustained Self-Improvement for Agentic System Deployment on Open-Ended Task Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01770)
*Zewen Liu,Zhan Shi,Yisi Sang,Bing He,Minhua Lin,Tianxin Wei,Dakuo Wang,Benoit Dumoulin,Wei Jin,Hanqing Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Adaptive Auto-Harness for sustained harness construction in open-ended task streams, outperforming existing auto-harness systems through evolution/adaptation loss decomposition and multi-agent routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current auto-harness systems are evaluated on fixed offline benchmarks, but real deployments involve open-ended task streams where performance degrades over time due to heterogeneous tasks, growing histories, and shifting problem distributions.

Method: The framework decomposes the gap to an oracle harness into evolution loss and adaptation loss. The system implements: a stateful multi-agent evolver, a harness tree with solve-time routing, and human-steering hooks for cases where history lacks needed signal.

Result: Adaptive Auto-Harness outperforms five existing auto-harness baselines across prediction-market, security-competition, and event-forecasting streams. Ablations show gains come from better construction, routing, and targeted human steering.

Conclusion: The Adaptive Auto-Harness system outperforms existing auto-harness baselines across multiple domains including prediction markets, security competitions, and event forecasting by effectively decomposing and addressing evolution and adaptation losses.

Abstract: Auto-harness systems such as A-Evolve, GEPA, and Meta-Harness improve LLM agents by optimizing prompts, skills, tools, memories, and supporting infrastructure from execution feedback, but they are typically evaluated on fixed offline benchmarks. Real deployments instead present open-ended task streams: histories grow without a fixed endpoint, heterogeneous tasks require different harnesses, and problem distributions shift over time. These challenges make a single repeatedly and densely updated harness brittle, causing performance degradation as accuracy peaks early and then declines. This motivates sustained harness construction with task-wise adaptation. We introduce Adaptive Auto-Harness, a framework and system for such streams. The framework decomposes the gap to an oracle harness into evolution loss and adaptation loss. The system addresses these losses with a stateful multi-agent evolver, a harness tree with solve-time routing, and human-steering hooks for cases where history lacks the needed signal. Across prediction-market, security-competition, and event-forecasting streams, Adaptive Auto-Harness outperforms five existing auto-harness baselines and ablations attribute gains to better construction, routing, or targeted human steering. Code is available in https://github.com/A-EVO-Lab/AdaptiveHarness .

</details>


### [370] [All Models are Wrong, Knowing Where is Useful: On Model Uncertainty in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01363)
*Bernd Frauenknecht,Devdutt Subhasish,Artur Eisele,Friedrich Solowjow,Sebastian Trimpe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: uncertainty-aware MBRL framework addresses model inaccuracy exploitation through targeted uncertainty handling, enabling data-efficient and safe learning on real hardware


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: model inaccuracies in learned dynamics models are often exploited by agents, limiting the effectiveness of MBRL methods for robotics applications like data-efficient learning and safe exploration

Method: leveraging probabilistic dynamics models and designing mechanisms to mitigate model exploitation by effectively handling uncertainty in the learned models

Result: successful demonstrations of learning directly on hardware and safe exploration through uncertainty-aware approaches

Conclusion: uncertainty-aware model-based reinforcement learning provides a promising framework for addressing key challenges in robotics including data efficiency and safety through targeted handling of model inaccuracies

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) infers information about the environment from a learned dynamics model and bears the potential to address open problems such as data efficient and safe learning in robotics. However, inaccuracies of the learned dynamics model are typically exploited by the agent, substantially hampering the capabilities of MBRL methods. We present a framework for dealing with inaccuracies of probabilistic models through targeted handling of uncertainty that effectively mitigates model exploitation. We present recent successes in learning directly on hardware and safe exploration, and discuss future directions for uncertainty-aware MBRL.

</details>


### [371] [FLARE: Diffusion for Hybrid Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01774)
*Yuchen Zhu,Jing Shi,Chongjian Ge,Hao Tan,Yiran Xu,Wanrong Zhu,Jason Kuen,Koustava Goswami,Rajiv Jain,Yongxin Chen,Molei Tao,Jiuxiang Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: string


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: string

Method: string

Result: string

Conclusion: string

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) large language models (LLMs) have achieved broad practical success, but sequential decoding remains a key bottleneck for low-latency deployment. Recent efficient-inference work has progressed along two axes: reducing the cost of each model invocation through efficient architectures, and reducing serial decoding steps through parallel generation. Hybrid attention backbones address the former, while diffusion language models (dLLMs) pursue the latter via iterative parallel denoising. Combining these advantages remains challenging: AR-to-dLLM conversion often fails to preserve seed-checkpoint capability, and hybrid-attention recurrent states and masking constraints make diffusion training and serving nontrivial. We present FLARE, a systematic conversion framework for hybrid-attention LLMs. Our analysis identifies transfer data quality as the primary determinant of capability preservation, outweighing loss formulation and attention-mask design. The resulting framework combines a token-equal AR-and-diffusion objective, hardware-aware kernels, and unified inference, enabling one checkpoint to support both AR-style verified decoding and diffusion-style parallel denoising. Starting from strong AR checkpoints with limited post-training data, FLARE is competitive with leading open-source dLLMs across model scales and delivers consistent throughput gains over open-source dLLM baselines in single-GPU concurrent serving. Our results further suggest that practical dLLMs are limited not only by decoding algorithms, but also by transfer data quality and the training inefficiency of current block-diffusion objectives, motivating joint design of data, objectives, architectures, and inference systems.

</details>


### [372] [From Performance to Viability: A Bootstrap Framework for Latent-Space Representation Learning in Adaptive Biological Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01374)
*Jacques Raynal,Pierre Slangen,Elsa Raynal,Jacques Margerit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A bootstrap framework for latent-space representation learning in adaptive biological systems, moving beyond performance-based analysis through five methodological levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Similar performances in adaptive biological systems can arise from distinct organizations, and configurations that appear comparable at a given time may follow different trajectories over time. This limitation motivates moving beyond performance-based interpretation without assuming a complete mechanistic model.

Method: The paper introduces a bootstrap framework for latent-space representation learning in adaptive biological systems, with five levels: observable performance, dynamic organization, latent organization, longitudinal viability, and internal predictive approximation. The framework is demonstrated through reanalysis of three previously reported gait-occlusion studies.

Result: The framework formalizes how performance analysis leads to latent organization, how static latent organization leads to longitudinal viability, and how observed viability leads to internal predictive approximation, showing how increasingly informative representations emerge from observational insufficiencies.

Conclusion: The paper proposes a methodological framework rather than specific algorithms, datasets, or protocols, emphasizing its bootstrap approach to representation learning in adaptive biological systems.

Abstract: Observable performance is commonly used to characterize biological systems. In adaptive systems, however, similar performances may arise from distinct organizations, and configurations that appear comparable at a given time may follow different longitudinal trajectories. This limitation motivates a methodological framework for moving beyond performance-based interpretation without assuming a complete mechanistic model in advance. This article proposes a bootstrap framework for latent-space representation learning in adaptive biological systems. Here, bootstrap is used in a methodological and epistemological sense: new analytical levels are introduced when the preceding representation becomes insufficient to account for observed adaptive dynamics. The framework is organized around five levels: observable performance, dynamic organization, latent organization, longitudinal viability, and internal predictive approximation. The framework is illustrated by three previously reported gait--occlusion studies, used here only as a methodological case sequence and not as new experimental evidence. The article formalizes how performance analysis led to latent organization, how static latent organization led to longitudinal viability, and how observed viability led to internal predictive approximation. The contribution is not a new learning algorithm, clinical protocol, or dataset, but a bootstrap framework for latent-space representation learning describing how increasingly informative representations can emerge from observational insufficiencies in adaptive biological data.

</details>


### [373] [Learning Implicit Bias in Generative Spaces for Accelerating Protein Dynamics Emulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01833)
*Kaihui Cheng,Zhiqiang Cai,Wenkai Xiang,Zhihang Hu,Siyu Zhu,Tzuhsiung Yang,Yuan Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adding a history-dependent bias to generative protein emulators accelerates exploration of rare conformations, improving diversity and coverage of low-energy states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative emulators of protein dynamics sample only within their training distribution and tend to revisit known states rather than explore rare conformations over long horizons.

Method: History-aware score estimator biases generation away from previously sampled structures; score-based refinement reprojects drifted samples onto data manifold; distance-weighted bias with environment-support term.

Result: Method increases diversity by 35% on DynamicPDB-80; on 12 zero-shot Fast-Folding proteins, bias alone reaches unbiased emulator coverage ~15x faster; bias+refinement reaches coverage ~37x faster while covering ~3x as many low-energy states.

Conclusion: Generative emulators pretrained on protein dynamics can produce diverse, low-energy trajectories faster when augmented with a history-dependent bias and refinement. The approach avoids revisiting known states and efficiently explores rare conformations.

Abstract: Generative emulators of protein dynamics produce plausible trajectories at a fraction of the cost of molecular dynamics, but they inherit their training distribution and tend to revisit known states rather than reach rare ones under long-horizon extrapolation. Inspired by classical enhanced sampling, we introduce an implicit, history-dependent bias in the generative space of a pretrained emulator. Specifically, a history-aware score estimator augments the frozen emulator with a distance-weighted bias that steers reverse-time sampling away from previously generated structures, regularized by an environment-support term. To preserve structural validity at long horizons, a score-based refinement step re-projects drifted samples onto the data manifold using the frozen emulator. Our experiments demonstrate that the method (i) raises diversity by $35\%$ on DynamicPDB-80; (ii) on $12$ zero-shot Fast-Folding proteins, the learned bias alone reaches the unbiased emulator's coverage up to ${\sim}15\times$ faster, and pairing it with refinement reaches the coverage up to ${\sim}37\times$ faster while covering ${\sim}3\times$ as many low-energy states. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [374] [Turning Back Without Forgetting: Selective Backward Refinement for Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01379)
*Anushka Tiwari,Kaiyi Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SABER enables backward knowledge transfer in prompt-based continual learning by selectively refining earlier task prompts using task-correlation criteria and safe updates in non-interfering directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-based parameter-efficient continual learning mitigates catastrophic forgetting by isolating task-specific prompts, but this isolation limits later tasks from improving earlier ones, leaving backward knowledge transfer underexplored.

Method: Selective bAckward refinement for positive Backward knowledge transfER (SABER) - a replay-free framework that determines when backward refinement is beneficial using complementary task-correlation criteria based on prompt-gradient geometry and loss-distribution similarity, and performs refinement safely by restricting updates to non-interfering directions in prompt parameter space.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple continual learning benchmarks and diverse pretrained backbones (T5-Large, LLaMA, Qwen) demonstrate SABER consistently achieves positive backward transfer while maintaining strong overall average performance.

Conclusion: SABER successfully addresses the backward transfer limitation in prompt-based continual learning by introducing selective backward refinement mechanisms based on task-correlation criteria and non-interfering updates, achieving positive backward knowledge transfer while maintaining strong overall performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: While prompt-based parameter-efficient continual learning mitigates catastrophic forgetting by isolating task-specific prompts, this isolation also limits later tasks from improving earlier ones, leaving backward knowledge transfer underexplored. We address this limitation by proposing Selective bAckward refinement for positive Backward knowledge transfER (SABER), a replay-free framework that enables controlled backward transfer in prompt-based continual learning. SABER determines when backward refinement is beneficial using complementary task-correlation criteria based on prompt-gradient geometry and loss-distribution similarity, and how to perform refinement safely by restricting updates to non-interfering directions in the prompt parameter space. Extensive experiments across multiple continual learning benchmarks and diverse pretrained backbones, including T5-Large, LLaMA, and Qwen, demonstrate that SABER consistently achieves positive backward transfer while maintaining strong overall average performance. Code is available at https://github.com/OptMN-Lab/SABER-ICML-2026/.

</details>


### [375] [Why Do Time Series Models Need Long Context Windows?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01999)
*Luca Butera,Giovanni De Felice,Andrea Cini,Cesare Alippi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Longer context windows in time series forecasting help by reducing uncertainty about which data-generating process is active. The paper shows forecasting involves two tasks: identifying the generative process and predicting future values, and proves why longer windows are needed for optimal performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand why longer observation windows improve forecasting performance in deep learning models, beyond just capturing long-range dependencies, by analyzing how global forecasting models actually leverage input observations.

Method: The authors propose a framework that separates forecasting into two objectives: generative process identification (inferring the data-generating process) and conditional forecasting (predicting future values). They prove theoretical bounds on required window sizes and demonstrate how decoupling GPI and CF improves scalability.

Result: Theoretical proof that even for processes with memory length P, input windows larger than P are necessary for minimal error. Experimental validation on synthetic and real-world data shows that decoupling GPI and CF improves computational scalability without compromising accuracy.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that viewing multivariate time series forecasting through the dual objectives of generative process identification (GPI) and conditional forecasting (CF) provides deeper insights into why longer context windows help, and shows how decoupling these tasks can improve computational scalability without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Modern deep learning models for forecasting groups of time series rely on increasingly longer observation windows. However, the benefit of increasing the window size is often simply attributed to capturing long-range dependencies, and broader discussion on how global forecasting models leverage input observations has been limited. In this paper, we show that forecasting groups of time series involves two objectives: (i) generative process identification (GPI), i.e., inferring the specific process generating the input sequence, and (ii) conditional forecasting (CF), i.e., predicting future values given input observations. From this perspective, optimal predictions can be interpreted as an average over plausible data-generating processes, weighted by their likelihood given the input window. This suggests another explanation for the benefits of long context windows: they reduce the uncertainty about which specific process is generating the input time series during operation. We prove that even for processes with memory length $P$, an input window size strictly larger than $P$ is necessary to achieve the minimum attainable error. Finally, we show how decoupling GPI and CF can improve computational scalability without compromising accuracy. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data validate our insights and their relevance for designing forecasting architectures.

</details>


### [376] [OpenWebRL: Demystifying Online Multi-turn Reinforcement Learning for Visual Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02031)
*Rui Yang,Qianhui Wu,Yuxi Chen,Hao Bai,Wenlin Yao,Hao Cheng,Baolin Peng,Huan Zhang,Tong Zhang,Jianfeng Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenWebRL is an open framework for training visual web agents using online reinforcement learning directly on live websites, achieving state-of-the-art performance on challenging web benchmarks with significantly less training data than previous approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current open visual web agents heavily depend on expensive supervised post-training over curated web trajectories, creating scalability bottlenecks due to the high cost of demonstration collection and limited coverage of the diverse, ever-changing open web. While online RL has shown promise for text-based agents, it remains underexplored for visual web agents.

Method: The authors introduce OpenWebRL, an open framework covering the full training pipeline: scalable live-browser infrastructure, supervised initialization, multimodal context management, trajectory-level success judging, and efficient multi-turn policy optimization. They use this framework to train OpenWebRL-4B with only 0.4K initialization trajectories and 2.2K open-ended RL training tasks.

Result: OpenWebRL-4B achieves 67.0% success on Online-Mind2Web and 64.0% on DeepShop benchmarks, outperforming prior open agents of similar or larger scale and remaining competitive with proprietary systems like OpenAI CUA and Gemini CUA. The framework demonstrates superior efficiency with minimal training data.

Conclusion: The work offers a practical path toward building more capable, reproducible, and cost-efficient open web agents by demonstrating the effectiveness of online RL for visual web agents. The authors will release training data, models, and code to support future research in this direction.

Abstract: Building capable visual web agents requires long-horizon reasoning, precise grounding, and robust interaction with dynamic real-world websites. Despite rapid progress, the strongest systems remain largely proprietary, while open agents still depend heavily on supervised post-training over large collections of curated web trajectories. This dependence creates a major scalability bottleneck: high-quality demonstrations are expensive to collect, and static datasets offer limited coverage of the diverse, ever-changing open web. Although online RL has shown promise for text-based agents, its potential for training visual web agents directly on live websites remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we introduce OpenWebRL, an open framework for training visual web agents with online multi-turn RL on real websites. OpenWebRL covers the full training pipeline, including scalable live-browser infrastructure, supervised initialization, multimodal context management, trajectory-level success judging, and efficient multi-turn policy optimization. Using this framework, we train OpenWebRL-4B, which establishes a new open-source state of the art on challenging live-web benchmarks. With only 0.4K initialization trajectories and 2.2K open-ended RL training tasks, OpenWebRL-4B achieves 67.0% success on Online-Mind2Web and 64.0% on DeepShop, outperforming prior open agents of similar or larger scale and remaining competitive with proprietary systems including OpenAI CUA and Gemini CUA. Beyond strong benchmark performance, we systematically study the key design choices that make online RL effective for visual web agents, and analyze how RL improves agentic reasoning. Overall, our work offers a practical path toward building more capable, reproducible, and cost-efficient open web agents. We will release our training data, models, and code to support future research.

</details>


### [377] [GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA: A Near-optimal Algorithm for Low-precision Quantization with Low-rank Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01412)
*Shihao Zhang,Rayan Saab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA combines low-bit quantization with low-rank correction in a single training-free pass, achieving better compression-quality tradeoffs than separate quantization and LoRA steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggressive low-bit post-training quantization often degrades model quality, and existing solutions that add low-rank corrections to quantized weights (W≈Q+LR) are suboptimal when applied separately from quantization.

Method: Proposes GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA: a training-free algorithm that incorporates low-rank correction directly into GPTQ-style quantization by augmenting the calibration Hessian. Also introduces Bid-Up, a fixed-grid quantization refinement step that can alternate with optimal low-rank compensation.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows layer-wise reconstruction error bounds where GPTQ's dependence on ||X||_F² is replaced by rank-r residual ||X-X_r||_F². Experiments on Qwen3 language models and DeiT vision transformers demonstrate improvements over GPTQ and GPTQ followed by separate LoRA compensation.

Conclusion: GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA provides a principled way to combine quantization and low-rank compensation, achieving near-optimal compression theoretically and better empirical performance than sequential approaches.

Abstract: Post-training quantization is widely used for compressing large neural networks, but aggressive low-bit quantization can significantly degrade model quality. A common remedy is to augment the quantized weights with a low-rank correction, leading to approximations of the form $W\approx Q+LR$. In this paper, we study this low-precision plus low-rank representation through the layer-wise reconstruction objective $\|XW-X(Q+LR)\|_F^2$, where $X$ is a calibration matrix. We establish, to our knowledge, the first information-theoretic lower bounds for this problem under finite-alphabet and bounded low-rank compensation constraints. We then propose GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA, a training-free algorithm that incorporates the low-rank correction directly into a GPTQ-style quantization pass by appropriately augmenting the calibration Hessian. For the choice $L=V_r$, where $V_r$ contains the top right singular vectors of $X$, we prove layer-wise reconstruction error bounds in which the usual GPTQ dependence on $\|X\|_F^2$ is replaced by the rank-$r$ residual $\|X-X_r\|_F^2$, up to regularization terms. Under natural structural assumptions, these bounds match the information-theoretic lower bounds in their dominant scaling, up to constants and mild factors. We also introduce Bid-Up, a fixed-grid quantization refinement step that can be alternated with optimal low-rank compensation with guaranteed non-increasing layer-wise reconstruction error. Experiments on Qwen3 language models and DeiT vision transformers show that GPTQ-intrinsic LoRA improves over GPTQ and GPTQ followed by low-rank compensation, with additional gains from refinement loops.

</details>


### [378] [Target localization, identification and sensing using latent symmetries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01421)
*David Dukov,Malte Röntgen,Bryn Davies*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sensing method using arrays with latent symmetries to detect intruder scatterers by measuring symmetry breaking, with Bayesian inference and neural networks outperforming dictionary approaches for noisy measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to develop a new sensing approach using arrays with designed latent ("hidden") symmetries. Traditional sensing methods may not fully exploit these hidden structural properties, and the researchers want to demonstrate that latent symmetries can be effectively used for sensing applications, particularly for detecting and characterizing intruders in three-dimensional systems.

Method: The researchers use the capacitance matrix as a canonical model for three-dimensional hybridization. They design arrays with latent symmetries and introduce an "intruder" scatterer that breaks these symmetries. They analyze the degree of symmetry breaking for each symmetry to identify the intruder's radius and localize its position. They compare three approaches: dictionary-based methods, Bayesian inference, and artificial neural networks (multi-layer perceptrons).

Result: The study demonstrates that latent symmetries can be successfully exploited for sensing problems. The researchers show they can identify the radius of an intruder scatterer and localize its position by analyzing symmetry breaking. Bayesian inference and artificial neural networks perform better than dictionary-based approaches in the presence of measurement noise. This represents the first successful application of latent symmetries to sensing problems and the first observation of latent symmetries in three-dimensional open systems that cannot be approximated by sparse graphs.

Conclusion: Latent symmetries provide a powerful framework for sensing applications, enabling detection and characterization of intruders in three-dimensional systems. The approach shows practical promise, especially when combined with robust statistical methods like Bayesian inference or machine learning techniques that handle measurement noise effectively.

Abstract: We show that an array of scatterers which has been designed to have latent ("hidden") symmetries can be used as a sensor. We use the capacitance matrix as a canonical model for three-dimensional hybridisation and study how the introduction of an "intruder'' scatterer breaks the latent symmetries. By analysing the degree to which each symmetry is broken, we identify the radius of the intruder and localize its position. This can be achieved using a dictionary-based approach, however Bayesian inference or an artificial neural network (multi-layer perceptron) perform better in the presence of measurement noise. To our knowledge, this is the first time latent symmetries have been exploited successfully for sensing problems. It is also the first time latent symmetries have been observed in a three-dimensional open system that cannot be approximated by a sparse graph.

</details>


### [379] [How Hard Can It Be? Hardness-Aware Multi-Objective Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02119)
*Jiangwei Chen,Xinyuan Niu,Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,Zhengyuan Liu,Nancy F. Chen,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HAMU is a novel machine unlearning algorithm that uses a hardness measure based on data similarity to guarantee specified improvements in forget quality while minimizing retain utility degradation through constrained optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unlearning algorithms use weighted loss combinations but lack guarantees that both objectives (improving forget quality and maintaining retain utility) can be improved by a specified extent for all forget and retain data.

Method: The paper proposes HAMU, a theoretically-grounded constrained optimization approach that quantifies the hardness of reconciling both objectives based on similarity between forget and retain data. The algorithm updates model weights using this hardness measure to guarantee specified forget quality improvement while minimizing retain utility degradation.

Result: HAMU demonstrates superior performance over baselines on both image and text datasets using large models. The algorithm is applicable to non-convex models, parallelizable, and provides a mechanism to detect when retain utility degradation is unavoidable, allowing for informed stopping decisions.

Conclusion: HAMU addresses a critical limitation in machine unlearning by providing theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements, making it a readily deployable solution for real-world unlearning scenarios where privacy, copyright, and bias concerns require selective data influence removal.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific forget training data due to privacy, copyright or bias concerns while maintaining the model performance on the remaining retain data. Existing unlearning algorithms, such as optimizing a weighted combination of losses, have tried to achieve these objectives of improving forget quality and maintaining retain utility. However, they do not guarantee that these objectives can be improved by a specified extent for all forget and retain data. In this work, we address this limitation with a novel and theoretically-grounded approach from a constrained optimization perspective. Firstly, we identify that the hardness of reconciling both objectives can be quantified by the similarity between the forget data and the retain data. Next, we derive an unlearning algorithm (HAMU) with the overall goal of guaranteeing a specified improvement in forget quality while minimizing the retain utility cost/degradation by updating the model weights based on our hardness measure. Our hardness measure also informs users when retain utility degradation is unavoidable, i.e., both objectives cannot be improved simultaneously, and stopping should be considered. Our algorithm is applicable to non-convex models and is easily parallelizable, making it readily deployable in real-world scenarios. We empirically demonstrate HAMU's superior performance over baselines on both image and text datasets using large models. Our code is available at https://github.com/aoi3142/HAMU.

</details>


### [380] [Learning-based Directed Graph Abstraction of Combinatorial Spaces for Order-Preserving Search in Mixed-Combinatorial Nonlinear Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01425)
*Gishnu Madhu,Feng Liu,Souma Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a GNN-based abstraction model for combinatorial spaces in mixed-combinatorial nonlinear programming, integrating it with optimization algorithms to achieve better performance than traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional integer/binary encoding for combinatorial spaces introduces spurious relations, increases dimensionality, and requires compatibility constraints. There's a need for better representations for combinatorial spaces in mixed-combinatorial nonlinear programming problems common in engineering design and planning applications.

Method: Proposes a structured abstraction of combinatorial space using an Edge Field Graph Network (EFGN) that learns a mapping from an undirected fully connected graph of combinations to a directed graph indicating improvement directions. Integrates this GNN-based recommender system with particle swarm optimization and genetic algorithm solvers.

Result: The GNN-based recommender consistently achieves better mean optimum values and robustness across multiple runs compared to baseline solvers using indexified combinations on three benchmark nonlinear problems with varying numbers of combinations and variables.

Conclusion: The direction-aware abstraction model provides a more scalable and interpretable retrieval of combinations, demonstrating the utility of GNN-based combinatorial space abstraction for solving mixed-combinatorial nonlinear programming problems.

Abstract: Mixed-combinatorial nonlinear programming (MCNLP) problems arise in many engineering design and planning applications, e.g., due to categorical, component, and geometric design choices, as well as joint task and motion planning. Traditional representations of combinatorial spaces, such as integer or binary encoding, often introduce spurious relations, increase dimensionality, and require additional compatibility constraints. Instead, this paper draws on recent developments in robot planning and vehicle/network routing domains that aim to learn search heuristics over combinatorial spaces using graph neural networks (GNNs). More specifically, this paper presents a first-of-its-kind structured abstraction of the combinatorial space by learning a mapping from an undirected fully connected graph of combinations to a directed graph indicating improvement directions using an Edge Field Graph Network (EFGN). To demonstrate the utility of this new way of abstracting the combinatorial space in solving MCNLPs, we adopt a recent optimization framework that purely searches over the non-combinatorial (e.g., continuous) variables and retrieves the best-suited combination for each candidate design by using the abstraction model, akin to a recommender system. The presented direction-aware abstraction model provides a potentially more scalable and interpretable retrieval of combinations compared to the original recommendation system in that framework. For evaluation, the proposed method is integrated with a well-known particle swarm optimization and genetic algorithm solvers on three benchmark nonlinear problems with varying numbers of combinations and variables. Compared to baseline solvers using indexified combinations, the GNN-based recommender consistently achieves better mean optimum values and robustness across multiple runs.

</details>


### [381] [Variational Learning for Insertion-based Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02133)
*Yangtian Zhang,Zhe Wang,Arthur Gretton,Rex Ying,David van Dijk,Michalis K. Titsias,Jiaxin Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic framework for learning insertion order in variable-length insertion models that formalizes correspondence between insertion trajectories and permutations, enabling native variable-length generation with learned data-driven insertion order preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing non-monotonic sequence generation methods (like masked diffusion models) have limitations: they're order-agnostic, rely on fixed-length grids, and can't support variable-length generation or adaptive insertion order. The paper addresses the need for models that can learn data-driven insertion preferences and handle variable-length sequences.

Method: The paper introduces a probabilistic framework with a bijective correspondence between insertion trajectories and permutations, enabling exact reparameterization of data likelihood as a sum over permutations. It proposes the Insertion Process (IP) - a stochastic generative model that jointly learns where to insert, what to insert, and when to terminate, trained via permutation-based variational inference.

Result: Experiments on goal-conditioned planning and molecular string generation demonstrate that learning insertion order improves both modeling quality and generalization, particularly in domains without canonical left-to-right structure. Unlike prior fixed-canvas approaches, IP natively supports variable-length generation and learns data-driven preferences over insertion orders.

Conclusion: The proposed Insertion Process framework successfully addresses limitations of existing non-monotonic generation methods by enabling variable-length generation and learning of insertion order preferences, demonstrating practical advantages in domains without inherent sequential structure.

Abstract: Non-monotonic sequence generation methods, such as masked diffusion models, provide a flexible alternative to left-to-right autoregressive modeling by allowing tokens to be generated in non-fixed and prescribed orders. Despite their practical advantages, most existing non-monotonic models are order-agnostic and rely on a fixed-length grid, limiting their ability to support variable-length generation and adaptive insertion order. In this work, we introduce a probabilistic framework for learning insertion order in variable-length insertion models. We formalize a bijective correspondence between insertion trajectories and permutations, which enables an exact reparameterization of the data likelihood as a sum over permutations. Building on this result, we propose the Insertion Process (IP), a stochastic generative model that jointly learns where to insert, what to insert, and when to terminate, trained via permutation-based variational inference. Unlike prior fixed-canvas approaches, IP natively supports variable-length generation and learns data-driven preferences over insertion orders. Experiments on goal-conditioned planning and molecular string generation demonstrate that learning insertion order improves both modeling quality and generalization in domains without a canonical left-to-right structure.

</details>


### [382] [Leaf Spectral Reflectance Prediction Using Multi-Head Attention Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01432)
*Parastoo Farajpoor,Alireza Pourreza,Mohammadreza Narimani,Ashraf El-Kereamy,Matthew W. Fidelibus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Developed a grapevine-specific leaf spectral reflectance prediction model using multi-head attention neural network that outperforms generalized radiative transfer models like PROSPECT-PRO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate modeling of leaf spectral reflectance from physiological and biochemical traits is essential for advancing remote sensing applications in plant science and precision agriculture.
