Expanding the supply water temperature range by 10 K yields negligible additional savings (USD 0.03/day), and further expansion triggers physical constraint violations. We additionally uncover a discount factor coupling (gamma_eff = 0.891) shrinking the effective planning horizon from 8.3 h to 46 min -- a benchmark-wide issue warranting audit. Systematic ablation across planning horizon, reward weights, and observation enrichment confirms all pre-filled-buffer configurations cluster within 0.7% (USD 37.18--USD 37.42), demonstrating that equipment minimum power -- not algorithmic design -- imposes the binding constraint.

</details>


### [408] [ATLAS: Agentic Test-time Learning-to-Allocate Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01667)
*Peijia Qin,Qi Cao,Pengtao Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ATLAS is an agentic test-time scaling framework where an LLM orchestrator controls the entire reasoning process, deciding when to gather evidence, when to stop, and how to synthesize answers, outperforming fixed orchestration methods with fewer computational resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time scaling has become a major way to improve large language model reasoning, but its orchestration has remained designer-engineered with fixed parameters (sample budget, refinement loop, scoring rule, search policy). This leaves the model in charge of solving but not of orchestration, creating a suboptimal division of labor.

Method: ATLAS uses an LLM orchestrator with an 'explore' action to control test-time scaling. The orchestrator decides when to gather more evidence, when to stop, and how to synthesize the final answer. The framework allows extensible action space with optional solver, reasoning effort, or prompting strategy specifications. ATLAS-MM extends this by exposing solver choice as an additional action dimension.

Result: ATLAS achieved strong results across benchmarks: 56.00% on HLE-Verified, 82.29% on LiveCodeBench, 85.75% on GPQA-Diamond, and 23.71% on BabyVision while using far fewer API calls than fixed-workflow baselines. The multi-model extension ATLAS-MM further improved HLE-Verified to 60.00% and LiveCodeBench to 85.63%, with consistent gains on GPQA-Diamond and BabyVision. Ablations showed that direct synthesis by the orchestrator is crucial for performance gains.

Conclusion: ATLAS demonstrates that LLM-based orchestration of test-time scaling can outperform fixed orchestration strategies while using fewer resources, with potential for further gains through multi-model extensions.

Abstract: Test-time scaling has become a major way to improve large language model reasoning, but its orchestration has remained designer-engineered: a fixed sample budget, a fixed refinement loop, a fixed scoring rule, or a fixed search policy decides how compute is spent, leaving the model in charge of solving but not of orchestration. We introduce ATLAS, an agentic test-time scaling framework in which an LLM orchestrator owns the control loop end-to-end. Through a single action, explore, which dispatches a fresh independent solver on the original problem, the orchestrator decides whether to gather more evidence, when to stop, and how to synthesize the final answer; the action space is extensible, with each explore call optionally specifying solver, reasoning effort, or prompting strategy. We evaluate ATLAS on four benchmarks covering scientific question answering, code generation, and multimodal reasoning under a Claude Sonnet 4.6 backbone, where it reaches 56.00% on HLE-Verified, 82.29% on LiveCodeBench, 85.75% on GPQA-Diamond, and 23.71% on BabyVision while using far fewer API calls than fixed-workflow baselines. A multi-model extension, ATLAS-MM, that exposes solver choice as an additional action dimension further improves HLE-Verified to 60.00% and LiveCodeBench to 85.63%, with consistent gains on GPQA-Diamond and BabyVision. Ablations replacing the orchestrator's direct synthesis with a separate integrator degrade or fail to improve accuracy on three of four benchmarks, consistent with the role of stateful evidence management in producing the gains.

</details>


### [409] [RDA: Reward Design Agent for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01672)
*Hojoon Lee,Ajay Subramanian,Ben Abbatematteo,Vijay Veerabadran,Pedro Matias,Karl Ridgeway,Nitin Kamra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RDA is a VLM-based agentic framework that improves reward design for robotic tasks by incorporating semantic understanding through visual trajectory evaluation, addressing the alignment gap between task success and human intentions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reinforcement learning requires hand-crafted reward functions that are slow to design and often misaligned with human intentions. While recent approaches like Eureka automate reward generation using LLMs, they rely on coarse success rate feedback, lacking semantic insight into learned behaviors, resulting in policies that achieve goals but poorly follow task instructions.

Method: RDA uses a VLM-based agentic framework that decomposes tasks, visually evaluates trajectories, summarizes failure modes, and iteratively revises reward code. This injects semantic understanding into reward design by analyzing the actual behavior of learned policies rather than just success rates.

Result: Across 12 tabletop manipulation tasks (ManiSkill) and 4 whole-body manipulation tasks (HumanoidBench), RDA produces policies substantially more instruction-aligned than other baselines while maintaining comparable task success rates.

Conclusion: Semantic understanding through visual evaluation of trajectories is crucial for better reward design and policy alignment with human instructions, addressing a key limitation in current automated reward generation approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has enabled the acquisition of impressive robotic skills, but typically requires hand-crafted reward functions that are slow to design and difficult to align with human intentions. Recent work, such as Eureka, automates reward design by using an LLM to iteratively generate and refine reward code from task descriptions. However, they rely on coarse feedback signals such as success rate, which provide little semantic insight into the learned behavior. As a result, their trained policies achieve the final goal but are frequently poorly aligned with task instructions. We introduce the Reward Design Agent (RDA), a VLM-based agentic framework that injects semantic understanding into reward design. RDA decomposes tasks, visually evaluates trajectories, summarizes failure modes, and iteratively revises reward code to better align with task instructions. Across 12 tabletop manipulation tasks from ManiSkill and 4 whole-body manipulation tasks from HumanoidBench, RDA produces policies substantially more instruction-aligned than those of other baselines, while achieving comparable task success rates. Videos and the generated reward code are available on https://nitinkamra1992.github.io/reward-design-agent.

</details>


### [410] [CANARY: Zero-Label Detection of Fine-Tuning Contamination in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01695)
*Swapnil Parekh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CANARY is a zero-label framework that detects hidden contamination in poisoned models by analyzing hidden-state geometry via Sparse Autoencoders, achieving perfect detection at 1% contamination levels and enabling remediation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversaries can implant latent harmful behavior by poisoning as few as 1% of fine-tuning examples, which remains invisible to output-level defenses until contamination exceeds 7.5%. This creates a critical security vulnerability in model supply chains.

Method: Introduces CANARY (Contamination Auditor via Neural Activation Representation Yield), a zero-label checkpoint auditor that detects hidden contamination through two forward passes over unlabeled prompts. It projects hidden-state differences through a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to filter style noise and isolate semantic drift.

Result: CANARY achieves AUROC = 1.000 at 1% contamination (95% CI = [0.997, 1.000]; Cohen's d = 3.28) across four model architectures and two training paradigms, detecting contamination 7.5x earlier than output-level methods. It shows zero false positives on benign fine-tuning and robustness to adaptive attacks. The SAE feature basis enables a complete governance pipeline with 5x higher latent harm detection, 4.2x red-teaming improvement, and reduces harm from 70% to 10% without perplexity penalty.

Conclusion: CANARY is the first zero-label framework to detect, verify, prioritize, and remediate supply-chain contamination from hidden states alone, offering a comprehensive defense pipeline against adversarial poisoning attacks.

Abstract: Adversaries can implant latent harmful behavior by poisoning as few as 1% of fine-tuning examples. The contamination is invisible to every output-level defense: harmful behavior lies dormant in the model's hidden-state geometry and does not appear in generated text until contamination exceeds 7.5%. We introduce CANARY (Contamination Auditor via Neural Activation Representation Yield), a zero-label checkpoint auditor that detects this hidden shift directly from two forward passes over an unlabeled prompt set. CANARY projects the hidden-state difference through a Sparse Autoencoder, filtering style noise to isolate meaningful semantic drift. It achieves AUROC = 1.000 at 1% contamination (95% CI = [0.997, 1.000]; Cohen's d = 3.28) across four model architectures and two training paradigms, 7.5x below where any output-level method fires, with zero false positives on benign fine-tuning and full robustness to style-matching and gradient-noise adaptive attacks. The same SAE feature basis drives a complete governance pipeline: SAE-filtered amplification surfaces latent harm at a 5x higher rate than standard generation; score-ranked prompts yield 4.2x red-teaming lift; and suppressing a handful of contamination-specific features at inference time reduces harm from 70% to 10% with no perplexity penalty. CANARY is the first zero-label framework to detect, verify, prioritize, and remediate supply-chain contamination from hidden states alone.

</details>


### [411] [Decentralized Instruction Tuning: Conflict-Aware Splitting and Weight Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01717)
*Minsik Choi,Geewook Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MERIT introduces a decentralized instruction tuning method that partitions multimodal task mixtures by gradient conflict analysis, trains partitions independently, and merges weights once to achieve better performance than joint training with minimal communication overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction tuning for large multimodal models faces bottlenecks when scaling to heterogeneous task mixtures due to gradient interference and heavy synchronization requirements, requiring a more efficient decentralized approach.

Method: The method involves: 1) estimating dataset-level gradient conflicts, 2) partitioning the mixture along top PCA conflict axes, 3) fine-tuning each partition independently without inter-partition communication, and 4) merging via token-weighted averaging based on theoretical insights about weight merging benefits.

Result: On Qwen2.5-VL-3B with 136 Vision-FLAN tasks, MERIT improved the 8-benchmark average from 54.3 (joint training) to 57.0. It scales to 7B models on 1.6M-example, 176-source mixtures, matching or exceeding centralized joint training with minimal overhead, and transfers to text-only FLAN.

Conclusion: MERIT provides an efficient decentralized instruction tuning pipeline that addresses gradient interference and synchronization bottlenecks through conflict-aware partitioning and one-time weight merging, demonstrating superior performance and scalability over traditional joint training.

Abstract: Instruction tuning aligns large language models, including multimodal ones, with diverse user intents, but scaling to heterogeneous mixtures is hindered by gradient interference and bandwidth-heavy synchronization. We ask whether these two bottlenecks can be addressed jointly by training parts of the mixture independently and reconciling them once in parameter space. We develop a local quadratic theory inside a shared flat basin that yields three results: weight merging produces a curvature-weighted variance reduction; PCA-aligned conflict splitting maximizes this gain along high-curvature directions; and merging additionally acts as spectral filtering with implicit norm regularization. These results directly motivate MERIT, a decentralized merge-ready instruction-tuning pipeline that estimates dataset-level gradient conflicts, partitions the mixture along the top PCA conflict axes, fine-tunes each partition independently with no inter-partition communication, and merges once via token-weighted averaging. On Qwen2.5-VL-3B with 136 Vision-FLAN tasks, MERIT improves the 8-benchmark average from 54.3 (joint training) to 57.0. The same recipe scales to a 7B model on a 1.6M-example, 176-source mixture -- matching or exceeding centralized joint training with minimal cost overhead -- and transfers to text-only FLAN. Our code is available at https://github.com/naver-ai/merit.

</details>


### [412] [A Note on Stability for Orthogonalized Matrix Momentum with Client Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01720)
*Da Chang,Qiankun Shi,Lvgang Zhang,Yu Li,Ruijie Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical analysis of finite-sample generalization bounds for distributed optimization with matrix-valued parameters, orthogonal momentum updates, and partial client participation, showing optimal error scaling under certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand generalization behavior in distributed optimization settings where only subsets of clients participate each round, with matrix-valued parameters and orthogonal momentum updates, addressing challenges like heterogeneous client data and unequal sample sizes.

Method: Uses coupled-neighbor stability recursion and weighted concentration analysis to derive finite-round upper-tail guarantees. Considers client-sampled distributed optimization with matrix-valued parameters, orthogonalized momentum updates, fixed aggregation weights, and independent heterogeneous client data.

Result: Derives a generalization bound that depends on client-selection counts through amplification factor Y_i(𝒞). In uniform full-participation full-batch regime, achieves optimal Õ(n^{-1}+n^{-1/2}) scaling when horizon-dependent amplification terms are controlled. Shows matrix-orthogonalization must be Lipschitz along paired trajectories (satisfied by regularized polar-type maps and normalized Newton-Schulz orthogonalizers).

Conclusion: Generalization in distributed matrix optimization requires careful handling of orthogonalization stability. Regularization or smoothing is necessary for unregularized operations, as demonstrated by a one-dimensional counterexample showing gap/smoothing/regularity conditions are essential for finite-sample guarantees.

Abstract: We study finite-sample generalization for a client-sampled distributed optimization scheme with matrix-valued parameters and orthogonalized momentum updates. The central quantity is the gap between the population and empirical objectives at the returned model when only a subset of clients participates in each round. Under independent heterogeneous client data, unequal local sample counts, and fixed aggregation weights, we derive a finite-round upper-tail guarantee from a coupled-neighbor stability recursion and a weighted concentration step. The bound keeps the client-selection counts through the amplification factor \(Y_i(\mathcal C)\); in the uniform full-participation full-batch regime, it yields \(\widetilde{\mathcal O}(n^{-1}+n^{-1/2})\) scaling whenever the horizon-dependent amplification terms are controlled. The matrix-orthogonalization rule is required to be Lipschitz along paired trajectories, a condition satisfied by regularized polar-type maps and normalized finite-step Newton--Schulz orthogonalizers. For the unregularized matrix sign, the same argument requires coupled spectral separation, whereas Gaussian smoothing gives a finite-round smoothed variant. A one-dimensional counterexample shows why a gap, smoothing, or regularity condition is necessary.

</details>


### [413] [Tree-Guided Identify-Then-Exploit: A Unified Framework of Best Arm Identification and Regret Minimization for Dueling Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01799)
*Pu Wang,Yao-Xiang Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TG-ITE is the first unified framework for dueling bandits that handles best-arm identification, weak regret, and strong regret with efficient O(N) guarantees without requiring stronger assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address three widely studied objectives in N-armed stochastic dueling bandits under Condorcet-winner assumption (best-arm identification, weak regret, and strong regret) with a unified approach that eliminates the need for stronger assumptions.

Method: Tree-Guided Identify-Then-Exploit (TG-ITE) framework with shared tree-guided identification approach to find a high-confidence incumbent using O(N) comparisons, followed by varied exploitation strategies tailored to specific objectives.

Result: TG-ITE achieves: (1) O(N) sample complexity in BAI without stronger assumptions; (2) O(N) weak regret using first winner-stays-style algorithm; (3) O(N log T) strong regret matching specialized approaches; (4) joint optimization of BAI and weak regret with O(N) guarantees for both, eliminating existing O(log N) gap.

Conclusion: The trade-off between best-arm identification and regret minimization in dueling bandits is relatively benign, and the proposed TG-ITE framework efficiently handles all three major objectives with competitive guarantees.

Abstract: We study $N$-armed stochastic dueling bandits under the Condorcet-winner assumption, where three widely adopted objectives are considered: best-arm identification (BAI), weak regret, and strong regret. We propose Tree-Guided Identify-Then-Exploit (TG-ITE), the first unified framework to tackle all these objectives to our knowledge. Without requiring stronger assumptions, we propose a shared tree-guided identification approach to find a high-confidence incumbent within $O(N)$ comparisons. We further propose varied exploitation strategies to utilize this warm-start stage to optimize the specific objectives at hand. This methodology enables our approach to (1) achieve $O(N)$ sample complexity in BAI without commonly adopted stronger assumptions; (2) build the first winner-stays-style algorithm to achieve $O(N)$ weak regret; (3) enjoy the same $O(N \log T)$ guarantee as specialized strong-regret approaches; (4) realize the joint optimization of BAI and weak regret with $O(N)$ guarantees for both, eliminating the sub-optimal gap of $O(\log N)$ in the existing approach. Our results provide evidence that the trade-off between BAI and regret minimization is relatively benign in dueling bandits.

</details>


### [414] [Mos-Gen: A Generative Molecular Framework for Mosquito Insecticide Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01846)
*Lina Wang,Yaning Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mos-Gen: AI framework generates novel allicin-based mosquito insecticides with 78% hit rate in experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mosquito-borne diseases cause over 700,000 deaths annually, and conventional chemical insecticides face serious resistance problems. Existing AI approaches lack de novo generation of novel molecular scaffolds for insecticide development.

Method: Proposed Mos-Gen: motif-aware generative collaborative framework coupling pretrained molecular representation model Uni-Mol with variational autoencoder (VAE), specifically tailored for design of disulfide-containing allicin derivatives as mosquito insecticides.

Result: Generated novel molecular candidates; 14 compounds (9 predicted positives, 5 predicted negatives) synthesized and tested. Hit rate among predicted positives reached 78%, while predicted negatives showed no mosquitocidal activity. Framework demonstrated high-precision screening capability.

Conclusion: Mos-Gen successfully bridges the gap in de novo generation of novel insecticide scaffolds, providing a highly effective and ecologically sustainable alternative to conventional chemical insecticides with demonstrated experimental validation.

Abstract: Mosquito-borne infectious diseases cause more than 700000 deaths worldwide each year. The long-term use of conventional chemical insecticides has induced serious resistance problems, creating an urgent need to develop novel, highly effective, and ecologically sustainable alternatives. While existing artificial intelligence approaches in this domain have focused primarily on activity prediction and classification, they leave a critical gap in the de~novo generation of novel molecular scaffolds. In this study, we propose Mos-Gen, a motif-aware generative collaborative framework that couples the pretrained molecular representation model Uni-Mol with a variational autoencoder (VAE), specifically tailored for the design of disulfide-containing allicin derivatives as mosquito insecticides. Among the generated candidates, fourteen compounds -- comprising nine predicted positives and five predicted negatives -- were selected for chemical synthesis and experimental validation. The hit rate among the predicted positives reached 78%, whereas none of the predicted negatives exhibited mosquitocidal activity. These experimental results fully validated the high-precision screening capability of the Mos-Gen framework.

</details>


### [415] [ContinuousBench: Can Differentially Private Synthetic Text Improve Capabilities?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01849)
*Peihan Liu,Lucas Rosenblatt,Weiwei Kong,Natalia Ponomareva,Gautam Kamath,Rachel Cummings,Roxana Geambasu,Yu Gan,Lillian Tsai,Alex Bie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ContinuousBench is a continuously regenerated benchmark that measures capability gain from differentially private synthetic text, showing that current DP synthesis methods fail to transfer knowledge even at high privacy budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for DP text synthesis rely on tasks that are nearly solvable without training, making it unclear whether DP synthetic data can genuinely transmit new knowledge from sensitive corpora or substitute for original data access.

Method: Introduced ContinuousBench - a continuously and automatically-regenerated benchmark released quarterly with new training corpora paired with QA sets designed to be: (1) unsolvable without the corpus, and (2) learnable under DP when knowledge is supported by hundreds of independent records. Researchers generate DP synthetic data and use standardized training/evaluation harness to measure gains. Two tracks: Geminon (procedurally-generated fictional creatures) and News (stream of newly crawled news articles).

Result: While standard benchmarks are nearly saturated, ContinuousBench reveals that non-private synthesis transfers substantial knowledge, but state-of-the-art DP synthesis methods generally fail to do so even at ε=100 (very high privacy budget).

Conclusion: Current differentially private text synthesis methods are inadequate for transferring genuine knowledge from sensitive corpora, highlighting the need for better DP synthesis techniques and more meaningful evaluation benchmarks.

Abstract: Differentially private (DP) text synthesis promises to unlock sensitive corpora for model training, but it remains unclear whether DP synthetic data transmits genuinely new knowledge and capabilities present only in those corpora. This is because existing evaluations rely on tasks that are nearly solvable without training, so strong benchmark performance does not establish that DP synthesis can substitute original data access. Thus, we introduce ContinuousBench, a continuously and automatically-regenerated benchmark that measures capability gain from DP synthetic text. Each quarter, a new release pairs a never-before-seen training corpus with a derived QA set, constructed to be: (1) unsolvable sans-corpus; and (2) learnable under DP, as the tested knowledge is supported by hundreds of independent records. Researchers produce DP synthetic data from the training corpus and run our standardized training and evaluation harness on their synthetic data to measure gains. We instantiate two tracks: Geminon, a procedurally-generated dataset about fictional creatures; and News, a stream of newly crawled public news articles. Although standard benchmarks are nearly saturated, on ContinuousBench we find that non-private synthesis transfers substantial knowledge from the original corpus, while state-of-the-art DP synthesis methods generally fail to do so, even at $\varepsilon=100$.

</details>


### [416] [A Theoretical Framework for Self-Play Theorem Proving Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01861)
*Thomas Chen,Zhiyuan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a theoretical framework for self-play theorem proving, showing exponential growth with well-connected theorem graphs and addressing empirical issues by proposing a diversity-maximizing conjecturing algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the self-improvement capabilities of self-play algorithms for theorem proving theoretically, and to address the empirical issue where self-play algorithms tend to generate artificially complex and non-fundamental theorems.

Method: The authors formalize theorems as a graph, introduce primitive assumptions about prover guarantees and conjecturer access, analyze a prover-conjecturer system using reversible random walks for exponential growth under connectivity, propose a diversity measure for training distributions, develop an improved conjecturing algorithm that maximizes diffusion similarity between neighboring theorems, and use contrastive learning to compute diffusion similarity via Euclidean embeddings.

Result: If the underlying theorem graph is well-connected, a prover-conjecturer system with reversible random walk-based conjecturing can exponentially grow the set of proved theorems. The improved diversity-maximizing conjecturing algorithm addresses the issue of generating artificially complex theorems by maximizing diffusion similarity.

Conclusion: The theoretical framework provides mathematical guarantees for self-play algorithms in theorem proving under connectivity assumptions, while the diversity-maximizing conjecturing algorithm addresses empirical issues of theorem complexity to ensure generation of fundamental theorems, offering a principled approach to self-improving theorem provers.

Abstract: Self-play, a type of training algorithm that enables a model to self-improve, has recently shown promising empirical results in the context of formal theorem proving using Large Language Models (LLMs). (Dong & Ma, 2025) instantiate self-play with two cooperating agents: a prover, which proves theorems, and a conjecturer, which generates new theorems as a curriculum to the prover. In this paper, we provide a theoretical framework for understanding the self-improvement capabilities of self-play algorithms for theorem proving. First, we formalize the set of theorems as a graph, with nodes as theorems and edges between pairs of theorems with similar semantics. We introduce a set of primitive assumptions that characterize the guarantees of a trained prover and how a conjecturer can access the structure of the graph. Second, we show that if the underlying graph of theorems is well-connected, then a prover-conjecturer system, where the conjecturing algorithm is based on a reversible random walk, is sufficient to grow the set of proved theorems exponentially. Third, motivated by an issue encountered empirically by self-play algorithms, where the conjecturer tends to generate artificially complex and non-fundamental theorems, we propose a diversity measure for a training distribution of theorems generated by a conjecturer and an improved conjecturing algorithm that locally maximizes this diversity measure, by computing the diffusion similarity between neighboring theorems in the theorem graph. Finally, we describe a method to compute the diffusion similarity by using contrastive learning to embed nodes into Euclidean space and then computing the inner-product between embeddings.

</details>


### [417] [Continual Learning as a Multiphase Moving-Boundary Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01863)
*Snigdha Chandan Khilar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stefan-CL is a novel continual learning approach that uses melting physics metaphors to balance knowledge retention (stability) with learning new tasks (plasticity), achieving near-zero forgetting without storing raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning faces the fundamental stability-plasticity dilemma: how to retain past knowledge while absorbing new information without catastrophic forgetting of previous tasks.

Method: The method frames consolidated knowledge as a protected "solid" phase and unused network capacity as adaptable "liquid." It uses a moving boundary between these phases governed by a "latent heat" parameter that controls expansion. The approach mathematically freezes learned interior parameters while allowing adaptation in new regions.

Result: Stefan-CL reduces forgetting to near zero, matching performance of memory-heavy baselines that store raw data, without requiring explicit data storage. It provides strong protection against catastrophic forgetting.

Conclusion: Stefan-CL offers an elegant, physics-grounded solution to continual learning by using melting thermodynamics as inspiration for balancing stability and plasticity, creating a principled approach that avoids forgetting without data storage overhead.

Abstract: Continual learning struggles to balance retaining past knowledge with absorbing new tasks. Stefan-CL elegantly resolves this stability-plasticity dilemma through the physics of melting. It frames consolidated knowledge as a protected "solid" and unused capacity as an adaptable "liquid." As the network learns, this boundary expands, governed by a "latent heat" tuning dial. By mathematically freezing the learned interior, Stefan-CL cuts forgetting to near zero, matching memory-heavy baselines without storing raw data, forging a beautiful, physics-grounded path for AI.

</details>


### [418] [Task-Induced Representational Invariances Depend on Learning Objective in Deep RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01868)
*Manu Srinath Halvagal,Sebastian Lee,SueYeon Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: generate a too long; didn't read summary


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: describe the motivation in this paper

Method: method of this paper

Result: result of this paper

Conclusion: conclusion of this paper

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has long served as a model for goal-directed animal behavior in neuroscience. Modern deep RL has shown remarkable success across many domains, further strengthening this connection. The ability to learn abstract representations of high-dimensional state spaces underlies much of this success. However, theoretical understanding of these learned representations remains limited, hindering direct comparisons between models and animal learning. We address this gap by analyzing deep RL representations through the lens of MDP reduction theory. Investigating canonical RL algorithms in a navigation task, we find that even when performance is comparable, the value-based method (DQN) learns representations that are invariant to MDP homomorphism symmetries, while the policy-gradient method (PPO) learns representations invariant to action symmetries. These differences emerge consistently across domains, have downstream consequences for transfer learning, and appear in LLMs in a prompt-dependent manner. Our findings provide a principled approach to comparing learned representations across RL algorithms, with demonstrated practical implications and possible insights for neural coding in the brain.

</details>


### [419] [G2LoRA: Gradient Orthogonal Low-Rank Adaptation Framework for Graph Continual Learning on Text-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01873)
*Yuhan Wang,Yibo Ding,Yutong Ye,Mufan Zhao,Wenbo Zhang,Ruijie Wang,Jianxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: G2LoRA addresses catastrophic forgetting in LLM-as-Aligner models for text-attributed graphs through gradient projection and modulation techniques for continual learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-as-Aligner models for Text-Attributed Graphs suffer from severe catastrophic forgetting when sequentially fine-tuned on streaming tasks, with existing parameter-efficient fine-tuning insufficient to resolve task interference and ineffective knowledge transfer.

Method: G2LoRA: a continual learning framework that unifies different task levels under graph-text alignment, uses category-aware gradient projection in structured subspaces to reduce interference, applies gradient magnitude modulation to coordinate updates between modalities, and enables conditional backward transfer.

Result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show G2LoRA consistently outperforms strong baselines across different backbone architectures, achieving superior continual performance and transferability.

Conclusion: The proposed G2LoRA framework effectively addresses the catastrophic forgetting problem in LLM-as-Aligner models for TAGS through gradient projection and modulation techniques, demonstrating superior performance across various continual learning settings.

Abstract: LLM-as-Aligner has emerged as a prevalent pre-training paradigm for Text-Attributed Graphs(TAGS), aligning graph and text modalities into a shared embedding space via CLIP-style contrastive learning. While effective on individual downstream tasks, we observe severe catastrophic forgetting when such models are sequentially fine-tuned on streaming tasks. Although parameter-efficient fine-tuning alleviates forgetting to some extent, it remains insufficient to resolve task interference and ineffective knowledge transfer. In this work, we study graph continual learning for LLM-as-Aligner models on TAGs, with the goal of mitigating interference while promoting positive transfer across tasks. This setting introduces two fundamental challenges: (1) heterogeneous downstream tasks induce shifting optimization objectives, hindering unified fine-tuning; and (2) graph and text encoders exhibit different sensitivities to adaptation, making uncoordinated updates prone to misalignment. To address these challenges, we propose G2LoRA, a continual learning framework for TAGs. G2LoRA unifies node-, link-, and graph-level tasks under a single graph--text alignment objective, and enables consistent optimization across domain/class/task incremental modes. To reduce task interference while encouraging positive transfer, G2LoRA performs category-aware gradient projection in structured subspaces, resolving conflicting updates and enabling conditional backward transfer to balance forward and backward knowledge flow. To further prevent cross-modal drift, G2LoRA introduces gradient magnitude modulation to coordinate update rates between graph and text encoders. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that G2LoRA consistently outperforms strong baselines across different backbone architectures, achieving superior continual performance and transferability.

</details>


### [420] [Beyond the Simplex: Balanced Prototype Geometry for Scorer-Agnostic Open-Set Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01883)
*Mayank Sharma,Rohit Kumar Mourya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theoretical analysis extends simplex-ratio open-set recognition to all embedding dimensions via balanced equal-norm codes, showing controlled degradation in low dimensions and exponential rejection rate improvements, but practical performance still depends on scoring rule choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simplex-based methods for open-set recognition lack theoretical justification, with existing analysis only applying when embedding dimension d is at least C-1 (where regular simplex exists), limiting understanding in low-dimensional settings common in practice.

Method: Analysis of balanced equal-norm codes (prototypes with equal lengths and zero sum) as generalized simplex geometry, theoretical study of squared ratio score sublevel sets, defect parameter analysis, and empirical evaluation across CIFAR and MedMNIST datasets.

Result: 1) Theoretical account of simplex-ratio OSR valid for all embedding dimensions (including d < C-1) 2) Balanced equal-norm codes have exact Euclidean ball sublevel sets that bracket acceptance regions 3) Sharp dichotomy: prototypes attain one-distance symmetry if and only if d ≥ C-1, with controlled degradation below threshold 4) False-acceptance rate decays exponentially in dimension 5) Balanced geometry provides useful structure but raw ratio scores underperform nearest-neighbor and logit-based alternatives in practice.

Conclusion: The theoretical framework clarifies the geometry of simplex-ratio open-set recognition across all embedding dimensions, showing it can provide useful structure but practical performance depends critically on the scoring rule used.

Abstract: Open-set recognition (OSR) requires a classifier to reject inputs from unseen classes which is essential in safety-critical settings such as medical imaging. Simplex based methods, which fix class prototypes at the vertices of a regular simplex and then reject via a distance-ratio score, perform well empirically but lack theoretical justification, and existing analysis applies only when the embedding dimension d is at least C-1, which is the regime in which a regular simplex exists.
  We give a theoretical account of simplex-ratio OSR that holds in every embedding dimension, including d < C-1. Our analysis centers on balanced equal-norm codes: prototype configurations with equal lengths and zero sum, which exist for all d >= 2 and include the regular simplex as a special case. For these codes we show that an auxiliary squared ratio score has sublevel sets that are exact unions of Euclidean balls, which in turn bracket the acceptance region of the operational score; and we prove a sharp dichotomy: the prototypes attain one-distance symmetry, behaving like a regular simplex, if and only if d >= C-1, with controlled degradation governed by an explicit defect parameter below that threshold. We further show the false-acceptance rate decays exponentially in d under natural isotropy assumptions, and that the operational score is globally Lipschitz with compact acceptance regions.
  Empirically, we study balanced prototype geometry as both an analytic tool and a representation-learning prior, rather than as a stand-alone state-of-the-art detector. Across CIFAR and MedMNIST open-set splits, the geometry provides useful structure, but OSR performance remains strongly dependent on the scoring rule: raw ratio scores typically underperform nearest-neighbor and logit-based alternatives.

</details>


### [421] [Segment-driven Structural Induction and Semantic Alignment for Heterogeneous Tabular Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01890)
*Woojun Jung,Susik Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NAVI is a segment-centric pretraining framework that addresses heterogeneous table challenges by treating header-value pairs as units for aggregating schema-level structural and column-level distributional evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing encoders struggle with heterogeneous tables where headers vary but attribute semantics are shared, underusing column-level value distributions and applying uniform objectives across attributes with different semantic roles

Method: Masked Segment Modeling and Entropy-driven Segment Alignment techniques that enforce structured header-value coupling and semantic alignment across stable and instance-specific attributes

Result: Improved reconstruction, semantic consistency, and downstream utility across evaluation settings on heterogeneous in-domain tables

Conclusion: NAVI's segment-centric approach demonstrates improved performance in handling heterogeneous tables by leveraging structural and distributional evidence, with strong results across diverse evaluation settings.

Abstract: Real-world domains often contain heterogeneous tables whose headers vary while their underlying attribute semantics are shared, making it difficult to induce domain-specialized semantics from table-local evidence alone. Existing encoders model parts of this problem, but often underuse column-level value distributions and apply uniform objectives across attributes with different semantic roles. We propose NAVI, a segment-centric pretraining framework that treats each header-value pair as the unit for aggregating schema-level structural evidence and column-level distributional evidence. We realize this design through Masked Segment Modeling and Entropy-driven Segment Alignment, which jointly enforce structured header-value coupling and semantic alignment across stable and instance-specific attributes. Experiments on heterogeneous in-domain tables show improved reconstruction, semantic consistency, and downstream utility across evaluation settings overall.

</details>


### [422] [Private and Stable Test-Time Adaptation with Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01908)
*Zefeng Li,Qiaoyue Tang,Mathias Lecuyer,Evan Shelhamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers develop differentially private versions of test-time adaptation methods by applying per-sample gradient clipping and Gaussian noise, achieving privacy with minimal accuracy loss and even improving adaptation stability in some cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time adaptation (TTA) updates models during inference on new data, which creates privacy risks because model parameters become dependent on all past test inputs. The authors aim to address this privacy concern in TTA methods.

Method: The authors cast multiple popular TTA methods (Tent, EATA, SAR, DeYO, and COME) into differential privacy forms using per-sample gradient clipping and Gaussian noise for all updates during inference.

Result: DP-TTA methods provide adequate privacy with small accuracy costs on ImageNet-C. In low-privacy regimes, the clipping mechanism actually improves accuracy and adaptation stability in continual settings. These improvements come with modest computational overhead.

Conclusion: The paper introduces DP-TTA as the first results on private test-time adaptation, raising awareness about privacy issues in TTA and showing how per-sample clipping can improve adaptation stability and accuracy while providing differential privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) can reduce error on new and different data by updating the model on these inputs during inference. However, these updates raise the issue of privacy w.r.t. the testing data, because the model parameters now depend on all past inputs. To control this privacy risk, we cast multiple popular TTA methods (Tent, EATA, SAR, DeYO, and COME) into differential privacy (DP) forms that apply per-sample gradient clipping and Gaussian noise for all updates. On ImageNet-C, our DP-TTA methods provide adequate privacy at small cost to accuracy, and in the low-privacy regime the clipping mechanism of DP can even improve the accuracy and stability of adaptation in the continual setting. These improvements to privacy and accuracy come at only modest computational overhead. These first results on private TTA raise awareness of the issue, inform the development of more private test-time updates, and identify per-sample clipping as an effective technique for improving the accuracy and stability of adaptation.

</details>


### [423] [HMPO: Hybrid Median-length Policy Optimization for Chain-of-Thought Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01934)
*Minghui Zheng,Hongxu Chen,Huimin Ren,Hongsheng Xin,Xiaoyang Qu,Ze Wang,Shuling Yang,Ziyu Peng,Kaike Zhang,Pan Zhou,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HMPO is a reinforcement learning framework that efficiently compresses chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs using adaptive length budgets and optimized reward formulations, achieving 19-46% token compression with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CoT compression methods have several limitations: inflexible manual length budgets, computationally expensive multi-stage training pipelines, and poor scalability restricted to small models. This creates substantial inference overhead for LLMs using extended chain-of-thought reasoning.

Method: Proposes HMPO (Hybrid Median-length Policy Optimization), a single-stage reinforcement learning framework with three key components: 1) adaptive median-based budget derived from successful rollouts, 2) cosine-decay token reward for smooth length penalization, and 3) multiplicative reward formulation that prioritizes answer correctness to prevent reward hacking. Trained exclusively on mathematical data.

Result: Achieves 19%-46% token compression with negligible accuracy degradation across math, code, science, and instruction-following tasks. Scales effectively from 9B to 122B parameters across both dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures. Significantly reduces training costs compared to existing multi-stage baselines.

Conclusion: HMPO provides a cost-effective, scalable solution for CoT compression that eliminates manual length tuning, reduces training complexity, and maintains strong performance across diverse domains while substantially compressing token usage.

Abstract: Large language models achieve remarkable performance via extended chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, yet this lengthy process incurs substantial inference overhead. Existing CoT compression methods struggle with inflexible manual length budgets, computationally expensive multi-stage training pipelines, and fragile scalability restricted to small models. We propose HMPO (Hybrid Median-length Policy Optimization), a cost-effective, single-stage reinforcement learning framework. HMPO efficiently compresses CoT via three synergistic components: an adaptive median-based budget derived from successful rollouts to eliminate manual tuning, a cosine-decay token reward for smooth length penalization, and a multiplicative reward formulation that substantially mitigates trivial reward hacking by strictly prioritizing answer correctness. Trained exclusively on mathematical data, HMPO generalizes seamlessly across math, code, science, and instruction-following tasks. Extensive experiments scaling from 9B to 122B parameters across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures demonstrate that HMPO achieves 19%--46% token compression with negligible accuracy degradation, all while drastically reducing training costs compared to existing multi-stage baselines.

</details>


### [424] [Randomized Least Squares Value Iteration itself is Joint Differentially Private](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01952)
*Haiyang Lu,Pratik Gajane,Shaojie Bai,Mohammad Sadegh Talebi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Randomized exploration in RL algorithms like RLSVI provides inherent privacy protection - the noise used for exploration also ensures differential privacy without additional mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As RL applications expand to sensitive domains like healthcare and recommendation systems, privacy protection becomes essential. Existing approaches often require additional privacy mechanisms, but the authors investigate how randomized exploration itself can provide privacy.

Method: The paper analyzes Randomized Least Squares Value Iteration (RLSVI) in episodic RL settings. The authors prove that the noise injected for exploration in RLSVI simultaneously provides privacy protection through a novel privacy analysis approach.

Result: The paper proves that RLSVI achieves (ε(δ), δ)-joint differential privacy in tabular MDPs with ε(δ) = 2AK/(H²log(2HSA)) + 2√(2AKlog(1/δ)/(H²log(2HSA))), where S and A are states and actions, H is episode length, and K is number of episodes.

Conclusion: Randomized exploration in RL algorithms provides inherent privacy benefits - the noise used for exploration doubles as privacy protection mechanism, offering a privacy-preserving approach without additional mechanisms in sensitive applications.

Abstract: As reinforcement learning (RL) increasingly applies to sensitive domains, such as health care and recommendation systems, privacy-preserving techniques have become essential to protect users' sensitive information. We investigate privacy-preserving RL under an episodic setting, focusing on algorithms based on randomized exploration, such as Randomized Least Squares Value Iteration (RLSVI). The overall goal is to study how randomized exploration interacts with the injected noise required by privacy mechanisms. In this work, we show a new privacy analysis that characterizes how the noise in RLSVI set for exploration simultaneously provides privacy protection. Specifically, we prove that RLSVI is $(\varepsilon(δ),δ)$-joint differentially private in tabular MDP as is with $\varepsilon(δ) = \frac{2AK}{H^2\log(2HSA)} + 2\sqrt{\frac{2AK\log(1/δ)}{H^2\log(2HSA)}}$, where $S$ and $A$ are the number of states and actions respectively, $H$ is the length of an episode and $K$ is the number of episodes.

</details>


### [425] [Flow-Transformed Implicit Processes for Function-Space Variational Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01954)
*Luis A. Ortega,Andrés R. Masegosa,Thomas D. Nielsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FTIP uses normalizing flows instead of Gaussians for variational inference with implicit-process priors, enabling richer posterior uncertainty representation including asymmetry and multimodality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Performing posterior inference with implicit-process priors is challenging because their function-space distributions aren't available in closed form. Existing Gaussian variational approximations over combination weights limit representation of complex posterior shapes like asymmetry, heavy tails, and multimodality.

Method: Flow-Transformed Implicit Processes (FTIP) replaces the Gaussian variational distribution over combination weights with a normalizing flow to define a richer variational distribution, trained using a Black-Box α objective.

Result: FTIP captures asymmetric and multimodal posterior structure in function space that Gaussian approximations smooth or collapse, demonstrating superior flexibility in representing complex uncertainty patterns.

Conclusion: FTIP offers a more expressive approach to Bayesian function-space modeling with implicit-process priors by using normalizing flows for variational inference, successfully capturing complex posterior structures like asymmetry and multimodality that Gaussian approximations cannot handle.

Abstract: Implicit-process priors define distributions over functions through flexible generative mechanisms, making them attractive for Bayesian function-space modelling. However, performing posterior inference with such priors is challenging because their induced function-space distributions are typically not available in closed form. One practical strategy is to approximate the prior using a finite collection of sampled functions, and then represent posterior functions as learned combinations of these samples. Existing approaches commonly place a Gaussian variational distribution over the combination weights. While tractable, this choice limits the shapes of posterior uncertainty that can be represented, especially when the true posterior is asymmetric, heavy-tailed, or multimodal. We propose Flow-Transformed Implicit Processes (FTIP), a variational inference method that makes this finite-dimensional function-space approximation more expressive. Instead of using a Gaussian distribution over the combination weights, FTIP uses a normalizing flow to define a richer variational distribution. This induces a flexible posterior distribution over functions while preserving tractable optimization. We train the model using a Black-Box α objective, allowing us to compare mass-covering and mode-seeking variational behaviour. Experiments show that FTIP captures asymmetric and multimodal posterior structure in function space that Gaussian coefficient approximations tend to smooth or collapse.

</details>


### [426] [A Closer Look at In-Distribution vs. Out-of-Distribution Accuracy for Open-Set Test-time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.01973)
*Zefeng Li,Evan Shelhamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Open-set TTA methods struggle with OOD detection while adapting to new data; benchmarking reveals trade-offs between ID accuracy and OOD rejection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underexplored capability of open-set test-time adaptation methods to accurately detect out-of-distribution unknown classes while updating models on new data with input shifts.

Method: Benchmarking robust open-set TTA methods (SAR, OSTTA, UniEnt, SoTTA) on CIFAR-10-C and ImageNet-C with corrupted OOD datasets; evaluating accuracy and confidence for InD vs. OOD recognition; testing OOD detection techniques; varying OOD proportions and rates; proposing sigmoid/multi-label output baseline.

Result: First analysis showing current open-set TTA methods struggle to balance InD and OOD accuracy and imperfectly filter OOD data for adaptation updates.

Conclusion: Current open-set TTA methods struggle to balance in-distribution recognition and out-of-distribution rejection, and they imperfectly filter OOD data during adaptation, calling for better solutions that can maintain ID accuracy while effectively detecting and handling OOD inputs.

Abstract: Open-set test-time adaptation (TTA) updates models on new data in the presence of input shifts and unknown output classes. While recent methods have made progress on improving in-distribution (InD) accuracy for known classes, their ability to accurately detect out-of-distribution (OOD) unknown classes remains underexplored. We benchmark robust and open-set TTA methods (SAR, OSTTA, UniEnt, and SoTTA) on the standard corruption benchmarks of CIFAR-10-C at the small scale and ImageNet-C at the large scale. For CIFAR-10-C, we use OOD data from SVHN and CIFAR-100 in their respective corrupted forms of SVHN-C and CIFAR-100-C. For ImageNet-C, we use OOD data from ImageNet-O and Textures in their respective corrupted forms of ImageNet-O-C and Textures-C. ImageNet-O is nearer to ImageNet, as unknown but related object classes (like ''garlic bread'' vs. ''hot dog'' for food, or ''highway'' vs. ''dam'' for infrastructure), while Textures is farther from ImageNet, as non-object patterns (like ''cracked'' mud, ''porous'' sponge, ''veined'' leaves). We evaluate the accuracy and confidence of TTA methods for InD vs. OOD recognition on CIFAR-10-C and ImageNet-C. We verify the accuracy of each method's own OOD detection technique on CIFAR-10-C. We also evaluate on ImageNet-C and report both accuracy and standard OOD detection metrics. We further examine more realistic settings, in which the proportions and rates of OOD data can vary. To explore the trade-off between InD recognition and OOD rejection, we propose a new baseline that replaces softmax/multi-class output with sigmoid/multi-label output. Our analysis shows for the first time that current open-set TTA methods struggle to balance InD and OOD accuracy and that they only imperfectly filter OOD data for their own adaptation updates.

</details>


### [427] [Evaluating Real-World Generalizability of Algorithm Selection Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02016)
*Gjorgjina Cenikj,Jakub Kudela,Eva Tuba,Tome Eftimov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Study examines how well algorithm selection models generalize across synthetic benchmarks (BBOB, CEC) and real-world problems (robotics, UAV path-planning), identifying strengths and weaknesses of current approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the generalization ability of algorithm selection (AS) models across both synthetic and real-world optimization landscapes, examining how AS models transfer between domains and identifying challenges in applying AS in realistic, domain-specific contexts.

Method: The researchers conducted a systematic cross-benchmark evaluation using two academic benchmark suites (BBOB and CEC) and two real-world problem sets (robotics trajectory optimization tasks and unmanned aerial vehicle path-planning problems). They analyzed how AS models transfer between domains and identified where generalization succeeds or breaks down.

Result: The researchers identified where generalization succeeds or breaks down, highlighted the challenges that arise when applying AS in realistic, domain-specific contexts, and provided insights into AS model robustness.

Conclusion: The study provides insights into the robustness of current algorithm selection approaches and informs the development of more reliable, broadly applicable AS systems for real-world optimization.

Abstract: Algorithm Selection (AS) aims to automatically identify the most suitable optimization algorithm for a given problem instance by leveraging measurable problem characteristics and historical performance data. In this study, we investigate the generalization ability of AS models across both synthetic and real-world optimization landscapes. We consider two widely used academic benchmark suites (BBOB and CEC) and two real-world problem sets (robotics trajectory optimization tasks and unmanned aerial vehicle path-planning problems). Through a systematic cross-benchmark evaluation, we analyze how AS models transfer between domains, identify where generalization succeeds or breaks down, and highlight the challenges that arise when applying AS in realistic, domain-specific contexts. Our findings provide insights into the robustness of current AS approaches and inform the development of more reliable, broadly applicable AS systems for real-world optimization.

</details>


### [428] [Realistic noise synthesis reduces bias and improves tissue microstructure estimation with supervised machine learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02044)
*Bradley G. Karat,Maëliss Jallais,Ali R. Khan,Santiago Aja-Fernández,Jelle Veraart,Marco Palombo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Realistic noise synthesis in training data reduces bias in diffusion MRI microstructure estimation by addressing covariate shift from noise mismatches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrepancies between noise characteristics of simulated training data and acquired signals in diffusion MRI create covariate shift, leading to biased microstructure parameter estimation, especially at low SNR.

Method: Proposed a Realistic Noise Synthesis (RNS) framework that incorporates both the Rician expectation and effective post-processing noise variance into simulated training signals. The Rician expectation is modeled using MPPCA-estimated noise standard deviation, while effective standard deviation is derived from spherical harmonic residuals of preprocessed data.

Result: Ignoring magnitude-induced noise effects produced systematic, SNR-dependent parameter bias (especially at low SNR). Incorporating Rician expectation reduced bias to noise-aware nonlinear least-squares fitting levels. Modeling effective standard deviation further improved precision. Performance was architecture-independent but sensitive to accurate noise estimation.

Conclusion: Incorporating realistic noise modeling (both Rician expectation and effective noise variance) into simulated training data is essential for unbiased supervised microstructure parameter estimation, particularly in low-SNR regimes. This approach mitigates signal-domain covariate shift and matches the performance of noise-aware nonlinear least-squares fitting.

Abstract: Diffusion MRI enables non-invasive probing of tissue microstructure, but accurate parameter estimation is challenged by noise-related effects. In supervised machine learning frameworks trained on simulated data, discrepancies between the noise characteristics of simulated and acquired signals introduce a form of covariate shift, whereby the input signal distribution differs between training and inference. We investigated the impact of this mismatch on microstructure parameter estimation and propose a realistic noise synthesis (RNS) framework to mitigate it. RNS incorporates both the Rician expectation and the effective post-processing noise variance into simulated training signals. The Rician expectation was modelled using a noise standard deviation estimated with MPPCA, while the effective standard deviation was derived from spherical harmonic residuals of preprocessed data. The method was evaluated using the cylinder-zeppelin and the SANDI models on simulated datasets across multiple SNR levels and on in vivo diffusion data with repeated acquisitions. Sensitivity to noise misestimation was also assessed. Ignoring magnitude-induced noise effects during training produced systematic, SNR-dependent parameter bias, particularly at low SNR. Incorporating the Rician expectation substantially reduced bias to the level of noise-aware nonlinear least-squares fitting. Modelling the effective standard deviation further improved precision. Performance was largely independent of regression architecture but sensitive to accurate noise estimation. These findings demonstrate that realistic noise modelling in simulated training data mitigates signal-domain covariate shift and is essential for unbiased supervised microstructure estimation, particularly in low-SNR regimes associated with high b-values or high spatial resolution.

</details>


### [429] [Ablating Archetypes: The Stability of Archetypal SAEs is an Artifact of Initialization and Metric Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02061)
*Michał Brzozowski,Neo Christopher Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Archetypal SAEs' stability claims are due to shared initialization, not the method itself. Removing initialization removes the advantage, highlighting need for better stability diagnostics in dictionary learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify the distinction between stability and stabilization in SAE feature extraction, and to critically evaluate whether archetypal SAEs genuinely improve stability or if their reported stability is due to shared initialization across runs.

Method: Analyzed archetypal SAEs by ablating their deterministic k-means decoder initialization and comparing inter-run dictionary distances. Also examined preprocessing-dependent cosine geometry issues affecting stability metrics.

Result: When the deterministic k-means initialization is removed, archetypal SAEs show no stabilization advantage over standard SAEs. The claimed stability is largely an artifact of identical initialization, not the architectural constraint.

Conclusion: The study emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between stability (agreement between independently trained models) and stabilization (convergence from different initializations), and shows that archetypal SAEs' claimed stability mainly stems from shared initialization rather than the architectural constraint itself. It highlights the need for trajectory diagnostics and initialization ablations in stability claims for SAEs.

Abstract: Dictionary learning with sparse autoencoders (SAEs) produces overcomplete bases from neural network activations that are often interpretable and reduces polysemanticity. However, features from SAEs vary substantially across random seeds -- a problem known as instability. Archetypal SAEs (Fel et al., 2025) were proposed as a general dictionary-learning intervention for more reliable concept extraction, and report more stable dictionaries at the end of training. We demonstrate that the stability claimed by archetypal SAEs is a result of setting identical initialization across multiple runs. Through our analyses, we attempt to clarify two distinct notions in mechanistic interpretability that may be ambiguously used: stability is agreement between two independently trained models, whereas stabilization is the convergence of independently initialized runs toward a common solution. This distinction is critical for mechanistic interpretability of natural language processing (NLP), where feature stability is increasingly used as evidence that SAE features are reusable units of analysis. Experiments from archetypal SAEs share a deterministic k-means decoder initialization, setting inter-run dictionary distance to zero before training begins. When this initialization is removed, the archetypal constraint provides no stabilization advantage in our setting. We further identify a preprocessing-dependent cosine geometry issue that complicates interpretation of endpoint stability metrics. Overall, our study supports the value of studying SAEs within the larger dictionary-learning tradition while showing that stability claims require trajectory diagnostics and initialization ablations.

</details>


### [430] [Planar Symmetric Pattern Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02073)
*Ning Lin,Luxi Chen,Huaguan Chen,Jiacheng Cen,Chongxuan Li,Wenbing Huang,Hao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a framework to transform any 2D continuous representation into one with planar group symmetry while preserving continuity, validated across visual and material design tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 2D continuous representations struggle to enforce planar group symmetries without disrupting continuity, particularly with non-reflective group elements. This limits practical applications requiring specific symmetries.

Method: Develops a symmetrization framework that mathematically transforms any 2D continuous representation into a symmetric one. Provides the formulation, proves approximation capability for symmetric functions, and details construction methodology.

Result: Experiments across four tasks (pattern design, paper-cutting design, stylized topology design, and material design) confirm the representation enables effective symmetry control and demonstrates broader applicability.

Conclusion: The paper successfully establishes a framework for creating symmetric 2D representations while maintaining continuity, with validation across both visual and material design domains.

Abstract: Generating objects with specific symmetries is essential in various real-world scenarios. However, adapting existing 2D continuous representations to enforce planar group symmetry remains a challenge, as the transformation of non-reflective group elements may disrupt continuity. To overcome this limitation, we propose a symmetrization framework for arbitrary planar groups. Our method transforms any 2D continuous representation into a symmetric one while preserving continuity. We provide the mathematical formulation of this representation, demonstrate its approximation capability for symmetric functions, and detail the construction methodology. We validate our approach through three visual design tasks (pattern design, paper-cutting design and stylized topology design) and one material design task. Experiments confirm that our representation enables effective symmetry control and demonstrate its broader applicability.

</details>


### [431] [Beyond $\ell_2$-norm and $\ell_\infty$-norm: A Curvature-Inspired $\ell_p$-Norm Scheme for Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02078)
*Jianhao Xu,Zhuang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel dynamic $ℓ_p$-norm optimizer that adapts to curvature changes in DNN training, starting with large p to suppress high-curvature dominance and gradually decreasing p for stable updates, achieving better generalization with theoretical convergence guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DNN optimizers using $ℓ_2$ or $ℓ_\infty$ norms fail to adapt to changing curvature anisotropy during training: $ℓ_2$-based optimizers get dominated by high-curvature directions, slowing convergence, while $ℓ_\infty$-based optimizers oscillate in flatter regions.

Method: Introduces LPSGD and LPSGDM optimizers based on a dynamic $ℓ_p$-norm scheme where p starts large (p>2) to suppress high-curvature direction dominance in early training, then gradually decreases toward 2 for stable refined updates using a cosine annealing strategy.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows $O(T^{-1/2})$ convergence rate for nonconvex settings. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K with VGG-11, ResNet-18, ResNet-50 demonstrate better generalization performance compared to existing optimizers.

Conclusion: Proposes a new $ℓ_p$-norm scheme with dynamic p values that bridges the gap between $ℓ_2$ and $ℓ_\infty$ norms, leading to optimizers (LPSGD and LPSGDM) that demonstrate better generalization performance and address curvature anisotropy issues throughout DNN training.

Abstract: The existing optimizers for deep neural networks (DNNs) typically rely on either the $\ell_2$ norm or the $\ell_\infty$ norm, resulting in optimizers that do not adapt well to substantial changes in curvature across parameter dimensions. Generally, the training process of DNNs often exhibits strong curvature anisotropy in the early period, whereas in the later period, the training process of DNNs tends to move toward flatter regions with weaker anisotropy. Particularly, optimizers based on the \(\ell_2\)-norm are usually dominated by high-curvature directions, restricting updates of optimizers along with lower curvature direction and thus leading to a slower convergence rate. While optimizers based on the \(\ell_\infty\)-norm are prone to oscillations in flatter regions, due to the coordinate-wise updates of the same magnitude. To address these two extreme cases generated by $\ell_2$ and $\ell_\infty$ norms, we propose a novel $\ell_p$-norm scheme with a dynamical value of $p$ and incorporate it into stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and SGD with momentum (SGDM), leading to two novel optimizers with better generalization performance: ${\ell_p}$-SGD (LPSGD) and ${\ell_p}$-SGDM (LPSGDM). Particularly, the resulting optimizers suppress the dominance of high-curvature directions in the early period by utilizing a large $p$ ($p>2$), followed by a gradual decrease of $p$ toward 2 to enable more stable and refined updates, where the latter process is motivated by the cosine annealing strategy. We establish theoretical guarantees of the resulting algorithms and analyze that both LPSGD and LPSGDM achieve an \(O(T^{-1/2})\) convergence rate for the nonconvex setting. Extensive experiments are conducted on benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-1K, with multiple DNNs such as VGG-11, ResNet-18, and ResNet-50.

</details>


### [432] [When Tabular Foundation Models Transfer Across Modalities: A Systematic Evaluation Across 95 Datasets, 7 Modalities, and Two Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02106)
*Julien Lafrance*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified classification pipeline using ETF preprocessing and tabular foundation model works across 7 modalities, competitive with lightweight baselines, 4-200x faster than fine-tuning, with reliable confidence calibration for deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a unified classification approach that works across multiple signal modalities without extensive task-specific tuning, while maintaining competitive performance and significantly faster inference speeds compared to full backbone fine-tuning.

Method: The method combines Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) preprocessing with a tabular foundation model for in-context inference. The pipeline processes data mapped to fixed vector representations across different modalities, with specific implementation details for deployment including training stopping without validation splits, in-context classifier setup, and probability calibration.

Result: The pipeline is broadly competitive with strong lightweight tuned baselines on frozen features across 95 datasets from seven modalities. It doesn't match the absolute best specialized models but stays close while running 4-200 times faster than full fine-tuning, often at comparable quality. The calibration process produces well-calibrated probabilities useful for confidence-gated deployment.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that this unified classification pipeline offers a practical alternative to specialized fine-tuning for many applications, providing competitive performance with dramatically faster inference speeds and reliable confidence calibration for deployment.

Abstract: We present a single classification pipeline that combines an Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF) preprocessing stage with a tabular foundation model for in-context inference, applied identically across modalities once data is mapped to fixed vector representations. We evaluate it on 95 datasets spanning seven signal modalities -- vision, audio, speech, text, molecular, time-series, and tabular. The main methodological contribution is to fix the comparison object: throughout the paper, performance is judged against the strongest lightweight tuned baseline on the same frozen features, while oracle selection, deployed selection, and specialized fine-tuning are reported separately.
  The pipeline is broadly competitive with strong lightweight tuned baselines on the same frozen features. It does not match the very best specialized models or heavily tuned pipelines on every task, but it stays close, and it runs much faster -- typically 4 to 200 times faster than full backbone fine-tuning, often at comparable quality.
  We describe how to deploy the pipeline in practice: when to apply ETF preprocessing, how to stop its training without a validation split, how to set up the in-context classifier, and how to calibrate the resulting probabilities. The calibration step is non-cosmetic: TabICL produces well-calibrated probabilities by construction, ETF preprocessing initially disrupts that calibration, and the post-hoc rescaling restores it -- yielding a per-prediction confidence signal that practitioners can use as a trust threshold for confidence-gated deployment. We also report where the pipeline should not be expected to help, and how to identify those cases in advance.

</details>


### [433] [Edge-aware Decoding for Neural Asymmetric Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02136)
*Li Liang,Jinbiao Chen,Zizhen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an edge-aware decoder for neural asymmetric routing that explicitly exposes transition-level edge information to address representation--decision mismatch, improving performance on asymmetric TSP and vehicle routing problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a representation--decision mismatch in neural asymmetric routing models where pairwise cost information is encoded upstream but the final candidate logit is parameterized as context--node compatibility, rather than explicitly considering the directed transition nature of routing decisions.

Method: Proposed an edge-aware decoder that adds candidate-specific terms for the current directed edge, return-to-start closure, and static lightweight lookahead while keeping the representation backbone fixed. Tested on ATSP and ACVRP problems with controlled backbones like SVD/Sinkhorn, using zero-shot evaluation and ablation studies.

Result: The edge-aware decoder improved performance over the RADAR reference model, reducing the ATSP-1000 gap from 4.13% to 2.73% when trained on ATSP-100 and evaluated zero-shot on various problem sizes. On ACVRP, similar qualitative improvements were observed. Ablation studies showed the current directed edge term had the strongest effect, with closure and static lookahead acting as heuristic continuation cues.

Conclusion: The study provides mechanistic insights into decoder design for neural asymmetric routing models, emphasizing the importance of explicitly exposing transition-level edge information at decision time to improve performance and reduce the representation--decision mismatch.

Abstract: Neural asymmetric routing models increasingly encode directionality through matrix representations and asymmetry-aware attention. The final routing action, however, is not a node in isolation but a directed transition chosen under the current partial route. This creates a representation--decision mismatch: pairwise cost information may be encoded upstream while the final candidate logit is still largely parameterized as context--node compatibility. We propose a decoder-design principle for neural asymmetric routing: the final score should explicitly expose transition-level quantities suggested by the problem's cost-to-go structure. We instantiate this principle with an edge-aware decoder that adds candidate-specific terms for the current directed edge, return-to-start closure, and static lightweight lookahead, while keeping the representation backbone fixed. On a controlled SVD/Sinkhorn asymmetric backbone, the decoder improves over the RADAR reference when trained on ATSP-100 and evaluated zero-shot on ATSP-100/200/500/1000, reducing the ATSP-1000 gap from $4.13\%$ to $2.73\%$. On ACVRP, the same score-level modification shows the same qualitative trend under a richer routing state. ATSP ablations and directed-transition diagnostics sharpen the mechanism: the strongest evidence concerns sensitivity to the current directed edge, while closure and static lookahead act as heuristic continuation cues. The results support a mechanism study: a key decoder-side signal in neural asymmetric routing is decision-time exposure of transition-level edge information.

</details>


### [434] [TimeBlocks: Foundational and Continual Time-Series Blockbase -- Extended Version](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.02142)
*David Campos,Bin Yang,Tung Kieu,Lei Chen,Chenjuan Guo,Christian S. Jensen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeBlocks: Modular framework for building lightweight, versatile time-series models for real-time streaming with continual calibration support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundational time-series models are too large and ineffective for real-time data stream processing due to computational constraints and lack of continual calibration support.

Method: Maintains a pool of interchangeable modular model blocks. Uses a routing strategy to iteratively select suitable blocks for constructing lightweight models specific to data. Employs StreamCore method to build representative subset of data stream for continual calibration.

Result: Experimental study on multiple datasets shows TimeBlocks enables building models that outperform existing baselines across multiple tasks.
